**Age 28 (674 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 29 (675 TR)** — He lay awake one night with nothing in particular on his mind. He got up before dawn and walked until it was light.
**Age 29 (675 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 30 (676 TR)** — A tutor, a priest, or simply a talented neighbour opened music to him.
**Age 31 (677 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 32 (678 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 32 (678 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Alura Talekar. week 40 Alura Talekar born (female)
**Age 33 (679 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 34 (680 TR)** — He was taken into a lord's household, a position of modest honour and real obligation.
**Age 34 (680 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 35 (681 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 35 (681 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Berith Talekar came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Berith Talekar born (female)
**Age 35 (681 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Yarath Talekar died (age 1)

**Age 36 (682 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 36 (682 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 37 (683 TR)** — Through hard work and some luck, the last creditor was satisfied.
**Age 38 (684 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.

**Age 38 (684 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 39 (685 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 40 (686 TR)** — The accounts and obligations of the estate fell to him.
**Age 40 (686 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (687 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 42 (688 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 42 (688 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.

**Age 43 (689 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing.
**Age 43 (689 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 44 (690 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.

**Age 44 (690 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 45 (691 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.

**Age 45 (691 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 46 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 46 (692 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Beren Talekar born (male, lame)
**Age 47 (693 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 47 (693 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 48 (694 TR)** — He rode at the lists and acquitted himself respectably.
**Age 48 (694 TR)** — His wife's family connections opened new doors.
**Age 49 (695 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.

**Age 49 (695 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed with a religious house. It was the best provision he could make. He visited when he could. He did not always visit. Beren Talekar placed in religious house (age 3, lame)
**Age 50 (696 TR)** — His father was dead and the land was his. Grief and obligation arrived together.
**Age 50 (696 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 8
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Alura Talekar's illegitimate child born (female, Alura Talekar age 19)
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — Alura Talekar's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Alura Talekar's face. Alura Talekar's child died (age 0)
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — His daughter Berith Talekar married Zoric Weaver. Berith Talekar married Zoric Weaver (artisan, age 16)
**Age 52 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (698 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 52 (698 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Berith Talekar's child born (male, Berith Talekar age 17)

**Age 53 (699 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 53 (699 TR)** — Berith Talekar left home to make his own way. Berith Talekar left home (age 18)
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — His daughter Alura Talekar married Yoric Bowyer. Alura Talekar married Yoric Bowyer (artisan, age 26)
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (701 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 55 (701 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 56 (702 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 56 (702 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.

**Age 57 (703 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 58 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 59 (705 TR)** — The Rekela's representative struck his shoulders in the mud of the breach, not at a ceremony but in the moment after the gate fell, with men dying around them and the standard still standing. He had not expected it. He had not expected the weight of it either — not the sword's weight, but the rest.
**Age 59 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (706 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 60 (706 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 60 (706 TR)** — Berith Talekar's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Berith Talekar's face. Berith Talekar's child died (age 1)
**Age 61 (707 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 62 (708 TR)** — He drew up his will and distributed what he could while he still could. It was a practical matter. He was surprised at how much it felt like something else.
**Age 62 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (708 TR)** — His son Beren Talekar married Emra Norman. Beren Talekar married Emra Norman (peasant, age 16)

**Age 63 (709 TR)** — He handed the last of it over — the keys, the ledgers, the daily decisions — and did not immediately know what to do with himself. The first month was strange. The second was less so. By the third he had discovered that there were things he had wanted to read for thirty years and had not, and that the garden was more interesting than he had appreciated when he was always somewhere else. He does not miss the work. He is surprised he does not miss it more.
**Age 63 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (709 TR)** — Beren Talekar died — the lame child he had worried over for years. Beren Talekar died (age 17)

**Age 64 (710 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 64 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (711 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 65 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 66 (712 TR)** — He set down what he remembered, for whoever might read it after him.
**Age 67 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 67 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 68 (714 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth.
**Age 68 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 68 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 69 (715 TR)** — The factor he had trusted with his accounts for four years had been selling information about his business to a competitor — or to someone who had hired the competitor as a front. He did not know where the thread ultimately led. He knew that every conversation in that office had been heard by someone else.
**Age 70 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 71 (717 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant.
**Age 71 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 71 (717 TR)** — Alura Talekar died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Alura Talekar died (age 39)
**Age 72 (718 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 72 (718 TR)** — Halla Talekar died. They had been married 48 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Halla Talekar died (approx. age 72)
**Age 72 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 73 (719 TR)** — Circumstance put him in front of an audience. He found he could hold them.
**Age 73 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 74 (720 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 74 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

name: Cade Talekar

narrative: **Age 19 (671 TR)** — He said something he had intended only for one person at a volume that reached several others. He is still not certain how much they heard.
**Age 20 (672 TR)** — An old soldier at a fire told a story about a battle he had no business being at. The detail was too precise to be invented.
**Age 21 (673 TR)** — Lineage mattered. He learned to read shields and know their histories.
**Age 22 (674 TR)** — A tutor, a priest, or simply a talented neighbour opened music to him.
**Age 23 (675 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 24 (676 TR)** — He walked into the wrong house by mistake. The people inside were eating. Everyone was very polite about it.
**Age 25 (677 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 26 (678 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.

**Age 27 (679 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.

**Age 28 (680 TR)** — He would not call it a miracle to someone he did not know. He would say: the standard had fallen and the line was breaking, and then the light changed, and the line held. He had seen men stand who had been running a moment before and he could not explain it. He did not try to explain it in the report. He wrote that the line held.

**Age 29 (681 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. He spent considerable time afterward determining what a year's service meant and whether he was fulfilling it adequately.
**Age 30 (682 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
**Age 31 (683 TR)** — His name was called from the gallery and he wore the favour of victory.
**Age 32 (684 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.

**Age 33 (685 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence.
**Age 34 (686 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Garith Farekar (noble)
**Age 35 (687 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Ceris Ralendi (noble, age 31)
**Age 35 (687 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Orla Ralendi born (female)
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — He stood at a window watching rain and lost track of time. He had things to do. He watched the rain.
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 37 (689 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.

**Age 38 (690 TR)** — He had been waiting months for access to a particular merchant. The introduction came from an unexpected direction — a Halean Shenasene at a private gathering who mentioned his name to the right person with the right word attached. He never learned what the priestess received in return. He assumed the accounting was accurate.
**Age 38 (690 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 39 (691 TR)** — He watched a true expert do something he had done himself for years. He understood the difference immediately.

**Age 40 (692 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.

**Age 40 (692 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 41 (693 TR)** — He rode at the lists and acquitted himself respectably.

**Age 41 (693 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 42 (694 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.
**Age 42 (694 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.

**Age 43 (695 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value.
**Age 43 (695 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — He spent evenings on the tower roof, mapping stars and reading futures.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 45 (697 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 45 (697 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 37
**Age 46 (698 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 47 (699 TR)** — He set down what he remembered, for whoever might read it after him.
**Age 47 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (701 TR)** — He had a dream that he kept to himself. He thought about it for years without ever describing it.
**Age 49 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 50 (702 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 50 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — A disagreement that should have been small became something permanent. He was not sure who was more at fault. Fell out with Garith Farekar
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 52 (704 TR)** — Another family's child came to live under his roof — to learn, to serve as a page, or simply as a pledge of alliance. He found himself responsible for someone else's son. Took in Disa Maramin as ward
**Age 52 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (705 TR)** — His wife's family connections opened new doors.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 55 (707 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 55 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (707 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Orla Ralendi's illegitimate child born (male, Orla Ralendi age 20)

**Age 56 (708 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 56 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.

**Age 60 (712 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
**Age 60 (712 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20
**Age 60 (712 TR)** — His daughter Orla Ralendi married Lucan Ralendi. Orla Ralendi married Lucan Ralendi (noble, age 29)
**Age 61 (713 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing.
**Age 61 (713 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Orla Ralendi's child born (male, Orla Ralendi age 26)
**Age 62 (714 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 63 (715 TR)** — A large snake crossed the road directly in front of him. The carter with him spat and refused to go on until they had prayed.

**Age 63 (715 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 63 (715 TR)** — Orla Ralendi left home to make his own way. Orla Ralendi left home (age 28)
**Age 64 (716 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 64 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (717 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.

**Age 66 (718 TR)** — He handed the last of it over — the keys, the ledgers, the daily decisions — and did not immediately know what to do with himself. The first month was strange. The second was less so. By the third he had discovered that there were things he had wanted to read for thirty years and had not, and that the garden was more interesting than he had appreciated when he was always somewhere else. He does not miss the work. He is surprised he does not miss it more.
**Age 66 (718 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.

**Age 67 (719 TR)** — The letter arrived with no signature. It described, in precise and accurate detail, something he had done three years ago that he had believed no one knew. The terms were not unreasonable. That was almost the worst part — whoever had sent it understood exactly what the information was worth and had priced it accordingly.
**Age 67 (719 TR)** — Ceris Ralendi died. They had been married 32 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Ceris Ralendi died (approx. age 63)
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — The accounts and obligations of the estate fell to him.

The detail was too precise to be invented. **Age 21 (673 TR)** — Lineage mattered. He would say: the standard had fallen and the line was breaking, and then the light changed, and the line held. He had seen men stand who had been running a moment before and he could not explain it. He did not try to explain it in the report. He wrote that the line held. **Age 29 (681 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 33 (685 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence. **Age 34 (686 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. name: Halric Ralendi

narrative: **Age 18 (676 TR)** — He fixed something that had been broken for years, that everyone had simply learned to work around. No one had asked him to.

**Age 19 (677 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.
**Age 20 (678 TR)** — Lineage mattered. He learned to read shields and know their histories.
**Age 21 (679 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 22 (680 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 23 (681 TR)** — Circumstance put him in front of an audience. He found he could hold them.
**Age 24 (682 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Calla Lalendi (noble, age 19)
**Age 24 (682 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Uther Lalendi — survived. week 40 Uther Lalendi born (male)
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 26 (684 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 26 (684 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Tovan Lalendi. week 40 Tovan Lalendi born (male)
**Age 27 (685 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 27 (685 TR)** — The child came at term and did not live. He had thought the danger past. week 38
**Age 28 (686 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands.
**Age 28 (686 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Breda Lalendi came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Breda Lalendi born (female)
**Age 29 (687 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 29 (687 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 30 (688 TR)** — The first real cold came and he realised summer was over. He stood in it for a moment before going inside.
**Age 30 (688 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 31 (689 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Sirath Lalendi born (female)

**Age 33 (691 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 33 (691 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Uther Lalendi fostered (age 9)

**Age 34 (692 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 34 (692 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 37 (695 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 37 (695 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 38 (696 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.

**Age 39 (697 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — His son Uther Lalendi married Melyn Hayward. Uther Lalendi married Melyn Hayward (artisan, age 16)
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Uther Lalendi's child born (female, Uther Lalendi age 16)
**Age 41 (699 TR)** — He gave a traveller confident directions and realised immediately after that he had them backwards. He could not find the man again.

**Age 42 (700 TR)** — There is a line between ruination and destitution that is crossed when the last room is gone. Ruination is a category with paperwork — debts, creditors, the humiliation of the guild's accounting. Destitution is simpler. He had a coat and a knife and knowledge of the city. He crossed the line and the city changed. The parts of it that had been background became foreground: which streets have shelter from the east wind, which baker puts unsold bread outside at what hour, which guild members ignore you and which look through you. He began using what he knew. Some of what he did in the first weeks he does not revisit.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — Yorin Lalendi died young — a child still. Yorin Lalendi died (age 7)
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Tovan Lalendi's illegitimate child born (male, Tovan Lalendi age 17)
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Idris Lalendi died (age 0)
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Orlan Tarsen died
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 46 (704 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 46 (704 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — His son Tovan Lalendi married Welith Lalendi. Tovan Lalendi married Welith Lalendi (noble, age 21)
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 48 (706 TR)** — He has learned to beg without performing poverty, which is counterintuitively more effective. The performance signals professional beggar to anyone who has lived in a city long enough; the authentic version — standing in the right place, making eye contact without demanding, holding position long enough that the discomfort of not giving becomes greater than the discomfort of giving — is slower but more reliable. He makes roughly what a day labourer makes, on a good day. On a bad day he makes nothing and he knows which doorway is least exposed to the night air.
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Britta Harman
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — Uther Lalendi's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Uther Lalendi's face. Uther Lalendi's child died (age 2)
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 50 (708 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 50 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 51 (709 TR)** — They had done everything that could be done. He had watched them do it. The Ebasethe had sat with her through the last night and had been there at the end in a way he had found, afterward and somewhat to his surprise, deeply comforting — not because it had changed anything, but because she had not been alone.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — Tovan Lalendi left home to make his own way. Tovan Lalendi left home (age 25)
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — Breda Lalendi left home to make his own way. Breda Lalendi left home (age 23)
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — His daughter Sirath Lalendi married Fardan Roring. Sirath Lalendi married Fardan Roring (unguilded, age 21)
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Rorik Ersen
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — His daughter Breda Lalendi married Rorik Yorsen. Breda Lalendi married Rorik Yorsen (unguilded, age 28)
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Tera Elsen
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — Breda Lalendi died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Breda Lalendi died (age 25)
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 29

**Age 55 (713 TR)** — The man needed someone to carry a package across town after dark without being searched at the night watch. He paid three times what the package was worth to carry. He did not ask what was in the package. He knew what was in the package. He carried it, collected the payment, and spent two weeks not spending it all at once so that no one would ask where it came from. He has done similar work three times since. He is aware of what he is building toward — not what he wants to build toward, but what the situation is building toward whether he chooses it or not.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 56 (714 TR)** — The young person he had invested time in had found their footing. He heard it from others before he saw it himself. He was glad, and slightly surprised at how glad. Tera Elsen found their own way
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 57 (715 TR)** — He took bread from a baker's cart when the baker was looking the other way. This is not the kind of theft he would have considered possible three years ago. He considers it now with a directness he finds clarifying: the baker will not miss the bread; he will; this is therefore the correct allocation of the bread. He notices that this kind of reasoning has become easier since he has been here. He keeps a record of the things that have become easier and monitors it, which is either wisdom or futility and he is not sure which.
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 19
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — Sirath Lalendi's child Kelan Lalendi died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Sirath Lalendi's child died (age 4)
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; he took the risk.
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — Calla Lalendi died. They had been married 34 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Calla Lalendi died (approx. age 53)
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — His son Uther Lalendi remarried. Uther Lalendi remarried Sirath Lalendi (noble, age 38)

**Age 59 (717 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 60 (718 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — He reached out, or they did. The thing between them was old enough to look smaller than it had. Reconciled with Rorik Ersen
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 62 (720 TR)** — He drew up his will and distributed what he could while he still could. It was a practical matter. He was surprised at how much it felt like something else.
**Age 62 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 28** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [noble → unguilded]
**Age 42** *(class change)* — Fell below ruination into genuine homelessness [unguilded → destitute]
narrativeText: **Age 18 (676 TR)** — He fixed something that had been broken for years, that everyone had simply learned to work around. No one had asked him to. **Age 19 (677 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting. **Age 20 (678 TR)** — Lineage mattered. He learned to read shields and know their histories. **Age 21 (679 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking. **Age 22 (680 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment. **Age 23 (681 TR)** — Circumstance put him in front of an audience. He found he could hold them. **Age 24 (682 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Calla Lalendi (noble, age 19) **Age 24 (682 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Uther Lalendi — survived. week 40 Uther Lalendi born (male) **Age 25 (683 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 25 (683 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 26 (684 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way. **Age 26 (684 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Tovan Lalendi. week 40 Tovan Lalendi born (male) **Age 27 (685 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew. **Age 27 (685 TR)** — The child came at term and did not live. He had thought the danger past. week 38 **Age 28 (686 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands. **Age 28 (686 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Breda Lalendi came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Breda Lalendi born (female) **Age 29 (687 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 29 (687 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 30 (688 TR)** — The first real cold came and he realised summer was over. He stood in it for a moment before going inside. **Age 30 (688 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 31 (689 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean. **Age 32 (690 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 32 (690 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Sirath Lalendi born (female) **Age 33 (691 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record. **Age 33 (691 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Uther Lalendi fostered (age 9) **Age 34 (692 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 34 (692 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 35 (693 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 35 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 36 (694 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name. **Age 36 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 37 (695 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 37 (695 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 38 (696 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 39 (697 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 39 (697 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 40 (698 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 40 (698 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 40 (698 TR)** — His son Uther Lalendi married Melyn Hayward. Uther Lalendi married Melyn Hayward (artisan, age 16) **Age 40 (698 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Uther Lalendi's child born (female, Uther Lalendi age 16) **Age 41 (699 TR)** — He gave a traveller confident directions and realised immediately after that he had them backwards. He could not find the man again. **Age 42 (700 TR)** — There is a line between ruination and destitution that is crossed when the last room is gone. Ruination is a category with paperwork — debts, creditors, the humiliation of the guild's accounting. Destitution is simpler. He had a coat and a knife and knowledge of the city. He crossed the line and the city changed. The parts of it that had been background became foreground: which streets have shelter from the east wind, which baker puts unsold bread outside at what hour, which guild members ignore you and which look through you. He began using what he knew. Some of what he did in the first weeks he does not revisit. **Age 42 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 42 (700 TR)** — Yorin Lalendi died young — a child still. Yorin Lalendi died (age 7) **Age 43 (701 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before. **Age 43 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 43 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 43 (701 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Tovan Lalendi's illegitimate child born (male, Tovan Lalendi age 17) **Age 44 (702 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 44 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 44 (702 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Idris Lalendi died (age 0) **Age 45 (703 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Orlan Tarsen died **Age 45 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (704 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency. **Age 46 (704 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered. **Age 47 (705 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 47 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (705 TR)** — His son Tovan Lalendi married Welith Lalendi. Tovan Lalendi married Welith Lalendi (noble, age 21) **Age 47 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (706 TR)** — He has learned to beg without performing poverty, which is counterintuitively more effective. He makes roughly what a day labourer makes, on a good day. On a bad day he makes nothing and he knows which doorway is least exposed to the night air. **Age 48 (706 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. name: Uther Lalendi

narrative: **Age 18 (682 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. He spent considerable time afterward determining what a year's service meant and whether he was fulfilling it adequately.
**Age 19 (683 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the forearm or hand that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 20 (684 TR)** — His father was dead and the land was his. Grief and obligation arrived together.
**Age 21 (685 TR)** — He was taken into a lord's household, a position of modest honour and real obligation.
**Age 22 (686 TR)** — He heard footsteps behind him stop when his stopped. He turned. There was no one there.

**Age 23 (687 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).

**Age 24 (688 TR)** — He would not call it a miracle to someone he did not know. He would say: the standard had fallen and the line was breaking, and then the light changed, and the line held. He had seen men stand who had been running a moment before and he could not explain it. He did not try to explain it in the report. He wrote that the line held.
**Age 25 (689 TR)** — A cousin or distant kinsman had decided the estate was rightfully his. The dispute would be settled in a lord's court, or not settled at all. Either way, it would cost years.
**Age 26 (690 TR)** — A secret entrusted to him found its way to those who would pay for it.

**Age 27 (691 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 28 (692 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 30 (694 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands.
**Age 31 (695 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life.

**Age 32 (696 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.

**Age 33 (697 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 34 (698 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 35 (699 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Mira Torsen (unguilded, age 35)
**Age 35 (699 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Levan Faramin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Levan Faramin born (male)

**Age 36 (700 TR)** — With his second parent gone he was, in some sense he had not felt before, finally alone in the world. The house he grew up in would be sold or given over. He found he was not ready for that.
**Age 36 (700 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Yoric Faramin born (male)
**Age 36 (700 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Yoric Faramin died (age 0)
**Age 37 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 38 (702 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 38 (702 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.

**Age 39 (703 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.

**Age 40 (704 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 40 (704 TR)** — Mira Torsen died. They had been married 5 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Mira Torsen died (approx. age 40)
**Age 41 (705 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 41 (705 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 42 (706 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 43 (707 TR)** — He ruined something he had spent a long time making. He did not speak for the rest of that day.
**Age 43 (707 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 44 (708 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.

**Age 45 (709 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 46 (710 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 46 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 47 (711 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 48 (712 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −3.
**Age 48 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 50 (714 TR)** — He dreamed of someone who had been dead for years. In the dream they spoke normally. He woke feeling he had been given something.
**Age 51 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.

**Age 52 (716 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 52 (716 TR)** — His son Levan Faramin married Tala Alman. Levan Faramin married Tala Alman (unguilded, age 16)
**Age 53 (717 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 54 (718 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 54 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (719 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 55 (719 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Levan Faramin's child born (male, Levan Faramin age 20)
**Age 56 (720 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 30** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [noble → unguilded]
narrativeText: **Age 18 (682 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. He spent considerable time afterward determining what a year's service meant and whether he was fulfilling it adequately. **Age 19 (683 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the forearm or hand that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 20 (684 TR)** — His father was dead and the land was his. Grief and obligation arrived together. **Age 21 (685 TR)** — He was taken into a lord's household, a position of modest honour and real obligation. **Age 22 (686 TR)** — He heard footsteps behind him stop when his stopped. He turned. There was no one there. **Age 23 (687 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1). **Age 24 (688 TR)** — He would not call it a miracle to someone he did not know. He would say: the standard had fallen and the line was breaking, and then the light changed, and the line held. He had seen men stand who had been running a moment before and he could not explain it. He did not try to explain it in the report. He wrote that the line held. **Age 25 (689 TR)** — A cousin or distant kinsman had decided the estate was rightfully his. The dispute would be settled in a lord's court, or not settled at all. Either way, it would cost years. **Age 26 (690 TR)** — A secret entrusted to him found its way to those who would pay for it. **Age 27 (691 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 28 (692 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment. **Age 30 (694 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands. **Age 31 (695 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life. **Age 32 (696 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can. **Age 33 (697 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing. **Age 34 (698 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out. **Age 35 (699 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Mira Torsen (unguilded, age 35) **Age 35 (699 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Levan Faramin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Levan Faramin born (male) **Age 36 (700 TR)** — With his second parent gone he was, in some sense he had not felt before, finally alone in the world. The house he grew up in would be sold or given over. He found he was not ready for that. **Age 36 (700 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Yoric Faramin born (male) **Age 36 (700 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Yoric Faramin died (age 0) **Age 37 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 38 (702 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 38 (702 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 39 (703 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood. **Age 40 (704 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record. **Age 40 (704 TR)** — Mira Torsen died. They had been married 5 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Mira Torsen died (approx. age 40) **Age 41 (705 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name. **Age 41 (705 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 42 (706 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was. **Age 46 (710 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 46 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 47 (711 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop. **Age 48 (712 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive.
