name: Nora Dustmantle

narrative: **Age 18 (671 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 19 (672 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Levan Silverscale (merchant, age 21)
**Age 19 (672 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.
**Age 20 (673 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.
**Age 20 (673 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 21 (674 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 22 (675 TR)** — She paused and realised the year had been, on balance, a good one. She felt reluctant to say so in case it changed.
**Age 22 (675 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 23 (676 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 24 (677 TR)** — She woke to a sound she could not account for. She lay still and listened until it stopped. Nothing explained it in the morning.

**Age 25 (678 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 25 (678 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — lame Selda Silverscale born (female, lame)

**Age 26 (679 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 26 (679 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 27 (680 TR)** — She could not bring herself to go inside. She sat and watched the light change until it had finished doing so.
**Age 27 (680 TR)** — She caught herself watching the child too long, calculating futures. She stopped herself and went back to work. The watching started again an hour later.
**Age 28 (681 TR)** — Someone she had just met corrected the way she was doing something she had done all her life. They were right.
**Age 29 (682 TR)** — Something that had always been there stopped. She noticed the quiet before she identified what had gone.
**Age 29 (682 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30 (683 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 30 (683 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Fardan Silverscale lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Fardan Silverscale born (male)
**Age 30 (683 TR)** — She lost Selda Silverscale — the lame child she had given so much to. Selda Silverscale died (age 5)
**Age 30 (683 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Fardan Silverscale died (age 0)
**Age 31 (684 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (686 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 33 (686 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Zara Silverscale lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Zara Silverscale born (female)

**Age 34 (687 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have.

**Age 35 (688 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 35 (688 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 36 (689 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 36 (689 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 25
**Age 37 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (690 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
**Age 38 (691 TR)** — She sneezed at the worst possible moment. The ceremony continued. The people nearest her found it difficult to maintain appropriate expressions.

**Age 38 (691 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 39 (692 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness.
**Age 39 (692 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Yorin Silverscale lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Yorin Silverscale born (male)
**Age 40 (693 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Deva Fartrader
**Age 40 (693 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 27
**Age 41 (694 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 41 (694 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 5

**Age 42 (695 TR)** — He was found in the counting house. A professional killing — the constable said as much and then appeared to regret having said it. She spent the years afterward unwinding his affairs and finding, layer by layer, which of his activities had made him worth a contract.
**Age 43 (696 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 43 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (697 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Zara Silverscale apprenticed (age 12)
**Age 46 (699 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 46 (699 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 47 (700 TR)** — She heard something she expected to break her and found it did not. She sat with that for a long time.
**Age 47 (700 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 48 (701 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 48 (701 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 49 (702 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done.
**Age 49 (702 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.
**Age 50 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (703 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 51 (704 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 51 (704 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 52 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (707 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 55 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (708 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 56 (709 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 56 (709 TR)** — Her son Yorin Silverscale married Welith Greatscale. Yorin Silverscale married Welith Greatscale (merchant, age 16)
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — She went by a route she had used since childhood and found it altered. She stood looking at where the path had been.
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — Her daughter Zara Silverscale married Elric Keenscale. Zara Silverscale married Elric Keenscale (merchant, age 28)
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Zara Silverscale's child born (male, Zara Silverscale age 24)
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — Yorin Silverscale's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Yorin Silverscale's child died (age 0)
**Age 58 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (711 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 59 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (713 TR)** — She found a name carved somewhere — a wall, a beam, a stone — and thought about the hand that had made it.

**Age 60 (713 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 61 (714 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 61 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (714 TR)** — Zara Silverscale lost Gorin Silverscale. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Zara Silverscale's child died (age 4)
**Age 62 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (716 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 63 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 64 (717 TR)** — The charges did not stick — lack of evidence, a well-placed word, or simple luck.
**Age 64 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 65 (718 TR)** — She found a place that was simply right and made a habit of sitting there.
**Age 65 (718 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 65 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 66 (719 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.
**Age 66 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 67 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (671 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it. **Age 19 (672 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Levan Silverscale (merchant, age 21) **Age 19 (672 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected. **Age 20 (673 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected. **Age 20 (673 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took. **Age 21 (674 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting. **Age 22 (675 TR)** — She paused and realised the year had been, on balance, a good one. She felt reluctant to say so in case it changed. **Age 22 (675 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine. **Age 23 (676 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 24 (677 TR)** — She woke to a sound she could not account for. She lay still and listened until it stopped. Nothing explained it in the morning. **Age 25 (678 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 25 (678 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — lame Selda Silverscale born (female, lame) **Age 26 (679 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at. **Age 26 (679 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day. **Age 27 (680 TR)** — She could not bring herself to go inside. She sat and watched the light change until it had finished doing so. **Age 27 (680 TR)** — She caught herself watching the child too long, calculating futures. She stopped herself and went back to work. The watching started again an hour later. **Age 28 (681 TR)** — Someone she had just met corrected the way she was doing something she had done all her life. They were right. **Age 29 (682 TR)** — Something that had always been there stopped. She noticed the quiet before she identified what had gone. **Age 29 (682 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 30 (683 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 30 (683 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Fardan Silverscale lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Fardan Silverscale born (male) **Age 30 (683 TR)** — She lost Selda Silverscale — the lame child she had given so much to. Selda Silverscale died (age 5) **Age 30 (683 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Fardan Silverscale died (age 0) **Age 31 (684 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 33 (686 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 33 (686 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Zara Silverscale lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Zara Silverscale born (female) **Age 34 (687 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have. **Age 35 (688 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 35 (688 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 36 (689 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 36 (689 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 25 **Age 37 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 37 (690 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it. **Age 38 (691 TR)** — She sneezed at the worst possible moment. The ceremony continued. The people nearest her found it difficult to maintain appropriate expressions. **Age 38 (691 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 39 (692 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness. **Age 39 (692 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Yorin Silverscale lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Yorin Silverscale born (male) **Age 40 (693 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Deva Fartrader **Age 40 (693 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 27 **Age 41 (694 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 41 (694 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 5 **Age 42 (695 TR)** — He was found in the counting house. A professional killing — the constable said as much and then appeared to regret having said it. She spent the years afterward unwinding his affairs and finding, layer by layer, which of his activities had made him worth a contract. **Age 43 (696 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 43 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 44 (697 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it. **Age 45 (698 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back. **Age 45 (698 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Zara Silverscale apprenticed (age 12) **Age 46 (699 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily. **Age 46 (699 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain. **Age 47 (700 TR)** — She heard something she expected to break her and found it did not. She sat with that for a long time. **Age 47 (700 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. name: Karith Dustmantle

narrative: **Age 18 (677 TR)** — She accompanied a caravan — widow, factor, or simply bold — to distant markets.
**Age 19 (678 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 20 (679 TR)** — Her horse stopped dead on a familiar road and would not be moved for an hour.

**Age 21 (680 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 22 (681 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 23 (682 TR)** — A bird nested in her window ledge. She watched the eggs hatch and the chicks go.

**Age 24 (683 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness.
**Age 25 (684 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 26 (685 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.
**Age 27 (686 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.
**Age 28 (687 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Narith Garekar (noble)
**Age 29 (688 TR)** — She finished something and found she was satisfied with it. That was rarer than it should have been.

**Age 30 (689 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 31 (690 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 32 (691 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 33 (692 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Hrolf
**Age 34 (693 TR)** — She sneezed at the worst possible moment. The ceremony continued. The people nearest her found it difficult to maintain appropriate expressions.
**Age 35 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (695 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 37 (696 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
**Age 38 (697 TR)** — Her quality of work was recognised within the guild.
**Age 39 (698 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Rodric Fairmarket
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 41 (700 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked.
**Age 42 (701 TR)** — Someone quoted a prophecy in her presence and looked at her meaningfully. She pretended not to notice.
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — She read what texts she could find, and the night sky taught her the rest.
**Age 44 (703 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 45 (704 TR)** — Her mother had not wanted to go. She had insisted, because the alternative was managing a dying woman in three rooms with two children underfoot. The Peonian house took her without fuss. She visited every day. Her mother died in spring, quietly, with a sister holding her hand because she had arrived twenty minutes too late.
**Age 46 (705 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 47 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 50 (709 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 51 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 55 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — She meant to glance at the rain and check on it. She stood there watching it for a long time.
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — A secret entrusted to her found its way to those who would pay for it.
**Age 60 (719 TR)** — The debt that had dogged her was finally cleared.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

name: Geda Rivergate

narrative: **Age 18 (683 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 19 (684 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.
**Age 20 (685 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 21 (686 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 22 (687 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 23 (688 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 24 (689 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 25 (690 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Disa (clergy)
**Age 26 (691 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.
**Age 27 (692 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.
**Age 28 (693 TR)** — When the coin was offered, she found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 29 (694 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 30 (695 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 31 (696 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 32 (697 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Theric Saltworth
**Age 33 (698 TR)** — She accompanied a caravan — widow, factor, or simply bold — to distant markets.

**Age 34 (699 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 35 (700 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 36 (701 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 37 (702 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 38 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (706 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
**Age 42 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 43 (708 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 44 (709 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (711 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 47 (712 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Balar Amberton
**Age 48 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (714 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.

**Age 50 (715 TR)** — The poison was in the wine, and she had drunk only a little before the taste turned her. Or perhaps she had not drunk enough. For a week she did not know which and spent the time in careful consideration of every decision she had made in the past two years that could have made her worth killing.
**Age 51 (716 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked.
**Age 52 (717 TR)** — Her mind went blank on a name she knew well. She covered it badly and has thought about it since.
**Age 53 (718 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 54 (719 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 55 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

name: Zarath Plaintrader

narrative: **Age 18 (689 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 19 (690 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 20 (691 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.
**Age 21 (692 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 22 (693 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Bram Coinsworth died
**Age 23 (694 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

**Age 24 (695 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 25 (696 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Dara Elenshen (noble)
**Age 26 (697 TR)** — A tune she caught half of at a festival lodged in her head for the rest of the season.
**Age 27 (698 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 28 (699 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.

**Age 29 (700 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 30 (701 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 31 (702 TR)** — She said what no one else was going to say. She had hoped to stay out of it. She did not stay out of it.
**Age 32 (703 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 33 (704 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 34 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (706 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Eara Wealthgate

**Age 36 (707 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.

**Age 37 (708 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 38 (709 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.

**Age 39 (710 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
**Age 40 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (712 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.

**Age 42 (713 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 43 (714 TR)** — Her quality of work was recognised within the guild.
**Age 44 (715 TR)** — She accompanied a caravan — widow, factor, or simply bold — to distant markets.
**Age 45 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (717 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (718 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 48 (719 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 49 (720 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.

name: Gara Fartrader

narrative: **Age 18 (718 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.

**Age 19 (719 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 20 (720 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Marath Valman died
narrativeText: **Age 18 (718 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 19 (719 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 20 (720 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Marath Valman died
name: Gerin Idkin

narrative: **Age 18 (666 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 19 (667 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.

**Age 20 (668 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills.
**Age 21 (669 TR)** — Drink, pride, or an old grudge turned into something harder. He gave as good as he got.
**Age 22 (670 TR)** — He took a wrong turn and came out somewhere he had never been. The village had a good bakehouse and the wrong road became one he always took.
**Age 23 (671 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Edda Korkin (warrior, age 18)
**Age 24 (672 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.
**Age 25 (673 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 25 (673 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Ulda Vorsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Ulda Vorsen born (female)
**Age 26 (674 TR)** — He bought something he had no use for. He did not regret it. He kept it.
**Age 26 (674 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.

**Age 27 (675 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 27 (675 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Meric Vorsen. week 40 Meric Vorsen born (male)
**Age 28 (676 TR)** — He made no secret of it. Some people thought him bold; most thought him reckless. His wife had opinions he did not ask for.

**Age 28 (676 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 29 (677 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone.

**Age 30 (678 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 30 (678 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.

**Age 31 (679 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 31 (679 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 32 (680 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 32 (680 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 18

**Age 33 (681 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value.
**Age 33 (681 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Laric Vorsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Laric Vorsen born (male)
**Age 34 (682 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.

**Age 34 (682 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — deaf Arnam Vorsen born (male, deaf)
**Age 35 (683 TR)** — His reliability and competence were noticed. Rank followed.
**Age 35 (683 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 36 (684 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 36 (684 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on.
**Age 36 (684 TR)** — Laric Vorsen died young — a child still. Laric Vorsen died (age 3)

**Age 37 (685 TR)** — He had about forty words of Pagaelin and a captive who knew what all forty of them were. The Pagaelin watched him assemble his questions with an expression that was not quite contempt — closer to professional assessment. He got nothing useful and the captive escaped two days later, which he found he could not entirely resent.
**Age 38 (686 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 38 (686 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 39 (687 TR)** — The injury was too deep. He would never move as freely again. A grievous wound to the abdomen. The damage was permanent — stamina reduced by 3.
**Age 39 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 40 (688 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 40 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (689 TR)** — It ran its course. He did not pretend otherwise. The village had already formed its opinions.
**Age 41 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 42 (690 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 42 (690 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Balar Vorsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Balar Vorsen born (male)
**Age 43 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (691 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Britta Vorsen born (female)

**Age 44 (692 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.
**Age 44 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (692 TR)** — His daughter Ulda Vorsen married Gotar Boring. Ulda Vorsen married Gotar Boring (warrior, age 20)
**Age 44 (692 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Ulda Vorsen's child born (male, Ulda Vorsen age 19)

**Age 45 (693 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.
**Age 45 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (694 TR)** — His child — the one he had feared for — had made something of themselves. Not what he had once imagined, but real. He felt something loosen in his chest.
**Age 46 (694 TR)** — His son Meric Vorsen married Emra Marsen. Meric Vorsen married Emra Marsen (peasant, age 16)
**Age 46 (694 TR)** — Meric Vorsen left home to make his own way. Meric Vorsen left home (age 19)

**Age 47 (695 TR)** — The letter arrived with no signature. It described, in precise and accurate detail, something he had done three years ago that he had believed no one knew. The terms were not unreasonable. That was almost the worst part — whoever had sent it understood exactly what the information was worth and had priced it accordingly.
**Age 47 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (696 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 48 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 49 (697 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.

narrativeText: **Age 18 (666 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 19 (667 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way. **Age 20 (668 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills. **Age 21 (669 TR)** — Drink, pride, or an old grudge turned into something harder. He gave as good as he got. **Age 22 (670 TR)** — He took a wrong turn and came out somewhere he had never been. The village had a good bakehouse and the wrong road became one he always took. **Age 23 (671 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Edda Korkin (warrior, age 18) **Age 24 (672 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go. **Age 25 (673 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew. **Age 25 (673 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Ulda Vorsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Ulda Vorsen born (female) **Age 26 (674 TR)** — He bought something he had no use for. He did not regret it. He kept it. **Age 26 (674 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 27 (675 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 27 (675 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Meric Vorsen. week 40 Meric Vorsen born (male) **Age 28 (676 TR)** — He made no secret of it. Some people thought him bold; most thought him reckless. His wife had opinions he did not ask for. **Age 28 (676 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 29 (677 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone. **Age 30 (678 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy. **Age 30 (678 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 31 (679 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was. **Age 31 (679 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 32 (680 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected. **Age 32 (680 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 18 **Age 33 (681 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value. **Age 33 (681 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Laric Vorsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Laric Vorsen born (male) **Age 34 (682 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 34 (682 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — deaf Arnam Vorsen born (male, deaf) **Age 35 (683 TR)** — His reliability and competence were noticed. Rank followed. **Age 35 (683 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 36 (684 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 36 (684 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on. **Age 36 (684 TR)** — Laric Vorsen died young — a child still. Laric Vorsen died (age 3) **Age 37 (685 TR)** — He had about forty words of Pagaelin and a captive who knew what all forty of them were. The Pagaelin watched him assemble his questions with an expression that was not quite contempt — closer to professional assessment. He got nothing useful and the captive escaped two days later, which he found he could not entirely resent. **Age 38 (686 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post. **Age 38 (686 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later. **Age 39 (687 TR)** — The injury was too deep. He would never move as freely again. A grievous wound to the abdomen. The damage was permanent — stamina reduced by 3. **Age 39 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 40 (688 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood. **Age 40 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 41 (689 TR)** — It ran its course. He did not pretend otherwise. The village had already formed its opinions. **Age 41 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 42 (690 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 42 (690 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Balar Vorsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Balar Vorsen born (male) **Age 43 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 43 (691 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Britta Vorsen born (female) **Age 44 (692 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes. **Age 44 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 44 (692 TR)** — His daughter Ulda Vorsen married Gotar Boring. Ulda Vorsen married Gotar Boring (warrior, age 20) **Age 44 (692 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Ulda Vorsen's child born (male, Ulda Vorsen age 19) **Age 45 (693 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting. **Age 45 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 46 (694 TR)** — His child — the one he had feared for — had made something of themselves. Not what he had once imagined, but real. He felt something loosen in his chest. **Age 46 (694 TR)** — His son Meric Vorsen married Emra Marsen. Meric Vorsen married Emra Marsen (peasant, age 16) **Age 46 (694 TR)** — Meric Vorsen left home to make his own way. Meric Vorsen left home (age 19) **Age 47 (695 TR)** — The letter arrived with no signature. It described, in precise and accurate detail, something he had done three years ago that he had believed no one knew. The terms were not unreasonable. That was almost the worst part — whoever had sent it understood exactly what the information was worth and had priced it accordingly. **Age 47 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 48 (696 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment. **Age 48 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. **Age 22 (676 TR)** — He told a story at a campfire that the others repeated for years. He had mostly made it up.

**Age 23 (677 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Marath Horing came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Marath Horing born (female)
**Age 24 (678 TR)** — He found a coin in the road unlike any he had seen before. The merchant he showed it to grew oddly quiet.
**Age 24 (678 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 25 (679 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 25 (679 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 26 (680 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 26 (680 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 27 (681 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 35 (689 TR)** — His lord had given an order he could not follow. Not would not — could not, in the sense that following it would have made him someone he did not want to be and could not have looked at in still water. He refused, formally, with witnesses. He was not punished. That surprised him more than the order had.
**Age 35 (689 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (690 TR)** — He made no secret of it. Some people thought him bold; most thought him reckless. His wife had opinions he did not ask for.
**Age 36 (690 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.

**Age 37 (691 TR)** — Not fluency — nothing close to it. Enough to tell from tone whether the next thing would be a toll demand or a spear throw. Enough to count. Enough to say no and mean it, or yes and be understood. The rest he learned to read from posture.
**Age 37 (691 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9
**Age 37 (691 TR)** — His son Harlan Horing married Rira Yorson. Harlan Horing married Rira Yorson (warrior, age 17)
**Age 37 (691 TR)** — Harlan Horing left home to make his own way. Harlan Horing left home (age 18)
**Age 38 (692 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 38 (692 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — His son Torin Horing married Tara Wolsen. Torin Horing married Tara Wolsen (warrior, age 16)
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — Torin Horing left home to make his own way. Torin Horing left home (age 19)

**Age 40 (694 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing.

**Age 40 (694 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.

**Age 41 (695 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 41 (695 TR)** — Kuric Horing died young — a child still. Kuric Horing died (age 3)
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 12
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Torin Horing's child born (male, Torin Horing age 22)
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — Delia Horing left home to make his own way. Delia Horing left home (age 20)
**Age 43 (697 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 43 (697 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 43 (697 TR)** — His son Aldric Horing married Aldis Smith. Aldric Horing married Aldis Smith (artisan, age 16)

**Age 44 (698 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
**Age 44 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 45 (699 TR)** — Through hard work and some luck, the last creditor was satisfied.
**Age 45 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 45 (699 TR)** — His daughter Delia Horing married Halden Noring. Delia Horing married Halden Noring (warrior, age 29)
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — Marath Horing left home to make his own way. Marath Horing left home (age 23)

**Age 47 (701 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — His daughter Marath Horing married Morin Smith. Marath Horing married Morin Smith (artisan, age 30)
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — His son Evan Horing married Kelyn Valing. Evan Horing married Kelyn Valing (peasant, age 16)
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — Evan Horing left home to make his own way. Evan Horing left home (age 19)

**Age 49 (703 TR)** — At some point the language stopped being a code he was cracking and became something he thought in, briefly, when he was tired. He did not tell anyone. In Kaldor, fluent Pagaelin is a skill that attracts the wrong kind of questions.
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 50 (704 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 50 (704 TR)** — Aldric Horing left home to make his own way. Aldric Horing left home (age 23)
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A minor wound to the back that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — His daughter Melda Horing married Roric Kalson. Melda Horing married Roric Kalson (peasant, age 18)
**Age 52 (706 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.
**Age 52 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — Harlan Horing's child Elric Horing died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Harlan Horing's child died (age 9)

**Age 54 (708 TR)** — He left. There was someone else — or there had been for long enough that this felt inevitable. He packed what he could carry and did not look back, though he thought about it for the rest of his life.
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — Torin Horing died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Torin Horing died (age 34)
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — Aldric Horing's child Evan Horing died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Aldric Horing's child died (age 3)
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — He did something he had been told as a child to ward off bad luck. He was aware of how it looked. He did it anyway.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — He stayed up far later than he meant to, watching the fire go down, or the moon move, or nothing in particular.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 57 (711 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.
**Age 57 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (712 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 58 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Iric Walkin
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — Melda Horing left home to make his own way. Melda Horing left home (age 24)

**Age 60 (714 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. He spent considerable time afterward determining what a year's service meant and whether he was fulfilling it adequately.
**Age 60 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — Garith Corsen died. They had been married 43 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Garith Corsen died (approx. age 59)
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — His daughter Melda Horing remarried. Melda Horing remarried Cador Malsen (warrior, age 24)
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — Marath Horing's child Tovan Horing died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Marath Horing's child died (age 4)
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 64 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 64 (718 TR)** — Delia Horing died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Delia Horing died (age 42)
**Age 65 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 66 (720 TR)** — He made an oath in the dark, in the ditch, bleeding. He does not remember the exact words. He remembers the sense of them clearly — what he was offering, what he was asking for. He survived. He has been paying the debt in small ways ever since, without ever being certain the accounting is correct.
**Age 66 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 66 (720 TR)** — Marath Horing's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Marath Horing's face. Marath Horing's child died (age 1)
**Age 66 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 66 (720 TR)** — Melda Horing died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Melda Horing died (age 31)
narrativeText: **Age 18 (672 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Garith Corsen (warrior, age 16) **Age 19 (673 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 19 (673 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Harlan Horing born (male) **Age 20 (674 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 20 (674 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Torin Horing. week 40 Torin Horing born (male) **Age 21 (675 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 22 (676 TR)** — He told a story at a campfire that the others repeated for years. He had mostly made it up. **Age 22 (676 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 23 (677 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it. **Age 23 (677 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Marath Horing came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Marath Horing born (female) **Age 24 (678 TR)** — He found a coin in the road unlike any he had seen before. The merchant he showed it to grew oddly quiet. **Age 24 (678 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 25 (679 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 25 (679 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered. **Age 26 (680 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking. **Age 26 (680 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 27 (681 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 27 (681 TR)** — His wife bore him a child.
