He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name. **Age 28 (682 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 29 (683 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood. **Age 29 (683 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 30 (684 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected. **Age 30 (684 TR)** — He endured. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 34 (688 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. name: Hrolf Horing

narrative: **Age 18 (678 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 19 (679 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the neck or throat that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 20 (680 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 21 (681 TR)** — He waited for a ferry for most of a morning. He had nothing to do. He discovered he did not mind.

**Age 22 (682 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 23 (683 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 24 (684 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.
**Age 25 (685 TR)** — He heard something outside in the night, got up to investigate, and found nothing. He went back to bed and lay awake for an hour.
**Age 26 (686 TR)** — He kept waiting for the right moment to say something and the moment passed. The thing went unsaid.
**Age 27 (687 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 28 (688 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Arwen Miller (artisan, age 21)
**Age 28 (688 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.

**Age 29 (689 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 29 (689 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 30 (690 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 30 (690 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 24
**Age 31 (691 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 31 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 32 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (692 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 33 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 34 (694 TR)** — His lord had given an order he could not follow. Not would not — could not, in the sense that following it would have made him someone he did not want to be and could not have looked at in still water. He refused, formally, with witnesses. He was not punished. That surprised him more than the order had.

**Age 34 (694 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 35 (695 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Garan Waring died
**Age 35 (695 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9
**Age 36 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 36 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (697 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 37 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 38 (698 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills.
**Age 39 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 39 (699 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.

**Age 40 (700 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 40 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (701 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 42 (702 TR)** — The promotion came quietly — a word from his officer, a new responsibility. He was not sure he wanted it until he found he was good at it.
**Age 42 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 43 (703 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
**Age 43 (703 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 44 (704 TR)** — He drank more than he intended at a gathering and said things he would not have said sober. He reviewed these the next day at length.
**Age 44 (704 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 45 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 45 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (706 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.

**Age 47 (707 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.

**Age 48 (708 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 48 (708 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 16
**Age 49 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (710 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 50 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 52 (712 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. He spent considerable time afterward determining what a year's service meant and whether he was fulfilling it adequately.
**Age 52 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (713 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A minor wound to the hip or groin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 53 (713 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 54 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (715 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 55 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (716 TR)** — __colour__
**Age 56 (716 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 7
**Age 57 (717 TR)** — He set out to do something and forgot what it was before he arrived. He stood there and tried to remember. He went back.
**Age 57 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (719 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Celyn Vorson
**Age 59 (719 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 60 (720 TR)** — He dreamed the same dream three nights running. It was vivid and made no sense. He did not speak of it.
**Age 60 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

name: Seric Loring

narrative: **Age 18 (684 TR)** — He stood at a window watching rain and lost track of time. He had things to do. He watched the rain.
**Age 19 (685 TR)** — He arrived at a crossing to find it flooded and had to go back the way he came. The detour cost him a day and a half.
**Age 20 (686 TR)** — He noticed the first sign that winter was ending — a sound, a smell, a plant pushing through — and felt unreasonably glad.

**Age 21 (687 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.
**Age 22 (688 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 23 (689 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.

**Age 24 (690 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 25 (691 TR)** — His reliability and competence were noticed. Rank followed.

**Age 26 (692 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
**Age 27 (693 TR)** — He was certain of something and wrong. He argued his position at length before the evidence became impossible to ignore.
**Age 28 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 29 (695 TR)** — The aftermath of a battle left him richer than he had any right to expect.
**Age 30 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 31 (697 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 32 (698 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. He spent considerable time afterward determining what a year's service meant and whether he was fulfilling it adequately.
**Age 33 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 34 (700 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 35 (701 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 36 (702 TR)** — Younger officers sought him out before the engagement. He told them what he knew.
**Age 37 (703 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 38 (704 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 39 (705 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 40 (706 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A minor wound to the lower leg or shin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 41 (707 TR)** — Ransom was paid, or a deal struck, or simply an opportunity taken. He walked out into daylight that felt wrong — too bright, too open. He did not speak of the time inside for a long while.

**Age 42 (708 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
**Age 43 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 44 (710 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 45 (711 TR)** — He saw two men fighting in the street. He watched to see if he should intervene. He did not intervene. He thought about this later.

**Age 46 (712 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value.
**Age 47 (713 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.

**Age 48 (714 TR)** — He had not planned to say anything. He had stopped at the hospital on another errand. The Reslava — the travelling one, the one who walked the districts — asked him how he was managing, in a tone that suggested she actually wanted to know. He talked for longer than he meant to.

**Age 49 (715 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 50 (716 TR)** — He was caught in a small lie — not an important one, but it was noticed and the noticing was worse than the lie.
**Age 51 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (718 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 53 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 35 (701 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it. **Age 36 (702 TR)** — Younger officers sought him out before the engagement. He told them what he knew. **Age 37 (703 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before. **Age 38 (704 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 39 (705 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 40 (706 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A minor wound to the lower leg or shin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 41 (707 TR)** — Ransom was paid, or a deal struck, or simply an opportunity taken. He walked out into daylight that felt wrong — too bright, too open. He did not speak of the time inside for a long while. **Age 42 (708 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits. **Age 43 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 44 (710 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 45 (711 TR)** — He saw two men fighting in the street. He watched to see if he should intervene. He did not intervene. He thought about this later. **Age 46 (712 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value. **Age 47 (713 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues. **Age 48 (714 TR)** — He had not planned to say anything. He had stopped at the hospital on another errand. The Reslava — the travelling one, the one who walked the districts — asked him how he was managing, in a tone that suggested she actually wanted to know. He talked for longer than he meant to. **Age 49 (715 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 50 (716 TR)** — He was caught in a small lie — not an important one, but it was noticed and the noticing was worse than the lie. **Age 51 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (718 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post. **Age 53 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 54 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
name: Seric Halman

narrative: **Age 18 (690 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 19 (691 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 20 (692 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.

**Age 21 (693 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.

**Age 22 (694 TR)** — He would not call it a miracle to someone he did not know. He would say: the standard had fallen and the line was breaking, and then the light changed, and the line held. He had seen men stand who had been running a moment before and he could not explain it. He did not try to explain it in the report. He wrote that the line held.
**Age 23 (695 TR)** — A fox sat at the edge of the field and watched him work. Neither moved for a long time.
**Age 24 (696 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 25 (697 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 26 (698 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 27 (699 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Kela Arsen (warrior, age 21)

**Age 27 (699 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 28 (700 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the lower leg or shin. The damage was permanent — agility reduced by 3.
**Age 28 (700 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Garan Wolsen — survived. week 40 Garan Wolsen born (male)

**Age 29 (701 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 29 (701 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.

**Age 30 (702 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.
**Age 30 (702 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 31 (703 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.

**Age 32 (704 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 32 (704 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Gerin Wolsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Gerin Wolsen born (male)
**Age 33 (705 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.
**Age 33 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 34 (706 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 34 (706 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 35 (707 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 36 (708 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 37 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (709 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 37 (709 TR)** — Gerin Wolsen died young — a child still. Gerin Wolsen died (age 5)

**Age 38 (710 TR)** — The Matakea had gone down fifty yards out and the line was already pulling back. He did not make a decision, precisely — he moved before he had decided to move and the thinking happened afterward. He got her back to the line. She was dead. He had not known she was dead when he went for her. He has not entirely resolved how he feels about that.

**Age 38 (710 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.

**Age 39 (711 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills.
**Age 39 (711 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Lara Wolsen born (female)
**Age 40 (712 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 40 (712 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 41 (713 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 41 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (714 TR)** — He forgot a holy day entirely and only remembered the next morning. He went to the temple and said nothing to anyone.
**Age 42 (714 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Tavin Wolsen. week 40 Tavin Wolsen born (male)
**Age 43 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (715 TR)** — Lara Wolsen died young — a child still. Lara Wolsen died (age 4)
**Age 44 (716 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.
**Age 44 (716 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 19

**Age 45 (717 TR)** — The letter arrived with no signature. It described, in precise and accurate detail, something he had done three years ago that he had believed no one knew. The terms were not unreasonable. That was almost the worst part — whoever had sent it understood exactly what the information was worth and had priced it accordingly.
**Age 45 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (718 TR)** — A stranger showed him a technique he had been doing wrong for years. The stranger was not unkind about it.
**Age 46 (718 TR)** — Kela Arsen died. They had been married 19 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Kela Arsen died (approx. age 40)

**Age 47 (719 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.

**Age 48 (720 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing.
**Age 48 (720 TR)** — Garan Wolsen left home to make his own way. Garan Wolsen left home (age 20)

name: Ulan Wolsen

narrative: **Age 18 (696 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 19 (697 TR)** — He walked into the wrong house by mistake. The people inside were eating. Everyone was very polite about it.
**Age 20 (698 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.

**Age 21 (699 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 22 (700 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 23 (701 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 24 (702 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 25 (703 TR)** — He made a promise on impulse and then had to keep it against all convenience. He was glad he did.
**Age 26 (704 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the abdomen. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
**Age 27 (705 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 28 (706 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 29 (707 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
**Age 30 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 31 (709 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning.

**Age 32 (710 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 33 (711 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 34 (712 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.

**Age 35 (713 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.

**Age 36 (714 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 37 (715 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Dalric Edsen

**Age 38 (716 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 39 (717 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 41 (719 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the lower leg or shin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).

**Age 42 (720 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in eyesight (−1).

He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Dalric Edsen **Age 38 (716 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 39 (717 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 41 (719 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the lower leg or shin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1). **Age 42 (720 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in eyesight (−1).
name: Halden Woling

narrative: **Age 18 (702 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Farath Noring (warrior, age 17)

**Age 19 (703 TR)** — The Matakea had gone down fifty yards out and the line was already pulling back. He did not make a decision, precisely — he moved before he had decided to move and the thinking happened afterward. He got her back to the line. She was dead. He had not known she was dead when he went for her. He has not entirely resolved how he feels about that.

**Age 20 (704 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 20 (704 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Ista Idman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Ista Idman born (female)
**Age 21 (705 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Marek Idman — survived. week 40 Marek Idman born (male)
**Age 22 (706 TR)** — He said something he had intended only for one person at a volume that reached several others. He is still not certain how much they heard.

**Age 23 (707 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 23 (707 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 23 (707 TR)** — Ista Idman died young — a child still. Ista Idman died (age 3)
**Age 24 (708 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 24 (708 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Kela Idman. week 40 Kela Idman born (female)
**Age 25 (709 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.
**Age 25 (709 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Tera Idman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Tera Idman born (female)
**Age 26 (710 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the upper arm. The damage was permanent — strength reduced by 3.
**Age 26 (710 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 27 (711 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 27 (711 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 12

**Age 28 (712 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
**Age 28 (712 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.

**Age 29 (713 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
**Age 29 (713 TR)** — Farath Noring died. They had been married 11 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Farath Noring died (approx. age 28)
**Age 29 (713 TR)** — Marek Idman died young — a child still. Marek Idman died (age 8)
**Age 30 (714 TR)** — He heard something outside in the night, got up to investigate, and found nothing. He went back to bed and lay awake for an hour.
**Age 30 (714 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 32 (716 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Elva Korkin (warrior, age 27)
**Age 33 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 33 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 34 (718 TR)** — The aftermath of a battle left him richer than he had any right to expect.
**Age 34 (718 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Tera Idman placed as page (age 9)

**Age 35 (719 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills.
**Age 35 (719 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9

**Age 36 (720 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.

name: Hrolf Idman

narrative: **Age 18 (708 TR)** — He found a coin in the road unlike any he had seen before. The merchant he showed it to grew oddly quiet.
**Age 19 (709 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 20 (710 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.

**Age 21 (711 TR)** — The Matakea had gone down fifty yards out and the line was already pulling back. He did not make a decision, precisely — he moved before he had decided to move and the thinking happened afterward. He got her back to the line. She was dead. He had not known she was dead when he went for her. He has not entirely resolved how he feels about that.
**Age 22 (712 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Arden Dorkin (peasant, age 18)
**Age 22 (712 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.

**Age 23 (713 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 23 (713 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 24 (714 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −1.

**Age 24 (714 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.

**Age 25 (715 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 25 (715 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 8
**Age 26 (716 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 27 (717 TR)** — He lost a piece of equipment through his own inattention. He replaced it before anyone noticed. The cost annoyed him for months.
**Age 27 (717 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 18
**Age 28 (718 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 28 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 29 (719 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
**Age 29 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30 (720 TR)** — He bought something he had no use for. He did not regret it. He kept it.
**Age 30 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

name: Erek Orman

narrative: **Age 18 (714 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
**Age 19 (715 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.

**Age 20 (716 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 21 (717 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
**Age 22 (718 TR)** — His horse shied at something he could not see. Nothing explained it later.
**Age 23 (719 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.

**Age 24 (720 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. He found the Matakea near the supply wagons at dusk and said what he needed to say. He had not spoken to a priest since boyhood. It was not as difficult as he had expected. He slept better than he had in weeks.

name: Garan Borsen

narrative: **Age 18 (720 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.

The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
name: Cade Aring

narrative: **Age 18 (668 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.
**Age 19 (669 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.

**Age 20 (670 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.

**Age 21 (671 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 22 (672 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.

**Age 23 (673 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 24 (674 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Ista Lorkin (warrior, age 17)
**Age 25 (675 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 26 (676 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
**Age 26 (676 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Yorin Ering born (male)
**Age 27 (677 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 27 (677 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Davin Ering came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Davin Ering born (male)
**Age 28 (678 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 28 (678 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Yorin Ering died (age 2)
**Age 29 (679 TR)** — The promotion came quietly — a word from his officer, a new responsibility. He was not sure he wanted it until he found he was good at it.
**Age 30 (680 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 30 (680 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Annis Ering. week 40 Annis Ering born (female)
**Age 31 (681 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 31 (681 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Lothar Ering came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Lothar Ering born (male)
**Age 32 (682 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 32 (682 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.

**Age 33 (683 TR)** — The Peonian sister arrived before he had finished sending for her and left after dawn with the kind of professional calm that made everything that had happened in between feel almost ordinary. He does not know her name. He knows the child is alive.
**Age 33 (683 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.

**Age 34 (684 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.
**Age 35 (685 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 35 (685 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 36 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 36 (686 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 37 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (687 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.

**Age 38 (688 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. He found the Matakea near the supply wagons at dusk and said what he needed to say. He had not spoken to a priest since boyhood. It was not as difficult as he had expected. He slept better than he had in weeks.
**Age 38 (688 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 39 (689 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (689 TR)** — Ista Lorkin died. They had been married 15 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Ista Lorkin died (approx. age 32)
**Age 40 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (691 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.
**Age 42 (692 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 42 (692 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 43 (693 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.

**Age 44 (694 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 45 (695 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Erek Vorman (warrior)
**Age 45 (695 TR)** — His son Davin Ering married Estra Ralman. Davin Ering married Estra Ralman (warrior, age 18)
**Age 46 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (696 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Davin Ering's child born (female, Davin Ering age 19)
**Age 46 (696 TR)** — Berith Ering left home to make his own way. Berith Ering left home (age 18)
**Age 46 (696 TR)** — Lothar Ering was nearly grown when he died. Lothar Ering died (age 15)
**Age 47 (697 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 47 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (698 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 49 (699 TR)** — He found a coin in the road unlike any he had seen before. The merchant he showed it to grew oddly quiet.
**Age 49 (699 TR)** — His daughter Annis Ering married Wulf Vorson. Annis Ering married Wulf Vorson (warrior, age 24)
**Age 49 (699 TR)** — Annis Ering left home to make his own way. Annis Ering left home (age 19)
**Age 50 (700 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 51 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (701 TR)** — His daughter Berith Ering married Ruald Garkin. Berith Ering married Ruald Garkin (warrior, age 20)
**Age 51 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (701 TR)** — Annis Ering's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Annis Ering's face. Annis Ering's child died (age 0)
**Age 52 (702 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.
**Age 52 (702 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.

**Age 53 (703 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 53 (703 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 53 (703 TR)** — Davin Ering left home to make his own way. Davin Ering left home (age 26)
**Age 54 (704 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.
**Age 54 (704 TR)** — Davin Ering's child Mara Ering died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Davin Ering's child died (age 8)
**Age 54 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (704 TR)** — Berith Ering's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Berith Ering's face. Berith Ering's child died (age 0)
**Age 55 (705 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Erek Vorman became an enemy

**Age 56 (706 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 57 (707 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.

**Age 58 (708 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 58 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (709 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 59 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 60 (710 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value.
**Age 60 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (711 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 61 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 62 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (713 TR)** — He lay awake one night with nothing in particular on his mind. He got up before dawn and walked until it was light.
**Age 63 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 64 (714 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant.
**Age 65 (715 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 66 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 67 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 67 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 68 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 69 (719 TR)** — The factor he had trusted with his accounts for four years had been selling information about his business to a competitor — or to someone who had hired the competitor as a front. He did not know where the thread ultimately led. He knew that every conversation in that office had been heard by someone else.
**Age 69 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 69 (719 TR)** — His daughter Annis Ering remarried. Annis Ering remarried Arven Alman (warrior, age 45)
**Age 70 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 70 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (668 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go. **Age 19 (669 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before. **Age 20 (670 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits. **Age 21 (671 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 22 (672 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues. **Age 23 (673 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 24 (674 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Ista Lorkin (warrior, age 17) **Age 25 (675 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 26 (676 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for. **Age 26 (676 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Yorin Ering born (male) **Age 27 (677 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it. **Age 27 (677 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Davin Ering came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Davin Ering born (male) **Age 28 (678 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 28 (678 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Yorin Ering died (age 2) **Age 29 (679 TR)** — The promotion came quietly — a word from his officer, a new responsibility. He was not sure he wanted it until he found he was good at it. **Age 30 (680 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 30 (680 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Annis Ering. week 40 Annis Ering born (female) **Age 31 (681 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew. **Age 31 (681 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Lothar Ering came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Lothar Ering born (male) **Age 32 (682 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 32 (682 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 33 (683 TR)** — The Peonian sister arrived before he had finished sending for her and left after dawn with the kind of professional calm that made everything that had happened in between feel almost ordinary. He does not know her name. He knows the child is alive. **Age 33 (683 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later. **Age 34 (684 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting. **Age 35 (685 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 35 (685 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years. **Age 36 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 36 (686 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 37 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 37 (687 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 38 (688 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. He found the Matakea near the supply wagons at dusk and said what he needed to say. He had not spoken to a priest since boyhood. It was not as difficult as he had expected. He slept better than he had in weeks. **Age 38 (688 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered. **Age 39 (689 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 39 (689 TR)** — Ista Lorkin died. They had been married 15 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Ista Lorkin died (approx. age 32) **Age 40 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (691 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving. **Age 42 (692 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 42 (692 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 43 (693 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 44 (694 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 45 (695 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Erek Vorman (warrior) **Age 45 (695 TR)** — His son Davin Ering married Estra Ralman. Davin Ering married Estra Ralman (warrior, age 18) **Age 46 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year.
