name: Theric Ering

narrative: **Age 18 (674 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 19 (675 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Elva Edkin (warrior, age 16)
**Age 19 (675 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 20 (676 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this.
**Age 20 (676 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Morin Walkin. week 40 Morin Walkin born (male)
**Age 21 (677 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Selyn Karson died
**Age 22 (678 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.

**Age 23 (679 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 24 (680 TR)** — He made no secret of it. Some people thought him bold; most thought him reckless. His wife had opinions he did not ask for.
**Age 24 (680 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 25 (681 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.

**Age 25 (681 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 26 (682 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.
**Age 26 (682 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Eara Walkin born (female)
**Age 27 (683 TR)** — She was gone. A note, or nothing — it did not matter much which. He was left with the children and whatever explanations he could give the neighbours.
**Age 27 (683 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 9
**Age 28 (684 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning.
**Age 28 (684 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 29 (685 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 30 (686 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 30 (686 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 31 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (687 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 32 (688 TR)** — It ran its course. He did not pretend otherwise. The village had already formed its opinions.
**Age 32 (688 TR)** — Morin Walkin was nearly grown when he died. Morin Walkin died (age 12)

**Age 33 (689 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 34 (690 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 34 (690 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 35 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 36 (692 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 36 (692 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Erlan Walkin placed as page (age 8)

**Age 37 (693 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. He found the Matakea near the supply wagons at dusk and said what he needed to say. He had not spoken to a priest since boyhood. It was not as difficult as he had expected. He slept better than he had in weeks.
**Age 37 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 38 (694 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Aldric Caldane (noble)
**Age 38 (694 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 39 (695 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 40 (696 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 40 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (697 TR)** — A secret entrusted to him found its way to those who would pay for it.
**Age 42 (698 TR)** — He told a story at a campfire that the others repeated for years. He had mostly made it up.
**Age 42 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 43 (699 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.

**Age 43 (699 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 44 (700 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 45 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (701 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 45 (701 TR)** — His daughter Eara Walkin married Noral Erkin. Eara Walkin married Noral Erkin (peasant, age 20)
**Age 46 (702 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 46 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (702 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Eara Walkin's child born (male, Eara Walkin age 20)
**Age 47 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (703 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 47 (703 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Erlan Walkin's illegitimate child born (male, Erlan Walkin age 19)
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (707 TR)** — He kept mending a garment long after a sensible person would have replaced it. It had no particular meaning. He just kept mending it.
**Age 51 (707 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 52 (708 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 52 (708 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.

**Age 55 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — Eara Walkin's child Soran Walkin died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Eara Walkin's child died (age 9)
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 57 (713 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right.
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — His daughter Eara Walkin remarried. Eara Walkin remarried Morak Yorsen (warrior, age 32)

**Age 61 (717 TR)** — The letter arrived with no signature. It described, in precise and accurate detail, something he had done three years ago that he had believed no one knew. The terms were not unreasonable. That was almost the worst part — whoever had sent it understood exactly what the information was worth and had priced it accordingly.
**Age 61 (717 TR)** — Elva Edkin died. They had been married 42 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Elva Edkin died (approx. age 58)

He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 24 (680 TR)** — He made no secret of it. name: Rhoric Walkin

narrative: **Age 18 (680 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 19 (681 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 20 (682 TR)** — His lord acknowledged the deed — a purse, better arms, a public word of praise. It was enough.

**Age 21 (683 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 22 (684 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 23 (685 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 24 (686 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 25 (687 TR)** — His reliability and competence were noticed. Rank followed.
**Age 26 (688 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.

**Age 27 (689 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. He spent considerable time afterward determining what a year's service meant and whether he was fulfilling it adequately.
**Age 28 (690 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 29 (691 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 30 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (693 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Marath Torson (warrior, age 30)
**Age 32 (694 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 32 (694 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 33 (695 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 33 (695 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Brand Edkin. week 40 Brand Edkin born (male)
**Age 34 (696 TR)** — He got turned around on a road he had walked a hundred times. He was an hour off course before he admitted it.
**Age 34 (696 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Reda Edkin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Reda Edkin born (female)
**Age 35 (697 TR)** — He overheard a conversation he was not meant to hear. He pretended otherwise and spent the next year wondering what to do with it.
**Age 35 (697 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 36 (698 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.
**Age 37 (699 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Tera Markin
**Age 37 (699 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 38 (700 TR)** — The tavern, the fireside, the garrison mess — they asked and he told them.
**Age 39 (701 TR)** — Drink, pride, or an old grudge turned into something harder. He gave as good as he got.
**Age 39 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 40 (702 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 40 (702 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 4
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Davin Lorsen died
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 42 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 43 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (705 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Halric Edkin died (age 0)
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 45 (707 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the upper arm. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1).
**Age 45 (707 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.

**Age 47 (709 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 47 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 48 (710 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.

**Age 49 (711 TR)** — Ransom was paid, or a deal struck, or simply an opportunity taken. He walked out into daylight that felt wrong — too bright, too open. He did not speak of the time inside for a long while.
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 20

**Age 50 (712 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value.

**Age 50 (712 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — The injury was too deep. He would never move as freely again. A serious wound to the lower leg or shin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Reda Edkin's illegitimate child born (female, Reda Edkin age 17)
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — An old soldier at a fire told a story about a battle he had no business being at. The detail was too precise to be invented.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Edris Edkin placed as page (age 11)
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Brand Edkin's illegitimate child born (male, Brand Edkin age 20)
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — His daughter Reda Edkin married Antor Alsen. Reda Edkin married Antor Alsen (warrior, age 20)
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — Aela Edkin was nearly grown when she died. Aela Edkin died (age 14)
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — His son Brand Edkin married Melda Salsen. Brand Edkin married Melda Salsen (warrior, age 17)
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — Reda Edkin left home to make his own way. Reda Edkin left home (age 20)

**Age 55 (717 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills.

**Age 55 (717 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — He found his first grey hair. He pulled it out, then felt foolish, then stopped caring.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Brand Edkin's child born (female, Brand Edkin age 23)
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — Brand Edkin's child Dorin Edkin died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Brand Edkin's child died (age 3)
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 58 (720 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — Marath Torson died. They had been married 27 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Marath Torson died (approx. age 57)

Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it. **Age 30 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 31 (693 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Marath Torson (warrior, age 30) **Age 32 (694 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 32 (694 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words. **Age 33 (695 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks. **Age 33 (695 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Brand Edkin. week 40 Brand Edkin born (male) **Age 34 (696 TR)** — He got turned around on a road he had walked a hundred times. He was an hour off course before he admitted it. **Age 34 (696 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Reda Edkin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Reda Edkin born (female) **Age 35 (697 TR)** — He overheard a conversation he was not meant to hear. He pretended otherwise and spent the next year wondering what to do with it. **Age 35 (697 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 36 (698 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving. **Age 37 (699 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Tera Markin **Age 37 (699 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 38 (700 TR)** — The tavern, the fireside, the garrison mess — they asked and he told them. **Age 39 (701 TR)** — Drink, pride, or an old grudge turned into something harder. He gave as good as he got. **Age 39 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 40 (702 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 40 (702 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 4 **Age 41 (703 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Davin Lorsen died **Age 41 (703 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong. **Age 42 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 42 (704 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Edris Edkin born (female) **Age 43 (705 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go. **Age 43 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 43 (705 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Halric Edkin died (age 0) **Age 44 (706 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 44 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 45 (707 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the upper arm. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1). **Age 45 (707 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 46 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (708 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 47 (709 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy. **Age 47 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (710 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes. **Age 49 (711 TR)** — Ransom was paid, or a deal struck, or simply an opportunity taken. He walked out into daylight that felt wrong — too bright, too open. He did not speak of the time inside for a long while. **Age 49 (711 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 20 **Age 50 (712 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value. **Age 50 (712 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 51 (713 TR)** — The injury was too deep. He would never move as freely again. A serious wound to the lower leg or shin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1). **Age 51 (713 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 51 (713 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Reda Edkin's illegitimate child born (female, Reda Edkin age 17) **Age 52 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 53 (715 TR)** — An old soldier at a fire told a story about a battle he had no business being at. The detail was too precise to be invented. **Age 53 (715 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Edris Edkin placed as page (age 11) **Age 53 (715 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Brand Edkin's illegitimate child born (male, Brand Edkin age 20) **Age 53 (715 TR)** — His daughter Reda Edkin married Antor Alsen. Reda Edkin married Antor Alsen (warrior, age 20) **Age 53 (715 TR)** — Aela Edkin was nearly grown when she died. Aela Edkin died (age 14) **Age 54 (716 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 54 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (716 TR)** — His son Brand Edkin married Melda Salsen. Brand Edkin married Melda Salsen (warrior, age 17) **Age 54 (716 TR)** — Reda Edkin left home to make his own way. Reda Edkin left home (age 20) **Age 55 (717 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills. **Age 55 (717 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — He found his first grey hair. He pulled it out, then felt foolish, then stopped caring. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Brand Edkin's child born (female, Brand Edkin age 23) **Age 56 (718 TR)** — Brand Edkin's child Dorin Edkin died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Brand Edkin's child died (age 3) **Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 57 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 58 (720 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear. **Age 58 (720 TR)** — Marath Torson died. They had been married 27 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Marath Torson died (approx. age 57)
name: Cade Edkin

narrative: **Age 18 (686 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 19 (687 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the thigh. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 20 (688 TR)** — Something moved at the edge of his vision and was gone before he turned his head. He was never sure what it was.
**Age 21 (689 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 22 (690 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Deric Ralman died

**Age 23 (691 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in eyesight (−1).

**Age 24 (692 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 25 (693 TR)** — He made something — a wooden figure, a knotted rope, a small carving — with no purpose in mind. He gave it away.
**Age 26 (694 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 27 (695 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 28 (696 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.

**Age 29 (697 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 30 (698 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 31 (699 TR)** — He said something that needed saying when everyone else was quiet. He would rather someone else had said it.

**Age 32 (700 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.

**Age 33 (701 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.

**Age 34 (702 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.

**Age 35 (703 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 36 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (705 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Brand Torkin (warrior)
**Age 38 (706 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.

**Age 39 (707 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.

**Age 40 (708 TR)** — Not fluency — nothing close to it. Enough to tell from tone whether the next thing would be a toll demand or a spear throw. Enough to count. Enough to say no and mean it, or yes and be understood. The rest he learned to read from posture.
**Age 41 (709 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 42 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 44 (712 TR)** — The Shenasene at the dinner was beautiful in the specific way of someone trained to be beautiful, which is to say very. She spoke to him about his business interests with what seemed like genuine curiosity and probably was, partly. He left with a slightly better opinion of himself than he had arrived with. He understood this was the service.

**Age 45 (713 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.
**Age 46 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (716 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 49 (717 TR)** — A crow appeared every morning for a month on the same post. He began to look for it. One day it was gone.

**Age 50 (718 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 51 (719 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 52 (720 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.

name: Meric Sorman

narrative: **Age 18 (692 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 19 (693 TR)** — He sparred with a stranger on a wager and came off worse than expected. He practiced harder the following month.
**Age 20 (694 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 21 (695 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Eara Ralkin (warrior, age 16)
**Age 23 (697 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 23 (697 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 24 (698 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying.
**Age 24 (698 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 15
**Age 25 (699 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 25 (699 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Arnam Alson came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Arnam Alson born (male)
**Age 26 (700 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Alara Laramin (noble)

**Age 27 (701 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 27 (701 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 28 (702 TR)** — He lost a ring — not valuable, but given to him by someone he no longer saw. He looked for it longer than he should have.
**Age 28 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 29 (703 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 29 (703 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 30 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (705 TR)** — He drank more than he intended at a gathering and said things he would not have said sober. He reviewed these the next day at length.
**Age 31 (705 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 32 (706 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.
**Age 32 (706 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.

**Age 33 (707 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.
**Age 33 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 34 (708 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.

**Age 35 (709 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
**Age 35 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 36 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 36 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (711 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 37 (711 TR)** — Eara Ralkin died. They had been married 16 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Eara Ralkin died (approx. age 32)
**Age 38 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (712 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 39 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 40 (714 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.

**Age 41 (715 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.
**Age 42 (716 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 42 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 43 (717 TR)** — The years finally told. He moved more carefully, tired more quickly. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 43 (717 TR)** — His son Arnam Alson married Amara Laning. Arnam Alson married Amara Laning (warrior, age 16)
**Age 44 (718 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 45 (719 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life.
**Age 45 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (720 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.

He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 28 (702 TR)** — He lost a ring — not valuable, but given to him by someone he no longer saw. He looked for it longer than he should have. **Age 28 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 29 (703 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected. **Age 29 (703 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer. **Age 30 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 31 (705 TR)** — He drank more than he intended at a gathering and said things he would not have said sober. He reviewed these the next day at length. **Age 31 (705 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 32 (706 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving. **Age 32 (706 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years. **Age 33 (707 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes. **Age 33 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 34 (708 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it. **Age 35 (709 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits. **Age 35 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 36 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 36 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 37 (711 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 37 (711 TR)** — Eara Ralkin died. They had been married 16 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Eara Ralkin died (approx. age 32) **Age 38 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 38 (712 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 39 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 40 (714 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy. **Age 41 (715 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting. **Age 42 (716 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 42 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 43 (717 TR)** — The years finally told. He moved more carefully, tired more quickly. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 43 (717 TR)** — His son Arnam Alson married Amara Laning. Arnam Alson married Amara Laning (warrior, age 16) **Age 44 (718 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 45 (719 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life. **Age 45 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (720 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
name: Theric Alson

narrative: **Age 18 (698 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 20 (700 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 21 (701 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Aldric Halkin died

**Age 22 (702 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
**Age 23 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 24 (704 TR)** — His lord acknowledged the deed — a purse, better arms, a public word of praise. It was enough.
**Age 25 (705 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 26 (706 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 27 (707 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Karith Wheeler (artisan, age 25)
**Age 27 (707 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Cavan Valkin. week 40 Cavan Valkin born (male)

**Age 28 (708 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 29 (709 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning.
**Age 29 (709 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Alara Valkin born (female)

**Age 30 (710 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 30 (710 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 31 (711 TR)** — He made a promise on impulse and then had to keep it against all convenience. He was glad he did.
**Age 31 (711 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 26
**Age 31 (711 TR)** — Cavan Valkin died young — a child still. Cavan Valkin died (age 4)

**Age 32 (712 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. He found the Matakea near the supply wagons at dusk and said what he needed to say. He had not spoken to a priest since boyhood. It was not as difficult as he had expected. He slept better than he had in weeks.
**Age 33 (713 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 33 (713 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Jorin Valkin — survived. week 40 Jorin Valkin born (male)
**Age 34 (714 TR)** — He arrived at a crossing to find it flooded and had to go back the way he came. The detour cost him a day and a half.
**Age 34 (714 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 35 (715 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 35 (715 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.

**Age 36 (716 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 36 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 38 (718 TR)** — He set out to do something and forgot what it was before he arrived. He stood there and tried to remember. He went back.
**Age 39 (719 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right.

**Age 40 (720 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.

**Age 40 (720 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.

narrative: **Age 18 (704 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 19 (705 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 20 (706 TR)** — An old soldier at a fire told a story about a battle he had no business being at. The detail was too precise to be invented.
**Age 21 (707 TR)** — He found a coin in the road unlike any he had seen before. The merchant he showed it to grew oddly quiet.

**Age 22 (708 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 23 (709 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 24 (710 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.

**Age 25 (711 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.

**Age 26 (712 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills.
**Age 27 (713 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 28 (714 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 29 (715 TR)** — He woke up one morning certain of something he had been unable to decide. He never understood why sleep had solved it.
**Age 31 (717 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 32 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 33 (719 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 34 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

narrative: **Age 18 (710 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.

**Age 19 (711 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 20 (712 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.

**Age 21 (713 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 22 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 23 (715 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Amara Valing (warrior, age 18)
**Age 23 (715 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.

**Age 24 (716 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 24 (716 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Kael Malsen — survived. week 40 Kael Malsen born (male)

**Age 25 (717 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 25 (717 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 19
**Age 26 (718 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
**Age 27 (719 TR)** — A dog followed him home one evening and refused to leave. He let it stay.

**Age 28 (720 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.

**Age 28 (720 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — deaf Noral Malsen born (male, deaf)
**Age 28 (720 TR)** — Noral Malsen died — the deaf child he had worried over for years. Noral Malsen died (age 0)

name: Kelan Malsen

**Age 19 (717 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 20 (718 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Yarath (clergy)
**Age 21 (719 TR)** — __colour__
**Age 22 (720 TR)** — A stranger pulled him aside on the road and told him not to take the left fork. He took the right. Nothing happened.

**Age 23 (669 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 23 (669 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 24 (670 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 24 (670 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Beren Tarson came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Beren Tarson born (male)
**Age 24 (670 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Beren Tarson died (age 0)

**Age 25 (671 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 25 (671 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 25

**Age 26 (672 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 26 (672 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 17
**Age 27 (673 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Tarath Voring died
**Age 28 (674 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 29 (675 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 29 (675 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Wulf Tarson. week 40 Wulf Tarson born (male)

**Age 30 (676 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.
**Age 30 (676 TR)** — Farath Mason died. They had been married 8 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Farath Mason died (approx. age 24)
**Age 31 (677 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 31 (677 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 32 (678 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 33 (679 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.

**Age 34 (680 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 35 (681 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 35 (681 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.

**Age 36 (682 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. He found the Matakea near the supply wagons at dusk and said what he needed to say. He had not spoken to a priest since boyhood. It was not as difficult as he had expected. He slept better than he had in weeks.
**Age 36 (682 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (683 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 37 (683 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.

**Age 38 (684 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 38 (684 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 39 (685 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 39 (685 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 40 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (687 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Trovan Wolson
**Age 41 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (688 TR)** — He heard midnight bells from a temple he did not know, in a town he was passing through. The sound carried a long way.

**Age 42 (688 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.

**Age 43 (689 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 43 (689 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 11
**Age 44 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (692 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (693 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 47 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (694 TR)** — He sat alone watching a fire go down until there was nothing left but ash. He had not intended to sit that long.
**Age 48 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (695 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 49 (695 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 50 (696 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 50 (696 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.

**Age 51 (697 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 52 (698 TR)** — He saw two men fighting in the street. He watched to see if he should intervene. He did not intervene. He thought about this later.
**Age 53 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — Belyn Tarson left home to make his own way. Belyn Tarson left home (age 18)
**Age 55 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (701 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 56 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (703 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the knee. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 57 (703 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 57 (703 TR)** — His daughter Belyn Tarson married Arnam Harper. Belyn Tarson married Arnam Harper (artisan, age 23)
**Age 58 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 59 (705 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.

**Age 59 (705 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 60 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (706 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 61 (707 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.
**Age 61 (707 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 62 (708 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
*(Ages 63–64 (709–710 TR): years of routine)*
**Age 65 (711 TR)** — He dreamed of someone who had been dead for years. In the dream they spoke normally. He woke feeling he had been given something.
**Age 65 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (711 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Belyn Tarson's child born (female, Belyn Tarson age 29)
**Age 66 (712 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 66 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 67 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 68 (714 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 69 (715 TR)** — A stranger pulled him aside on the road and told him not to take the left fork. He took the right. Nothing happened.
**Age 69 (715 TR)** — Kelyn Soring died. They had been married 34 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Kelyn Soring died (approx. age 69)
**Age 69 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 70 (716 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant.
**Age 70 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 71 (717 TR)** — He ate the best meal of his life at an unremarkable place, served by someone who did not seem aware of the achievement.
**Age 71 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 72 (718 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 73 (719 TR)** — He completed a day's work and stood back and thought: yes, that is done properly.
**Age 74 (720 TR)** — Someone was unexpectedly kind to him at a moment when he had not expected it. He was more affected by this than he showed.
**Age 74 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 21 (667 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not.
