name: Halric Tarson

narrative: **Age 18 (670 TR)** — His lord had given an order he could not follow. Not would not — could not, in the sense that following it would have made him someone he did not want to be and could not have looked at in still water. He refused, formally, with witnesses. He was not punished. That surprised him more than the order had.

**Age 19 (671 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
**Age 20 (672 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 21 (673 TR)** — He told a joke that landed perfectly. He had no idea where he had heard it. He told it at every opportunity for the next three years.

**Age 22 (674 TR)** — He made an oath in the dark, in the ditch, bleeding. He does not remember the exact words. He remembers the sense of them clearly — what he was offering, what he was asking for. He survived. He has been paying the debt in small ways ever since, without ever being certain the accounting is correct.
**Age 23 (675 TR)** — He stood at a window watching rain and lost track of time. He had things to do. He watched the rain.
**Age 24 (676 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 25 (677 TR)** — He noticed the absence of a sound he had not known he was used to. The silence where it should have been was loud.
**Age 26 (678 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it.

**Age 27 (679 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 28 (680 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.

**Age 29 (681 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 30 (682 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care.

**Age 31 (683 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 32 (684 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Tovan Coppergate (merchant)
**Age 33 (685 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 34 (686 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Aerin Harkin (peasant, age 34)

**Age 35 (687 TR)** — He had about forty words of Pagaelin and a captive who knew what all forty of them were. The Pagaelin watched him assemble his questions with an expression that was not quite contempt — closer to professional assessment. He got nothing useful and the captive escaped two days later, which he found he could not entirely resent.
**Age 35 (687 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — He could not decide between two options and tossed a coin. The coin showed one side and he immediately knew he had wanted the other.
**Age 37 (689 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.
**Age 37 (689 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 14

**Age 38 (690 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
**Age 39 (691 TR)** — He heard something outside in the night, got up to investigate, and found nothing. He went back to bed and lay awake for an hour.
**Age 40 (692 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 40 (692 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 34

**Age 41 (693 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 41 (693 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 8
**Age 42 (694 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 42 (694 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 43 (695 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 43 (695 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 45 (697 TR)** — The promotion came quietly — a word from his officer, a new responsibility. He was not sure he wanted it until he found he was good at it.
**Age 45 (697 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 46 (698 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 46 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (699 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.

**Age 47 (699 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.

**Age 49 (701 TR)** — The Matakea had gone down fifty yards out and the line was already pulling back. He did not make a decision, precisely — he moved before he had decided to move and the thinking happened afterward. He got her back to the line. She was dead. He had not known she was dead when he went for her. He has not entirely resolved how he feels about that.
**Age 49 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 50 (702 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.

**Age 51 (703 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (704 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 52 (704 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 10
**Age 53 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 55 (707 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 55 (707 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 23
**Age 56 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the abdomen. The damage was permanent — stamina reduced by 1.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 59 (711 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (712 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Tovan Coppergate became an enemy
**Age 60 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (713 TR)** — He saw something that was probably his business and kept walking. He thought about it for several weeks.
**Age 62 (714 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 62 (714 TR)** — Aerin Harkin died. They had been married 28 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Aerin Harkin died (approx. age 62)
**Age 63 (715 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A grievous wound to the hip or groin. The damage was permanent — agility reduced by 2.
**Age 64 (716 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 65 (717 TR)** — He stopped at a temple he had not intended to visit. He sat there for longer than made sense.
**Age 65 (717 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 66 (718 TR)** — He drew up his will and distributed what he could while he still could. It was a practical matter. He was surprised at how much it felt like something else.
**Age 66 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — The tavern, the fireside, the garrison mess — they asked and he told them.

He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits. **Age 20 (672 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 21 (673 TR)** — He told a joke that landed perfectly. He had no idea where he had heard it. He told it at every opportunity for the next three years. **Age 22 (674 TR)** — He made an oath in the dark, in the ditch, bleeding. He does not remember the exact words. He remembers the sense of them clearly — what he was offering, what he was asking for. He survived. He has been paying the debt in small ways ever since, without ever being certain the accounting is correct. **Age 23 (675 TR)** — He stood at a window watching rain and lost track of time. name: Roric Lorman

narrative: **Age 18 (676 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 19 (677 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Horic Edkin
**Age 20 (678 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 21 (679 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 22 (680 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 23 (681 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.

**Age 24 (682 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 26 (684 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. He found the Matakea near the supply wagons at dusk and said what he needed to say. He had not spoken to a priest since boyhood. It was not as difficult as he had expected. He slept better than he had in weeks.
**Age 27 (685 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A minor wound to the lower leg or shin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 28 (686 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 29 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 30 (688 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.

**Age 31 (689 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 33 (691 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.
**Age 34 (692 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (695 TR)** — He worked out how long ago something had happened and found the number was larger than he had assumed.
**Age 38 (696 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — Something moved at the edge of his vision and was gone before he turned his head. He was never sure what it was.
**Age 41 (699 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end. It changed how he thought about faith.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — He heard a song at a market and could not stop hearing it for three months. He did not know the words.
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right.
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 49 (707 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.

**Age 50 (708 TR)** — He would not call it a miracle to someone he did not know. He would say: the standard had fallen and the line was breaking, and then the light changed, and the line held. He had seen men stand who had been running a moment before and he could not explain it. He did not try to explain it in the report. He wrote that the line held.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — He lost a ring — not valuable, but given to him by someone he no longer saw. He looked for it longer than he should have.
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 62 (720 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing.

He kept taking notes. **Age 34 (692 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking. **Age 35 (693 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment. **Age 36 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 37 (695 TR)** — He worked out how long ago something had happened and found the number was larger than he had assumed. **Age 38 (696 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 39 (697 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning. **Age 40 (698 TR)** — Something moved at the edge of his vision and was gone before he turned his head. He was never sure what it was. **Age 41 (699 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end. It changed how he thought about faith. **Age 43 (701 TR)** — He heard a song at a market and could not stop hearing it for three months. He did not know the words. **Age 44 (702 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 45 (703 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 47 (705 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right. **Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 49 (707 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation. **Age 50 (708 TR)** — He would not call it a miracle to someone he did not know. He would say: the standard had fallen and the line was breaking, and then the light changed, and the line held. He had seen men stand who had been running a moment before and he could not explain it. He did not try to explain it in the report. He wrote that the line held. **Age 51 (709 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 52 (710 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks. **Age 53 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 55 (713 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 56 (714 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 57 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 58 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 59 (717 TR)** — He lost a ring — not valuable, but given to him by someone he no longer saw. He looked for it longer than he should have. **Age 60 (718 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook. **Age 61 (719 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands. **Age 62 (720 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing.
name: Wulf Harson

narrative: **Age 18 (682 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Edris Sorman (peasant, age 16)
**Age 19 (683 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.
**Age 19 (683 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 20 (684 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 21 (685 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 21 (685 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Rarith Borman born (female)
**Age 22 (686 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 22 (686 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Talar Borman — survived. week 40 Talar Borman born (male)

**Age 23 (687 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.

**Age 24 (688 TR)** — He had about forty words of Pagaelin and a captive who knew what all forty of them were. The Pagaelin watched him assemble his questions with an expression that was not quite contempt — closer to professional assessment. He got nothing useful and the captive escaped two days later, which he found he could not entirely resent.
**Age 24 (688 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 25 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 25 (689 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 26 (690 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 26 (690 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 27 (691 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 27 (691 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 28 (692 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 28 (692 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 28

**Age 29 (693 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
**Age 29 (693 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Rarith Borman placed as page (age 8)
**Age 30 (694 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 31 (695 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 31 (695 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Eara Borman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Eara Borman born (female)
**Age 32 (696 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 32 (696 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 8
**Age 33 (697 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 33 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 34 (698 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.

**Age 35 (699 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 35 (699 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 36 (700 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this.
**Age 36 (700 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 37 (701 TR)** — A door he expected to open did not. He pulled, then pushed, then pulled again, then read the situation correctly. There were witnesses.
**Age 38 (702 TR)** — He noticed the first sign that winter was ending — a sound, a smell, a plant pushing through — and felt unreasonably glad.

**Age 38 (702 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 38 (702 TR)** — His son Talar Borman married Leira Wheeler. Talar Borman married Leira Wheeler (artisan, age 16)
**Age 39 (703 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 39 (703 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.

**Age 40 (704 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 40 (704 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 11
**Age 41 (705 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 41 (705 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 41 (705 TR)** — His son Talar Borman remarried. Talar Borman remarried Kela Alman (peasant, age 16)
**Age 42 (706 TR)** — Drink, pride, or an old grudge turned into something harder. He gave as good as he got.
**Age 42 (706 TR)** — Edris Sorman died. They had been married 24 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Edris Sorman died (approx. age 40)
**Age 43 (707 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 43 (707 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Talar Borman's child born (male, Talar Borman age 21)
**Age 44 (708 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 44 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (708 TR)** — Talar Borman's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Talar Borman's face. Talar Borman's child died (age 1)
**Age 45 (709 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 45 (709 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 46 (710 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 46 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 47 (711 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 47 (711 TR)** — Talar Borman left home to make his own way. Talar Borman left home (age 25)

**Age 48 (712 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 48 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (713 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 49 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (713 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Eara Borman's illegitimate child born (female, Eara Borman age 18)

**Age 50 (714 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 50 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 51 (715 TR)** — The Shenasene at the dinner was beautiful in the specific way of someone trained to be beautiful, which is to say very. She spoke to him about his business interests with what seemed like genuine curiosity and probably was, partly. He left with a slightly better opinion of himself than he had arrived with. He understood this was the service.
**Age 51 (715 TR)** — His daughter Rarith Borman married Laric Paring. Rarith Borman married Laric Paring (peasant, age 36)
**Age 52 (716 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Wulf Paring
**Age 52 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (716 TR)** — Eara Borman left home to make his own way. Eara Borman left home (age 21)
**Age 52 (716 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Kelyn Borman's illegitimate child born (female, Kelyn Borman age 19)
**Age 53 (717 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the abdomen. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1).
**Age 53 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (719 TR)** — The years finally told. He moved more carefully, tired more quickly. The illness left a lasting mark: strength −1, stamina −1.
**Age 55 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

name: Deric Borman

narrative: **Age 18 (688 TR)** — He was caught in a small lie — not an important one, but it was noticed and the noticing was worse than the lie.
**Age 19 (689 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care.
**Age 20 (690 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 21 (691 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 22 (692 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.

**Age 23 (693 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes.
**Age 24 (694 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 25 (695 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Leda Vorson died

**Age 26 (696 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
**Age 27 (697 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
**Age 28 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 29 (699 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 30 (700 TR)** — An old soldier at a fire told a story about a battle he had no business being at. The detail was too precise to be invented.
**Age 31 (701 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 32 (702 TR)** — He completed a day's work and stood back and thought: yes, that is done properly.
**Age 33 (703 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.

**Age 34 (704 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. He found the Matakea near the supply wagons at dusk and said what he needed to say. He had not spoken to a priest since boyhood. It was not as difficult as he had expected. He slept better than he had in weeks.
**Age 35 (705 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.

**Age 36 (706 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.

**Age 37 (707 TR)** — The factor he had trusted with his accounts for four years had been selling information about his business to a competitor — or to someone who had hired the competitor as a front. He did not know where the thread ultimately led. He knew that every conversation in that office had been heard by someone else.
**Age 38 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 39 (709 TR)** — He gave someone a gift and discovered another person had given exactly the same thing. He recovered without too much awkwardness.

**Age 40 (710 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
**Age 41 (711 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 42 (712 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 43 (713 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
**Age 44 (714 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 45 (715 TR)** — __colour__
**Age 46 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (717 TR)** — He forgot a holy day entirely and only remembered the next morning. He went to the temple and said nothing to anyone.

**Age 48 (718 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 49 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 50 (720 TR)** — He stayed in an inn so bad he could not tell which noises were rats and which were other guests. He spoke of it for years.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (688 TR)** — He was caught in a small lie — not an important one, but it was noticed and the noticing was worse than the lie. **Age 19 (689 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care. **Age 20 (690 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name. **Age 21 (691 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 22 (692 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 23 (693 TR)** — He was given a year and a Pagaelin informant — a slave, technically, who was paid in better food and occasional freedom of movement for cooperating. He took notes. His informant found the exercise baffling and said so, repeatedly. He kept taking notes. **Age 24 (694 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that. **Age 25 (695 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Leda Vorson died **Age 26 (696 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood. **Age 27 (697 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it. **Age 28 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 29 (699 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 30 (700 TR)** — An old soldier at a fire told a story about a battle he had no business being at. The detail was too precise to be invented. **Age 31 (701 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 32 (702 TR)** — He completed a day's work and stood back and thought: yes, that is done properly. **Age 33 (703 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 34 (704 TR)** — The engagement was the following morning and everyone who was paying attention knew the numbers. name: Dalric Karson

narrative: **Age 18 (717 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 19 (718 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.

**Age 20 (719 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 21 (720 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.

name: Calla Torson

narrative: **Age 18 (665 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 19 (666 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.

**Age 20 (667 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.

**Age 21 (668 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 22 (669 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Zoric Saramin (noble)

**Age 23 (670 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.

**Age 24 (671 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.

**Age 25 (672 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.
**Age 26 (673 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 27 (674 TR)** — She was caught out in something small. The embarrassment was disproportionate to the thing itself.

**Age 28 (675 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 29 (676 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30 (677 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 31 (678 TR)** — The tactical situation made going back inadvisable. She went anyway. The knight she brought back was alive, which had not been certain from a distance, and was subsequently able to walk, which had also not been certain. He never thanked her directly. He gave her his second sword when he returned to civilian life, which she understood to be the same thing.
**Age 32 (679 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 33 (680 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 34 (681 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 35 (682 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.

**Age 36 (683 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.
**Age 37 (684 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.

**Age 38 (685 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 39 (686 TR)** — She healed in a way she had not expected — better in one place, worse in another.

**Age 40 (687 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
**Age 41 (688 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.

**Age 42 (689 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.
**Age 43 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (692 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (693 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 47 (694 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −1.

**Age 48 (695 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice.
**Age 49 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 50 (697 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.

**Age 51 (698 TR)** — She is a widow visiting her sister's family, which is why she is on this road, which is why she has stopped at this inn, which is why she is asking about the road ahead to the next village. None of this is true. All of it holds. She has learned that the most sustainable cover is one that requires the minimum number of lies — the one that lets the truth do as much of the work as possible.
**Age 52 (699 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 53 (700 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Uther Edsen died

**Age 54 (701 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.

**Age 55 (702 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have.
**Age 56 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (704 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 58 (705 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 59 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (707 TR)** — The same dream visited her three nights. She woke each time unsettled and unable to say why.
**Age 61 (708 TR)** — She became the person others consulted. This had been happening for years; it was only recently that she recognised it as a pattern.
**Age 62 (709 TR)** — A person she had met once, briefly, remembered her name. She remembered nothing about them. She felt guilty about this.
**Age 63 (710 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 64 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 66 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 67 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 68 (715 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 69 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 71 (718 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 72 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 73 (720 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.

Life continued. **Age 71 (718 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 72 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 73 (720 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
name: Helda Parman

narrative: **Age 18 (671 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 19 (672 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

**Age 20 (673 TR)** — The tactical situation made going back inadvisable. She went anyway. The knight she brought back was alive, which had not been certain from a distance, and was subsequently able to walk, which had also not been certain. He never thanked her directly. He gave her his second sword when he returned to civilian life, which she understood to be the same thing.

**Age 21 (674 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 22 (675 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Mael Parkin (soldier, age 26)
**Age 22 (675 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.

**Age 23 (676 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.

**Age 23 (676 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Cade Parkin. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Cade Parkin born (male)

**Age 24 (677 TR)** — Her Pagaelin was better than the captive expected, which bought her thirty seconds of genuine surprise before they closed down again. She used those thirty seconds. Not for information — for observation. She learned more from watching someone decide not to answer than she had from the previous three interrogations.
**Age 25 (678 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 25 (678 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.

**Age 26 (679 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 26 (679 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Yoric Parkin lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Yoric Parkin born (male)
**Age 27 (680 TR)** — When the coin was offered, she found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 27 (680 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 28 (681 TR)** — She found companionship elsewhere. The risks were real, and she took them anyway.
**Age 28 (681 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 8
**Age 29 (682 TR)** — She remembered one particular market day with unusual clarity — the smells, the light, the sequence of purchases — without knowing why that one.
**Age 29 (682 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Fenric Parkin — lived. week 40 Fenric Parkin born (male)
**Age 30 (683 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30 (683 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 4
**Age 31 (684 TR)** — She performed an old ritual her grandmother had taught her. She felt better afterwards and did not explain it.
**Age 31 (684 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 32 (685 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 32 (685 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.

**Age 33 (686 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.
**Age 34 (687 TR)** — A tune she caught half of at a festival lodged in her head for the rest of the season.
**Age 34 (687 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 35 (688 TR)** — She heard steps behind her that went quiet when she slowed. There was no one when she looked.
**Age 35 (688 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 35 (688 TR)** — She lost Yoric Parkin — still a child, not yet grown. Yoric Parkin died (age 9)

**Age 36 (689 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 36 (689 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 37 (690 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 37 (690 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.

**Age 38 (691 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.
**Age 38 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 39 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (693 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 41 (694 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 41 (694 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 41 (694 TR)** — Cade Parkin left to make her own way. Cade Parkin left home (age 18)
**Age 42 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (695 TR)** — Mael Parkin died after 20 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Mael Parkin died (approx. age 46)
**Age 42 (695 TR)** — Fenric Parkin had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Fenric Parkin died (age 13)
**Age 43 (696 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 44 (697 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.

**Age 45 (698 TR)** — The order was to burn the village. The people were Morgathian sympathisers, the officer said, which may even have been true. She refused in front of the column and said Larani's name when she did it. There was a silence. The officer did not repeat the order. She has never understood why.
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — He was among the men taken. She had no formal standing to object, no right to see him off. She stood at the edge of the road and watched the column go.
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — Her son Cade Parkin married Sera Edson. Cade Parkin married Sera Edson (peasant, age 18)
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Cade Parkin's child born (female, Cade Parkin age 22)
**Age 46 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 47 (700 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
**Age 48 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (701 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.

**Age 49 (702 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 49 (702 TR)** — He came back. The relief she felt told her things about herself she had not quite admitted before.

**Age 50 (703 TR)** — She picked it up the way all border people do — imperfectly, practically, with no interest in the grammar. Numbers, directions, threat indicators, the specific word for leave-now-or-die. She had heard it used correctly once and that was enough.
**Age 50 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (704 TR)** — She said something quietly to one person and realised from the silence around her that it had carried.
**Age 51 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (705 TR)** — She wept at something small and ordinary. She was not embarrassed. She let it pass.
**Age 52 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (705 TR)** — Cade Parkin's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Cade Parkin's child died (age 1)
**Age 53 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (708 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 56 (709 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.

**Age 57 (710 TR)** — She is a widow visiting her sister's family, which is why she is on this road, which is why she has stopped at this inn, which is why she is asking about the road ahead to the next village. None of this is true. All of it holds. She has learned that the most sustainable cover is one that requires the minimum number of lies — the one that lets the truth do as much of the work as possible.
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 58 (711 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice.
**Age 58 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (712 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 59 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (714 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 61 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 62 (715 TR)** — She stayed up until the candle was gone. She was not sure what she had been doing for the last hour.
**Age 62 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 63 (716 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 63 (716 TR)** — Cade Parkin died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Cade Parkin died (age 40)
**Age 64 (717 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 65 (718 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 66 (719 TR)** — She forgot an observance she had kept every year of her adult life. She was not sure what that meant.

**Age 67 (720 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
