name: Mara Eding

narrative: **Age 18 (677 TR)** — She lent something she valued and did not get it back. She said nothing. The matter sat between her and the other person for a long time.
**Age 19 (678 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked.
**Age 20 (679 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Rorik Walman (soldier, age 26)
**Age 20 (679 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Pedar Walman lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Pedar Walman born (male)
**Age 21 (680 TR)** — She told a joke she had been holding for the right moment. The right moment arrived. The joke was everything she had hoped.
**Age 22 (681 TR)** — She found companionship elsewhere. The risks were real, and she took them anyway.

**Age 22 (681 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 23 (682 TR)** — The vow was made in private, to the sword-altar, in language she had not planned and could not quite reproduce afterward. She survived. She has been paying the debt ever since in ways she has not always been able to name — small acts, a certain quality of attention, a refusal of one particular easy choice, repeated.
**Age 23 (682 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 24 (683 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 25 (684 TR)** — The secret came out. She faced the judgment of everyone she knew.
**Age 25 (684 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 26 (685 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.
**Age 26 (685 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 27 (686 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.

**Age 27 (686 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Ulric Walman. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Ulric Walman born (male)
**Age 28 (687 TR)** — She had to sleep outside. She had not planned for it. It was not as bad as she had expected and she was not going to say so.
**Age 28 (687 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.
**Age 29 (688 TR)** — She followed a cat through somewhere she did not know and found something she was glad to know was there.
**Age 30 (689 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 30 (689 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 31 (690 TR)** — She was seized by laughter at the worst possible time. She managed to contain it. Barely.
**Age 31 (690 TR)** — Rorik Walman died after 11 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Rorik Walman died (approx. age 37)

**Age 32 (691 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 32 (691 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 33 (692 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 33 (692 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 34 (693 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 34 (693 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 35 (694 TR)** — When the coin was offered, she found reasons to look the other way.

**Age 36 (695 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 36 (695 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 37 (696 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.

**Age 38 (697 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness.
**Age 38 (697 TR)** — Her son Pedar Walman married Rarith Brewer. Pedar Walman married Rarith Brewer (artisan, age 16)
**Age 39 (698 TR)** — She saw a situation developing that she might have done something about. She walked on. The question of whether she was right to has not fully settled.
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — Pedar Walman left to make her own way. Pedar Walman left home (age 20)
**Age 41 (700 TR)** — A woman she had never met told her to stay home that day. She went anyway. Nothing happened.
**Age 41 (700 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 42 (701 TR)** — She sat up talking to a stranger at an inn far longer than she intended. In the morning she felt rested anyway.
**Age 42 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Ulric Walman's illegitimate child born (female, Ulric Walman age 16)

**Age 44 (703 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 44 (703 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 45 (704 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 45 (704 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Pedar Walman's child born (male, Pedar Walman age 25)
**Age 45 (704 TR)** — Ulric Walman left to make her own way. Ulric Walman left home (age 18)
**Age 46 (705 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (706 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 47 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — Pedar Walman lost Ulan Walman. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Pedar Walman's child died (age 3)
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — She found the right words when someone else could not. She was surprised by herself.
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — Pedar Walman's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Pedar Walman's child died (age 2)

**Age 50 (709 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have.
**Age 50 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (710 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — Her son Ulric Walman married Ulda Lorsen. Ulric Walman married Ulda Lorsen (soldier, age 24)
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (714 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Laric Karman

**Age 56 (715 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — Ulric Walman lost Garith Walman. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Ulric Walman's child died (age 18)

name: Mara Vorsen

narrative: **Age 18 (683 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 19 (684 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.

**Age 20 (685 TR)** — She had been told that Larani answered prayer in extremity. She had not believed this with any conviction. On the slope above Erone she saw a man with a belly wound walk two hundred yards to the surgeon's tent under his own power. The surgeon confirmed what the wound implied. She has carried this carefully ever since, not sure what to do with it.

**Age 21 (686 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.

**Age 22 (687 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
**Age 23 (688 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Tavin Idkin (soldier, age 20)
**Age 23 (688 TR)** — She bore twins — Lothar Idkin and Dalric Idkin. The birth was hard and left her exhausted for weeks, but both children lived. twins Lothar Idkin and Dalric Idkin born (twins)
**Age 24 (689 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 24 (689 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 8
**Age 25 (690 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 25 (690 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
**Age 26 (691 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 26 (691 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.

**Age 27 (692 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 27 (692 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 28 (693 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 28 (693 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 29 (694 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
**Age 30 (695 TR)** — Something that had always been there stopped. She noticed the quiet before she identified what had gone.
**Age 30 (695 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.

**Age 31 (696 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 32 (697 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 32 (697 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.

**Age 33 (698 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
**Age 33 (698 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 31
**Age 34 (699 TR)** — She pulled a door that pushed and then pushed it and it still did not open and someone eventually told her it was bolted.
**Age 34 (699 TR)** — She lost Dalric Idkin — still a child, not yet grown. Dalric Idkin died (age 11)
**Age 35 (700 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 35 (700 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 36 (701 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 37 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (702 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.

**Age 38 (703 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.

**Age 38 (703 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 39 (704 TR)** — She is a widow visiting her sister's family, which is why she is on this road, which is why she has stopped at this inn, which is why she is asking about the road ahead to the next village. None of this is true. All of it holds. She has learned that the most sustainable cover is one that requires the minimum number of lies — the one that lets the truth do as much of the work as possible.
**Age 39 (704 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 40 (705 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 41 (706 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 41 (706 TR)** — Lothar Idkin left to make her own way. Lothar Idkin left home (age 18)
**Age 42 (707 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 42 (707 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 10
**Age 42 (707 TR)** — Her son Lothar Idkin married Caris Aling. Lothar Idkin married Caris Aling (soldier, age 18)

**Age 43 (708 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 43 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (708 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Lothar Idkin's child born (female, Lothar Idkin age 20)
**Age 44 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (709 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.
**Age 45 (710 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (710 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 46 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (711 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 46 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 47 (712 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.
**Age 47 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 48 (713 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have.
**Age 48 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (714 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Erith (clergy)
**Age 49 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (715 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

**Age 51 (716 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.
**Age 51 (716 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 52 (717 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 52 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (718 TR)** — She had to sleep outside. She had not planned for it. It was not as bad as she had expected and she was not going to say so.
**Age 53 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (683 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity. **Age 19 (684 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication. **Age 20 (685 TR)** — She had been told that Larani answered prayer in extremity. She had not believed this with any conviction. On the slope above Erone she saw a man with a belly wound walk two hundred yards to the surgeon's tent under his own power. The surgeon confirmed what the wound implied. She has carried this carefully ever since, not sure what to do with it. **Age 21 (686 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant. **Age 22 (687 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness. **Age 23 (688 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Tavin Idkin (soldier, age 20) **Age 23 (688 TR)** — She bore twins — Lothar Idkin and Dalric Idkin. The birth was hard and left her exhausted for weeks, but both children lived. twins Lothar Idkin and Dalric Idkin born (twins) **Age 24 (689 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 24 (689 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 8 **Age 25 (690 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down. **Age 25 (690 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it. **Age 26 (691 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community. **Age 26 (691 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier. **Age 27 (692 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not. **Age 27 (692 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day. **Age 28 (693 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it. **Age 28 (693 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself. **Age 29 (694 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone. **Age 30 (695 TR)** — Something that had always been there stopped. She noticed the quiet before she identified what had gone. **Age 30 (695 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back. **Age 31 (696 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like. **Age 32 (697 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues. **Age 32 (697 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took. **Age 33 (698 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively. **Age 33 (698 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 31 **Age 34 (699 TR)** — She pulled a door that pushed and then pushed it and it still did not open and someone eventually told her it was bolted. **Age 34 (699 TR)** — She lost Dalric Idkin — still a child, not yet grown. Dalric Idkin died (age 11) **Age 35 (700 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted. **Age 35 (700 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 36 (701 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears. **Age 37 (702 TR)** — He endured. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride. **Age 38 (703 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you. **Age 38 (703 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 39 (704 TR)** — She is a widow visiting her sister's family, which is why she is on this road, which is why she has stopped at this inn, which is why she is asking about the road ahead to the next village. None of this is true. All of it holds. She has learned that the most sustainable cover is one that requires the minimum number of lies — the one that lets the truth do as much of the work as possible. **Age 39 (704 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 40 (705 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community. **Age 41 (706 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 41 (706 TR)** — Lothar Idkin left to make her own way. Lothar Idkin left home (age 18) **Age 42 (707 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 42 (707 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. name: Belyn Laning

narrative: **Age 18 (689 TR)** — When the coin was offered, she found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 19 (690 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.

**Age 20 (691 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.

**Age 21 (692 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.

**Age 22 (693 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.
**Age 23 (694 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked.
**Age 24 (695 TR)** — She was taken — whether hostage, prisoner, or worse. She endured. A serious wound to the upper arm. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1).
**Age 25 (696 TR)** — She bore a child. Erek Vorson came into the world healthy. week 40 Erek Vorson born (male)
**Age 26 (697 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Rorik Warman (soldier, age 25)

**Age 26 (697 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 27 (698 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
**Age 27 (698 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 4
**Age 28 (699 TR)** — She was released — ransomed, exchanged, or freed by her own cunning. She left without looking back. Whatever she had learned in there she kept.
**Age 29 (700 TR)** — She let a coin decide. It came down one way. Her reaction told her which way she had hoped it would land.
**Age 29 (700 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 30 (701 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 30 (701 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 31 (702 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Rodric Lankin died
**Age 31 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (703 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 32 (703 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 33 (704 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 33 (704 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 34 (705 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 34 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 35 (706 TR)** — She found her first grey hair and looked at it for a long time before deciding it was just a hair.
**Age 35 (706 TR)** — Rorik Warman died after 9 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Rorik Warman died (approx. age 34)
**Age 36 (707 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 37 (708 TR)** — She made a remark that landed badly in ways she had not anticipated. She apologised. She is not sure it helped.

**Age 38 (709 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 38 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 39 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 40 (711 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
**Age 40 (711 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 41 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (712 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 42 (713 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.
**Age 42 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (713 TR)** — Her son Erek Vorson married Tara Warkin. Erek Vorson married Tara Warkin (peasant, age 16)
**Age 43 (714 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 44 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 44 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 45 (716 TR)** — She picked it up the way all border people do — imperfectly, practically, with no interest in the grammar. Numbers, directions, threat indicators, the specific word for leave-now-or-die. She had heard it used correctly once and that was enough.
**Age 45 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (716 TR)** — Erek Vorson left to make her own way. Erek Vorson left home (age 20)

**Age 46 (717 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 47 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (718 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 48 (719 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 48 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (719 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Erek Vorson's child born (female, Erek Vorson age 23)
**Age 49 (720 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Kavan Korman

She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 31 (702 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Rodric Lankin died **Age 31 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 32 (703 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read. **Age 32 (703 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day. **Age 33 (704 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not. **Age 33 (704 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough. **Age 34 (705 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question. **Age 34 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 35 (706 TR)** — She found her first grey hair and looked at it for a long time before deciding it was just a hair. **Age 35 (706 TR)** — Rorik Warman died after 9 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Rorik Warman died (approx. age 34) **Age 36 (707 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 37 (708 TR)** — She made a remark that landed badly in ways she had not anticipated. She apologised. She is not sure it helped. **Age 38 (709 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant. **Age 38 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 39 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 40 (711 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively. **Age 40 (711 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 41 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (712 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular. **Age 42 (713 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual. **Age 42 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 42 (713 TR)** — Her son Erek Vorson married Tara Warkin. Erek Vorson married Tara Warkin (peasant, age 16) **Age 43 (714 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 44 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 44 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 45 (716 TR)** — She picked it up the way all border people do — imperfectly, practically, with no interest in the grammar. Numbers, directions, threat indicators, the specific word for leave-now-or-die. She had heard it used correctly once and that was enough. **Age 45 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 45 (716 TR)** — Erek Vorson left to make her own way. Erek Vorson left home (age 20) **Age 46 (717 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you. **Age 47 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 47 (718 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left. **Age 48 (719 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her. **Age 48 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (719 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Erek Vorson's child born (female, Erek Vorson age 23) **Age 49 (720 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Kavan Korman
name: Caris Vorson

narrative: **Age 18 (695 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 19 (696 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in appearance (−1).

**Age 20 (697 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.

**Age 21 (698 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 22 (699 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Brand Valing (peasant, age 27)

**Age 22 (699 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 23 (700 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 23 (700 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.

**Age 24 (701 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 24 (701 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 4

**Age 25 (702 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.
**Age 25 (702 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.

**Age 26 (703 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 27 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 27 (704 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Kela Valing. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Kela Valing born (female)
**Age 28 (705 TR)** — She found companionship elsewhere. The risks were real, and she took them anyway.
**Age 28 (705 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 29 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 29 (706 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 35

**Age 30 (707 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
**Age 30 (707 TR)** — Brand Valing died after 8 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Brand Valing died (approx. age 35)
**Age 31 (708 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 32 (709 TR)** — A task she had estimated at two hours became an entire day. She adjusted.
**Age 33 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 34 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 35 (712 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 35 (712 TR)** — He was among the men taken. She had no formal standing to object, no right to see him off. She stood at the edge of the road and watched the column go.
**Age 36 (713 TR)** — She pulled a door that pushed and then pushed it and it still did not open and someone eventually told her it was bolted.
**Age 37 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (714 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 38 (715 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Seric Markin
**Age 39 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (716 TR)** — He came back. The relief she felt told her things about herself she had not quite admitted before.
**Age 40 (717 TR)** — The secret came out. She faced the judgment of everyone she knew.
**Age 41 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 42 (719 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 42 (719 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 43 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 43 (720 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 43 (720 TR)** — Her daughter Kela Valing married Aldric Garman. Kela Valing married Aldric Garman (soldier, age 18)

name: Talith Dorkin

narrative: **Age 18 (701 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.

**Age 19 (702 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 20 (703 TR)** — A woman she had never seen before paid for her meal and was gone before she could object.
**Age 21 (704 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Laric Rorman (soldier, age 22)
**Age 21 (704 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Lothar Rorman lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Lothar Rorman born (male)

**Age 22 (705 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 22 (705 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 23 (706 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 23 (706 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 24 (707 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.

**Age 25 (708 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 25 (708 TR)** — Laric Rorman died before they had found their way into a life together. She had barely learned what she was losing. Laric Rorman died (approx. age 26)
**Age 26 (709 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.

**Age 27 (710 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.

**Age 28 (711 TR)** — The vow was made in private, to the sword-altar, in language she had not planned and could not quite reproduce afterward. She survived. She has been paying the debt ever since in ways she has not always been able to name — small acts, a certain quality of attention, a refusal of one particular easy choice, repeated.
**Age 29 (712 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 29 (712 TR)** — She lost Lothar Rorman — still a child, not yet grown. Lothar Rorman died (age 8)
**Age 30 (713 TR)** — She did something precise and physical, once, that she had no explanation for and could not do again.
**Age 31 (714 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 31 (714 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.

**Age 32 (715 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.
**Age 33 (716 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.

**Age 34 (717 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 35 (718 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 16
**Age 36 (719 TR)** — She remembered one particular market day with unusual clarity — the smells, the light, the sequence of purchases — without knowing why that one.

**Age 37 (720 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.
**Age 37 (720 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.

She has been paying the debt ever since in ways she has not always been able to name — small acts, a certain quality of attention, a refusal of one particular easy choice, repeated. **Age 29 (712 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time. **Age 29 (712 TR)** — She lost Lothar Rorman — still a child, not yet grown. Lothar Rorman died (age 8) **Age 30 (713 TR)** — She did something precise and physical, once, that she had no explanation for and could not do again. **Age 31 (714 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public. **Age 31 (714 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her. **Age 32 (715 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough. **Age 33 (716 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question. **Age 34 (717 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like. **Age 35 (718 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 16 **Age 36 (719 TR)** — She remembered one particular market day with unusual clarity — the smells, the light, the sequence of purchases — without knowing why that one. **Age 37 (720 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway. **Age 37 (720 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
name: Edda Kaling

narrative: **Age 18 (707 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 19 (708 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Erlan Brewer (artisan, age 22)
**Age 19 (708 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.

**Age 20 (709 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
**Age 20 (709 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 21 (710 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Yoric Garsen
**Age 21 (710 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.

**Age 22 (711 TR)** — She is a widow visiting her sister's family, which is why she is on this road, which is why she has stopped at this inn, which is why she is asking about the road ahead to the next village. None of this is true. All of it holds. She has learned that the most sustainable cover is one that requires the minimum number of lies — the one that lets the truth do as much of the work as possible.

**Age 22 (711 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 23 (712 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 24 (713 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 24 (713 TR)** — She bore a child. Rorath Brewer came into the world healthy. week 40 Rorath Brewer born (female)
**Age 25 (714 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.

**Age 25 (714 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Antor Brewer. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Antor Brewer born (male)
**Age 26 (715 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the shoulder that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 26 (715 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 27 (716 TR)** — She caught the smell of smoke in a room where nothing had been lit. She looked and found nothing.
**Age 27 (716 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.

**Age 28 (717 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 29 (718 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 29 (718 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 30 (719 TR)** — She went the wrong way and found a place she would go back to many times after.
**Age 30 (719 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20
**Age 31 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (720 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Orin Brewer lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Orin Brewer born (male)

name: Elith Garkin

narrative: **Age 18 (713 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 19 (714 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 20 (715 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 21 (716 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Vorin Ralman

**Age 22 (717 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.

**Age 23 (718 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.

**Age 24 (719 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.

**Age 25 (720 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
