name: Yara Toring

narrative: **Age 18 (667 TR)** — She had been told that Larani answered prayer in extremity. She had not believed this with any conviction. On the slope above Erone she saw a man with a belly wound walk two hundred yards to the surgeon's tent under his own power. The surgeon confirmed what the wound implied. She has carried this carefully ever since, not sure what to do with it.

**Age 19 (668 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful.
**Age 20 (669 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 21 (670 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 22 (671 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 23 (672 TR)** — She found the right words when someone else could not. She was surprised by herself.
**Age 24 (673 TR)** — She was caught out in something small. The embarrassment was disproportionate to the thing itself.
**Age 25 (674 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward.
**Age 26 (675 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Loran Marson (soldier, age 28)
**Age 26 (675 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 10
**Age 27 (676 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 27 (676 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.

**Age 28 (677 TR)** — The pre-battle Alamirata was not what she had been taught in the chapel. It was shorter, rougher, conducted in cold and half-dark over people who were afraid. The Matakea did not pretend otherwise. She said the goddess was present in fear as in courage and that they were all equally hers. It was enough.
**Age 28 (677 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 11
**Age 29 (678 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 29 (678 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.

**Age 30 (679 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.
**Age 30 (679 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 31 (680 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 31 (680 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 32 (681 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 33 (682 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Sera Idsen (soldier)
**Age 33 (682 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 4

**Age 34 (683 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 34 (683 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 35 (684 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.

**Age 35 (684 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Durst Marson. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Durst Marson born (male)
**Age 36 (685 TR)** — She asked for something specific and it came. Not dramatically. Just — it came.
**Age 36 (685 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 35
**Age 37 (686 TR)** — She found her first grey hair and looked at it for a long time before deciding it was just a hair.
**Age 37 (686 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Idra Marson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Idra Marson born (female)
**Age 38 (687 TR)** — She watched her fire die. She had somewhere to be. She stayed until the last coal went grey.
**Age 38 (687 TR)** — Loran Marson died after 12 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Loran Marson died (approx. age 40)

**Age 39 (688 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
**Age 39 (688 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 39 (688 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Idra Marson died (age 2)

**Age 40 (689 TR)** — The order was to burn the village. The people were Morgathian sympathisers, the officer said, which may even have been true. She refused in front of the column and said Larani's name when she did it. There was a silence. The officer did not repeat the order. She has never understood why.
**Age 41 (690 TR)** — A woman she had never seen before paid for her meal and was gone before she could object.
**Age 41 (690 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 42 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 43 (692 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 44 (693 TR)** — She made a remark that landed badly in ways she had not anticipated. She apologised. She is not sure it helped.
**Age 45 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 47 (696 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
**Age 47 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (697 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 49 (698 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 50 (699 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 51 (700 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 51 (700 TR)** — Her son Durst Marson married Elara Ralman. Durst Marson married Elara Ralman (soldier, age 16)
**Age 52 (701 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 52 (701 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Durst Marson's child born (female, Durst Marson age 17)

**Age 53 (702 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Ida Vorsen became a close friend

**Age 54 (703 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 54 (703 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 54 (703 TR)** — Durst Marson left to make her own way. Durst Marson left home (age 19)
**Age 55 (704 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 56 (705 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Sera Idsen has died
**Age 56 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 57 (706 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 58 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (709 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 61 (710 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 61 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 62 (711 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 63 (712 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 63 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 64 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 64 (713 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 65 (714 TR)** — She bought something impractical and unnecessary. She kept it without apology.
**Age 66 (715 TR)** — She became the person others consulted. This had been happening for years; it was only recently that she recognised it as a pattern.
**Age 67 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 67 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 68 (717 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 68 (717 TR)** — Durst Marson lost Kael Marson. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Durst Marson's child died (age 5)
**Age 69 (718 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 69 (718 TR)** — Durst Marson lost Nara Marson. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Durst Marson's child died (age 13)
**Age 70 (719 TR)** — The debt that had dogged her was finally cleared.
**Age 71 (720 TR)** — She pulled a door that pushed and then pushed it and it still did not open and someone eventually told her it was bolted.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (667 TR)** — She had been told that Larani answered prayer in extremity. She had not believed this with any conviction. On the slope above Erone she saw a man with a belly wound walk two hundred yards to the surgeon's tent under his own power. The surgeon confirmed what the wound implied. She has carried this carefully ever since, not sure what to do with it. **Age 19 (668 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful. **Age 20 (669 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 21 (670 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her. **Age 22 (671 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read. **Age 23 (672 TR)** — She found the right words when someone else could not. She was surprised by herself. **Age 24 (673 TR)** — She was caught out in something small. The embarrassment was disproportionate to the thing itself. **Age 25 (674 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward. **Age 26 (675 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Loran Marson (soldier, age 28) **Age 26 (675 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 10 **Age 27 (676 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option. **Age 27 (676 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it. **Age 28 (677 TR)** — The pre-battle Alamirata was not what she had been taught in the chapel. It was shorter, rougher, conducted in cold and half-dark over people who were afraid. The Matakea did not pretend otherwise. She said the goddess was present in fear as in courage and that they were all equally hers. It was enough. **Age 28 (677 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 11 **Age 29 (678 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 29 (678 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny. **Age 30 (679 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual. **Age 30 (679 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride. **Age 31 (680 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 31 (680 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 32 (681 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover. **Age 33 (682 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Sera Idsen (soldier) **Age 33 (682 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 4 **Age 34 (683 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at. **Age 34 (683 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 35 (684 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant. **Age 35 (684 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Durst Marson. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Durst Marson born (male) **Age 36 (685 TR)** — She asked for something specific and it came. Not dramatically. Just — it came. **Age 36 (685 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 35 **Age 37 (686 TR)** — She found her first grey hair and looked at it for a long time before deciding it was just a hair. **Age 37 (686 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Idra Marson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Idra Marson born (female) **Age 38 (687 TR)** — She watched her fire die. She had somewhere to be. She stayed until the last coal went grey. **Age 38 (687 TR)** — Loran Marson died after 12 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Loran Marson died (approx. age 40) **Age 39 (688 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness. **Age 39 (688 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough. **Age 39 (688 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Idra Marson died (age 2) **Age 40 (689 TR)** — The order was to burn the village. The people were Morgathian sympathisers, the officer said, which may even have been true. She refused in front of the column and said Larani's name when she did it. There was a silence. The officer did not repeat the order. She has never understood why. **Age 41 (690 TR)** — A woman she had never seen before paid for her meal and was gone before she could object. **Age 41 (690 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 42 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 43 (692 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you. **Age 44 (693 TR)** — She made a remark that landed badly in ways she had not anticipated. She apologised. She is not sure it helped. **Age 45 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 45 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (696 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively. **Age 47 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 48 (697 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 49 (698 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 50 (699 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness. **Age 51 (700 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues. **Age 51 (700 TR)** — Her son Durst Marson married Elara Ralman. Durst Marson married Elara Ralman (soldier, age 16) **Age 52 (701 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 52 (701 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Durst Marson's child born (female, Durst Marson age 17) **Age 53 (702 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Ida Vorsen became a close friend **Age 54 (703 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it. **Age 54 (703 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 54 (703 TR)** — Durst Marson left to make her own way. Durst Marson left home (age 19) **Age 55 (704 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down. **Age 56 (705 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Sera Idsen has died **Age 56 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (706 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears. **Age 58 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 59 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 60 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 60 (709 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 61 (710 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers. **Age 61 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 62 (711 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it. **Age 63 (712 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 63 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 64 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 64 (713 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left. **Age 65 (714 TR)** — She bought something impractical and unnecessary. She kept it without apology. **Age 66 (715 TR)** — She became the person others consulted. This had been happening for years; it was only recently that she recognised it as a pattern. **Age 67 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 67 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 68 (717 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her. **Age 68 (717 TR)** — Durst Marson lost Kael Marson. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Durst Marson's child died (age 5) **Age 69 (718 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs. **Age 69 (718 TR)** — Durst Marson lost Nara Marson. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Durst Marson's child died (age 13) **Age 70 (719 TR)** — The debt that had dogged her was finally cleared. **Age 71 (720 TR)** — She pulled a door that pushed and then pushed it and it still did not open and someone eventually told her it was bolted.
name: Edda Lorman

narrative: **Age 18 (673 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 19 (674 TR)** — She lost something she was responsible for. She found it three weeks later exactly where she had left it and forgotten.

**Age 20 (675 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.

**Age 21 (676 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness.
**Age 22 (677 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward.
**Age 23 (678 TR)** — She stood somewhere and saw something beautiful. She kept the image of it longer than most things.

**Age 24 (679 TR)** — The vow was made in private, to the sword-altar, in language she had not planned and could not quite reproduce afterward. She survived. She has been paying the debt ever since in ways she has not always been able to name — small acts, a certain quality of attention, a refusal of one particular easy choice, repeated.
**Age 25 (680 TR)** — The morning was so misty that she could see only a few steps ahead. She walked slowly and arrived late and was glad of both.

**Age 26 (681 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 27 (682 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Torin Harson died

**Age 28 (683 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 29 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 30 (685 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.

**Age 31 (686 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
**Age 32 (687 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.

**Age 33 (688 TR)** — She watched the Matakea work the line under fire — not running, not taking cover, just walking. Speaking. The priest who does this understands something she does not, she thinks. Or believes something she does not. The difference may matter.
**Age 34 (689 TR)** — She bought something impractical and unnecessary. She kept it without apology.
**Age 35 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 36 (691 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 37 (692 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Dalric Aldane (noble)

**Age 38 (693 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.

**Age 39 (694 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.

**Age 40 (695 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.

**Age 41 (696 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 42 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (698 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Dalric Aldane has died
**Age 44 (699 TR)** — She called someone by their dead sibling's name. The silence that followed lasted a long time.
**Age 45 (700 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (701 TR)** — She stopped at an old waymarker she knew well. She had never stopped there before. She could not say what made her stop.
**Age 47 (702 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 48 (703 TR)** — She came back from a journey and never ate a specific thing again. She did not discuss it.
**Age 49 (704 TR)** — A woman she had never met told her to stay home that day. She went anyway. Nothing happened.

**Age 50 (705 TR)** — She had been told that Larani answered prayer in extremity. She had not believed this with any conviction. On the slope above Erone she saw a man with a belly wound walk two hundred yards to the surgeon's tent under his own power. The surgeon confirmed what the wound implied. She has carried this carefully ever since, not sure what to do with it.
**Age 51 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 53 (708 TR)** — The order was to burn the village. The people were Morgathian sympathisers, the officer said, which may even have been true. She refused in front of the column and said Larani's name when she did it. There was a silence. The officer did not repeat the order. She has never understood why.
**Age 54 (709 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
**Age 55 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 57 (712 TR)** — The tactical situation made going back inadvisable. She went anyway. The knight she brought back was alive, which had not been certain from a distance, and was subsequently able to walk, which had also not been certain. He never thanked her directly. He gave her his second sword when he returned to civilian life, which she understood to be the same thing.
**Age 58 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (714 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.

**Age 60 (715 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have.
**Age 61 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (717 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 63 (718 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 64 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
*(Ages 65–65: quiet years)*

She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it. **Age 42 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 43 (698 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Dalric Aldane has died **Age 44 (699 TR)** — She called someone by their dead sibling's name. The silence that followed lasted a long time. **Age 45 (700 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 46 (701 TR)** — She stopped at an old waymarker she knew well. She had never stopped there before. She could not say what made her stop. **Age 47 (702 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs. **Age 48 (703 TR)** — She came back from a journey and never ate a specific thing again. She did not discuss it. **Age 49 (704 TR)** — A woman she had never met told her to stay home that day. She went anyway. Nothing happened. **Age 50 (705 TR)** — She had been told that Larani answered prayer in extremity. She had not believed this with any conviction. On the slope above Erone she saw a man with a belly wound walk two hundred yards to the surgeon's tent under his own power. The surgeon confirmed what the wound implied. She has carried this carefully ever since, not sure what to do with it. **Age 51 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 53 (708 TR)** — The order was to burn the village. The people were Morgathian sympathisers, the officer said, which may even have been true. She refused in front of the column and said Larani's name when she did it. There was a silence. The officer did not repeat the order. She has never understood why. **Age 54 (709 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift. **Age 55 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 56 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (712 TR)** — The tactical situation made going back inadvisable. She went anyway. The knight she brought back was alive, which had not been certain from a distance, and was subsequently able to walk, which had also not been certain. He never thanked her directly. He gave her his second sword when he returned to civilian life, which she understood to be the same thing. **Age 58 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 59 (714 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete. **Age 60 (715 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have. **Age 61 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 62 (717 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 63 (718 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity. **Age 64 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. *(Ages 65–65: quiet years)*
name: Leda Waring

narrative: **Age 18 (679 TR)** — She heard something through a door that changed how she thought about two people she knew. She said nothing.

**Age 19 (680 TR)** — The tactical situation made going back inadvisable. She went anyway. The knight she brought back was alive, which had not been certain from a distance, and was subsequently able to walk, which had also not been certain. He never thanked her directly. He gave her his second sword when he returned to civilian life, which she understood to be the same thing.
**Age 20 (681 TR)** — A fox paused on the path and looked at her with calm, appraising eyes before trotting away.
**Age 21 (682 TR)** — She thought of what she should have said long after there was any chance to say it.

**Age 22 (683 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.

**Age 23 (684 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
**Age 24 (685 TR)** — She was taken — whether hostage, prisoner, or worse. She endured. A minor wound to the lower leg or shin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 25 (686 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 26 (687 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.

**Age 27 (688 TR)** — She was not carrying a chivalric weapon. She was carrying her employer's letter in a sealed case, and the seal looked like a heraldic device to a guard who did not know what a heraldic device actually looked like, and the guard decided that the case itself constituted bearing arms without right and detained her for half a day. The alderman who resolved it apologised. The guard was reprimanded. She filed a complaint with the guild that employed the guard service. She received a written acknowledgment. She carries the employer's letter unsealed now, which the employer finds aesthetically displeasing and which she considers a reasonable trade.

**Age 28 (689 TR)** — The vow was made in private, to the sword-altar, in language she had not planned and could not quite reproduce afterward. She survived. She has been paying the debt ever since in ways she has not always been able to name — small acts, a certain quality of attention, a refusal of one particular easy choice, repeated.

**Age 29 (690 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.

**Age 30 (691 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 31 (692 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
**Age 32 (693 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Britta Branson died

**Age 33 (694 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
**Age 34 (695 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 35 (696 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 36 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (698 TR)** — The ferry was not running. She waited. Another passenger talked at her. She listened politely for two hours.

**Age 38 (699 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 39 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 40 (701 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 41 (702 TR)** — She was up before anyone and watched the sun come up from somewhere good. She did not tell anyone about it.
**Age 42 (703 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.

**Age 43 (704 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 44 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 45 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (707 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (708 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 48 (709 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 49 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 50 (711 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.
**Age 51 (712 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.

**Age 52 (713 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.
**Age 53 (714 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 54 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (718 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.

**Age 58 (719 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 59 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
