name: Valith Iding

narrative: **Age 18 (685 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Joran Valsen (soldier, age 22)

**Age 19 (686 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.

**Age 19 (686 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 20 (687 TR)** — She told a story that spread beyond the people she had told it to. She was never sure if that was good.
**Age 20 (687 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 15
**Age 21 (688 TR)** — A secret entrusted to her found its way to those who would pay for it.

**Age 22 (689 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.

**Age 22 (689 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Tovan Valsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Tovan Valsen born (male)

**Age 23 (690 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 23 (690 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 24 (691 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked.
**Age 24 (691 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 25 (692 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Koral Warsen died
**Age 25 (692 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.

**Age 26 (693 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.
**Age 27 (694 TR)** — She was taken — whether hostage, prisoner, or worse. She endured. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in eyesight (−1).
**Age 27 (694 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 28 (695 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.

**Age 29 (696 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.

**Age 29 (696 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Yara Valsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Yara Valsen born (female)
**Age 30 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 30 (697 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Tovan Valsen hired out (age 8)
**Age 31 (698 TR)** — She let a coin decide. It came down one way. Her reaction told her which way she had hoped it would land.
**Age 31 (698 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 32 (699 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 32 (699 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 33 (700 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Lothar Roring
**Age 34 (701 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.

**Age 34 (701 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Halric Valsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Halric Valsen born (male)
**Age 35 (702 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 35 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (703 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Gotar Dalsen (soldier)
**Age 36 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (704 TR)** — She sat up talking to a stranger at an inn far longer than she intended. In the morning she felt rested anyway.

**Age 37 (704 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 38 (705 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 38 (705 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 38 (705 TR)** — Her son Tovan Valsen married Branna Glazier. Tovan Valsen married Branna Glazier (artisan, age 16)
**Age 38 (705 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Tovan Valsen's child born (female, Tovan Valsen age 16)
**Age 39 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (707 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 40 (707 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 9
**Age 40 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (708 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.

**Age 42 (709 TR)** — The order was to burn the village. The people were Morgathian sympathisers, the officer said, which may even have been true. She refused in front of the column and said Larani's name when she did it. There was a silence. The officer did not repeat the order. She has never understood why.
**Age 42 (709 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 43 (710 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 44 (711 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 45 (712 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 45 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (713 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 46 (713 TR)** — Tovan Valsen's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Tovan Valsen's child died (age 1)
**Age 47 (714 TR)** — The debt that had dogged her was finally cleared.
**Age 47 (714 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Yara Valsen's illegitimate child born (male, Yara Valsen age 18)
**Age 47 (714 TR)** — Yara Valsen left to make her own way. Yara Valsen left home (age 18)
**Age 48 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 48 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (717 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 51 (718 TR)** — She stood somewhere and saw something beautiful. She kept the image of it longer than most things.
**Age 51 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (718 TR)** — Her son Halric Valsen married Narith Dorkin. Halric Valsen married Narith Dorkin (soldier, age 16)
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (720 TR)** — Joran Valsen died after 35 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Joran Valsen died (approx. age 57)
narrativeText: **Age 18 (685 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Joran Valsen (soldier, age 22) **Age 19 (686 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not. **Age 19 (686 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny. **Age 20 (687 TR)** — She told a story that spread beyond the people she had told it to. She was never sure if that was good. **Age 20 (687 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 15 **Age 21 (688 TR)** — A secret entrusted to her found its way to those who would pay for it. **Age 22 (689 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual. **Age 22 (689 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Tovan Valsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Tovan Valsen born (male) **Age 23 (690 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it. **Age 23 (690 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself. **Age 24 (691 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked. **Age 24 (691 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 25 (692 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Koral Warsen died **Age 25 (692 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it. **Age 26 (693 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication. **Age 27 (694 TR)** — She was taken — whether hostage, prisoner, or worse. She endured. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in eyesight (−1). **Age 27 (694 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day. **Age 28 (695 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness. **Age 29 (696 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her. **Age 29 (696 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Yara Valsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Yara Valsen born (female) **Age 30 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 30 (697 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Tovan Valsen hired out (age 8) **Age 31 (698 TR)** — She let a coin decide. It came down one way. Her reaction told her which way she had hoped it would land. **Age 31 (698 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 32 (699 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like. **Age 32 (699 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically. **Age 33 (700 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Lothar Roring **Age 34 (701 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 34 (701 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Halric Valsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Halric Valsen born (male) **Age 35 (702 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 35 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 36 (703 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Gotar Dalsen (soldier) **Age 36 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 37 (704 TR)** — She sat up talking to a stranger at an inn far longer than she intended. In the morning she felt rested anyway. **Age 37 (704 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 38 (705 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it. **Age 38 (705 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days. **Age 38 (705 TR)** — Her son Tovan Valsen married Branna Glazier. Tovan Valsen married Branna Glazier (artisan, age 16) **Age 38 (705 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Tovan Valsen's child born (female, Tovan Valsen age 16) **Age 39 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 40 (707 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time. **Age 40 (707 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 9 **Age 40 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (708 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine. **Age 42 (709 TR)** — The order was to burn the village. The people were Morgathian sympathisers, the officer said, which may even have been true. She refused in front of the column and said Larani's name when she did it. There was a silence. The officer did not repeat the order. She has never understood why. **Age 42 (709 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular. **Age 43 (710 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 44 (711 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 45 (712 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 45 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 46 (713 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 46 (713 TR)** — Tovan Valsen's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Tovan Valsen's child died (age 1) **Age 47 (714 TR)** — The debt that had dogged her was finally cleared. **Age 47 (714 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Yara Valsen's illegitimate child born (male, Yara Valsen age 18) **Age 47 (714 TR)** — Yara Valsen left to make her own way. Yara Valsen left home (age 18) **Age 48 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 48 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 49 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 49 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 50 (717 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 51 (718 TR)** — She stood somewhere and saw something beautiful. She kept the image of it longer than most things. **Age 51 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 51 (718 TR)** — Her son Halric Valsen married Narith Dorkin. Halric Valsen married Narith Dorkin (soldier, age 16) **Age 52 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (719 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would. **Age 52 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 53 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 53 (720 TR)** — Joran Valsen died after 35 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Joran Valsen died (approx. age 57)
name: Lara Toring

narrative: **Age 18 (691 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.

**Age 19 (692 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 20 (693 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Wulf Alson (soldier, age 18)
**Age 20 (693 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Kelyn Alson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Kelyn Alson born (female)

**Age 21 (694 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 21 (694 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.

**Age 22 (695 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.
**Age 22 (695 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 22 (695 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Kelyn Alson died (age 2)

**Age 23 (696 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.
**Age 24 (697 TR)** — She called someone by their dead sibling's name. The silence that followed lasted a long time.
**Age 24 (697 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Norath Alson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Norath Alson born (female)
**Age 25 (698 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 25 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 26 (699 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 27 (700 TR)** — She was caught out in something small. The embarrassment was disproportionate to the thing itself.

**Age 27 (700 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Ronan Alson. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Ronan Alson born (male)
**Age 28 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 29 (702 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 29 (702 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 28

**Age 30 (703 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.
**Age 30 (703 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 31 (704 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 32 (705 TR)** — She ducked into a temple to get out of the rain and stayed far longer than the rain lasted.
**Age 33 (706 TR)** — A crow took to sitting near where she worked. She talked to it eventually. She did not mention this to anyone.

**Age 33 (706 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Horic Alson. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Horic Alson born (male)
**Age 33 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 34 (707 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 35 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 35 (708 TR)** — She bore a child. Fenric Alson came into the world healthy. week 40 Fenric Alson born (male)
**Age 36 (709 TR)** — She looked for something she had known all her life and found it had been taken down. She stood where it had been.
**Age 36 (709 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Selyn Alson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Selyn Alson born (female)
**Age 37 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (711 TR)** — She told a story that spread beyond the people she had told it to. She was never sure if that was good.

**Age 38 (711 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 39 (712 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Talith Cargoham (merchant)
**Age 39 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (713 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.

**Age 41 (714 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Talith Cargoham became a close friend
**Age 41 (714 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 42 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 43 (716 TR)** — The tactical situation made going back inadvisable. She went anyway. The knight she brought back was alive, which had not been certain from a distance, and was subsequently able to walk, which had also not been certain. He never thanked her directly. He gave her his second sword when he returned to civilian life, which she understood to be the same thing.
**Age 44 (717 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 44 (717 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 45 (718 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.

**Age 46 (719 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 46 (719 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 47 (720 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice.

**Age 21 (700 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 22 (701 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 22 (701 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.

**Age 23 (702 TR)** — The Alamirata was not her usual practice but she had been passing and the door was open. She sat through the recitation of the Tirannon and found herself moved by the rhythm of it despite not believing everything it implied. Faith, she thought, was a complicated thing to leave behind cleanly.
**Age 23 (702 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 24 (703 TR)** — She sat up talking to a stranger at an inn far longer than she intended. In the morning she felt rested anyway.
**Age 25 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 25 (704 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 26 (705 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 27 (706 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 28 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 28 (707 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.

**Age 29 (708 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 29 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 30 (709 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have.

**Age 31 (710 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
**Age 31 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 32 (711 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice.
**Age 32 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 33 (712 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 34 (713 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 34 (713 TR)** — Osric Tarsen had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Osric Tarsen died (age 14)

**Age 35 (714 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 36 (715 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 37 (716 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
**Age 38 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 39 (718 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.

**Age 41 (720 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.

name: Belyn Tarsen

narrative: **Age 18 (703 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 19 (704 TR)** — She was not carrying a chivalric weapon. She was carrying her employer's letter in a sealed case, and the seal looked like a heraldic device to a guard who did not know what a heraldic device actually looked like, and the guard decided that the case itself constituted bearing arms without right and detained her for half a day. The alderman who resolved it apologised. The guard was reprimanded. She filed a complaint with the guild that employed the guard service. She received a written acknowledgment. She carries the employer's letter unsealed now, which the employer finds aesthetically displeasing and which she considers a reasonable trade.
**Age 20 (705 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Erith (clergy)

**Age 21 (706 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.

**Age 22 (707 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.

**Age 23 (708 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.

**Age 24 (709 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 25 (710 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Reda Orman died
**Age 26 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 27 (712 TR)** — She waved at someone who looked at her with polite puzzlement. The wave had been for someone behind her.
**Age 28 (713 TR)** — She was taken — whether hostage, prisoner, or worse. She endured. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).

**Age 29 (714 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.

**Age 30 (715 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
**Age 31 (716 TR)** — The change had been gradual and then sudden. She could trace exactly when it had stopped being repairable. Erith became an enemy
**Age 32 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 33 (718 TR)** — She ducked into a temple to get out of the rain and stayed far longer than the rain lasted.

**Age 34 (719 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 35 (720 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm.

narrative: **Age 18 (709 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 19 (710 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 20 (711 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 21 (712 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Osric Tarkin
**Age 22 (713 TR)** — When the coin was offered, she found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 23 (714 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 24 (715 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Osric Tarkin

**Age 25 (716 TR)** — The Alamirata was not her usual practice but she had been passing and the door was open. She sat through the recitation of the Tirannon and found herself moved by the rhythm of it despite not believing everything it implied. Faith, she thought, was a complicated thing to leave behind cleanly.

**Age 26 (717 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 27 (718 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 28 (719 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
*(Ages 29–29: years of routine)*

**Age 20 (717 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 21 (718 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Soran Lorman (soldier, age 22)
**Age 21 (718 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.

**Age 22 (719 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. **Age 22 (719 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 5
**Age 23 (720 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 23 (720 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 13
narrativeText: **Age 18 (715 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway. **Age 19 (716 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week. **Age 20 (717 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it. **Age 21 (718 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Soran Lorman (soldier, age 22) **Age 21 (718 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would. **Age 22 (719 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant. **Age 22 (719 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 5 **Age 23 (720 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community. **Age 23 (720 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 13
name: Talith Torsen

narrative: **Age 18 (663 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Beren Kalman
**Age 19 (664 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 20 (665 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 21 (666 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
**Age 22 (667 TR)** — She watched her fire die. She had somewhere to be. She stayed until the last coal went grey.

**Age 23 (668 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.

**Age 24 (669 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.
**Age 25 (670 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Rodric Saltworth (merchant)

**Age 26 (671 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 28 (673 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked.

**Age 29 (674 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 30 (675 TR)** — Her horse stopped dead on a familiar road and would not be moved for an hour.

**Age 31 (676 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.
**Age 32 (677 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (678 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward.
**Age 34 (679 TR)** — She meant to glance at the rain and check on it. She stood there watching it for a long time.
**Age 35 (680 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Beren Kalman
**Age 36 (681 TR)** — A secret entrusted to her found its way to those who would pay for it.
**Age 37 (682 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 38 (683 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.

**Age 39 (684 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 40 (685 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 43 (688 TR)** — She picked it up the way all border people do — imperfectly, practically, with no interest in the grammar. Numbers, directions, threat indicators, the specific word for leave-now-or-die. She had heard it used correctly once and that was enough.

**Age 44 (689 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 45 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (691 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Andar Sorkin died
**Age 47 (692 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 48 (693 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 49 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (696 TR)** — She found she could feel rain coming a day before it arrived. The feeling was accurate and not welcome.
**Age 53 (698 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 54 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (700 TR)** — She came back to somewhere she had not seen in years. She stood and looked at it for a long time before going in.
**Age 56 (701 TR)** — When the coin was offered, she found reasons to look the other way.

**Age 57 (702 TR)** — The pre-battle Alamirata was not what she had been taught in the chapel. It was shorter, rougher, conducted in cold and half-dark over people who were afraid. The Matakea did not pretend otherwise. She said the goddess was present in fear as in courage and that they were all equally hers. It was enough.
**Age 58 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (706 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.

**Age 62 (707 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 63 (708 TR)** — The same dream visited her three nights. She woke each time unsettled and unable to say why.
**Age 64 (709 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 65 (710 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 66 (711 TR)** — The old pain returned, and this time it stayed. A minor wound to the shoulder that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 67 (712 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 68 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 69 (714 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Disa Yorman
**Age 70 (715 TR)** — She became the person others consulted. This had been happening for years; it was only recently that she recognised it as a pattern.
**Age 71 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 72 (717 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.

**Age 73 (718 TR)** — She understood the stories now — not just the demands and the threats but the arguments, the insults, the jokes that were not quite jokes. She filed that away. Understanding a people's humour is understanding what they consider themselves to be. It was not a comfortable thing to know.
**Age 74 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 75 (720 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
