She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual. **Age 22 (685 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Valith Tarkin lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Valith Tarkin born (female) **Age 23 (686 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover. **Age 23 (686 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Tovan Tarkin. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Tovan Tarkin born (male) **Age 24 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 24 (687 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 5 **Age 25 (688 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the lower leg or shin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 25 (688 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain. **Age 25 (688 TR)** — She lost Branna Tarkin — still a child, not yet grown. Branna Tarkin died (age 4) **Age 26 (689 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Loran Wolkin **Age 27 (690 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected. **Age 27 (690 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 28 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 29 (692 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful. **Age 30 (693 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk. **Age 30 (693 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Valith Tarkin hired out (age 8) **Age 31 (694 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option. **Age 31 (694 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular. **Age 32 (695 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it. **Age 32 (695 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Osric Tarkin. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Osric Tarkin born (male) **Age 33 (696 TR)** — She waited in line. She was next. The thing she had been waiting for was gone. **Age 33 (696 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Zara Tarkin lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Zara Tarkin born (female) **Age 34 (697 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 34 (697 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took. **Age 34 (697 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Osric Tarkin died (age 2) **Age 35 (698 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears. **Age 35 (698 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Arden Tarkin. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Arden Tarkin born (female) **Age 36 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 36 (699 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Berith Tarkin lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Berith Tarkin born (female) **Age 37 (700 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that. **Age 37 (700 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would. **Age 38 (701 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness. **Age 38 (701 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Antor Tarkin lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Antor Tarkin born (male) **Age 39 (702 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice. **Age 39 (702 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Noral Tarkin lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Noral Tarkin born (male) **Age 40 (703 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 40 (703 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 19 **Age 40 (703 TR)** — Her son Idris Tarkin married Mira Wolkin. Idris Tarkin married Mira Wolkin (soldier, age 16) **Age 41 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 41 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 41 (704 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Idris Tarkin's child born (male, Idris Tarkin age 21) **Age 42 (705 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her. **Age 43 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 43 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 43 (706 TR)** — Her daughter Valith Tarkin married Malik Malkin. Valith Tarkin married Malik Malkin (soldier, age 20) **Age 43 (706 TR)** — Tovan Tarkin left to make her own way. Tovan Tarkin left home (age 20) **Age 43 (706 TR)** — She lost Noral Tarkin — still a child, not yet grown. Noral Tarkin died (age 4) **Age 44 (707 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway. **Age 44 (707 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny. **Age 44 (707 TR)** — Idris Tarkin left to make her own way. Idris Tarkin left home (age 24) **Age 44 (707 TR)** — Her son Tovan Tarkin married Halla Eding. Tovan Tarkin married Halla Eding (soldier, age 19) **Age 45 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 45 (708 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself. **Age 45 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (709 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 46 (709 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 47 (710 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down. **Age 47 (710 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 47 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 48 (711 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs. **Age 48 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 49 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 50 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 50 (713 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride. **Age 51 (714 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 51 (714 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it. **Age 51 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (715 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left. **Age 52 (715 TR)** — Her daughter Zara Tarkin married Noric Kaling. Zara Tarkin married Noric Kaling (soldier, age 18) **Age 52 (715 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Arden Tarkin's illegitimate child born (male, Arden Tarkin age 17) **Age 52 (715 TR)** — Her daughter Berith Tarkin married Ranulf Millward. Berith Tarkin married Ranulf Millward (artisan, age 16) **Age 53 (716 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 53 (716 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 54 (717 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 54 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (717 TR)** — Arden Tarkin left to make her own way. Arden Tarkin left home (age 19) **Age 55 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 55 (718 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 55 (718 TR)** — Her son Antor Tarkin married Edda Smith. Antor Tarkin married Edda Smith (artisan, age 16) **Age 56 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (719 TR)** — Idris Tarkin lost Warin Tarkin. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Idris Tarkin's child died (age 15) **Age 56 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (719 TR)** — Tovan Tarkin's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Tovan Tarkin's child died (age 0) **Age 56 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (719 TR)** — Antor Tarkin left to make her own way. Antor Tarkin left home (age 18) **Age 57 (720 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Aldis Bransen **Age 57 (720 TR)** — Her daughter Arden Tarkin married Noral Horing. Arden Tarkin married Noral Horing (soldier, age 19) **Age 57 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
name: Delia Karing

narrative: **Age 18 (687 TR)** — The pre-battle Alamirata was not what she had been taught in the chapel. It was shorter, rougher, conducted in cold and half-dark over people who were afraid. The Matakea did not pretend otherwise. She said the goddess was present in fear as in courage and that they were all equally hers. It was enough.
**Age 19 (688 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.

**Age 20 (689 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.

**Age 21 (690 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.

**Age 22 (691 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 23 (692 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Voran Warsen (soldier, age 23)
**Age 23 (692 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 13
**Age 24 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 24 (693 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.

**Age 25 (694 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.

**Age 26 (695 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.

**Age 26 (695 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 27 (696 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Bram Rorson
**Age 27 (696 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.

**Age 28 (697 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice.

**Age 29 (698 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 29 (698 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 30 (699 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 30 (699 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Elric Warsen lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Elric Warsen born (male)

**Age 31 (700 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 31 (700 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
**Age 32 (701 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 32 (701 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 8

**Age 33 (702 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.
**Age 33 (702 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Orith Warsen — lived. week 40 Orith Warsen born (female)
**Age 34 (703 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward.
**Age 34 (703 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.

**Age 35 (704 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
**Age 35 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (705 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 36 (705 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 37 (706 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Tavin (clergy)
**Age 37 (706 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.

**Age 38 (707 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 38 (707 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 39 (708 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Bram Rorson
**Age 39 (708 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.
**Age 40 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 40 (709 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 41 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (710 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 41 (710 TR)** — She lost Elric Warsen — still a child, not yet grown. Elric Warsen died (age 11)
**Age 42 (711 TR)** — She used the last of something she had been saving and felt the loss of it more than made sense.
**Age 43 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 43 (712 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 44 (713 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Tavin
**Age 45 (714 TR)** — She caught the smell of smoke in a room where nothing had been lit. She looked and found nothing.
**Age 45 (714 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 35
**Age 46 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (716 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 48 (717 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
**Age 49 (718 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 49 (718 TR)** — Her daughter Orith Warsen married Loran Elsen. Orith Warsen married Loran Elsen (soldier, age 16)
**Age 50 (719 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 50 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (720 TR)** — She lit a candle for someone she had not thought of in years. She did not examine this.
**Age 51 (720 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 51 (720 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Orith Warsen's child born (male, Orith Warsen age 18)

The silence lasted four days. **Age 35 (704 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not. **Age 35 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 36 (705 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity. **Age 36 (705 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular. **Age 37 (706 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Tavin (clergy) **Age 37 (706 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself. **Age 38 (707 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her. **Age 38 (707 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 39 (708 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Bram Rorson **Age 39 (708 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed. **Age 40 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 40 (709 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took. **Age 41 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 41 (710 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain. **Age 41 (710 TR)** — She lost Elric Warsen — still a child, not yet grown. Elric Warsen died (age 11) **Age 42 (711 TR)** — She used the last of something she had been saving and felt the loss of it more than made sense. **Age 43 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 43 (712 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 44 (713 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Tavin **Age 45 (714 TR)** — She caught the smell of smoke in a room where nothing had been lit. She looked and found nothing. **Age 45 (714 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 35 **Age 46 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 47 (716 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 48 (717 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it. **Age 49 (718 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down. **Age 49 (718 TR)** — Her daughter Orith Warsen married Loran Elsen. Orith Warsen married Loran Elsen (soldier, age 16) **Age 50 (719 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline. **Age 50 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 51 (720 TR)** — She lit a candle for someone she had not thought of in years. She did not examine this. **Age 51 (720 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically. **Age 51 (720 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Orith Warsen's child born (male, Orith Warsen age 18)
name: Aldis Coring

narrative: **Age 18 (716 TR)** — A door he expected to open did not. He pulled, then pushed, then pulled again, then read the situation correctly. There were witnesses.

**Age 19 (717 TR)** — He reached for it out of habit — the third hook by the door, the left-hand drawer, the box that used to sit on the shelf — and found nothing. The absence is more present to him than the object ever was.
**Age 20 (718 TR)** — He went somewhere that was muddy and came back in a state that was worse than he had anticipated.
**Age 21 (719 TR)** — Something moved him to tears he had not expected. He was alone, which was fortunate.

**Age 22 (720 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.

name: Horic Corsen

narrative: **Age 18 (664 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 19 (665 TR)** — A swarm of bees settled on his fence post. The beekeeper said it was good luck. He was not reassured.
**Age 20 (666 TR)** — He lost a ring — not valuable, but given to him by someone he no longer saw. He looked for it longer than he should have.
**Age 21 (667 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 22 (668 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected.
**Age 23 (669 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 24 (670 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 25 (671 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.

**Age 26 (672 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.

**Age 27 (673 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 28 (674 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying.

**Age 29 (675 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 30 (676 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 31 (677 TR)** — He noticed the first sign that winter was ending — a sound, a smell, a plant pushing through — and felt unreasonably glad.
**Age 32 (678 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (679 TR)** — He noticed the absence of a sound he had not known he was used to. The silence where it should have been was loud.
**Age 34 (680 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Antor Lorman died

**Age 36 (682 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 37 (683 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 38 (684 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 39 (685 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.

**Age 40 (686 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 41 (687 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 42 (688 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 43 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (690 TR)** — He saw two men fighting in the street. He watched to see if he should intervene. He did not intervene. He thought about this later.
**Age 45 (691 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

**Age 46 (692 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 47 (693 TR)** — He heard a song at a market and could not stop hearing it for three months. He did not know the words.

**Age 48 (694 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 49 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (696 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the chest or torso. The damage was permanent — stamina reduced by 2.
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 52 (698 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 53 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.
**Age 55 (701 TR)** — After long searching, he found deeper meaning in the rites of another god.

**Age 56 (702 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 57 (703 TR)** — He fell over in a public place in a way that was difficult to explain. He recovered quickly and moved on without making eye contact.
**Age 58 (704 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the shoulder. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1).
**Age 59 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 60 (706 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Halden Karkin
**Age 61 (707 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 62 (708 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 63 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 64 (710 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 65 (711 TR)** — He noticed, around the middle of the year, that things had been going well. He decided not to think about this too much.

**Age 66 (712 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 67 (713 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Leira Valkin
**Age 68 (714 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it.
**Age 69 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 70 (716 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Leira Valkin became a close friend
**Age 71 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 72 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 73 (719 TR)** — He reached out, or they did. The thing between them was old enough to look smaller than it had. Reconciled with Halden Karkin
**Age 74 (720 TR)** — He drew up his will and distributed what he could while he still could. It was a practical matter. He was surprised at how much it felt like something else.
**Age 55** *(faith change)* — Converted to new faith — re-roll public deity

narrative: **Age 18 (670 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 19 (671 TR)** — He got turned around on a road he had walked a hundred times. He was an hour off course before he admitted it.

**Age 20 (672 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 21 (673 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Mara Thatcher (artisan, age 21)
**Age 21 (673 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 22 (674 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 22 (674 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Gorin Kalman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Gorin Kalman born (male)
**Age 23 (675 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.

**Age 23 (675 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 24 (676 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.

**Age 24 (676 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — simple Aerin Kalman born (female, simple-minded)
**Age 24 (676 TR)** — Aerin Kalman died — the simple-minded child he had worried over for years. Aerin Kalman died (age 0)
**Age 25 (677 TR)** — Something got out — cattle, a goat, a pig — and had to be chased across a field he would rather not have crossed.
**Age 25 (677 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.

**Age 26 (678 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 26 (678 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 27 (679 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Vorin Walson
**Age 27 (679 TR)** — Gorin Kalman died young — a child still. Gorin Kalman died (age 5)
**Age 28 (680 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 28 (680 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 29 (681 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 29 (681 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Marta Kalman. week 40 Marta Kalman born (female)
**Age 30 (682 TR)** — He noticed, around the middle of the year, that things had been going well. He decided not to think about this too much.
**Age 30 (682 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 31 (683 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 31 (683 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Xarin Kalman born (male)
**Age 32 (684 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 32 (684 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 33 (685 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 33 (685 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 34 (686 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 34 (686 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.

**Age 35 (687 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 35 (687 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — Evan Kalman died young — a child still. Evan Kalman died (age 3)
**Age 37 (689 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 38 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 39 (691 TR)** — He arrived considerably earlier than expected. He waited outside for a long time rather than announce himself.
**Age 39 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (691 TR)** — Marta Kalman died young — a child still. Marta Kalman died (age 10)
**Age 40 (692 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; he took the risk.
**Age 40 (692 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Cade Kalman — survived. week 40 Cade Kalman born (male)
**Age 41 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 42 (694 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 43 (695 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.

**Age 43 (695 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 19
**Age 45 (697 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care.
**Age 46 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (699 TR)** — He was certain of something and wrong. He argued his position at length before the evidence became impossible to ignore.
**Age 47 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — His son Xarin Kalman married Zarath Erkin. Xarin Kalman married Zarath Erkin (peasant, age 16)
**Age 49 (701 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Vorin Walson
**Age 49 (701 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Xarin Kalman's child born (male, Xarin Kalman age 18)
**Age 50 (702 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 50 (702 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.

**Age 52 (704 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 52 (704 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 53 (705 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — Xarin Kalman left home to make his own way. Xarin Kalman left home (age 23)
**Age 55 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (708 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 56 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — Xarin Kalman's child Wulf Kalman died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Xarin Kalman's child died (age 8)
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — Xarin Kalman's child Jorin Kalman died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Xarin Kalman's child died (age 3)
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — Mara Thatcher died. They had been married 37 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Mara Thatcher died (approx. age 58)
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — Cade Kalman died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Cade Kalman died (age 19)
**Age 60 (712 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 61 (713 TR)** — He found a second wife — companionship more than alliance now. Married Berith Gonman (peasant, age 57)
**Age 61 (713 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 62 (714 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 62 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (715 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Beren Dalsen

**Age 64 (716 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 64 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 64 (716 TR)** — His son Soran Kalman married Ista Grinder. Soran Kalman married Ista Grinder (artisan, age 19)
**Age 65 (717 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 65 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 66 (718 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it.

**Age 67 (719 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant.
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — His back gave out at the worst possible time. He spent three days walking like an old man and pretending otherwise.
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — Soran Kalman left home to make his own way. Soran Kalman left home (age 27)

What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. name: Harlan Kalman

narrative: **Age 18 (676 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 19 (677 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 20 (678 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 21 (679 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 22 (680 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.

**Age 23 (681 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 24 (682 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Nara Korkin (unguilded, age 22)
**Age 24 (682 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 13

**Age 25 (683 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Wulf Horing — survived. week 40 Wulf Horing born (male)
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Wulf Horing died (age 0)
**Age 26 (684 TR)** — He said something that needed saying when everyone else was quiet. He would rather someone else had said it.

**Age 26 (684 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — simple Orla Horing born (female, simple-minded)
**Age 26 (684 TR)** — Orla Horing died — the simple-minded child he had worried over for years. Orla Horing died (age 0)

**Age 27 (685 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 27 (685 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Edris Horing. week 40 Edris Horing born (female)
**Age 28 (686 TR)** — He called an important person by the wrong name in front of everyone. He thought about it for months.
**Age 28 (686 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 8
**Age 29 (687 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.

**Age 29 (687 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.

**Age 30 (688 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 30 (688 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 31 (689 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 27

**Age 32 (690 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 33 (691 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 33 (691 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 34 (692 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 34 (692 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 37 (695 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 38 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 38 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Branna Horing came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Branna Horing born (female)

**Age 40 (698 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 9
**Age 41 (699 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 41 (699 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.

**Age 43 (701 TR)** — His wife bore twins — Selyn Horing and Lara Horing. The labour was long and hard; she was weakened for months. Both children lived. twins Selyn Horing and Lara Horing born (twins)
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — His daughter Edris Horing married Varen Alkin. Edris Horing married Varen Alkin (unguilded, age 17)
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the hip or groin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).

**Age 45 (703 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 46 (704 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 46 (704 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Edris Horing's child born (male, Edris Horing age 19)
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 14
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — Norath Horing died young — a child still. Norath Horing died (age 9)
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — Edris Horing left home to make his own way. Edris Horing left home (age 22)
**Age 50 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end. It changed how he thought about faith.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — Edris Horing's child Arwen Horing died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Edris Horing's child died (age 4)
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Selyn Horing fostered (age 10)
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — His daughter Edris Horing remarried. Edris Horing remarried Varen Dalman (unguilded, age 28)
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — Something got out — cattle, a goat, a pig — and had to be chased across a field he would rather not have crossed.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.

**Age 57 (715 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — He noticed the absence of a sound he had not known he was used to. The silence where it should have been was loud.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — A door he expected to open did not. He pulled, then pushed, then pulled again, then read the situation correctly. There were witnesses.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — His daughter Branna Horing married Kael Torkin. Branna Horing married Kael Torkin (peasant, age 26)
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — He saw light under a closed door late at night when no one should have been awake. He knocked. There was a reasonable explanation.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — Lara Horing left home to make his own way. Lara Horing left home (age 18)
**Age 62 (720 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 62 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (720 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Selyn Horing's illegitimate child born (female, Selyn Horing age 19)
**Age 18** *(class change)* — Arrived in town as urban poor [peasant → unguilded]
narrativeText: **Age 18 (676 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out. **Age 19 (677 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 20 (678 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 21 (679 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 22 (680 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting. **Age 23 (681 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms. **Age 24 (682 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Nara Korkin (unguilded, age 22) **Age 24 (682 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 13 **Age 25 (683 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood. **Age 25 (683 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Wulf Horing — survived. week 40 Wulf Horing born (male) **Age 25 (683 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Wulf Horing died (age 0) **Age 26 (684 TR)** — He said something that needed saying when everyone else was quiet. He would rather someone else had said it. **Age 26 (684 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — simple Orla Horing born (female, simple-minded) **Age 26 (684 TR)** — Orla Horing died — the simple-minded child he had worried over for years. Orla Horing died (age 0) **Age 27 (685 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing. **Age 27 (685 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Edris Horing. week 40 Edris Horing born (female) **Age 28 (686 TR)** — He called an important person by the wrong name in front of everyone. He thought about it for months. **Age 28 (686 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 8 **Age 29 (687 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 29 (687 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 30 (688 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 30 (688 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 31 (689 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move.
