name: Pedar Horing

narrative: **Age 18 (682 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 19 (683 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 20 (684 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.

**Age 21 (685 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 22 (686 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 23 (687 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it.
**Age 24 (688 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.

**Age 25 (689 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.

**Age 26 (690 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.

**Age 27 (691 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 28 (692 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 29 (693 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Idra Dalsen (peasant, age 23)
**Age 30 (694 TR)** — He discovered he had been misremembering a prayer he had said every day for years. He corrected it. It felt different.
**Age 30 (694 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 8
**Age 31 (695 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Noric Arsen died
**Age 31 (695 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 32 (696 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 32 (696 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Caris Garsen. week 40 Caris Garsen born (female)
**Age 33 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 33 (697 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 34 (698 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 34 (698 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Caris Garsen died (age 2)
**Age 35 (699 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 35 (699 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.

**Age 36 (700 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 36 (700 TR)** — Idra Dalsen died. They had been married 7 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Idra Dalsen died (approx. age 30)

**Age 37 (701 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 38 (702 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 39 (703 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Kavan Vorsen (peasant)
**Age 39 (703 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.

**Age 40 (704 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 40 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (705 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.

**Age 42 (706 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 43 (707 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 43 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (708 TR)** — He learned how to make something — a stew, a bread, a sauce — that others started asking for.
**Age 45 (709 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 46 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 47 (711 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 48 (712 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 49 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (714 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 51 (715 TR)** — A stranger showed him a technique he had been doing wrong for years. The stranger was not unkind about it.
**Age 52 (716 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care.
**Age 53 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (719 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 56 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (682 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean. **Age 19 (683 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 20 (684 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 21 (685 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 22 (686 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 23 (687 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it. **Age 24 (688 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 25 (689 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 26 (690 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected. **Age 27 (691 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record. **Age 28 (692 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 29 (693 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Idra Dalsen (peasant, age 23) **Age 30 (694 TR)** — He discovered he had been misremembering a prayer he had said every day for years. He corrected it. It felt different. **Age 30 (694 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 8 **Age 31 (695 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Noric Arsen died **Age 31 (695 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words. **Age 32 (696 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 32 (696 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Caris Garsen. week 40 Caris Garsen born (female) **Age 33 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 33 (697 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 34 (698 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 34 (698 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Caris Garsen died (age 2) **Age 35 (699 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected. **Age 35 (699 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 36 (700 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood. **Age 36 (700 TR)** — Idra Dalsen died. They had been married 7 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Idra Dalsen died (approx. age 30) **Age 37 (701 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was. **Age 38 (702 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that. **Age 39 (703 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Kavan Vorsen (peasant) **Age 39 (703 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 40 (704 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 40 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (705 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go. **Age 42 (706 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear. **Age 43 (707 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours. **Age 43 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 44 (708 TR)** — He learned how to make something — a stew, a bread, a sauce — that others started asking for. **Age 45 (709 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 46 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 47 (711 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill. **Age 48 (712 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 49 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 50 (714 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 51 (715 TR)** — A stranger showed him a technique he had been doing wrong for years. The stranger was not unkind about it. **Age 52 (716 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care. **Age 53 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 55 (719 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before. **Age 56 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
name: Levan Garsen

narrative: **Age 18 (688 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Yarath Valman (peasant, age 16)
**Age 18 (688 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Gareth Sorman born (male)
**Age 19 (689 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; he took the risk.
**Age 19 (689 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 20 (690 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 20 (690 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 21 (691 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 21 (691 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 22 (692 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 22 (692 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 23 (693 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 23 (693 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 20
**Age 24 (694 TR)** — He said something he intended as neutral and watched the other person's face change. He replayed the conversation for weeks.
**Age 24 (694 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Yarath Sorman. week 40 Yarath Sorman born (female)
**Age 25 (695 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone.
**Age 25 (695 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Aldis Sorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Aldis Sorman born (female)

**Age 26 (696 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 26 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 27 (697 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected.

**Age 27 (697 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — simple Brand Sorman born (male, simple-minded)

**Age 28 (698 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 29 (699 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 29 (699 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Varen Sorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Varen Sorman born (male)
**Age 30 (700 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care.
**Age 30 (700 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Marath Sorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Marath Sorman born (female)
**Age 30 (700 TR)** — Brand Sorman died — the simple-minded child he had worried over for years. Brand Sorman died (age 3)

**Age 31 (701 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.

**Age 31 (701 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.

**Age 32 (702 TR)** — Two years underground made him a freeman. The guild gave him a tin medallion as legal proof, which he wore for a decade before it was stolen and which he has spent twenty years wishing he still had. His former lord sent someone to contest the freedom in the first year. The mine moot ruled for him. He did not return to his home village. He has not been back in thirty years and thinks about it occasionally.
**Age 32 (702 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Rara Sorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Rara Sorman born (female)

**Age 33 (703 TR)** — He stood for the position of journeyman's representative on the guild council and received eleven votes. The man who won received seventeen. He spent some time considering what he had done differently from the man who won seventeen and concluded the answer was: less time being publicly angry about the way the guild allocates commissions. He has not changed his opinion about the commission allocation. He has changed how he expresses it. He is considering running again.
**Age 33 (703 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 33 (703 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Rara Sorman died (age 1)

**Age 34 (704 TR)** — The recipe required a specific root that does not grow within a two-day radius of the city. He spent three days travelling to a marsh he had been told about by a woman who sold dried plants at the market. The root was there. He harvested a year's supply and came back with mud to his knees and more knowledge about the marsh ecology than he had intended to acquire. The knowledge has turned out to be useful for other ingredients. He goes back once a year now.
**Age 34 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 35 (705 TR)** — The Laranian temple placed an order for twelve arming swords, standard knight specification, four-month delivery. He fulfilled it. The quality requirements were specific and the inspection rigorous — the order came with a temple armsman who tested each blade personally and returned two for reworking. He reworked them without complaint. The order paid well, was paid promptly, and resulted in a letter of commendation from the temple that has since generated three more commissions. He does not attend Laranian services. He attends to the quality of his work.
**Age 35 (705 TR)** — His son Gareth Sorman married Helda Fletcher. Gareth Sorman married Helda Fletcher (artisan, age 16)

**Age 36 (706 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 36 (706 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 37 (707 TR)** — He passed a milestone stone he had passed a hundred times and stopped for no reason he could explain. He stood there a while.
**Age 37 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (707 TR)** — His son Gareth Sorman remarried. Gareth Sorman remarried Elith Turner (artisan, age 27)
**Age 38 (708 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 38 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 39 (709 TR)** — His master had no sons, no daughters who wanted the trade, no one who had served as long or learned as much. The arrangements had taken six months: the guild transfer fee, the guild vote, the debts on the workshop — smaller than he feared. He took down the old sign and put up his own. He kept the master's workbench.

**Age 39 (709 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 39 (709 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Gareth Sorman's child born (female, Gareth Sorman age 21)

**Age 40 (710 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth.
**Age 40 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (710 TR)** — Gareth Sorman left home to make his own way. Gareth Sorman left home (age 22)
**Age 41 (711 TR)** — He received news that was good, weeks after it would have mattered. He was glad anyway.
**Age 41 (711 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 41 (711 TR)** — His daughter Yarath Sorman married Warin Miller. Yarath Sorman married Warin Miller (artisan, age 18)
**Age 41 (711 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Aldis Sorman's illegitimate child born (male, Aldis Sorman age 16)
**Age 42 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (712 TR)** — His daughter Aldis Sorman married Cade Walson. Aldis Sorman married Cade Walson (peasant, age 23)
**Age 43 (713 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 43 (713 TR)** — Aldis Sorman left home to make his own way. Aldis Sorman left home (age 18)
**Age 44 (714 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 44 (714 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 44 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (715 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 45 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 46 (716 TR)** — The face at the edge of the market. He is almost certain. The same way of moving — unhurried, too unhurried, watching without watching. He keeps his hand on his purse and decides not to decide anything yet.
**Age 46 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (716 TR)** — Aldis Sorman's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Aldis Sorman's face. Aldis Sorman's child died (age 0)
**Age 47 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (717 TR)** — His son Varen Sorman married Sirath Oring. Varen Sorman married Sirath Oring (peasant, age 16)
**Age 47 (717 TR)** — His daughter Marath Sorman married Cador Lansen. Marath Sorman married Cador Lansen (peasant, age 22)
**Age 48 (718 TR)** — He bought something he had no use for. He did not regret it. He kept it.

**Age 48 (718 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 48 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (720 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 50 (720 TR)** — Torin Sorman was nearly grown when he died. Torin Sorman died (age 13)
**Age 32** *(class change)* — Mine service earned legal freedom; entered guild as apprentice [peasant → artisan]

He is continuing. **Age 39 (709 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Gareth Sorman's child born (female, Gareth Sorman age 21) **Age 40 (710 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth. **Age 40 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 40 (710 TR)** — Gareth Sorman left home to make his own way. Gareth Sorman left home (age 22) **Age 41 (711 TR)** — He received news that was good, weeks after it would have mattered. He was glad anyway. **Age 41 (711 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 41 (711 TR)** — His daughter Yarath Sorman married Warin Miller. Yarath Sorman married Warin Miller (artisan, age 18) **Age 41 (711 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Aldis Sorman's illegitimate child born (male, Aldis Sorman age 16) **Age 42 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 42 (712 TR)** — His daughter Aldis Sorman married Cade Walson. Aldis Sorman married Cade Walson (peasant, age 23) **Age 43 (713 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 43 (713 TR)** — Aldis Sorman left home to make his own way. Aldis Sorman left home (age 18) **Age 44 (714 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 44 (714 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 44 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 45 (715 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 45 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 46 (716 TR)** — The face at the edge of the market. He is almost certain. The same way of moving — unhurried, too unhurried, watching without watching. He keeps his hand on his purse and decides not to decide anything yet. **Age 46 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (716 TR)** — Aldis Sorman's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Aldis Sorman's face. Aldis Sorman's child died (age 0) **Age 47 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (717 TR)** — His son Varen Sorman married Sirath Oring. Varen Sorman married Sirath Oring (peasant, age 16) **Age 47 (717 TR)** — His daughter Marath Sorman married Cador Lansen. Marath Sorman married Cador Lansen (peasant, age 22) **Age 48 (718 TR)** — He bought something he had no use for. He did not regret it. He kept it. **Age 48 (718 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 48 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 49 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 50 (720 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 50 (720 TR)** — Torin Sorman was nearly grown when he died. Torin Sorman died (age 13) **Age 32** *(class change)* — Mine service earned legal freedom; entered guild as apprentice [peasant → artisan]
name: Seric Sorman

narrative: **Age 18 (694 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 19 (695 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 20 (696 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.

**Age 21 (697 TR)** — Two years underground made him a freeman. The guild gave him a tin medallion as legal proof, which he wore for a decade before it was stolen and which he has spent twenty years wishing he still had. His former lord sent someone to contest the freedom in the first year. The mine moot ruled for him. He did not return to his home village. He has not been back in thirty years and thinks about it occasionally.

**Age 22 (698 TR)** — The master spent an hour showing him how to seat a join so it would hold under load. Not the simplified version he had been shown before, but the real method — why the angle mattered, what the grain was doing, what he should feel for in his fingers before he committed the piece. He has been trying to produce that feeling ever since. He gets it right about half the time. The master says this is acceptable for his stage. He intends it to be better than acceptable.
**Age 23 (699 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.

**Age 24 (700 TR)** — The recipe required a specific root that does not grow within a two-day radius of the city. He spent three days travelling to a marsh he had been told about by a woman who sold dried plants at the market. The root was there. He harvested a year's supply and came back with mud to his knees and more knowledge about the marsh ecology than he had intended to acquire. The knowledge has turned out to be useful for other ingredients. He goes back once a year now.

**Age 25 (701 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.

**Age 26 (702 TR)** — He stood for the position of journeyman's representative on the guild council and received eleven votes. The man who won received seventeen. He spent some time considering what he had done differently from the man who won seventeen and concluded the answer was: less time being publicly angry about the way the guild allocates commissions. He has not changed his opinion about the commission allocation. He has changed how he expresses it. He is considering running again.

**Age 27 (703 TR)** — The workshop has a formal structure — master, journeyman, apprentice — and a real structure that only becomes apparent over months of close attention. Who the master actually listens to. Which journeyman is building toward his own franchise and which has accepted that this is as far as he goes. Who sets the pace on quality, and who sets the pace on volume, and what happens when those two things conflict. He has been mapping it. He knows now which relationships to invest in and which to leave alone.
**Age 28 (704 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 29 (705 TR)** — He gave a traveller confident directions and realised immediately after that he had them backwards. He could not find the man again.

**Age 30 (706 TR)** — The Laranian temple placed an order for twelve arming swords, standard knight specification, four-month delivery. He fulfilled it. The quality requirements were specific and the inspection rigorous — the order came with a temple armsman who tested each blade personally and returned two for reworking. He reworked them without complaint. The order paid well, was paid promptly, and resulted in a letter of commendation from the temple that has since generated three more commissions. He does not attend Laranian services. He attends to the quality of his work.
**Age 31 (707 TR)** — He misjudged someone's state entirely. He approached in the wrong way at the wrong time and the conversation went badly.

**Age 32 (708 TR)** — The piece is done. He has done everything he knows how to do to it. Now it sits on the bench and he waits for the master to look at it and say something, which the master has not yet done because the master is finishing something else. This is a large fraction of an apprentice's life: doing the work, then waiting to find out what the work meant. He is getting better at the waiting. He uses the time to look at the piece from different angles and find the things the master will find before the master finds them.

**Age 33 (709 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth.

**Age 34 (710 TR)** — He left the workshop when it became clear that the master had nothing left to teach him and a strong interest in keeping him around anyway. This is a recognised pattern in the craft; the guild has formal procedures for it. The procedures are slow and favour the master. He used them correctly and left correctly, which means he has a clean record and a reference he can use, and he has had to be careful about what he says about why he left. The reason he left is on record in his own head, which is where it will stay.

**Age 35 (711 TR)** — Between workshops he took work on his own account for eight months — small commissions, household repairs, the kind of work that does not require guild oversight because it does not rise to the level that attracts guild oversight. The guild technically discourages this. He technically did not do it. He learned more in those eight months about the economics of the craft — what clients actually want, what they will pay, what the guild's pricing protects and what it suppresses — than in the preceding three years.
**Age 36 (712 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 37 (713 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Morak Rivergate (merchant)

**Age 38 (714 TR)** — The piece is not working. He knows what it should be. His hands know what it should be. The gap between what he knows and what he is producing is either a problem with his technique or a problem with the design, and he cannot determine which because both the technique and the design look correct to him on examination, and yet the piece is not right. He is going to put it down and do something else and come back to it in three days. He has learned that forcing the difficult piece produces the mediocre piece.
**Age 39 (715 TR)** — The goods spoiled, or the market had moved. He lost more than he could afford.
**Age 40 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 42 (718 TR)** — The delivery took him through the glassblowers' quarter and he watched for an hour before he realised he was late. What struck him was not the skill — he understood skilled work — but the specific kind of problem-solving: the way the blower was working against the material's tendency, not with it. His trade works with the material's tendency. The two approaches produced different relationships between the craftsman and the thing being made. He has been thinking about which produces better work.

**Age 43 (719 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 44 (720 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 21** *(class change)* — Mine service earned legal freedom; entered guild as apprentice [peasant → artisan]

name: Alric Corman

narrative: **Age 18 (700 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth.

**Age 19 (701 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 20 (702 TR)** — Something got out — cattle, a goat, a pig — and had to be chased across a field he would rather not have crossed.

**Age 21 (703 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 22 (704 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 23 (705 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 24 (706 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 25 (707 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Delyn Tarsen (peasant, age 22)
**Age 25 (707 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Koral Vorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Koral Vorman born (male)
**Age 26 (708 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 26 (708 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 22

**Age 27 (709 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.

**Age 28 (710 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 28 (710 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Noric Vorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Noric Vorman born (male)
**Age 29 (711 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.

**Age 30 (712 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 30 (712 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 31 (713 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 32 (714 TR)** — He found someone asleep in an unexpected place — a doorway, a yard, a storage room — and left them to it.
**Age 32 (714 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Emra Vorman. week 40 Emra Vorman born (female)
**Age 32 (714 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Emra Vorman died (age 0)
**Age 33 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 34 (716 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 34 (716 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 15
**Age 35 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 36 (718 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 37 (719 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 37 (719 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 37 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 38 (720 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 38 (720 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Garan Vorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Garan Vorman born (male)

Winter would be lean. **Age 32 (714 TR)** — He found someone asleep in an unexpected place — a doorway, a yard, a storage room — and left them to it. **Age 32 (714 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Emra Vorman. week 40 Emra Vorman born (female) **Age 32 (714 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Emra Vorman died (age 0) **Age 33 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 34 (716 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood. **Age 34 (716 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 15 **Age 35 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 35 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 36 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 36 (718 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 37 (719 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 37 (719 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 37 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 38 (720 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 38 (720 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Garan Vorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Garan Vorman born (male)
name: Xarin Vorman

narrative: **Age 18 (706 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 19 (707 TR)** — He gave someone a gift and discovered another person had given exactly the same thing. He recovered without too much awkwardness.

**Age 20 (708 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 21 (709 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 22 (710 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 23 (711 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 24 (712 TR)** — He found a small hidden space — behind a stone, under a floorboard — that someone had made long before him. There was something in it.
**Age 25 (713 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Xarin Valkin died
**Age 26 (714 TR)** — A stranger showed him a technique he had been doing wrong for years. The stranger was not unkind about it.

**Age 27 (715 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 28 (716 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Morath Soring (peasant, age 27)
**Age 28 (716 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 20
**Age 29 (717 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 29 (717 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 30 (718 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 30 (718 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Tavin Toring born (male)
**Age 31 (719 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 31 (719 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 32 (720 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 32 (720 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Yara Toring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Yara Toring born (female)
**Age 32 (720 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Yara Toring died (age 0)

name: Fardan Toring

narrative: **Age 18 (712 TR)** — He said exactly the right thing at the right moment. He would not have been able to do it again.
**Age 19 (713 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 20 (714 TR)** — He sat through a sermon he disagreed with and said nothing. He was not sure whether this was wisdom or cowardice.
**Age 21 (715 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Zarath Brewer (artisan, age 17)
**Age 21 (715 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 22 (716 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 22 (716 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Estra Ralman. week 40 Estra Ralman born (female)
**Age 23 (717 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 23 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 24 (718 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 24 (718 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Britta Ralman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Britta Ralman born (female)
**Age 25 (719 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 25 (719 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 12
**Age 26 (720 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 26 (720 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.

name: Cade Ralman

narrative: **Age 18 (718 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.

**Age 19 (719 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 20 (720 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 20 (720 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
name: Varen Laning

narrative: **Age 19 (667 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Marath Vorson (peasant, age 16)
**Age 19 (667 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 7

**Age 20 (668 TR)** — Two years underground made him a freeman. The guild gave him a tin medallion as legal proof, which he wore for a decade before it was stolen and which he has spent twenty years wishing he still had. His former lord sent someone to contest the freedom in the first year. The mine moot ruled for him. He did not return to his home village. He has not been back in thirty years and thinks about it occasionally.
**Age 20 (668 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Inda Horson — survived. week 40 Inda Horson born (female)
**Age 21 (669 TR)** — His apprentice had learned what could be taught. He packed his tools and left, and felt the workshop quieter for it.
**Age 21 (669 TR)** — His wife's labour was difficult. She was weakened for months, and the birth aged them both.

**Age 22 (670 TR)** — The recipe required a specific root that does not grow within a two-day radius of the city. He spent three days travelling to a marsh he had been told about by a woman who sold dried plants at the market. The root was there. He harvested a year's supply and came back with mud to his knees and more knowledge about the marsh ecology than he had intended to acquire. The knowledge has turned out to be useful for other ingredients. He goes back once a year now.
**Age 22 (670 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 23 (671 TR)** — He sat staring at the grain of a table or a wall and found a face or an animal in the pattern of the wood. He sat there longer than was useful.
**Age 23 (671 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.

**Age 24 (672 TR)** — The contract was for three years, renewable at mutual pleasure. The noble's steward set the terms and they were generous — room, board, a guaranteed wage at the bonded rate, and access to materials he could not have afforded himself. He had not held a franchise and did not miss it. The work was harder here, the expectations higher, and the silence of the private workshop was worth the constraint.
**Age 24 (672 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 25 (673 TR)** — He found a small hidden space — behind a stone, under a floorboard — that someone had made long before him. There was something in it.
**Age 25 (673 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 26 (674 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 27 (675 TR)** — The Laranian temple placed an order for twelve arming swords, standard knight specification, four-month delivery. He fulfilled it. The quality requirements were specific and the inspection rigorous — the order came with a temple armsman who tested each blade personally and returned two for reworking. He reworked them without complaint. The order paid well, was paid promptly, and resulted in a letter of commendation from the temple that has since generated three more commissions. He does not attend Laranian services. He attends to the quality of his work.

**Age 28 (676 TR)** — He woke to his wife's screaming. The cradle was empty. The window was open. There was no ransom demand — there never is, with this kind. He spent three years looking. Eventually he stopped looking, which was not the same as accepting what had happened.
**Age 28 (676 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 29 (677 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 29 (677 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.

**Age 30 (678 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 30 (678 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 14

**Age 31 (679 TR)** — His master had no sons, no daughters who wanted the trade, no one who had served as long or learned as much. The arrangements had taken six months: the guild transfer fee, the guild vote, the debts on the workshop — smaller than he feared. He took down the old sign and put up his own. He kept the master's workbench.
**Age 32 (680 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the knee. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 32 (680 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 33 (681 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 33 (681 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 34 (682 TR)** — He heard midnight bells from a temple he did not know, in a town he was passing through. The sound carried a long way.

**Age 34 (682 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 35 (683 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 35 (683 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Erith Horson born (female)
**Age 36 (684 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.
**Age 36 (684 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 36 (684 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Inda Horson's illegitimate child born (male, Inda Horson age 16)

**Age 37 (685 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 37 (685 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 38 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (687 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.

**Age 40 (688 TR)** — The piece is not working. He knows what it should be. His hands know what it should be. The gap between what he knows and what he is producing is either a problem with his technique or a problem with the design, and he cannot determine which because both the technique and the design look correct to him on examination, and yet the piece is not right. He is going to put it down and do something else and come back to it in three days. He has learned that forcing the difficult piece produces the mediocre piece.

**Age 41 (689 TR)** — The piece is done. He has done everything he knows how to do to it. Now it sits on the bench and he waits for the master to look at it and say something, which the master has not yet done because the master is finishing something else. This is a large fraction of an apprentice's life: doing the work, then waiting to find out what the work meant. He is getting better at the waiting. He uses the time to look at the piece from different angles and find the things the master will find before the master finds them.
**Age 41 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 42 (690 TR)** — The order's arms-master arrived with a specification that was, in his professional opinion, genuinely demanding. He accepted. The work took four months and required him to source steel he would not normally stock. When the commission was complete and inspected, the arms-master said it met the standard, which, from an arms-master of that order, was the highest compliment available. He raised his prices the following season.
**Age 42 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (690 TR)** — Inda Horson left home to make his own way. Inda Horson left home (age 22)
**Age 43 (691 TR)** — Fire gutted his shop. Years of tools and materials, gone.

**Age 43 (691 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.

**Age 44 (692 TR)** — He stood for the position of journeyman's representative on the guild council and received eleven votes. The man who won received seventeen. He spent some time considering what he had done differently from the man who won seventeen and concluded the answer was: less time being publicly angry about the way the guild allocates commissions. He has not changed his opinion about the commission allocation. He has changed how he expresses it. He is considering running again.
**Age 44 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (693 TR)** — She was gone. A note, or nothing — it did not matter much which. He was left with the children and whatever explanations he could give the neighbours.
**Age 45 (693 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Erith Horson apprenticed (age 10)

**Age 46 (694 TR)** — The guild hall on Soratir evening: half a dozen journeymen, the fire, the specific quality of conversation that happens between men who do the same work and know it well enough to argue about it productively. He came in irritated about something and left having resolved nothing about the irritation but having learned something useful about a finishing technique that he had been doing slightly wrong for two years. This is what the guild hall is actually for. The formal meetings are for the record. The evenings are for the craft.
**Age 46 (694 TR)** — Marath Vorson died. They had been married 27 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Marath Vorson died (approx. age 43)
**Age 47 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (696 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 49 (697 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 49 (697 TR)** — Erith Horson was nearly grown when she died. Erith Horson died (age 14)
**Age 50 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (699 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Edda Mason

**Age 52 (700 TR)** — The vote had been closer than flattering. He now sat on the board of syndics and was expected to attend the disputes, sign the examination results, and represent the chapter at the Mangai quarterly. He had wanted the recognition. He had not fully accounted for the meetings.
**Age 53 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (701 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 54 (702 TR)** — A stranger pulled him aside on the road and told him not to take the left fork. He took the right. Nothing happened.
**Age 54 (702 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 54 (702 TR)** — His daughter Inda Horson married Kuric Tanner. Inda Horson married Kuric Tanner (artisan, age 39)
**Age 55 (703 TR)** — He found someone who knew more than he did and paid in time and deference. Studied under Pedar Turner
**Age 55 (703 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 56 (704 TR)** — He fell over in a public place in a way that was difficult to explain. He recovered quickly and moved on without making eye contact.
**Age 57 (705 TR)** — He found a second wife — companionship more than alliance now. Married Dalla Carter (artisan, age 54)
**Age 57 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (706 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 20

**Age 59 (707 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Inda Horson's child born (female, Inda Horson age 39)
**Age 60 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (709 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 61 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (709 TR)** — His son Tovan Horson married Orla Thatcher. Tovan Horson married Orla Thatcher (artisan, age 16)
**Age 62 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (710 TR)** — Tovan Horson's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Tovan Horson's face. Tovan Horson's child died (age 0)
**Age 62 (710 TR)** — Tovan Horson left home to make his own way. Tovan Horson left home (age 20)
**Age 63 (711 TR)** — The goods spoiled, or the market had moved. He lost more than he could afford.
**Age 64 (712 TR)** — Someone in a village he had passed through once called him by name. He could not remember them at all.
**Age 64 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (713 TR)** — A boundary marker, a contested will, a neighbour's greed — the dispute dragged on.
**Age 65 (713 TR)** — His daughter Inda Horson remarried. Inda Horson remarried Iric Fuller (artisan, age 49)
**Age 67 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 67 (715 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 68 (716 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it.
**Age 69 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 69 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 69 (717 TR)** — Inda Horson's child Terath Horson died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Inda Horson's child died (age 10)
**Age 69 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 70 (718 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 71 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
