narrativeText: **Age 19 (667 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Marath Vorson (peasant, age 16) **Age 19 (667 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 7 **Age 20 (668 TR)** — Two years underground made him a freeman. The guild gave him a tin medallion as legal proof, which he wore for a decade before it was stolen and which he has spent twenty years wishing he still had. His former lord sent someone to contest the freedom in the first year. The mine moot ruled for him. He did not return to his home village. He has not been back in thirty years and thinks about it occasionally. **Age 20 (668 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Inda Horson — survived. week 40 Inda Horson born (female) **Age 21 (669 TR)** — His apprentice had learned what could be taught. He packed his tools and left, and felt the workshop quieter for it. **Age 21 (669 TR)** — His wife's labour was difficult. She was weakened for months, and the birth aged them both. **Age 22 (670 TR)** — The recipe required a specific root that does not grow within a two-day radius of the city. He spent three days travelling to a marsh he had been told about by a woman who sold dried plants at the market. The root was there. He harvested a year's supply and came back with mud to his knees and more knowledge about the marsh ecology than he had intended to acquire. The knowledge has turned out to be useful for other ingredients. He goes back once a year now. **Age 22 (670 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later. **Age 23 (671 TR)** — He sat staring at the grain of a table or a wall and found a face or an animal in the pattern of the wood. He sat there longer than was useful. **Age 23 (671 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 24 (672 TR)** — The contract was for three years, renewable at mutual pleasure. The noble's steward set the terms and they were generous — room, board, a guaranteed wage at the bonded rate, and access to materials he could not have afforded himself. He had not held a franchise and did not miss it. The work was harder here, the expectations higher, and the silence of the private workshop was worth the constraint. **Age 24 (672 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 25 (673 TR)** — He found a small hidden space — behind a stone, under a floorboard — that someone had made long before him. There was something in it. **Age 25 (673 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 26 (674 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 27 (675 TR)** — The Laranian temple placed an order for twelve arming swords, standard knight specification, four-month delivery. He fulfilled it. The quality requirements were specific and the inspection rigorous — the order came with a temple armsman who tested each blade personally and returned two for reworking. He reworked them without complaint. The order paid well, was paid promptly, and resulted in a letter of commendation from the temple that has since generated three more commissions. He does not attend Laranian services. He attends to the quality of his work. **Age 28 (676 TR)** — He woke to his wife's screaming. The cradle was empty. The window was open. There was no ransom demand — there never is, with this kind. He spent three years looking. Eventually he stopped looking, which was not the same as accepting what had happened. **Age 28 (676 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 29 (677 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 29 (677 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer. **Age 30 (678 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like. **Age 30 (678 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 14 **Age 31 (679 TR)** — His master had no sons, no daughters who wanted the trade, no one who had served as long or learned as much. The arrangements had taken six months: the guild transfer fee, the guild vote, the debts on the workshop — smaller than he feared. He took down the old sign and put up his own. He kept the master's workbench. **Age 32 (680 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the knee. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1). **Age 32 (680 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 33 (681 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 33 (681 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 34 (682 TR)** — He heard midnight bells from a temple he did not know, in a town he was passing through. The sound carried a long way. **Age 34 (682 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 35 (683 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected. **Age 35 (683 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Erith Horson born (female) **Age 36 (684 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go. **Age 36 (684 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 36 (684 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Inda Horson's illegitimate child born (male, Inda Horson age 16) **Age 37 (685 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 37 (685 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 38 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 39 (687 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name. **Age 40 (688 TR)** — The piece is not working. He knows what it should be. His hands know what it should be. The gap between what he knows and what he is producing is either a problem with his technique or a problem with the design, and he cannot determine which because both the technique and the design look correct to him on examination, and yet the piece is not right. He is going to put it down and do something else and come back to it in three days. He has learned that forcing the difficult piece produces the mediocre piece. **Age 41 (689 TR)** — The piece is done. He has done everything he knows how to do to it. Now it sits on the bench and he waits for the master to look at it and say something, which the master has not yet done because the master is finishing something else. This is a large fraction of an apprentice's life: doing the work, then waiting to find out what the work meant. He is getting better at the waiting. He uses the time to look at the piece from different angles and find the things the master will find before the master finds them. **Age 41 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 42 (690 TR)** — The order's arms-master arrived with a specification that was, in his professional opinion, genuinely demanding. He accepted. The work took four months and required him to source steel he would not normally stock. When the commission was complete and inspected, the arms-master said it met the standard, which, from an arms-master of that order, was the highest compliment available. He raised his prices the following season. **Age 42 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 42 (690 TR)** — Inda Horson left home to make his own way. Inda Horson left home (age 22) **Age 43 (691 TR)** — Fire gutted his shop. Years of tools and materials, gone. **Age 43 (691 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 44 (692 TR)** — He stood for the position of journeyman's representative on the guild council and received eleven votes. The man who won received seventeen. He spent some time considering what he had done differently from the man who won seventeen and concluded the answer was: less time being publicly angry about the way the guild allocates commissions. He has not changed his opinion about the commission allocation. He has changed how he expresses it. He is considering running again. **Age 44 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 45 (693 TR)** — She was gone. A note, or nothing — it did not matter much which. He was left with the children and whatever explanations he could give the neighbours. **Age 45 (693 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Erith Horson apprenticed (age 10) **Age 46 (694 TR)** — The guild hall on Soratir evening: half a dozen journeymen, the fire, the specific quality of conversation that happens between men who do the same work and know it well enough to argue about it productively. He came in irritated about something and left having resolved nothing about the irritation but having learned something useful about a finishing technique that he had been doing slightly wrong for two years. This is what the guild hall is actually for. The formal meetings are for the record. The evenings are for the craft. **Age 46 (694 TR)** — Marath Vorson died. They had been married 27 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Marath Vorson died (approx. age 43) **Age 47 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 48 (696 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 49 (697 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 49 (697 TR)** — Erith Horson was nearly grown when she died. Erith Horson died (age 14) **Age 50 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 51 (699 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Edda Mason **Age 52 (700 TR)** — The vote had been closer than flattering. He now sat on the board of syndics and was expected to attend the disputes, sign the examination results, and represent the chapter at the Mangai quarterly. He had wanted the recognition. He had not fully accounted for the meetings. **Age 53 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 53 (701 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 54 (702 TR)** — A stranger pulled him aside on the road and told him not to take the left fork. He took the right. Nothing happened. **Age 54 (702 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 54 (702 TR)** — His daughter Inda Horson married Kuric Tanner. Inda Horson married Kuric Tanner (artisan, age 39) **Age 55 (703 TR)** — He found someone who knew more than he did and paid in time and deference. Studied under Pedar Turner **Age 55 (703 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 56 (704 TR)** — He fell over in a public place in a way that was difficult to explain. He recovered quickly and moved on without making eye contact. **Age 57 (705 TR)** — He found a second wife — companionship more than alliance now. Married Dalla Carter (artisan, age 54) **Age 57 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (706 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 20 **Age 59 (707 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 59 (707 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered. **Age 59 (707 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Inda Horson's child born (female, Inda Horson age 39) **Age 60 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 61 (709 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 61 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 61 (709 TR)** — His son Tovan Horson married Orla Thatcher. Tovan Horson married Orla Thatcher (artisan, age 16) **Age 62 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 62 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 62 (710 TR)** — Tovan Horson's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Tovan Horson's face. Tovan Horson's child died (age 0) **Age 62 (710 TR)** — Tovan Horson left home to make his own way. Tovan Horson left home (age 20) **Age 63 (711 TR)** — The goods spoiled, or the market had moved. He lost more than he could afford. **Age 64 (712 TR)** — Someone in a village he had passed through once called him by name. He could not remember them at all. **Age 64 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 65 (713 TR)** — A boundary marker, a contested will, a neighbour's greed — the dispute dragged on. **Age 65 (713 TR)** — His daughter Inda Horson remarried. Inda Horson remarried Iric Fuller (artisan, age 49) **Age 67 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 67 (715 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 68 (716 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it. **Age 69 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 69 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 69 (717 TR)** — Inda Horson's child Terath Horson died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Inda Horson's child died (age 10) **Age 69 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 70 (718 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected. **Age 71 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 72 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 72 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 20** *(class change)* — Mine service earned legal freedom; entered guild as apprentice [peasant → artisan]
name: Gotar Horson

narrative: **Age 18 (672 TR)** — A door he expected to open did not. He pulled, then pushed, then pulled again, then read the situation correctly. There were witnesses.
**Age 19 (673 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.

**Age 20 (674 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 21 (675 TR)** — He noticed the absence of a sound he had not known he was used to. The silence where it should have been was loud.
**Age 22 (676 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 23 (677 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Helra Kalsen (unguilded, age 19)
**Age 23 (677 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 24 (678 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Borik Maring

**Age 24 (678 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 25 (679 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.

**Age 26 (680 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.

**Age 27 (681 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 28 (682 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.

**Age 28 (682 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Aldis Borsen born (female, lame)
**Age 29 (683 TR)** — He heard a song at a market and could not stop hearing it for three months. He did not know the words.
**Age 29 (683 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 30 (684 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 30 (684 TR)** — Helra Kalsen died. They had been married 7 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Helra Kalsen died (approx. age 26)
**Age 31 (685 TR)** — A fox sat at the edge of the field and watched him work. Neither moved for a long time.
**Age 31 (685 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on.
**Age 32 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (686 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 33 (687 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 34 (688 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Helda Dustmantle (merchant)
**Age 34 (688 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.

**Age 35 (689 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.

**Age 36 (690 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 36 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (691 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 37 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 38 (692 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 38 (692 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Aldis Borsen fostered (age 10)
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 40 (694 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 41 (695 TR)** — He touched something — rough stone, worn leather, woven cloth — and found himself somewhere else entirely.
**Age 41 (695 TR)** — His child — the one he had feared for — had made something of themselves. Not what he had once imagined, but real. He felt something loosen in his chest.

**Age 42 (696 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing.
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 43 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 44 (698 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 44 (698 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 45 (699 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Varen Gonson died
**Age 45 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 47 (701 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Helda Dustmantle became a close friend
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — He went to a place he had been many times and found the building was not there. Something else was.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (704 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 50 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — He noticed that an old ache started before rain. He became reliable at predicting the weather and this annoyed him.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.

**Age 54 (708 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — He said something that needed saying when everyone else was quiet. He would rather someone else had said it.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — He picked up a stone of unusual shape and carried it for several miles before setting it down. He did not know why he had picked it up.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 58 (712 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (714 TR)** — Something he expected to take an hour took most of the day. He was not angry about it. It was simply what it was.
**Age 60 (714 TR)** — His daughter Aldis Borsen married Warin Edman. Aldis Borsen married Warin Edman (unguilded, age 38)
**Age 60 (714 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Aldis Borsen's child born (male, Aldis Borsen age 32)
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the shoulder. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1).
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — He made something — a wooden figure, a knotted rope, a small carving — with no purpose in mind. He gave it away.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — Aldis Borsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Aldis Borsen's face. Aldis Borsen's child died (age 0)
**Age 64 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 64 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 65 (719 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it.
**Age 65 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 66 (720 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 66 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 19** *(class change)* — Arrived in town as urban poor [peasant → unguilded]
narrativeText: **Age 18 (672 TR)** — A door he expected to open did not. He pulled, then pushed, then pulled again, then read the situation correctly. There were witnesses. **Age 19 (673 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out. **Age 20 (674 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like. **Age 21 (675 TR)** — He noticed the absence of a sound he had not known he was used to. The silence where it should have been was loud. **Age 22 (676 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 23 (677 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Helra Kalsen (unguilded, age 19) **Age 23 (677 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 24 (678 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Borik Maring **Age 24 (678 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 25 (679 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold. **Age 26 (680 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately. **Age 27 (681 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 28 (682 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home. **Age 28 (682 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Aldis Borsen born (female, lame) **Age 29 (683 TR)** — He heard a song at a market and could not stop hearing it for three months. He did not know the words. **Age 29 (683 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 30 (684 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 30 (684 TR)** — Helra Kalsen died. They had been married 7 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Helra Kalsen died (approx. age 26) **Age 31 (685 TR)** — A fox sat at the edge of the field and watched him work. Neither moved for a long time. **Age 31 (685 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on. **Age 32 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 32 (686 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 33 (687 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it. **Age 34 (688 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Helda Dustmantle (merchant) **Age 34 (688 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 35 (689 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 36 (690 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation. **Age 36 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 37 (691 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that. **Age 37 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 38 (692 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency. **Age 38 (692 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Aldis Borsen fostered (age 10) **Age 39 (693 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 39 (693 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 40 (694 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues. **Age 41 (695 TR)** — He touched something — rough stone, worn leather, woven cloth — and found himself somewhere else entirely. **Age 41 (695 TR)** — His child — the one he had feared for — had made something of themselves. Not what he had once imagined, but real. He felt something loosen in his chest. **Age 42 (696 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing. **Age 42 (696 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 43 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 43 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 44 (698 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 44 (698 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 45 (699 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Varen Gonson died **Age 45 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (700 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 47 (701 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Helda Dustmantle became a close friend **Age 47 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (702 TR)** — He went to a place he had been many times and found the building was not there. Something else was. **Age 48 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 49 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 49 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 50 (704 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 50 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 51 (705 TR)** — He noticed that an old ache started before rain. He became reliable at predicting the weather and this annoyed him. **Age 51 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 53 (707 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 54 (708 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting. **Age 54 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 55 (709 TR)** — He said something that needed saying when everyone else was quiet. He would rather someone else had said it. **Age 55 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (710 TR)** — He picked up a stone of unusual shape and carried it for several miles before setting it down. He did not know why he had picked it up. **Age 56 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 58 (712 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was. **Age 59 (713 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 59 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 60 (714 TR)** — Something he expected to take an hour took most of the day. He was not angry about it. It was simply what it was. **Age 60 (714 TR)** — His daughter Aldis Borsen married Warin Edman. Aldis Borsen married Warin Edman (unguilded, age 38) **Age 60 (714 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Aldis Borsen's child born (male, Aldis Borsen age 32) **Age 61 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 62 (716 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the shoulder. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1). **Age 62 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 63 (717 TR)** — He made something — a wooden figure, a knotted rope, a small carving — with no purpose in mind. He gave it away. **Age 63 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 63 (717 TR)** — Aldis Borsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Aldis Borsen's face. Aldis Borsen's child died (age 0) **Age 64 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 64 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 65 (719 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it. **Age 65 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 66 (720 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 66 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 19** *(class change)* — Arrived in town as urban poor [peasant → unguilded]
name: Beren Borsen

narrative: **Age 18 (678 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 19 (679 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 20 (680 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 21 (681 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.

**Age 22 (682 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 23 (683 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 24 (684 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 25 (685 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Breda Joiner (artisan, age 21)
**Age 25 (685 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 26 (686 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 27 (687 TR)** — He completed a day's work and stood back and thought: yes, that is done properly.

**Age 27 (687 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Marta Erson born (female, lame)
**Age 27 (687 TR)** — Marta Erson died — the lame child he had worried over for years. Marta Erson died (age 0)
**Age 28 (688 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 28 (688 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Edric Erson came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Edric Erson born (male)
**Age 29 (689 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.
**Age 29 (689 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.

**Age 30 (690 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 30 (690 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 18
**Age 31 (691 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 31 (691 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 32 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 32 (692 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Roric Erson came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Roric Erson born (male)
**Age 33 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 34 (694 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 34 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (695 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 35 (695 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Zarath Erson born (female)
**Age 35 (695 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Zarath Erson died (age 0)
**Age 36 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 36 (696 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 37 (697 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone.
**Age 37 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 38 (698 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.

**Age 38 (698 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 39 (699 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Ruald Parson
**Age 39 (699 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 39 (699 TR)** — Roric Erson died young — a child still. Roric Erson died (age 7)
**Age 40 (700 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 40 (700 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 41 (701 TR)** — The young person he had invested time in had found their footing. He heard it from others before he saw it himself. He was glad, and slightly surprised at how glad. Ruald Parson found their own way
**Age 41 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (702 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 42 (702 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 43 (703 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Ruald Parson has died
**Age 43 (703 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Garith Erson — survived. week 40 Garith Erson born (female)

**Age 44 (704 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 45 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 45 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (706 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 46 (706 TR)** — Breda Joiner died. They had been married 21 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Breda Joiner died (approx. age 42)
**Age 46 (706 TR)** — His son Edric Erson married Elara Ralsen. Edric Erson married Elara Ralsen (peasant, age 17)
**Age 47 (707 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 47 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (708 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 48 (708 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 48 (708 TR)** — Edric Erson left home to make his own way. Edric Erson left home (age 20)
**Age 49 (709 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 49 (709 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 50 (710 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.
**Age 52 (712 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 52 (712 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 53 (713 TR)** — He was certain of something and wrong. He argued his position at length before the evidence became impossible to ignore.
**Age 54 (714 TR)** — He sneezed at exactly the wrong moment during something solemn. He managed to recover with reasonable dignity.
**Age 54 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 56 (716 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 57 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 57 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (718 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 58 (718 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 59 (719 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 59 (719 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 60 (720 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.

narrative: **Age 18 (684 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 19 (685 TR)** — Rats got into the stores. He dealt with it. It took three weeks longer than it should have.
**Age 20 (686 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 21 (687 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.

**Age 22 (688 TR)** — The Peonian sister arrived before he had finished sending for her and left after dawn with the kind of professional calm that made everything that had happened in between feel almost ordinary. He does not know her name. He knows the child is alive.
**Age 23 (689 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Carith Parsen (peasant, age 17)
**Age 23 (689 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Arith Dorkin born (female)
**Age 24 (690 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 24 (690 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 25 (691 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 25 (691 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 26 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 26 (692 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.

**Age 27 (693 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 27 (693 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 28 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 28 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 29 (695 TR)** — He heard something outside in the night, got up to investigate, and found nothing. He went back to bed and lay awake for an hour.
**Age 30 (696 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 31 (697 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 32 (698 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 32 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 33 (699 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 33 (699 TR)** — Carith Parsen died. They had been married 10 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Carith Parsen died (approx. age 27)
**Age 34 (700 TR)** — He received news that was good, weeks after it would have mattered. He was glad anyway.
**Age 34 (700 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 35 (701 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 36 (702 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 37 (703 TR)** — He ruined something he had spent a long time making. He did not speak for the rest of that day.
**Age 38 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 39 (705 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 39 (705 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 40 (706 TR)** — He hung the washing out. It rained within the hour. He took it in again. He did not mention this to anyone.
**Age 40 (706 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 41 (707 TR)** — He found his first grey hair. He pulled it out, then felt foolish, then stopped caring.
**Age 41 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (708 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 42 (708 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 43 (709 TR)** — He worked out how long ago something had happened and found the number was larger than he had assumed.
**Age 43 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 44 (710 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
**Age 44 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 45 (711 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 45 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 46 (712 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 46 (712 TR)** — His daughter Arith Dorkin married Zoric Dalman. Arith Dorkin married Zoric Dalman (peasant, age 28)
**Age 47 (713 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Idris Lansen
**Age 47 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 49 (715 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.

**Age 50 (716 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 50 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (717 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Dara Horkin (peasant, age 48)
**Age 51 (717 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 52 (718 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.

**Age 52 (718 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 52 (718 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Arith Dorkin's child born (male, Arith Dorkin age 29)
**Age 53 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (720 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 54 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52** *(class change)* — Arrived in town as urban poor [peasant → unguilded]

The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 28 (700 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 29 (701 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 30 (702 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 31 (703 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Breda Kaling (peasant, age 26)

**Age 37 (709 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 37 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (710 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end. It changed how he thought about faith.
**Age 38 (710 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Gareth Boring. week 40 Gareth Boring born (male)
**Age 39 (711 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 39 (711 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 40 (712 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 40 (712 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 40 (712 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Gareth Boring died (age 2)
**Age 41 (713 TR)** — He lost a ring — not valuable, but given to him by someone he no longer saw. He looked for it longer than he should have.
**Age 41 (713 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 42 (714 TR)** — A boundary marker, a contested will, a neighbour's greed — the dispute dragged on.
**Age 42 (714 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Norath Boring hired out (age 8)
**Age 43 (715 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 43 (715 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.

**Age 44 (716 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 44 (716 TR)** — The child came at term and did not live. He had thought the danger past. week 38
**Age 45 (717 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 45 (717 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 6
**Age 46 (718 TR)** — A bird built its nest in the eaves above his door. He left it alone all season.
**Age 46 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (719 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.

**Age 47 (719 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 48 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (690 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands. **Age 19 (691 TR)** — A neighbour's cat deposited a dead bird on his doorstep. He was not sure what to make of the gesture. **Age 20 (692 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life. **Age 21 (693 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 22 (694 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 23 (695 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it. **Age 24 (696 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 25 (697 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold. **Age 26 (698 TR)** — She would not stay behind. She packed what she could and followed the column. He was not sure whether to be relieved or terrified. **Age 27 (699 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 28 (700 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home. **Age 29 (701 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 30 (702 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean. **Age 31 (703 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Breda Kaling (peasant, age 26) **Age 31 (703 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 32 (704 TR)** — He told a joke that landed perfectly. He had no idea where he had heard it. He told it at every opportunity for the next three years. **Age 32 (704 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Tala Boring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Tala Boring born (female) **Age 33 (705 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his. **Age 33 (705 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 34 (706 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his. **Age 34 (706 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Norath Boring — survived. week 40 Norath Boring born (female) **Age 35 (707 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it. **Age 35 (707 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Calla Boring born (female) **Age 36 (708 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 36 (708 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 37 (709 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 37 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 38 (710 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end.
