narrative: **Age 18 (696 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 19 (697 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 20 (698 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Terath Rorsen (peasant, age 17)
**Age 20 (698 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Sirath Halsen born (female)

**Age 21 (699 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 21 (699 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 14
**Age 22 (700 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this.

**Age 23 (701 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 23 (701 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 24 (702 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 25 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 25 (703 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 26 (704 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 26 (704 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 27 (705 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected.
**Age 27 (705 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 28 (706 TR)** — He gave a traveller confident directions and realised immediately after that he had them backwards. He could not find the man again.
**Age 28 (706 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 29 (707 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 30 (708 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 30 (708 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 31 (709 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 31 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 32 (710 TR)** — He sneezed at exactly the wrong moment during something solemn. He managed to recover with reasonable dignity.
**Age 33 (711 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 20
**Age 34 (712 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.

**Age 35 (713 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 35 (713 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 36 (714 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 36 (714 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 36 (714 TR)** — His daughter Sirath Halsen married Rorik Korson. Sirath Halsen married Rorik Korson (peasant, age 19)
**Age 37 (715 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the forearm or hand. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in dexterity (−1).
**Age 38 (716 TR)** — A smell he encountered in passing — bread, woodsmoke, something sharper — put him back somewhere he had not thought of in years.
**Age 38 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (717 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 39 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 39 (717 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Sirath Halsen's child born (female, Sirath Halsen age 19)
**Age 40 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (718 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 40 (718 TR)** — Sirath Halsen left home to make his own way. Sirath Halsen left home (age 20)

**Age 41 (719 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 41 (719 TR)** — Terath Rorsen died. They had been married 21 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Terath Rorsen died (approx. age 38)
**Age 42 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 22 (706 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 22 (706 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.

**Age 23 (707 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. **Age 23 (707 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Edda Yoring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Edda Yoring born (female)
**Age 24 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 24 (708 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 24

**Age 25 (709 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 25 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 26 (710 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. **Age 26 (710 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 26 (710 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Ulric Yoring died (age 1)
**Age 27 (711 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone.
**Age 27 (711 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Malik Yoring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Malik Yoring born (male)
**Age 28 (712 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 28 (712 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 29 (713 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning.
**Age 29 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 30 (714 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 30 (714 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.

**Age 31 (715 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth.
**Age 31 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (716 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 33 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (717 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 34 (718 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 35 (719 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 35 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (720 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −1.
**Age 36 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (702 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that. **Age 19 (703 TR)** — He overheard a conversation he was not meant to hear. He pretended otherwise and spent the next year wondering what to do with it. **Age 20 (704 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected. **Age 21 (705 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Helra Smith (artisan, age 21) **Age 21 (705 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Edric Yoring born (male) **Age 22 (706 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 22 (706 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong. **Age 23 (707 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 23 (707 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Edda Yoring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Edda Yoring born (female) **Age 24 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 24 (708 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 24 **Age 25 (709 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms. **Age 25 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 26 (710 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 26 (710 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 26 (710 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Ulric Yoring died (age 1) **Age 27 (711 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone. **Age 27 (711 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Malik Yoring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Malik Yoring born (male) **Age 28 (712 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 28 (712 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 29 (713 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning. **Age 29 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 30 (714 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. The fear lasted two years. **Age 34 (718 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 35 (719 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. name: Corin Yoring

narrative: **Age 18 (708 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 19 (709 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 20 (710 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 21 (711 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 22 (712 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 23 (713 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.

**Age 24 (714 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 25 (715 TR)** — Something got out — cattle, a goat, a pig — and had to be chased across a field he would rather not have crossed.
**Age 26 (716 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.

**Age 27 (717 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 28 (718 TR)** — He attempted something he was not qualified for and did it well. He put this down to luck and moved on.
**Age 29 (719 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the lower leg or shin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
*(Ages 30–30: quiet years)*

narrative: **Age 18 (714 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 19 (715 TR)** — Through hard work and some luck, the last creditor was satisfied.

**Age 20 (716 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 21 (717 TR)** — He bought something he had no use for. He did not regret it. He kept it.
**Age 22 (718 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 23 (719 TR)** — He hung the washing out. It rained within the hour. He took it in again. He did not mention this to anyone.
**Age 24 (720 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Valith Lankin (peasant, age 23)
**Age 24 (720 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Zara Marsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Zara Marsen born (female)

narrative: **Age 18 (720 TR)** — He made a throw — of a stone, a knife, a horseshoe — that was perfect and that he was never able to repeat. There were witnesses.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (720 TR)** — He made a throw — of a stone, a knife, a horseshoe — that was perfect and that he was never able to repeat. There were witnesses.
name: Jorin Tarkin

narrative: **Age 18 (668 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 19 (669 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right.
**Age 20 (670 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Sera Ulsen (peasant, age 19)
**Age 20 (670 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Arven Voring born (male)
**Age 21 (671 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 21 (671 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 22 (672 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 23 (673 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 23 (673 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.

**Age 24 (674 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 24 (674 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 25 (675 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 25 (675 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 10
**Age 26 (676 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 26 (676 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 27 (677 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 28 (678 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 28 (678 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 29 (679 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 29 (679 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 30 (680 TR)** — He walked out early into thick mist. The world shrank to a few yards. He found he did not mind.
**Age 30 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (681 TR)** — He was locked out of his own house. He sat outside for an hour before resolving the situation.
**Age 31 (681 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (682 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 32 (682 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Ulan Voring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Ulan Voring born (male)
**Age 32 (682 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Ulan Voring died (age 0)
**Age 33 (683 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 33 (683 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Morin Voring — survived. week 40 Morin Voring born (male)

**Age 34 (684 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 34 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 34 (684 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 35 (685 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.

**Age 36 (686 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 36 (686 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 36 (686 TR)** — His son Arven Voring married Geira Branman. Arven Voring married Geira Branman (peasant, age 16)
**Age 36 (686 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Arven Voring's child born (female, Arven Voring age 16)
**Age 37 (687 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it.
**Age 37 (687 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 38 (688 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 39 (689 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in eyesight (−1).
**Age 39 (689 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 17
**Age 39 (689 TR)** — Arven Voring left home to make his own way. Arven Voring left home (age 19)

**Age 40 (690 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 41 (691 TR)** — He borrowed something and never returned it. He still has it. He is aware of this.

**Age 42 (692 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 43 (693 TR)** — He paused on a doorstep he had crossed a hundred times and could not immediately go in. He stood there for a moment and then continued.
**Age 43 (693 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 44 (694 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Beren Branson died
**Age 44 (694 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 45 (695 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 45 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (696 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 46 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (698 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 48 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (698 TR)** — Edris Voring was nearly grown when she died. Edris Voring died (age 14)
**Age 49 (699 TR)** — __colour__
**Age 49 (699 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 50 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 51 (701 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 51 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (703 TR)** — Arven Voring died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Arven Voring died (age 33)
**Age 54 (704 TR)** — A crow appeared every morning for a month on the same post. He began to look for it. One day it was gone.
**Age 54 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 55 (705 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 56 (706 TR)** — __colour__
**Age 56 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (707 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Cade Goodbarrel (merchant)
**Age 57 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (708 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Gara Ulson

**Age 58 (708 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 59 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (710 TR)** — Someone said something genuinely kind about his work. He deflected it awkwardly and thought about it for a week.
**Age 60 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 61 (711 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 61 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (712 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life.
**Age 62 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 63 (713 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing.
**Age 63 (713 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 64 (714 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 65 (715 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 66 (716 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 67 (717 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 67 (717 TR)** — Sera Ulsen died. They had been married 47 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Sera Ulsen died (approx. age 66)
**Age 68 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 69 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 70 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood. **Age 24 (674 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years. **Age 25 (675 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home. **Age 25 (675 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 10 **Age 26 (676 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 26 (676 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 27 (677 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 28 (678 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues. **Age 28 (678 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 29 (679 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 29 (679 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 30 (680 TR)** — He walked out early into thick mist. The world shrank to a few yards. He found he did not mind. **Age 30 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 31 (681 TR)** — He was locked out of his own house. He sat outside for an hour before resolving the situation. **Age 31 (681 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 32 (682 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 32 (682 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Ulan Voring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Ulan Voring born (male) **Age 32 (682 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Ulan Voring died (age 0) **Age 33 (683 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 33 (683 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Morin Voring — survived. week 40 Morin Voring born (male) **Age 34 (684 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 34 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 34 (684 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 35 (685 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 36 (686 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency. **Age 36 (686 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 36 (686 TR)** — His son Arven Voring married Geira Branman. Arven Voring married Geira Branman (peasant, age 16) **Age 36 (686 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Arven Voring's child born (female, Arven Voring age 16) **Age 37 (687 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it. **Age 37 (687 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer. **Age 38 (688 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 39 (689 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in eyesight (−1). **Age 39 (689 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 17 **Age 39 (689 TR)** — Arven Voring left home to make his own way. Arven Voring left home (age 19) **Age 40 (690 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 41 (691 TR)** — He borrowed something and never returned it. He still has it. He is aware of this. **Age 42 (692 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 43 (693 TR)** — He paused on a doorstep he had crossed a hundred times and could not immediately go in. He stood there for a moment and then continued. **Age 43 (693 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 44 (694 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Beren Branson died **Age 44 (694 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 45 (695 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 45 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (696 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name. **Age 46 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 47 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (698 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 48 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 48 (698 TR)** — Edris Voring was nearly grown when she died. Edris Voring died (age 14) **Age 49 (699 TR)** — __colour__ **Age 49 (699 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 50 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 51 (701 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 51 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 52 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 53 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 53 (703 TR)** — Arven Voring died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Arven Voring died (age 33) **Age 54 (704 TR)** — A crow appeared every morning for a month on the same post. He began to look for it. One day it was gone. **Age 54 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 55 (705 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation. **Age 56 (706 TR)** — __colour__ **Age 56 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 57 (707 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Cade Goodbarrel (merchant) **Age 57 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (708 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Gara Ulson **Age 58 (708 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 59 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 59 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 60 (710 TR)** — Someone said something genuinely kind about his work. He deflected it awkwardly and thought about it for a week. **Age 60 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 61 (711 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 61 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 62 (712 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life. **Age 62 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 63 (713 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing. **Age 63 (713 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 64 (714 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 65 (715 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 66 (716 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. name: Roric Voring

narrative: **Age 18 (674 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.

**Age 19 (675 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.

**Age 20 (676 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 21 (677 TR)** — He drank more than he intended at a gathering and said things he would not have said sober. He reviewed these the next day at length.
**Age 22 (678 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the shoulder that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 23 (679 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 24 (680 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.

**Age 25 (681 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.
**Age 26 (682 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 27 (683 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it.
**Age 28 (684 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Orith Turner (artisan, age 24)
**Age 28 (684 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.

**Age 29 (685 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 29 (685 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Kern Malkin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Kern Malkin born (male)

**Age 30 (686 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 30 (686 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Corin Malkin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Corin Malkin born (male)
**Age 31 (687 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 31 (687 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9
**Age 32 (688 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 32 (688 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 18
**Age 33 (689 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 33 (689 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 34 (690 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 34 (690 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 35 (691 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 35 (691 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Aerin Malkin. week 40 Aerin Malkin born (female)
**Age 36 (692 TR)** — He stayed up far later than he meant to, watching the fire go down, or the moon move, or nothing in particular.
**Age 36 (692 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 37 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (693 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 38 (694 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 38 (694 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 27
**Age 39 (695 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 39 (695 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 40 (696 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life.
**Age 40 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (697 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Rodric Arson died
**Age 41 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (698 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 42 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (699 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 43 (699 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 44 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 44 (700 TR)** — Orith Turner died. They had been married 16 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Orith Turner died (approx. age 40)
**Age 45 (701 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 45 (701 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 46 (702 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.

**Age 47 (703 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — Kern Malkin left home to make his own way. Kern Malkin left home (age 19)
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — His son Corin Malkin married Larith Carpenter. Corin Malkin married Larith Carpenter (artisan, age 18)
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Corin Malkin's child born (female, Corin Malkin age 20)
**Age 51 (707 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Lara Sorsen
**Age 51 (707 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Evan Malkin hired out (age 10)
**Age 52 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (709 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.

**Age 54 (710 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — He found a second wife — companionship more than alliance now. Married Welith Maring (peasant, age 53)
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — His daughter Aerin Malkin married Ulric Horson. Aerin Malkin married Ulric Horson (peasant, age 20)
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Theric Sorman
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — His son Kern Malkin married Elora Vorkin. Kern Malkin married Elora Vorkin (peasant, age 21)
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — Corin Malkin's child Carith Malkin died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Corin Malkin's child died (age 7)
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — A boundary marker, a contested will, a neighbour's greed — the dispute dragged on.
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — Aerin Malkin left home to make his own way. Aerin Malkin left home (age 23)
**Age 59 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 59 (715 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 59 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (716 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 60 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (717 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 62 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 63 (719 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 63 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 63 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 64 (720 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 64 (720 TR)** — Welith Maring died. They had been married 9 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Welith Maring died (approx. age 62)
**Age 64 (720 TR)** — His son Evan Malkin married Berith Marman. Evan Malkin married Berith Marman (peasant, age 18)
