Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong. **Age 29 (685 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms. **Age 29 (685 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Kern Malkin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Kern Malkin born (male) **Age 30 (686 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 30 (686 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Corin Malkin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Corin Malkin born (male) **Age 31 (687 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean. **Age 31 (687 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9 **Age 32 (688 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 32 (688 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 18 **Age 33 (689 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it. **Age 33 (689 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 34 (690 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 34 (690 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 35 (691 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold. **Age 35 (691 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Aerin Malkin. week 40 Aerin Malkin born (female) **Age 36 (692 TR)** — He stayed up far later than he meant to, watching the fire go down, or the moon move, or nothing in particular. **Age 36 (692 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 37 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 37 (693 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 38 (694 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home. **Age 38 (694 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 27 **Age 39 (695 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks. **Age 39 (695 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later. **Age 40 (696 TR)** — He saw a view — a valley at dawn, a river in flood, a hillside in autumn — that stayed with him for the rest of his life. **Age 40 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 41 (697 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Rodric Arson died **Age 41 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 42 (698 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 42 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 43 (699 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 43 (699 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer. **Age 44 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 44 (700 TR)** — Orith Turner died. They had been married 16 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Orith Turner died (approx. age 40) **Age 45 (701 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 45 (701 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 46 (702 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name. **Age 47 (703 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 48 (704 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 48 (704 TR)** — Kern Malkin left home to make his own way. Kern Malkin left home (age 19) **Age 49 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 49 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 49 (705 TR)** — His son Corin Malkin married Larith Carpenter. Corin Malkin married Larith Carpenter (artisan, age 18) **Age 50 (706 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 50 (706 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Corin Malkin's child born (female, Corin Malkin age 20) **Age 51 (707 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Lara Sorsen **Age 51 (707 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Evan Malkin hired out (age 10) **Age 52 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 52 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 53 (709 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 54 (710 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 55 (711 TR)** — He found a second wife — companionship more than alliance now. Married Welith Maring (peasant, age 53) **Age 55 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 55 (711 TR)** — His daughter Aerin Malkin married Ulric Horson. Aerin Malkin married Ulric Horson (peasant, age 20) **Age 56 (712 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Theric Sorman **Age 56 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (713 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 57 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (713 TR)** — His son Kern Malkin married Elora Vorkin. Kern Malkin married Elora Vorkin (peasant, age 21) **Age 57 (713 TR)** — Corin Malkin's child Carith Malkin died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Corin Malkin's child died (age 7) **Age 58 (714 TR)** — A boundary marker, a contested will, a neighbour's greed — the dispute dragged on. **Age 58 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 58 (714 TR)** — Aerin Malkin left home to make his own way. Aerin Malkin left home (age 23) **Age 59 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 59 (715 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 59 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 60 (716 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 60 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 61 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 61 (717 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 62 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 63 (719 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop. **Age 63 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 63 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 64 (720 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook. **Age 64 (720 TR)** — Welith Maring died. They had been married 9 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Welith Maring died (approx. age 62) **Age 64 (720 TR)** — His son Evan Malkin married Berith Marman. Evan Malkin married Berith Marman (peasant, age 18)
name: Halden Malkin

narrative: **Age 18 (680 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 19 (681 TR)** — A neighbour's cat deposited a dead bird on his doorstep. He was not sure what to make of the gesture.
**Age 20 (682 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.

**Age 21 (683 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 22 (684 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.

**Age 23 (685 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 24 (686 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 25 (687 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 26 (688 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 27 (689 TR)** — A stranger showed him a technique he had been doing wrong for years. The stranger was not unkind about it.
**Age 28 (690 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 29 (691 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 30 (692 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.

**Age 31 (693 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 32 (694 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Selda Korsen (peasant, age 29)
**Age 32 (694 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 17

**Age 33 (695 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 33 (695 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 34 (696 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 34 (696 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Aldric Dalsen born (male)

**Age 35 (697 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.

**Age 35 (697 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 36 (698 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 36 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (699 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.

**Age 38 (700 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.

**Age 39 (701 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
**Age 40 (702 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 40 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 41 (703 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 42 (704 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 42 (704 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 43 (705 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.

**Age 43 (705 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 45 (707 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 45 (707 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Xarin Coring
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Aldric Dalsen hired out (age 12)
**Age 47 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (709 TR)** — Selda Korsen died. They had been married 15 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Selda Korsen died (approx. age 44)
**Age 48 (710 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the abdomen. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
**Age 48 (710 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 50 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (712 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.

**Age 51 (713 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — His son Aldric Dalsen married Ida Elman. Aldric Dalsen married Ida Elman (peasant, age 16)
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Aldric Dalsen's child born (male, Aldric Dalsen age 20)
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 56 (718 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — His daughter Nara Dalsen married Ruald Boring. Nara Dalsen married Ruald Boring (peasant, age 24)

A serious wound to the abdomen. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1). **Age 48 (710 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 49 (711 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 50 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 50 (712 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 51 (713 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record. **Age 51 (713 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 52 (714 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 53 (715 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 53 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 54 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 54 (716 TR)** — His son Aldric Dalsen married Ida Elman. Aldric Dalsen married Ida Elman (peasant, age 16) **Age 54 (716 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Aldric Dalsen's child born (male, Aldric Dalsen age 20) **Age 55 (717 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 55 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (719 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 57 (719 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 58 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (720 TR)** — His daughter Nara Dalsen married Ruald Boring. Nara Dalsen married Ruald Boring (peasant, age 24)
name: Orlan Dalsen

narrative: **Age 18 (686 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.

**Age 19 (687 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 20 (688 TR)** — He kept waiting for the right moment to say something and the moment passed. The thing went unsaid.
**Age 21 (689 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 22 (690 TR)** — He lay awake one night with nothing in particular on his mind. He got up before dawn and walked until it was light.
**Age 23 (691 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Mira Garson (peasant, age 23)
**Age 23 (691 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 10
**Age 24 (692 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 25 (693 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 25 (693 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 26 (694 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Malik Lorman died
**Age 26 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 27 (695 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 28 (696 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone.
**Age 28 (696 TR)** — Mira Garson died. They had been married 5 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Mira Garson died (approx. age 28)
**Age 29 (697 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

**Age 30 (698 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 31 (699 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 31 (699 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 32 (700 TR)** — He was clearly overcharged and paid without arguing. He walked away thinking about the dozen things he could have said.
**Age 33 (701 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Ista Sorson (peasant, age 27)
**Age 33 (701 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 13
**Age 34 (702 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 35 (703 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 36 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 36 (704 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 37 (705 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 37 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (706 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Mora Vorman
**Age 39 (707 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 39 (707 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 21
**Age 40 (708 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 40 (708 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Halden Salkin. week 40 Halden Salkin born (male)

**Age 41 (709 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 42 (710 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 42 (710 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 43 (711 TR)** — A boundary marker, a contested will, a neighbour's greed — the dispute dragged on.
**Age 43 (711 TR)** — Ista Sorson died. They had been married 10 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Ista Sorson died (approx. age 37)
**Age 44 (712 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 44 (712 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 45 (713 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 46 (714 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 46 (714 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 47 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (715 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 48 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (718 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 51 (719 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 52 (720 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 52 (720 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Halden Salkin hired out (age 12)

name: Horic Salkin

narrative: **Age 19 (716 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped.

**Age 20 (717 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (718 TR)** — She bore a child. Vala Carter came into the world healthy. week 40 Vala Carter born (female)
**Age 22 (719 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Rhoric Butcher (artisan, age 26)
**Age 22 (719 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.

**Age 23 (720 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.

She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
name: Mira Carter

narrative: **Age 18 (663 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.

**Age 19 (664 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.

**Age 20 (665 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (666 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 22 (667 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 23 (668 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 24 (669 TR)** — She repaired something that belonged to her neighbour while they were away. No one ever mentioned it.
**Age 25 (670 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 26 (671 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Halric Sorsen (peasant, age 26)

**Age 26 (671 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Estra Sorsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Estra Sorsen born (female)

**Age 27 (672 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
**Age 27 (672 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 28 (673 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful.
**Age 28 (673 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.

**Age 29 (674 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 29 (674 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 30 (675 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.

**Age 31 (676 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
**Age 32 (677 TR)** — She was caught in a downpour with nowhere to go. She stood in it until it passed and felt, obscurely, better for it.
**Age 32 (677 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 12

**Age 33 (678 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 34 (679 TR)** — She repaired something — a step, a latch, a cracked fitting — that people had walked past broken for so long they had stopped seeing it.
**Age 34 (679 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 34 (679 TR)** — She lost Estra Sorsen — still a child, not yet grown. Estra Sorsen died (age 8)
**Age 35 (680 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 35 (680 TR)** — She bore a child. Tala Sorsen came into the world healthy. week 40 Tala Sorsen born (female)

**Age 36 (681 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 36 (681 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Tala Sorsen died (age 1)
**Age 37 (682 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 37 (682 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Orlan Sorsen — lived. week 40 Orlan Sorsen born (male)

**Age 38 (683 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.

**Age 39 (684 TR)** — The guild jury turned down the masterwork. She has spent three weeks looking at it from every angle, asking herself whether the jury was right. Her honest assessment: the craftsmanship was not at fault. The design was unconventional and the jury found it outside the expected form. She is deciding whether to submit a conventional masterwork to achieve the master grade and do the unconventional work afterward, or to submit the unconventional work again and argue for it, which will take longer and may not succeed. She has not decided.

**Age 39 (684 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Noric Sorsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Noric Sorsen born (male)

**Age 40 (685 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.
**Age 40 (685 TR)** — She bore a child. Ranulf Sorsen came into the world healthy. week 40 Ranulf Sorsen born (male)

**Age 41 (686 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 41 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 42 (687 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.
**Age 43 (688 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 44 (689 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 44 (689 TR)** — Halric Sorsen died after 18 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Halric Sorsen died (approx. age 44)
**Age 45 (690 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.

**Age 46 (691 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 47 (692 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 47 (692 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.

**Age 48 (693 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 49 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 51 (696 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
**Age 51 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (697 TR)** — She changed her stated position openly. Several people found this more unsettling than if she had been consistently wrong.
**Age 52 (697 TR)** — Orlan Sorsen had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Orlan Sorsen died (age 15)
**Age 53 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (700 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 55 (700 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 56 (701 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 56 (701 TR)** — Her son Noric Sorsen married Sarath Grinder. Noric Sorsen married Sarath Grinder (artisan, age 16)
**Age 57 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (703 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 58 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (703 TR)** — Her son Ranulf Sorsen married Rara Butcher. Ranulf Sorsen married Rara Butcher (artisan, age 16)
**Age 59 (704 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 59 (704 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 59 (704 TR)** — Noric Sorsen left to make her own way. Noric Sorsen left home (age 20)

**Age 60 (705 TR)** — She was not carrying a chivalric weapon. She was carrying her employer's letter in a sealed case, and the seal looked like a heraldic device to a guard who did not know what a heraldic device actually looked like, and the guard decided that the case itself constituted bearing arms without right and detained her for half a day. The alderman who resolved it apologised. The guard was reprimanded. She filed a complaint with the guild that employed the guard service. She received a written acknowledgment. She carries the employer's letter unsealed now, which the employer finds aesthetically displeasing and which she considers a reasonable trade.
**Age 61 (706 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 62 (707 TR)** — Ranulf Sorsen left to make her own way. Ranulf Sorsen left home (age 22)

**Age 63 (708 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.

**Age 64 (709 TR)** — She had managed the household, the accounts, the children's marriages, and the succession of a substantial property for four decades, and she stopped. What she found on the other side of it was time. She had forgotten what time felt like when it belonged to her. She is still learning.
**Age 64 (709 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Noric Sorsen's child born (male, Noric Sorsen age 25)
**Age 65 (710 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 66 (711 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 67 (712 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.
**Age 68 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 68 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 69 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 69 (714 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 69 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 70 (715 TR)** — She forgot an observance she had kept every year of her adult life. She was not sure what that meant.
**Age 70 (715 TR)** — Noric Sorsen lost Corin Sorsen. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Noric Sorsen's child died (age 6)
**Age 71 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 71 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 72 (717 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient.
**Age 72 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 73 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 73 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 73 (718 TR)** — Ranulf Sorsen lost Fenric Sorsen. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Ranulf Sorsen's child died (age 4)
**Age 74 (719 TR)** — Her apprentice was ready. She sent them out with the tools they had earned. She had taught well. She was not sure she had taught everything.
**Age 74 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 75 (720 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (663 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands. **Age 19 (664 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays. **Age 20 (665 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (666 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them. **Age 22 (667 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 23 (668 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 24 (669 TR)** — She repaired something that belonged to her neighbour while they were away. No one ever mentioned it. **Age 25 (670 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 26 (671 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Halric Sorsen (peasant, age 26) **Age 26 (671 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Estra Sorsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Estra Sorsen born (female) **Age 27 (672 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council. **Age 27 (672 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 28 (673 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful. **Age 28 (673 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically. **Age 29 (674 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone. **Age 29 (674 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular. **Age 30 (675 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 31 (676 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do. **Age 32 (677 TR)** — She was caught in a downpour with nowhere to go. She stood in it until it passed and felt, obscurely, better for it. **Age 32 (677 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 12 **Age 33 (678 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary. **Age 34 (679 TR)** — She repaired something — a step, a latch, a cracked fitting — that people had walked past broken for so long they had stopped seeing it. **Age 34 (679 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 34 (679 TR)** — She lost Estra Sorsen — still a child, not yet grown. Estra Sorsen died (age 8) **Age 35 (680 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 35 (680 TR)** — She bore a child. Tala Sorsen came into the world healthy. week 40 Tala Sorsen born (female) **Age 36 (681 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain.
