name: Tala Slater

narrative: **Age 18 (669 TR)** — She bought something impractical and unnecessary. She kept it without apology.

**Age 19 (670 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 20 (671 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (672 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.

**Age 22 (673 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
**Age 23 (674 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 24 (675 TR)** — A secret entrusted to her found its way to those who would pay for it.

**Age 25 (676 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 26 (677 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 27 (678 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
**Age 28 (679 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 29 (680 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 30 (681 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Lucan Fartrader

**Age 31 (682 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.

**Age 32 (683 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 33 (684 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Borik Weaver (artisan, age 38)
**Age 33 (684 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 34 (685 TR)** — Her apprentice was ready. She sent them out with the tools they had earned. She had taught well. She was not sure she had taught everything.
**Age 34 (685 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Dara Weaver — lived. week 40 Dara Weaver born (female)
**Age 35 (686 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
**Age 35 (686 TR)** — She bore a child. Sera Weaver came into the world healthy. week 40 Sera Weaver born (female)

**Age 36 (687 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.

**Age 37 (688 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 37 (688 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 23
**Age 38 (689 TR)** — She attempted something she expected to fail at and did it adequately on the first try.
**Age 38 (689 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 39 (690 TR)** — She brought the same gift as someone else. The recipient was gracious. She thought about it longer than the situation warranted.
**Age 39 (690 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 40 (691 TR)** — She had trusted this person. That trust had been a tool someone else picked up and used. Betrayed by Lucan Fartrader
**Age 41 (692 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 41 (692 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.

**Age 42 (693 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 42 (693 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.

**Age 43 (694 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
**Age 43 (694 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.

**Age 44 (695 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
**Age 44 (695 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 6
**Age 45 (696 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 45 (696 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 46 (697 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Lucan Fartrader
**Age 46 (697 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.

**Age 47 (698 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 47 (698 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.
**Age 48 (699 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 48 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (700 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 49 (700 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.
**Age 50 (701 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.
**Age 50 (701 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 50 (701 TR)** — Her daughter Dara Weaver married Garan Millward. Dara Weaver married Garan Millward (artisan, age 17)
**Age 51 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (702 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Dara Weaver's child born (female, Dara Weaver age 17)

**Age 52 (703 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Lucan Fartrader became a close friend
**Age 52 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 53 (704 TR)** — Her reputation had found its way to someone with the means to act on it. The commission was the largest she had ever accepted. She took it. Commissioned by Harlan Lalekar (noble)
**Age 53 (704 TR)** — Her daughter Sera Weaver married Aldric Turner. Sera Weaver married Aldric Turner (artisan, age 18)

**Age 54 (705 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.

**Age 54 (705 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 54 (705 TR)** — Her daughter Dara Weaver remarried. Dara Weaver remarried Borik Hayward (artisan, age 26)
**Age 54 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (707 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands.
**Age 56 (707 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.

**Age 57 (708 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 57 (708 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.
**Age 57 (708 TR)** — Dara Weaver left to make her own way. Dara Weaver left home (age 23)
**Age 57 (708 TR)** — Sera Weaver left to make her own way. Sera Weaver left home (age 22)
**Age 58 (709 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 59 (710 TR)** — She could not sleep. She lay still and listened to the night and tried to work out what was wrong. Nothing was.
**Age 59 (710 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 60 (711 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 60 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (711 TR)** — Sera Weaver died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Sera Weaver died (age 25)
**Age 61 (712 TR)** — The needle, the knife, the spinning rod — whatever she relied on most — broke the day she needed it.
**Age 61 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (713 TR)** — She delivered an apology she had been composing for years. The other person was surprised and then moved.
**Age 62 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 62 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (714 TR)** — She became the person others consulted. This had been happening for years; it was only recently that she recognised it as a pattern.
**Age 63 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 64 (715 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Lucan Fartrader
**Age 64 (715 TR)** — Borik Weaver died after 31 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Borik Weaver died (approx. age 69)
**Age 65 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 66 (717 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 66 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 67 (718 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 67 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 68 (719 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 69 (720 TR)** — She saw a mark on someone she knew and understood that there was a history there she would not be invited into.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (669 TR)** — She bought something impractical and unnecessary. She kept it without apology. **Age 19 (670 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct. **Age 20 (671 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (672 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone. **Age 22 (673 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice. **Age 23 (674 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 24 (675 TR)** — A secret entrusted to her found its way to those who would pay for it. **Age 25 (676 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays. **Age 26 (677 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 27 (678 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger. **Age 28 (679 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 29 (680 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 30 (681 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. name: Edda Sawyer

narrative: **Age 18 (675 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Horic Soring (peasant, age 21)
**Age 19 (676 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient.

**Age 19 (676 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Seric Soring. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Seric Soring born (male)

**Age 20 (677 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (678 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 21 (678 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 11
**Age 21 (678 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Seric Soring died (age 2)

**Age 22 (679 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.

**Age 22 (679 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 23 (680 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 23 (680 TR)** — Horic Soring died after 5 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Horic Soring died (approx. age 26)

**Age 24 (681 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 25 (682 TR)** — She saw a situation developing that she might have done something about. She walked on. The question of whether she was right to has not fully settled.
**Age 26 (683 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 28 (685 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 29 (686 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Torin Turner (artisan, age 34)
**Age 29 (686 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 8

**Age 30 (687 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 30 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 31 (688 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.
**Age 31 (688 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 32 (689 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality.
**Age 32 (689 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 33 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (690 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 34 (691 TR)** — She found a coin in an unexpected place — old, worn, and minted somewhere far away.
**Age 34 (691 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 20

**Age 35 (692 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
**Age 35 (692 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Joran Turner lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Joran Turner born (male)
**Age 36 (693 TR)** — She said something quietly to one person and realised from the silence around her that it had carried.

**Age 36 (693 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 37 (694 TR)** — She repaired something repeatedly that had been repaired too many times already. She was not sure why she did not let it go.

**Age 37 (694 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Jorin Turner. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Jorin Turner born (male)

**Age 38 (695 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
**Age 38 (695 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 19
**Age 39 (696 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 40 (697 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 40 (697 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.

**Age 41 (698 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 42 (699 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient.
**Age 42 (699 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.

**Age 43 (700 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.
**Age 43 (700 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 44 (701 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 45 (702 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
**Age 45 (702 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 46 (703 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (704 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
**Age 48 (705 TR)** — A snake crossed her path and the old women of the village argued for a week about what it meant.
**Age 48 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (706 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.

**Age 50 (707 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 50 (707 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 51 (708 TR)** — She heard something that would have been wonderful at the time but reached her so late that it was only quietly pleasant.
**Age 51 (708 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Jorin Turner apprenticed (age 14)
**Age 51 (708 TR)** — Her son Joran Turner married Disa Millward. Joran Turner married Disa Millward (artisan, age 16)
**Age 52 (709 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 53 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (711 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.
**Age 54 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (711 TR)** — Joran Turner left to make her own way. Joran Turner left home (age 19)
**Age 54 (711 TR)** — Her son Jorin Turner married Dalla Miller. Jorin Turner married Dalla Miller (artisan, age 16)
**Age 55 (712 TR)** — She stayed up until the candle was gone. She was not sure what she had been doing for the last hour.
**Age 56 (713 TR)** — Her reputation had found its way to someone with the means to act on it. The commission was the largest she had ever accepted. She took it. Commissioned by Sira (clergy)
**Age 56 (713 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Joran Turner's child born (female, Joran Turner age 21)
**Age 56 (713 TR)** — Joran Turner's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Joran Turner's child died (age 0)
**Age 57 (714 TR)** — She changed her stated position openly. Several people found this more unsettling than if she had been consistently wrong.
**Age 57 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (715 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 58 (715 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 58 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (716 TR)** — Her quality of work was recognised within the guild.
**Age 59 (716 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 60 (717 TR)** — Teaching a younger hand renewed her sense of the craft's demands. Took on Tavin Turner as apprentice
**Age 60 (717 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 61 (718 TR)** — The morning was so misty that she could see only a few steps ahead. She walked slowly and arrived late and was glad of both.
**Age 61 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (719 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
**Age 62 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 62 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 63 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

name: Farath Butcher

narrative: **Age 18 (681 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Dalric Harper

**Age 19 (682 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.

**Age 20 (683 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (684 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 22 (685 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Osric
**Age 23 (686 TR)** — She dreamed something that she decided not to speak of. She has never spoken of it.

**Age 24 (687 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 25 (688 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 26 (689 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 27 (690 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.
**Age 28 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 29 (692 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 30 (693 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.

**Age 31 (694 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.
**Age 32 (695 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 33 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 34 (697 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.

**Age 35 (698 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
**Age 36 (699 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Osric

**Age 37 (700 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 38 (701 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 39 (702 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
**Age 40 (703 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 41 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (705 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.

**Age 44 (707 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.

**Age 46 (709 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 47 (710 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 48 (711 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands.
**Age 49 (712 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 50 (713 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.

**Age 51 (714 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful.
**Age 52 (715 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 53 (716 TR)** — Her long years of service were noticed and formally recognised.

**Age 54 (717 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.

**Age 55 (718 TR)** — She was not carrying a chivalric weapon. She was carrying her employer's letter in a sealed case, and the seal looked like a heraldic device to a guard who did not know what a heraldic device actually looked like, and the guard decided that the case itself constituted bearing arms without right and detained her for half a day. The alderman who resolved it apologised. The guard was reprimanded. She filed a complaint with the guild that employed the guard service. She received a written acknowledgment. She carries the employer's letter unsealed now, which the employer finds aesthetically displeasing and which she considers a reasonable trade.
**Age 56 (719 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.

**Age 57 (720 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (681 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Dalric Harper **Age 19 (682 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary. **Age 20 (683 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (684 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination. **Age 22 (685 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Osric **Age 23 (686 TR)** — She dreamed something that she decided not to speak of. She has never spoken of it. **Age 24 (687 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them. **Age 25 (688 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 26 (689 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 27 (690 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her. **Age 28 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 29 (692 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 30 (693 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness. **Age 31 (694 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands. **Age 32 (695 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community. **Age 33 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 34 (697 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 35 (698 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice. **Age 36 (699 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Osric **Age 37 (700 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty. **Age 38 (701 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity. **Age 39 (702 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret. **Age 40 (703 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 41 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 42 (705 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back. **Age 44 (707 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council. **Age 46 (709 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that. **Age 47 (710 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 48 (711 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands. **Age 49 (712 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 50 (713 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at. **Age 51 (714 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful. **Age 52 (715 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option. **Age 53 (716 TR)** — Her long years of service were noticed and formally recognised. **Age 54 (717 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name. **Age 55 (718 TR)** — She was not carrying a chivalric weapon. She was carrying her employer's letter in a sealed case, and the seal looked like a heraldic device to a guard who did not know what a heraldic device actually looked like, and the guard decided that the case itself constituted bearing arms without right and detained her for half a day. The alderman who resolved it apologised. The guard was reprimanded. She filed a complaint with the guild that employed the guard service. She received a written acknowledgment. She carries the employer's letter unsealed now, which the employer finds aesthetically displeasing and which she considers a reasonable trade. **Age 56 (719 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction. **Age 57 (720 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. name: Elara Cordwainer

narrative: **Age 18 (687 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
**Age 19 (688 TR)** — Fire gutted her workshop. Starting again was the only option.

**Age 20 (689 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (690 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.

**Age 22 (691 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.

**Age 23 (692 TR)** — She had been offered the bonded contract by a lord's factor who had heard of her skill. It meant giving up her journeyman freedom, but the workshop he described — fitted to her specifications, supplied without bargaining — was not something she could have built herself. She signed for five years and extended twice.

**Age 24 (693 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 25 (694 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.

**Age 26 (695 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.

**Age 27 (696 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.

**Age 28 (697 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.
**Age 29 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 30 (699 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 31 (700 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.

**Age 32 (701 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 33 (702 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.

**Age 34 (703 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 35 (704 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 36 (705 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 37 (706 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 38 (707 TR)** — A woman she had never seen before paid for her meal and was gone before she could object.
**Age 39 (708 TR)** — She performed an old ritual her grandmother had taught her. She felt better afterwards and did not explain it.
**Age 40 (709 TR)** — She was caught in a downpour with nowhere to go. She stood in it until it passed and felt, obscurely, better for it.
**Age 41 (710 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Beren
**Age 42 (711 TR)** — She was corrected on a prayer she had said since childhood. She had been saying a word wrong all that time.

**Age 43 (712 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.
**Age 44 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (715 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 47 (716 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands.
**Age 48 (717 TR)** — A secret entrusted to her found its way to those who would pay for it.
**Age 49 (718 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 50 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

name: Elva Slater

narrative: **Age 18 (693 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 19 (694 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.

**Age 20 (695 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (696 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
**Age 22 (697 TR)** — She found a hidden place in a wall or floor that someone had used before her. Whatever had been kept there was long gone.

**Age 23 (698 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.

**Age 24 (699 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.

**Age 25 (700 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 26 (701 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 28 (703 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.

**Age 29 (704 TR)** — She took her loom to her mother's house and worked from there for a year. The guild does not certify home-weavers; this is an area where the guild's coverage is incomplete by design, because the women who weave at home are not the women the guild is designed to serve. She is not sure whether this is convenient or an insult. She sold everything she produced and used the money to buy better materials. Then she went back to the guild workshop.
**Age 30 (705 TR)** — She arrived too early. She walked around the block several times before going in.
**Age 31 (706 TR)** — She felt the change in the air before she saw it and understood that the year was turning. She had not been ready.

**Age 32 (707 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.

**Age 33 (708 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.

**Age 34 (709 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 35 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 36 (711 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped.

**Age 37 (712 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.

**Age 38 (713 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 39 (714 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 40 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 42 (717 TR)** — The guild jury turned down the masterwork. She has spent three weeks looking at it from every angle, asking herself whether the jury was right. Her honest assessment: the craftsmanship was not at fault. The design was unconventional and the jury found it outside the expected form. She is deciding whether to submit a conventional masterwork to achieve the master grade and do the unconventional work afterward, or to submit the unconventional work again and argue for it, which will take longer and may not succeed. She has not decided.

**Age 43 (718 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.

**Age 44 (719 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 45 (720 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (693 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 19 (694 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them. **Age 20 (695 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (696 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork. **Age 22 (697 TR)** — She found a hidden place in a wall or floor that someone had used before her. Whatever had been kept there was long gone. **Age 23 (698 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that. **Age 24 (699 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice. **Age 25 (700 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct. **Age 26 (701 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity. **Age 28 (703 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands. **Age 29 (704 TR)** — She took her loom to her mother's house and worked from there for a year. The guild does not certify home-weavers; this is an area where the guild's coverage is incomplete by design, because the women who weave at home are not the women the guild is designed to serve. She is not sure whether this is convenient or an insult. She sold everything she produced and used the money to buy better materials. Then she went back to the guild workshop. **Age 30 (705 TR)** — She arrived too early. She walked around the block several times before going in. **Age 31 (706 TR)** — She felt the change in the air before she saw it and understood that the year was turning. She had not been ready. **Age 32 (707 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. She has not decided which she will do. **Age 33 (708 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her. **Age 34 (709 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 35 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 36 (711 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped. **Age 37 (712 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing.
