name: Rorath Bowyer

narrative: **Age 18 (699 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.

**Age 19 (700 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 20 (701 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (702 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 22 (703 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Morin Harper (artisan, age 21)

**Age 22 (703 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Woran Harper. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Woran Harper born (male)
**Age 23 (704 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 23 (704 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 17

**Age 24 (705 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 25 (706 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Kelan Potter (artisan)

**Age 26 (707 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 26 (707 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 27 (708 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 27 (708 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 5

**Age 28 (709 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 28 (709 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 29 (710 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.

**Age 29 (710 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 30 (711 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 30 (711 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Lucan Harper lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Lucan Harper born (male)
**Age 31 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 31 (712 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 32 (713 TR)** — A snake crossed her path and the old women of the village argued for a week about what it meant.
**Age 32 (713 TR)** — She bore a child. Malik Harper came into the world healthy. week 40 Malik Harper born (male)
**Age 33 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (714 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 28

**Age 34 (715 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
**Age 34 (715 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 35 (716 TR)** — She woke knowing what she needed to do. She had gone to sleep with no idea. She did not question it.
**Age 35 (716 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 35 (716 TR)** — She lost Malik Harper — still a child, not yet grown. Malik Harper died (age 3)

**Age 36 (717 TR)** — She had been examined by three syndics, one of whom was clearly hoping she would fail. She did not fail. She answered every question exactly, produced the requested demonstration piece, and thanked each of them individually afterward. The one who had been hoping she would fail did not meet her eyes. She found she did not mind this.
**Age 36 (717 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Woran Harper apprenticed (age 14)
**Age 37 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (718 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 38 (719 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 38 (719 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 39 (720 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 39 (720 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.

**Age 25 (712 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Sera Millward — lived. week 40 Sera Millward born (female)
**Age 26 (713 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 26 (713 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.
**Age 26 (713 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Kelan Millward died (age 2)
**Age 27 (714 TR)** — She heard something through a door that changed how she thought about two people she knew. She said nothing.
**Age 27 (714 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 11

**Age 28 (715 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 28 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 29 (716 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 29 (716 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 30 (717 TR)** — The cat left a mouse on her pillow. She accepted this as the highest compliment.
**Age 30 (717 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 32
**Age 31 (718 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped.
**Age 31 (718 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.

**Age 32 (719 TR)** — She took her loom to her mother's house and worked from there for a year. The guild does not certify home-weavers; this is an area where the guild's coverage is incomplete by design, because the women who weave at home are not the women the guild is designed to serve. She is not sure whether this is convenient or an insult. She sold everything she produced and used the money to buy better materials. Then she went back to the guild workshop.
**Age 32 (719 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 33 (720 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 33 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (705 TR)** — The road was impassable. She turned back and took the long way. She had been in a hurry. She arrived late and said nothing about why. **Age 19 (706 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone. **Age 20 (707 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (708 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Noric Millward (artisan, age 27) **Age 21 (708 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her. **Age 22 (709 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct. **Age 22 (709 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Elda Millward lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Elda Millward born (female) **Age 23 (710 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 23 (710 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride. **Age 24 (711 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one. **Age 24 (711 TR)** — She bore a child. Kelan Millward came into the world healthy. week 40 Kelan Millward born (male) **Age 25 (712 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 25 (712 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. name: Celyn Cordwainer

narrative: **Age 18 (711 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.

**Age 19 (712 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 20 (713 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (714 TR)** — Her apprentice was ready. She sent them out with the tools they had earned. She had taught well. She was not sure she had taught everything.
**Age 22 (715 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Gareth Carpenter (artisan, age 21)
**Age 23 (716 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Antor Ulekar
**Age 23 (716 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.

**Age 24 (717 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality.

**Age 25 (718 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.

**Age 26 (719 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.
**Age 27 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

name: Selyn Brewer

The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.
**Age 19 (718 TR)** — She watched someone who really knew what they were doing. She had thought she knew. She saw now that she had been adequate.

**Age 20 (719 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. **Age 21 (720 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

name: Marta Fletcher

narrative: **Age 18 (665 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 19 (666 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.

**Age 20 (667 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 21 (668 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 22 (669 TR)** — She attends every chapter meeting. She is usually the only woman and she has found that this confers an accidental advantage: when she speaks, the room goes quiet in a way it does not always for the others, whether from respect or from the novelty she cannot always tell, but she uses it consistently.
**Age 23 (670 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Fenric Blacksmith (artisan, age 21)
**Age 23 (670 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.

**Age 24 (671 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
**Age 24 (671 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 25 (672 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 25 (672 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.

**Age 26 (673 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 27 (674 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 27 (674 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.

**Age 28 (675 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 28 (675 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 29 (676 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.

**Age 29 (676 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Inda Blacksmith. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Inda Blacksmith born (female)
**Age 30 (677 TR)** — She stayed up until the candle was gone. She was not sure what she had been doing for the last hour.
**Age 30 (677 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 31 (678 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 31 (678 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 4
**Age 32 (679 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Morak Potter (artisan)

**Age 32 (679 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 33 (680 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient.
**Age 33 (680 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 34 (681 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands.
**Age 34 (681 TR)** — She bore a child. Meric Blacksmith came into the world healthy. week 40 Meric Blacksmith born (male)
**Age 35 (682 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 36 (683 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (683 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 37 (684 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.

**Age 38 (685 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 38 (685 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 39 (686 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Morak Potter became a close friend
**Age 39 (686 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Melda Blacksmith — lived. week 40 Melda Blacksmith born (female)

**Age 40 (687 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 40 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (688 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Morak Potter has died
**Age 41 (688 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 42 (689 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
**Age 42 (689 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Talith Blacksmith lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Talith Blacksmith born (female)
**Age 43 (690 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 43 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (691 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 44 (691 TR)** — She bore a child. Belyn Blacksmith came into the world healthy. week 40 Belyn Blacksmith born (female)

**Age 45 (692 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
**Age 45 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (693 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 46 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (693 TR)** — Her daughter Inda Blacksmith married Tovan Boring. Inda Blacksmith married Tovan Boring (peasant, age 22)
**Age 46 (693 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Inda Blacksmith's child born (female, Inda Blacksmith age 17)
**Age 47 (694 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (694 TR)** — Fenric Blacksmith died after 24 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Fenric Blacksmith died (approx. age 45)

**Age 48 (695 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 48 (695 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 48 (695 TR)** — Inda Blacksmith left to make her own way. Inda Blacksmith left home (age 19)
**Age 49 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (696 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 49 (696 TR)** — She lost Melda Blacksmith — still a child, not yet grown. Melda Blacksmith died (age 10)

**Age 50 (697 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.

**Age 51 (698 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 51 (698 TR)** — Her son Meric Blacksmith married Arden Hayward. Meric Blacksmith married Arden Hayward (artisan, age 16)
**Age 52 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (699 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 52 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 53 (700 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 53 (700 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 53 (700 TR)** — Inda Blacksmith lost Garith Blacksmith. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Inda Blacksmith's child died (age 7)

**Age 54 (701 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.
**Age 55 (702 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Orlan Carter
**Age 55 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (702 TR)** — Inda Blacksmith's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Inda Blacksmith's child died (age 0)
**Age 55 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (702 TR)** — Meric Blacksmith's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Meric Blacksmith's child died (age 0)
**Age 56 (703 TR)** — She followed a cat through somewhere she did not know and found something she was glad to know was there.
**Age 56 (703 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 57 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 58 (705 TR)** — The Ebasethe arrived before dawn and stayed until the crisis passed. She had sent for her because the midwife said to. She had not expected it to matter. The child was alive. She had a different understanding of the Peonian church after that — less abstract, more personal.
**Age 58 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (706 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Orlan Elendyl
**Age 59 (706 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Belyn Blacksmith apprenticed (age 15)
**Age 60 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (708 TR)** — Talith Blacksmith left to make her own way. Talith Blacksmith left home (age 19)
**Age 62 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 62 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 63 (710 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.
**Age 63 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 64 (711 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Orlan Carter
**Age 64 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (712 TR)** — She repaired something repeatedly that had been repaired too many times already. She was not sure why she did not let it go.
**Age 65 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (712 TR)** — Inda Blacksmith lost Delyn Blacksmith. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Inda Blacksmith's child died (age 13)
**Age 65 (712 TR)** — Her daughter Talith Blacksmith married Varen Blacksmith. Talith Blacksmith married Varen Blacksmith (artisan, age 26)
**Age 66 (713 TR)** — The creditors came. What she had built was gone.
**Age 66 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 66 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 66 (713 TR)** — Meric Blacksmith lost Ulric Blacksmith. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Meric Blacksmith's child died (age 4)
**Age 66 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 67 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 67 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (714 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Belyn Blacksmith's illegitimate child born (male, Belyn Blacksmith age 23)
**Age 68 (715 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 68 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 68 (715 TR)** — Belyn Blacksmith's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Belyn Blacksmith's child died (age 1)
**Age 69 (716 TR)** — She caught the smell of smoke in a room where nothing had been lit. She looked and found nothing.
**Age 69 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 70 (717 TR)** — Her daughter's marriage was arranged and the merchet was due to the lord before the wedding. She sent the payment with the reeve and received the lord's written approval in return. The written approval is a document she keeps with the other land documents, because written approval means the marriage cannot later be contested on grounds of non-compliance with the lord's right. She is practical about the lord's rights. They exist. She pays them correctly and keeps the records.
**Age 70 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 70 (717 TR)** — Inda Blacksmith died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Inda Blacksmith died (age 41)

**Age 71 (718 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 71 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 71 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 72 (719 TR)** — Her late husband had been a freeholder. The new lord's steward arrived with a document she had never seen before claiming her husband had accepted serf obligations six years prior. The document was real; her husband had signed it during a hard winter when the choice was that or losing the holding. She had not known. She appealed to the hundred moot. The moot found that the serf arrangement had applied to her husband and passed to her by inheritance. She accepted this and began saving to buy her way to freeholder status. It took four years. The lord took what she offered and issued the document. She keeps both documents together.
**Age 72 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 73 (720 TR)** — A tune she caught half of at a festival lodged in her head for the rest of the season.
**Age 73 (720 TR)** — Her daughter Talith Blacksmith remarried. Talith Blacksmith remarried Noric Miller (artisan, age 32)
**Age 66** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [artisan → peasant]
narrativeText: **Age 18 (665 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 19 (666 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice. **Age 20 (667 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them. **Age 21 (668 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity. **Age 22 (669 TR)** — She attends every chapter meeting. She is usually the only woman and she has found that this confers an accidental advantage: when she speaks, the room goes quiet in a way it does not always for the others, whether from respect or from the novelty she cannot always tell, but she uses it consistently. **Age 23 (670 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Fenric Blacksmith (artisan, age 21) **Age 23 (670 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically. **Age 24 (671 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do. **Age 24 (671 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 25 (672 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline. **Age 25 (672 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride. **Age 26 (673 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty. **Age 27 (674 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness. **Age 27 (674 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would. **Age 28 (675 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination. **Age 28 (675 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day. **Age 29 (676 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 29 (676 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Inda Blacksmith. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Inda Blacksmith born (female) **Age 30 (677 TR)** — She stayed up until the candle was gone. She was not sure what she had been doing for the last hour. **Age 30 (677 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 31 (678 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next. **Age 31 (678 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 4 **Age 32 (679 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Morak Potter (artisan) **Age 32 (679 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 33 (680 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient. **Age 33 (680 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 34 (681 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands. **Age 34 (681 TR)** — She bore a child. Meric Blacksmith came into the world healthy. week 40 Meric Blacksmith born (male) **Age 35 (682 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 36 (683 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 36 (683 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days. **Age 37 (684 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 38 (685 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five. **Age 38 (685 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 39 (686 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Morak Potter became a close friend **Age 39 (686 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Melda Blacksmith — lived. week 40 Melda Blacksmith born (female) **Age 40 (687 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct. **Age 40 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 41 (688 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Morak Potter has died **Age 41 (688 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 42 (689 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger. **Age 42 (689 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Talith Blacksmith lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Talith Blacksmith born (female) **Age 43 (690 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 43 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 44 (691 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers. **Age 44 (691 TR)** — She bore a child. Belyn Blacksmith came into the world healthy. week 40 Belyn Blacksmith born (female) **Age 45 (692 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council. **Age 45 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 46 (693 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 46 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 46 (693 TR)** — Her daughter Inda Blacksmith married Tovan Boring. Inda Blacksmith married Tovan Boring (peasant, age 22) **Age 46 (693 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Inda Blacksmith's child born (female, Inda Blacksmith age 17) **Age 47 (694 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 47 (694 TR)** — Fenric Blacksmith died after 24 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Fenric Blacksmith died (approx. age 45) **Age 48 (695 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work. **Age 48 (695 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough. **Age 48 (695 TR)** — Inda Blacksmith left to make her own way. Inda Blacksmith left home (age 19) **Age 49 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 49 (696 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 49 (696 TR)** — She lost Melda Blacksmith — still a child, not yet grown. Melda Blacksmith died (age 10) **Age 50 (697 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so. **Age 51 (698 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that. **Age 51 (698 TR)** — Her son Meric Blacksmith married Arden Hayward. Meric Blacksmith married Arden Hayward (artisan, age 16) **Age 52 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (699 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 52 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 53 (700 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show. **Age 53 (700 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain. **Age 53 (700 TR)** — Inda Blacksmith lost Garith Blacksmith. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Inda Blacksmith's child died (age 7) **Age 54 (701 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them. **Age 55 (702 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Orlan Carter **Age 55 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 55 (702 TR)** — Inda Blacksmith's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. name: Arden Skinner

narrative: **Age 18 (671 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 19 (672 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Cador Tanner (artisan)

**Age 20 (673 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (674 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 22 (675 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.

**Age 23 (676 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 24 (677 TR)** — An old man told her a story of a siege she had only heard in ballads. She kept thinking about it for weeks.

**Age 25 (678 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 26 (679 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Jorin

**Age 27 (680 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
**Age 28 (681 TR)** — She finished something and found she was satisfied with it. That was rarer than it should have been.
**Age 29 (682 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 30 (683 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.
**Age 31 (684 TR)** — She received kindness from a direction she had not anticipated. She thought about it for a long time.
**Age 32 (685 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Cador Tanner has died

**Age 33 (686 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.

**Age 34 (687 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 35 (688 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Malik Malson (peasant, age 40)
**Age 36 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (689 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Erith Malson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Erith Malson born (female)

**Age 37 (690 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 37 (690 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.

**Age 38 (691 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.

**Age 38 (691 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 39 (692 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.
**Age 39 (692 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 40 (693 TR)** — She attempted something she expected to fail at and did it adequately on the first try.
**Age 40 (693 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 41 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (694 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 42 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 43 (696 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
**Age 43 (696 TR)** — Malik Malson died after 8 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Malik Malson died (approx. age 48)
**Age 44 (697 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — The old pain returned, and this time it stayed. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
**Age 46 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (699 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Erith Malson apprenticed (age 10)
**Age 47 (700 TR)** — A woman she had never met told her to stay home that day. She went anyway. Nothing happened.
**Age 47 (700 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 48 (701 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
**Age 48 (701 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 49 (702 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 50 (703 TR)** — She repaired something — a step, a latch, a cracked fitting — that people had walked past broken for so long they had stopped seeing it.
**Age 50 (703 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 51 (704 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 51 (704 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 52 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (706 TR)** — She helped a woman she had never seen carry a load up a hill. The woman tried to pay her; she refused.
**Age 53 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (709 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 56 (709 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Erith Malson's illegitimate child born (female, Erith Malson age 20)
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 58 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (713 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
**Age 60 (713 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 60 (713 TR)** — Her daughter Erith Malson married Corin Turner. Erith Malson married Corin Turner (artisan, age 26)

**Age 61 (714 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 62 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 62 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 64 (717 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
**Age 65 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (718 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Erith Malson's child born (female, Erith Malson age 29)
**Age 66 (719 TR)** — She woke knowing what she needed to do. She had gone to sleep with no idea. She did not question it.
**Age 66 (719 TR)** — Erith Malson died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Erith Malson died (age 30)
**Age 67 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work. **Age 19 (672 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Cador Tanner (artisan) **Age 20 (673 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (674 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 22 (675 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so. **Age 23 (676 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 24 (677 TR)** — An old man told her a story of a siege she had only heard in ballads. She kept thinking about it for weeks. **Age 25 (678 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 26 (679 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention.
