name: Delia Mason

narrative: **Age 18 (677 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 19 (678 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 20 (679 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (680 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.

**Age 22 (681 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 23 (682 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 24 (683 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 25 (684 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Ulan Morman (peasant, age 22)
**Age 25 (684 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 11
**Age 26 (685 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 26 (685 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 24
**Age 27 (686 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 28 (687 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.

**Age 28 (687 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Lothar Morman. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Lothar Morman born (male)

**Age 29 (688 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.
**Age 29 (688 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Pedar Morman lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Pedar Morman born (male)
**Age 30 (689 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 30 (689 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.

**Age 31 (690 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 31 (690 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Edris Morman lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Edris Morman born (female)
**Age 32 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 33 (692 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (692 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.
**Age 33 (692 TR)** — She lost Lothar Morman — still a child, not yet grown. Lothar Morman died (age 5)

**Age 35 (694 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.

**Age 35 (694 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Tera Morman. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Tera Morman born (female)

**Age 36 (695 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 36 (695 TR)** — She bore a child. Emra Morman came into the world healthy. week 40 Emra Morman born (female)

**Age 37 (696 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
**Age 38 (697 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Elric Harper
**Age 39 (698 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 39 (698 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Halden Rivergate (merchant)
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — She bore a child. Breda Morman came into the world healthy. week 40 Breda Morman born (female)
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Breda Morman died (age 0)

**Age 41 (700 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 41 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (701 TR)** — The sickness passed but left her diminished for months — thinner, slower to tire, more careful about cold. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.

**Age 42 (701 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.

**Age 43 (702 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 10
**Age 44 (703 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 44 (703 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Brand Morman — lived. week 40 Brand Morman born (male)

**Age 45 (704 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 45 (704 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 46 (705 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (705 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 47 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — Her son Pedar Morman married Sarath Marson. Pedar Morman married Sarath Marson (peasant, age 16)
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — Pedar Morman left to make her own way. Pedar Morman left home (age 19)
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Pedar Morman's child born (male, Pedar Morman age 20)
**Age 50 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 51 (710 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
**Age 51 (710 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — Tera Morman left to make her own way. Tera Morman left home (age 18)
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — Her daughter Tera Morman married Bram Harper. Tera Morman married Bram Harper (artisan, age 21)
**Age 55 (714 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 55 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — Her daughter Edris Morman married Orlan Skinner. Edris Morman married Orlan Skinner (artisan, age 27)
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — Ulan Morman died after 32 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Ulan Morman died (approx. age 54)
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (717 TR)** — She went the wrong way and found a place she would go back to many times after.
**Age 58 (717 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — Her daughter Emra Morman married Marek Valing. Emra Morman married Marek Valing (peasant, age 24)
**Age 60 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (677 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty. **Age 19 (678 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 20 (679 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (680 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so. **Age 22 (681 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them. **Age 23 (682 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 24 (683 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays. **Age 25 (684 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Ulan Morman (peasant, age 22) **Age 25 (684 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 11 **Age 26 (685 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 26 (685 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 24 **Age 27 (686 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity. **Age 28 (687 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them. **Age 28 (687 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Lothar Morman. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Lothar Morman born (male) **Age 29 (688 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her. **Age 29 (688 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Pedar Morman lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Pedar Morman born (male) **Age 30 (689 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option. **Age 30 (689 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took. **Age 31 (690 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at. **Age 31 (690 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Edris Morman lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Edris Morman born (female) **Age 32 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 33 (692 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 33 (692 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected. **Age 33 (692 TR)** — She lost Lothar Morman — still a child, not yet grown. Lothar Morman died (age 5) **Age 35 (694 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary. **Age 35 (694 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Tera Morman. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Tera Morman born (female) **Age 36 (695 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work. **Age 36 (695 TR)** — She bore a child. Emra Morman came into the world healthy. week 40 Emra Morman born (female) **Age 37 (696 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council. **Age 38 (697 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Elric Harper **Age 39 (698 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness. **Age 39 (698 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 40 (699 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Halden Rivergate (merchant) **Age 40 (699 TR)** — She bore a child. Breda Morman came into the world healthy. week 40 Breda Morman born (female) **Age 40 (699 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Breda Morman died (age 0) **Age 41 (700 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that. **Age 41 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 42 (701 TR)** — The sickness passed but left her diminished for months — thinner, slower to tire, more careful about cold. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 42 (701 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny. **Age 43 (702 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone. **Age 43 (702 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. name: Geda Harper

narrative: **Age 18 (683 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.

**Age 19 (684 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.

**Age 20 (685 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (686 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Levan Dustmantle
**Age 22 (687 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 23 (688 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.

**Age 24 (689 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 25 (690 TR)** — An old man told her a story of a siege she had only heard in ballads. She kept thinking about it for weeks.

**Age 26 (691 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Levan Dustmantle became a close friend

**Age 27 (692 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.

**Age 28 (693 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 29 (694 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 30 (695 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.

**Age 31 (696 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.
**Age 32 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 33 (698 TR)** — She bore twins — Rorik Butcher and Aldis Butcher. The birth was hard and left her exhausted for weeks, but both children lived. week 40 Rorik Butcher and Aldis Butcher born (twins)
**Age 34 (699 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 34 (699 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 35 (700 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 35 (700 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.
**Age 36 (701 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 36 (701 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 37 (702 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.

**Age 38 (703 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
**Age 39 (704 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 40 (705 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.

**Age 41 (706 TR)** — A fighting order commissioning from a woman was irregular enough that the arms-master who arrived had clearly been told to pretend he did not find it so. She found this easier to work with than direct hostility would have been. The commission was for mail, which is what she does best, and when they came to inspect it, the pretense of indifference became straightforwardly professional. They have sent three subsequent commissions.
**Age 41 (706 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 41 (706 TR)** — She lost Rorik Butcher — still a child, not yet grown. Rorik Butcher died (age 8)
**Age 42 (707 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 43 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (708 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 44 (709 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 44 (709 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 45 (710 TR)** — Her apprentice was ready. She sent them out with the tools they had earned. She had taught well. She was not sure she had taught everything.
**Age 45 (710 TR)** — Aldis Butcher had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Aldis Butcher died (age 12)
**Age 46 (711 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 47 (712 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.

**Age 48 (713 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.

**Age 49 (714 TR)** — The guild jury turned down the masterwork. She has spent three weeks looking at it from every angle, asking herself whether the jury was right. Her honest assessment: the craftsmanship was not at fault. The design was unconventional and the jury found it outside the expected form. She is deciding whether to submit a conventional masterwork to achieve the master grade and do the unconventional work afterward, or to submit the unconventional work again and argue for it, which will take longer and may not succeed. She has not decided.
**Age 50 (715 TR)** — She was pulled into a quarrel between two people she liked equally. She attempted fairness. This satisfied no one.

**Age 51 (716 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.
**Age 52 (717 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 53 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (719 TR)** — Her back seized up at an inconvenient moment. She managed. She told no one how much it hurt.
**Age 55 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 25 (690 TR)** — An old man told her a story of a siege she had only heard in ballads. She kept thinking about it for weeks. **Age 26 (691 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Levan Dustmantle became a close friend **Age 27 (692 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why. **Age 28 (693 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct. **Age 29 (694 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone. **Age 30 (695 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction. **Age 31 (696 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name. **Age 32 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 33 (698 TR)** — She bore twins — Rorik Butcher and Aldis Butcher. The birth was hard and left her exhausted for weeks, but both children lived. week 40 Rorik Butcher and Aldis Butcher born (twins) **Age 34 (699 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 34 (699 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 35 (700 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 35 (700 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her. **Age 36 (701 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily. **Age 36 (701 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough. **Age 37 (702 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question. **Age 38 (703 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do. **Age 39 (704 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness. **Age 40 (705 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public. **Age 41 (706 TR)** — A fighting order commissioning from a woman was irregular enough that the arms-master who arrived had clearly been told to pretend he did not find it so. She found this easier to work with than direct hostility would have been. The commission was for mail, which is what she does best, and when they came to inspect it, the pretense of indifference became straightforwardly professional. They have sent three subsequent commissions. **Age 41 (706 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 41 (706 TR)** — She lost Rorik Butcher — still a child, not yet grown. Rorik Butcher died (age 8) **Age 42 (707 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline. **Age 43 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 43 (708 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 44 (709 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while. **Age 44 (709 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 45 (710 TR)** — Her apprentice was ready. She sent them out with the tools they had earned. She had taught well. She was not sure she had taught everything. **Age 45 (710 TR)** — Aldis Butcher had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Aldis Butcher died (age 12) **Age 46 (711 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 47 (712 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her. **Age 48 (713 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council. **Age 49 (714 TR)** — The guild jury turned down the masterwork. She has spent three weeks looking at it from every angle, asking herself whether the jury was right. Her honest assessment: the craftsmanship was not at fault. The design was unconventional and the jury found it outside the expected form. She is deciding whether to submit a conventional masterwork to achieve the master grade and do the unconventional work afterward, or to submit the unconventional work again and argue for it, which will take longer and may not succeed. She has not decided. **Age 50 (715 TR)** — She was pulled into a quarrel between two people she liked equally. She attempted fairness. This satisfied no one. **Age 51 (716 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger. **Age 52 (717 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time. **Age 53 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (719 TR)** — Her back seized up at an inconvenient moment. She managed. She told no one how much it hurt. **Age 55 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
name: Mira Butcher

narrative: **Age 18 (689 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
**Age 19 (690 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Deric Fuller died

**Age 20 (691 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (692 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.

**Age 22 (693 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.

**Age 23 (694 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.

**Age 24 (695 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
**Age 25 (696 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 26 (697 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.

**Age 27 (698 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
**Age 28 (699 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 29 (700 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 30 (701 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.

**Age 31 (702 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 32 (703 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.

**Age 33 (704 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 34 (705 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

**Age 35 (706 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
**Age 36 (707 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Iric Carter
**Age 37 (708 TR)** — Fire gutted her workshop. Starting again was the only option.
**Age 38 (709 TR)** — She meant to glance at the rain and check on it. She stood there watching it for a long time.
**Age 39 (710 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.
**Age 40 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (712 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Zoric Wright
**Age 42 (713 TR)** — The change had been gradual and then sudden. She could trace exactly when it had stopped being repairable. Zoric Wright became an enemy

**Age 44 (715 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 45 (716 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 46 (717 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.

**Age 47 (718 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 48 (719 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Mora Saltworth (merchant)
**Age 49 (720 TR)** — She brought the same gift as someone else. The recipient was gracious. She thought about it longer than the situation warranted.

name: Farath Grinder

narrative: **Age 18 (695 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 19 (696 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

**Age 20 (697 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (698 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.

**Age 22 (699 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 23 (700 TR)** — She drank from a well and the water was so cold and clean that she stood for a moment before going on.

**Age 24 (701 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 25 (702 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.

**Age 26 (703 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.

**Age 27 (704 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 28 (705 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 29 (706 TR)** — She had been examined by three syndics, one of whom was clearly hoping she would fail. She did not fail. She answered every question exactly, produced the requested demonstration piece, and thanked each of them individually afterward. The one who had been hoping she would fail did not meet her eyes. She found she did not mind this.
**Age 30 (707 TR)** — She bought something impractical and unnecessary. She kept it without apology.
**Age 31 (708 TR)** — She sneezed at the worst possible moment. The ceremony continued. The people nearest her found it difficult to maintain appropriate expressions.

**Age 32 (709 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 33 (710 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 34 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 35 (712 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.

**Age 36 (713 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.

**Age 37 (714 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 38 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 39 (716 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the thigh. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 40 (717 TR)** — She calculated when something had occurred and found it was far further back than she had thought.
**Age 41 (718 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.

**Age 42 (719 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.
**Age 43 (720 TR)** — She repaired something — a step, a latch, a cracked fitting — that people had walked past broken for so long they had stopped seeing it.

She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (698 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork. **Age 22 (699 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary. **Age 23 (700 TR)** — She drank from a well and the water was so cold and clean that she stood for a moment before going on. **Age 24 (701 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct. **Age 25 (702 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do. **Age 26 (703 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays. **Age 27 (704 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her. **Age 28 (705 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity. **Age 29 (706 TR)** — She had been examined by three syndics, one of whom was clearly hoping she would fail. She did not fail. She answered every question exactly, produced the requested demonstration piece, and thanked each of them individually afterward. The one who had been hoping she would fail did not meet her eyes. She found she did not mind this. **Age 30 (707 TR)** — She bought something impractical and unnecessary. She kept it without apology. **Age 31 (708 TR)** — She sneezed at the worst possible moment. The ceremony continued. The people nearest her found it difficult to maintain appropriate expressions. **Age 32 (709 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination. **Age 33 (710 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next. **Age 34 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 35 (712 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret. **Age 36 (713 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with. **Age 37 (714 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 38 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 39 (716 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the thigh. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1). **Age 40 (717 TR)** — She calculated when something had occurred and found it was far further back than she had thought. **Age 41 (718 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community. **Age 42 (719 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands. **Age 43 (720 TR)** — She repaired something — a step, a latch, a cracked fitting — that people had walked past broken for so long they had stopped seeing it.
name: Rora Skinner

narrative: **Age 18 (701 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.

**Age 19 (702 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

**Age 20 (703 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (704 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 22 (705 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.

**Age 23 (706 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.
**Age 24 (707 TR)** — She found a place that was simply right and made a habit of sitting there.
**Age 25 (708 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Fardan Brewer (artisan)
**Age 26 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 27 (710 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Ronan Tanner (artisan, age 26)

**Age 27 (710 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Breda Tanner. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Breda Tanner born (female)
**Age 28 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 28 (711 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Mara Tanner lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Mara Tanner born (female)

**Age 29 (712 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 29 (712 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.

**Age 30 (713 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Fenric Tanner. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Fenric Tanner born (male)

**Age 31 (714 TR)** — She had been examined by three syndics, one of whom was clearly hoping she would fail. She did not fail. She answered every question exactly, produced the requested demonstration piece, and thanked each of them individually afterward. The one who had been hoping she would fail did not meet her eyes. She found she did not mind this.
**Age 31 (714 TR)** — She lost Breda Tanner — still a child, not yet grown. Breda Tanner died (age 4)
**Age 32 (715 TR)** — The ferry was not running. She waited. Another passenger talked at her. She listened politely for two hours.
**Age 32 (715 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.

**Age 33 (716 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 33 (716 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.

**Age 34 (717 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
**Age 34 (717 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.

**Age 35 (718 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 35 (718 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 36 (719 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
**Age 37 (720 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

name: Tara Fuller

narrative: **Age 18 (707 TR)** — She heard of something happening somewhere distant and found it affected her more than it should have.

**Age 19 (708 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

**Age 20 (709 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (710 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Noral Garekar (noble)

**Age 22 (711 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
**Age 23 (712 TR)** — She drank more than was wise and behaved in ways she thought about for several days afterwards.

**Age 24 (713 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.

**Age 25 (714 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.

**Age 26 (715 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
**Age 27 (716 TR)** — She said what no one else was going to say. She had hoped to stay out of it. She did not stay out of it.
**Age 28 (717 TR)** — She helped a woman she had never seen carry a load up a hill. The woman tried to pay her; she refused.

**Age 29 (718 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 30 (719 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Xarin
**Age 31 (720 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
