name: Edris Cooper

narrative: **Age 18 (713 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Ulan Brewer

**Age 19 (714 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 20 (715 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (716 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 22 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 23 (718 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 24 (719 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality.

**Age 25 (720 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.

The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 23 (718 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 24 (719 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality. **Age 25 (720 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.
name: Rara Harper

narrative: **Age 18 (719 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.

**Age 19 (720 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

name: Irda Skinner

narrative: **Age 18 (667 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Halric Valing (peasant, age 22)
**Age 18 (667 TR)** — She bore a child. Laric Valing came into the world healthy. week 40 Laric Valing born (male)

**Age 19 (668 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 19 (668 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.

**Age 20 (669 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 20 (669 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.

**Age 21 (670 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 21 (670 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.

**Age 22 (671 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 22 (671 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 18

**Age 23 (672 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.
**Age 23 (672 TR)** — She bore a child. Durst Valing came into the world healthy. week 40 Durst Valing born (male)

**Age 24 (673 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.

**Age 24 (673 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Edric Valing. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Edric Valing born (male)
**Age 25 (674 TR)** — She waited in line. She was next. The thing she had been waiting for was gone.
**Age 25 (674 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Selda Valing lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Selda Valing born (female)

**Age 26 (675 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
**Age 26 (675 TR)** — Halric Valing died after 8 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Halric Valing died (approx. age 30)
**Age 27 (676 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 27 (676 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 28 (677 TR)** — She went the wrong way and found a place she would go back to many times after.

**Age 29 (678 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 29 (678 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 30 (679 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 30 (679 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 31 (680 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality.

**Age 32 (681 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 33 (682 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 34 (683 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 35 (684 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 35 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (684 TR)** — Laric Valing had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Laric Valing died (age 17)
**Age 36 (685 TR)** — She felt the change in the air before she saw it and understood that the year was turning. She had not been ready.
**Age 36 (685 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 37 (686 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 37 (686 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 38 (687 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 38 (687 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 39 (688 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 39 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 41 (690 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
**Age 41 (690 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Edric Valing's illegitimate child born (female, Edric Valing age 17)
**Age 42 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (691 TR)** — Her daughter Selda Valing married Roric Sawyer. Selda Valing married Roric Sawyer (artisan, age 16)

**Age 43 (692 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 43 (692 TR)** — Her daughter Selda Valing remarried. Selda Valing remarried Rorik Thatcher (artisan, age 25)
**Age 43 (692 TR)** — Selda Valing left to make her own way. Selda Valing left home (age 18)
**Age 44 (693 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 44 (693 TR)** — Her son Edric Valing married Inda Weaver. Edric Valing married Inda Weaver (artisan, age 16)
**Age 45 (694 TR)** — She woke to a sound she could not account for. She lay still and listened until it stopped. Nothing explained it in the morning.
**Age 45 (694 TR)** — Durst Valing left to make her own way. Durst Valing left home (age 22)
**Age 45 (694 TR)** — Edric Valing lost Karith Valing. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Edric Valing's child died (age 4)
**Age 45 (694 TR)** — Selda Valing died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Selda Valing died (age 20)

**Age 46 (695 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 46 (695 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Edric Valing's child born (male, Edric Valing age 22)
**Age 47 (696 TR)** — She told someone what she thought they should do. They did it. She was not sure she had been right.
**Age 47 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (696 TR)** — Edric Valing left to make her own way. Edric Valing left home (age 23)

**Age 48 (697 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.
**Age 48 (697 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 49 (698 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Ida Turner
**Age 50 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 52 (701 TR)** — The Ebasethe arrived before dawn and stayed until the crisis passed. She had sent for her because the midwife said to. She had not expected it to matter. The child was alive. She had a different understanding of the Peonian church after that — less abstract, more personal.
**Age 52 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (702 TR)** — She was corrected on a prayer she had said since childhood. She had been saying a word wrong all that time.
**Age 53 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (703 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 55 (704 TR)** — Her mind went blank on a name she knew well. She covered it badly and has thought about it since.
**Age 55 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (705 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient.

**Age 57 (706 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 57 (706 TR)** — Edric Valing died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Edric Valing died (age 33)
**Age 58 (707 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Irda Grinder died
**Age 58 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (708 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 59 (708 TR)** — Her son Durst Valing married Kela Koring. Durst Valing married Kela Koring (peasant, age 34)
**Age 60 (709 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
**Age 61 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 62 (711 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
**Age 62 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (712 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
**Age 64 (713 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.
**Age 65 (714 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 66 (715 TR)** — She held back what she wanted to say for too long. By the time she was ready to say it, the occasion had closed.
**Age 66 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 67 (716 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 68 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 68 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 69 (718 TR)** — She set her affairs in order with the same methodical care she had brought to everything. She felt lighter for it.
**Age 70 (719 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Orlan Wright
**Age 70 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 71 (720 TR)** — She repaired something repeatedly that had been repaired too many times already. She was not sure why she did not let it go.
**Age 71 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

Edric Valing's child born (male, Edric Valing age 22) **Age 47 (696 TR)** — She told someone what she thought they should do. They did it. She was not sure she had been right. **Age 47 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (696 TR)** — Edric Valing left to make her own way. Edric Valing left home (age 23) **Age 48 (697 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands. **Age 48 (697 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left. **Age 49 (698 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Ida Turner **Age 50 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 51 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 51 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (701 TR)** — The Ebasethe arrived before dawn and stayed until the crisis passed. She had sent for her because the midwife said to. She had not expected it to matter. The child was alive. She had a different understanding of the Peonian church after that — less abstract, more personal. **Age 52 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 53 (702 TR)** — She was corrected on a prayer she had said since childhood. She had been saying a word wrong all that time. **Age 53 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (703 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down. **Age 55 (704 TR)** — Her mind went blank on a name she knew well. She covered it badly and has thought about it since. **Age 55 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 56 (705 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient. **Age 57 (706 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her. **Age 57 (706 TR)** — Edric Valing died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Edric Valing died (age 33) **Age 58 (707 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Irda Grinder died **Age 58 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 59 (708 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 59 (708 TR)** — Her son Durst Valing married Kela Koring. Durst Valing married Kela Koring (peasant, age 34) **Age 60 (709 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back. **Age 61 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 62 (711 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council. **Age 62 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 63 (712 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone. **Age 64 (713 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her. **Age 65 (714 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete. **Age 66 (715 TR)** — She held back what she wanted to say for too long. By the time she was ready to say it, the occasion had closed. **Age 66 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 67 (716 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 68 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 68 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 69 (718 TR)** — She set her affairs in order with the same methodical care she had brought to everything. She felt lighter for it. **Age 70 (719 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Orlan Wright **Age 70 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 71 (720 TR)** — She repaired something repeatedly that had been repaired too many times already. She was not sure why she did not let it go. **Age 71 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
name: Rira Dyer

narrative: **Age 18 (673 TR)** — A swarm of bees chose her roof beam. Everyone said it was an omen. No one agreed on which kind.

**Age 19 (674 TR)** — The other apprentice is the master's nephew. The nephew is not particularly skilled. The nephew will probably advance faster than her regardless of the quality differential, which she has accepted as a fact of the situation rather than a grievance, because grievances are expensive and facts are information. She is deciding what information to extract from the fact: mainly that she needs to be better than the nephew in ways the master cannot ignore, which is already true, and she is working on making it more so.

**Age 20 (675 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (676 TR)** — She was caught in a downpour with nowhere to go. She stood in it until it passed and felt, obscurely, better for it.
**Age 22 (677 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Sara Wainworth (merchant)
**Age 23 (678 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Kelan Morson (peasant, age 20)

**Age 24 (679 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
**Age 25 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 25 (680 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.

**Age 26 (681 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 26 (681 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.

**Age 27 (682 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 28 (683 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 29 (684 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 30 (685 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 30 (685 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 13

**Age 31 (686 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 31 (686 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.

**Age 32 (687 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 32 (687 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.

**Age 33 (688 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.
**Age 33 (688 TR)** — The child came at term and did not breathe. She had been so close. week 39

**Age 34 (689 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 34 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 35 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 36 (691 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.
**Age 37 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 38 (693 TR)** — The guild jury turned down the masterwork. She has spent three weeks looking at it from every angle, asking herself whether the jury was right. Her honest assessment: the craftsmanship was not at fault. The design was unconventional and the jury found it outside the expected form. She is deciding whether to submit a conventional masterwork to achieve the master grade and do the unconventional work afterward, or to submit the unconventional work again and argue for it, which will take longer and may not succeed. She has not decided.
**Age 38 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (694 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 39 (694 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Ranulf Morson — lived. week 40 Ranulf Morson born (male)
**Age 40 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 41 (696 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.

**Age 42 (697 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 42 (697 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 43 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 44 (699 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.

**Age 45 (700 TR)** — She took her loom to her mother's house and worked from there for a year. The guild does not certify home-weavers; this is an area where the guild's coverage is incomplete by design, because the women who weave at home are not the women the guild is designed to serve. She is not sure whether this is convenient or an insult. She sold everything she produced and used the money to buy better materials. Then she went back to the guild workshop.

**Age 45 (700 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 46 (701 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.
**Age 46 (701 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20
**Age 47 (702 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (702 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.

**Age 48 (703 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 48 (703 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 49 (704 TR)** — She put out the washing. It began to rain before she reached the door. She stood for a moment and then went back for it.
**Age 50 (705 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 50 (705 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 51 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 52 (707 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
**Age 52 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 53 (708 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 53 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 54 (709 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality.
**Age 54 (709 TR)** — Ranulf Morson had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Ranulf Morson died (age 15)
**Age 55 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 55 (710 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
**Age 56 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 57 (712 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 58 (713 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.
**Age 58 (713 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 59 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 60 (715 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 60 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 61 (716 TR)** — She had managed the household, the accounts, the children's marriages, and the succession of a substantial property for four decades, and she stopped. What she found on the other side of it was time. She had forgotten what time felt like when it belonged to her. She is still learning.
**Age 61 (716 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 62 (717 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.
**Age 62 (717 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 63 (718 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Nora Thatcher
**Age 63 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 64 (719 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Idris Miller
**Age 64 (719 TR)** — Kelan Morson died after 41 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Kelan Morson died (approx. age 61)

**Age 65 (720 TR)** — She described what she could do and what she could not. The client had heard what she could do and was not interested in what she could not. She took the commission and spent a week working out how to do the part she had said she could not. When she delivered it, the client said it was not what they had imagined. She asked what they had imagined. What they described was worse. She kept her opinion of this to herself.

She is waiting for the name. **Age 46 (701 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20 **Age 47 (702 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 47 (702 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would. **Age 48 (703 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary. **Age 48 (703 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 49 (704 TR)** — She put out the washing. It began to rain before she reached the door. She stood for a moment and then went back for it. **Age 50 (705 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read. **Age 50 (705 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular. **Age 51 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 51 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (707 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council. **Age 52 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 53 (708 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone. **Age 53 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (709 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality. **Age 54 (709 TR)** — Ranulf Morson had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Ranulf Morson died (age 15) **Age 55 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 55 (710 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it. **Age 56 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 57 (712 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny. **Age 58 (713 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction. **Age 58 (713 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride. **Age 59 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 59 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 60 (715 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth. **Age 60 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 61 (716 TR)** — She had managed the household, the accounts, the children's marriages, and the succession of a substantial property for four decades, and she stopped. What she found on the other side of it was time. She had forgotten what time felt like when it belonged to her. She is still learning. **Age 61 (716 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day. **Age 62 (717 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger. **Age 62 (717 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days. **Age 63 (718 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Nora Thatcher **Age 63 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 64 (719 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Idris Miller **Age 64 (719 TR)** — Kelan Morson died after 41 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Kelan Morson died (approx. age 61) **Age 65 (720 TR)** — She described what she could do and what she could not. The client had heard what she could do and was not interested in what she could not. She took the commission and spent a week working out how to do the part she had said she could not. When she delivered it, the client said it was not what they had imagined. She asked what they had imagined. What they described was worse. She kept her opinion of this to herself.
name: Deva Mason

narrative: **Age 18 (679 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 19 (680 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 20 (681 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (682 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.

**Age 22 (683 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.

**Age 23 (684 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
**Age 24 (685 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Beren Hayward (artisan, age 26)
**Age 24 (685 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.

**Age 25 (686 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
**Age 26 (687 TR)** — She bore a child. Evan Hayward came into the world healthy. week 40 Evan Hayward born (male)
**Age 27 (688 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.

**Age 27 (688 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Tera Hayward. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Tera Hayward born (female)

**Age 28 (689 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.

**Age 28 (689 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — simple Ulra Hayward born (female, simple-minded)
**Age 29 (690 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 29 (690 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 29 (690 TR)** — She lost Ulra Hayward — the simple-minded child she had given so much to. Ulra Hayward died (age 1)
**Age 30 (691 TR)** — She lost a small thing she had carried for years. She did not speak of it but felt its absence for a long time.
**Age 31 (692 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 32 (693 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
**Age 32 (693 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.

**Age 33 (694 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 33 (694 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 34 (695 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.

**Age 35 (696 TR)** — The inspection had been prompted by a competitor, she was nearly certain. The syndic who conducted it was scrupulously fair but thorough. She passed every check. When the syndic left, she went back to work and did not spend more than ten minutes wondering who had called the inspection, which was about nine minutes longer than she had intended.
**Age 35 (696 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Balar Hayward lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Balar Hayward born (male)
**Age 36 (697 TR)** — She performed an old ritual her grandmother had taught her. She felt better afterwards and did not explain it.
**Age 36 (697 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 37 (698 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.

**Age 38 (699 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.

**Age 39 (700 TR)** — The sisters had worked for two weeks. They had been honest with her about what was coming and had given her time to say what she needed to say. She was grateful for that. She was also bereft. The two feelings sat together without resolving.
**Age 39 (700 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.

**Age 40 (701 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.
**Age 40 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (702 TR)** — She opened what she thought was the right door. It was not. She apologised to the people inside and withdrew.
**Age 42 (703 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.
**Age 42 (703 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Evan Hayward's illegitimate child born (male, Evan Hayward age 16)
**Age 43 (704 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the forearm or hand. The damage was permanent — dexterity reduced by 1.
**Age 43 (704 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 43 (704 TR)** — Her son Evan Hayward married Eara Millward. Evan Hayward married Eara Millward (artisan, age 16)
**Age 43 (704 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Tera Hayward's illegitimate child born (male, Tera Hayward age 16)
**Age 44 (705 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
**Age 44 (705 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 45 (706 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
**Age 45 (706 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 45 (706 TR)** — Evan Hayward left to make her own way. Evan Hayward left home (age 19)
**Age 46 (707 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 46 (707 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 47 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (709 TR)** — A swarm of bees chose her roof beam. Everyone said it was an omen. No one agreed on which kind.
**Age 48 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (709 TR)** — Her daughter Tera Hayward married Bram Parsen. Tera Hayward married Bram Parsen (peasant, age 25)
**Age 49 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (710 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Evan Hayward's child born (male, Evan Hayward age 23)

**Age 50 (711 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 50 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (711 TR)** — Tera Hayward left to make her own way. Tera Hayward left home (age 23)

**Age 51 (712 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 51 (712 TR)** — Tera Hayward lost Kael Hayward. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Tera Hayward's child died (age 8)
**Age 51 (712 TR)** — Her son Balar Hayward married Eara Bowyer. Balar Hayward married Eara Bowyer (artisan, age 16)
**Age 52 (713 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 52 (713 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 53 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (715 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −1.
**Age 54 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (716 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 56 (717 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 56 (717 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 57 (718 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands.
**Age 57 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (719 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.
**Age 58 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
