Evan Hayward's child born (male, Evan Hayward age 23) **Age 50 (711 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work. **Age 50 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 50 (711 TR)** — Tera Hayward left to make her own way. Tera Hayward left home (age 23) **Age 51 (712 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why. **Age 51 (712 TR)** — Tera Hayward lost Kael Hayward. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Tera Hayward's child died (age 8) **Age 51 (712 TR)** — Her son Balar Hayward married Eara Bowyer. Balar Hayward married Eara Bowyer (artisan, age 16) **Age 52 (713 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 52 (713 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 53 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 53 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 54 (715 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −1. **Age 54 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 55 (716 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 56 (717 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community. **Age 56 (717 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left. **Age 57 (718 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands. **Age 57 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (719 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed. **Age 58 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 59 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 59 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 59 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
name: Helda Millward

narrative: **Age 18 (685 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.

**Age 19 (686 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 20 (687 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (688 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 22 (689 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 23 (690 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.

**Age 24 (691 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.
**Age 25 (692 TR)** — She watched her fire die. She had somewhere to be. She stayed until the last coal went grey.

**Age 26 (693 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
**Age 27 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 28 (695 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.

**Age 29 (696 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.

**Age 30 (697 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 31 (698 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 32 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 33 (700 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 34 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 35 (702 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.

**Age 36 (703 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.

**Age 37 (704 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 38 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 39 (706 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
**Age 40 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 43 (710 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 44 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (712 TR)** — She went the wrong way and found a place she would go back to many times after.
**Age 46 (713 TR)** — She set up her own premises. No great ceremony; she simply stopped being someone else's pair of hands.

**Age 47 (714 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 48 (715 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 49 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 50 (717 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
**Age 51 (718 TR)** — Her long years of service were noticed and formally recognised.
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.

**Age 53 (720 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.

name: Eara Miller

narrative: **Age 18 (714 TR)** — A crow appeared every morning for a month on the same post. He began to look for it. One day it was gone.
**Age 19 (715 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.

**Age 20 (716 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 21 (717 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 22 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 23 (719 TR)** — He took a wrong turn and came out somewhere he had never been. The village had a good bakehouse and the wrong road became one he always took.
**Age 24 (720 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

name: Soran Horsen

narrative: **Age 18 (720 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (720 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
name: Beren Korman

narrative: **Age 18 (668 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 19 (669 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.

**Age 20 (670 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 21 (671 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 22 (672 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Corin Walekar (noble)
**Age 23 (673 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.

**Age 24 (674 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 25 (675 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Sara Norkin (unguilded, age 23)
**Age 25 (675 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 33
**Age 26 (676 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 26 (676 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Beren Dalkin. week 40 Beren Dalkin born (male)
**Age 27 (677 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands.
**Age 27 (677 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 28 (678 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 29 (679 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.

**Age 29 (679 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 30 (680 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 30 (680 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 10
**Age 31 (681 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 31 (681 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (682 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 33 (683 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 33 (683 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 19

**Age 34 (684 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 34 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (685 TR)** — He found someone asleep in an unexpected place — a doorway, a yard, a storage room — and left them to it.
**Age 36 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (686 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 37 (687 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 37 (687 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 38 (688 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 38 (688 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (689 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 39 (689 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 40 (690 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 40 (690 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.

**Age 41 (691 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 41 (691 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 42 (692 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 42 (692 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 42 (692 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Beren Dalkin's illegitimate child born (female, Beren Dalkin age 16)
**Age 43 (693 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 43 (693 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 44 (694 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 48 (698 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence.

**Age 70 (720 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.

narrativeText: **Age 18 (668 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 19 (669 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 20 (670 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing. **Age 21 (671 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues. **Age 22 (672 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Corin Walekar (noble) **Age 23 (673 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 24 (674 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 25 (675 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Sara Norkin (unguilded, age 23) **Age 25 (675 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 33 **Age 26 (676 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 26 (676 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Beren Dalkin. week 40 Beren Dalkin born (male) **Age 27 (677 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands. **Age 27 (677 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 28 (678 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 29 (679 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 29 (679 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 30 (680 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew. **Age 30 (680 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 10 **Age 31 (681 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 31 (681 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 32 (682 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out. **Age 33 (683 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 33 (683 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 19 **Age 34 (684 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 34 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 35 (685 TR)** — He found someone asleep in an unexpected place — a doorway, a yard, a storage room — and left them to it. **Age 36 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 36 (686 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer. **Age 37 (687 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 37 (687 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 38 (688 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 38 (688 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 39 (689 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 39 (689 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 40 (690 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands. **Age 40 (690 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 41 (691 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting. **Age 41 (691 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 42 (692 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency. **Age 42 (692 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 42 (692 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Beren Dalkin's illegitimate child born (female, Beren Dalkin age 16) **Age 43 (693 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. name: Morak Dalkin

narrative: **Age 18 (674 TR)** — He tried something on a whim and found he was good at it. Not exceptional — just good. It surprised him.
**Age 19 (675 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.

**Age 20 (676 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 21 (677 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 22 (678 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
**Age 23 (679 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 24 (680 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.

**Age 25 (681 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.

**Age 26 (682 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.

**Age 27 (683 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.

**Age 28 (684 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 29 (685 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.

**Age 30 (686 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 31 (687 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Wela Yorman (unguilded, age 27)
**Age 31 (687 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 20
**Age 32 (688 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 32 (688 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 33 (689 TR)** — He bought something he had no use for. He did not regret it. He kept it.
**Age 34 (690 TR)** — Rats got into the stores. He dealt with it. It took three weeks longer than it should have.
**Age 35 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (691 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 36 (692 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 36 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 37 (693 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 37 (693 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 38 (694 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Loran Valson (unguilded)
**Age 38 (694 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Caris Boring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Caris Boring born (female)
**Age 39 (695 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Kavan Arsen
**Age 39 (695 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Halden Boring came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Halden Boring born (male)
**Age 40 (696 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 40 (696 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 41 (697 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the lower leg or shin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 41 (697 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Amara Boring. week 40 Amara Boring born (female)

**Age 42 (698 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.

**Age 42 (698 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — simple Celyn Boring born (female, simple-minded)
**Age 43 (699 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 43 (699 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 44 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (700 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.

**Age 45 (701 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Orin Wolman became a close friend
**Age 45 (701 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on.
**Age 46 (702 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.

**Age 46 (702 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 47 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (703 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 49 (705 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — Something got out — cattle, a goat, a pig — and had to be chased across a field he would rather not have crossed.
**Age 51 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (708 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 53 (709 TR)** — He stopped at a temple he had not intended to visit. He sat there for longer than made sense.
**Age 53 (709 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 4

**Age 54 (710 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 54 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Kavan Arsen became an enemy
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — His child — the one he had feared for — had made something of themselves. Not what he had once imagined, but real. He felt something loosen in his chest.
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — Caris Boring left home to make his own way. Caris Boring left home (age 18)
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — His son Halden Boring married Delyn Marson. Halden Boring married Delyn Marson (unguilded, age 16)
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Halden Boring's child born (male, Halden Boring age 17)
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — He gave a traveller confident directions and realised immediately after that he had them backwards. He could not find the man again.
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — Halden Boring left home to make his own way. Halden Boring left home (age 18)
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 59 (715 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 59 (715 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Celyn Boring's illegitimate child born (female, Celyn Boring age 17)
**Age 60 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (717 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 61 (717 TR)** — Wela Yorman died. They had been married 30 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Wela Yorman died (approx. age 57)

**Age 62 (718 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 62 (718 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 62 (718 TR)** — His daughter Caris Boring married Erek Orsen. Caris Boring married Erek Orsen (peasant, age 25)
**Age 62 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 62 (718 TR)** — His daughter Amara Boring married Varen Valing. Amara Boring married Varen Valing (unguilded, age 20)
**Age 63 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 64 (720 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 64 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 64 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (674 TR)** — He tried something on a whim and found he was good at it. Not exceptional — just good. It surprised him. **Age 19 (675 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 20 (676 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 21 (677 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold. **Age 22 (678 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit. **Age 23 (679 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 24 (680 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home. **Age 25 (681 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 26 (682 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 27 (683 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 28 (684 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 29 (685 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it. **Age 30 (686 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear. **Age 31 (687 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Wela Yorman (unguilded, age 27) **Age 31 (687 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 20 **Age 32 (688 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 32 (688 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms. **Age 37 (693 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. **Age 23 (685 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Andar Branson — survived. week 40 Andar Branson born (female)

**Age 24 (686 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 24 (686 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 25 (687 TR)** — Something he expected to take an hour took most of the day. He was not angry about it. It was simply what it was.
**Age 25 (687 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 26 (688 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it.
**Age 26 (688 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 27 (689 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 28 (690 TR)** — He was caught in a small lie — not an important one, but it was noticed and the noticing was worse than the lie.

**Age 28 (690 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.

**Age 29 (691 TR)** — His wife bore twins — Inda Branson and Ida Branson. The labour was long and hard; she was weakened for months. Both children lived. twins Inda Branson and Ida Branson born (twins)
**Age 30 (692 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 30 (692 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 17
**Age 31 (693 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 31 (693 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 32 (694 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

**Age 32 (694 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.

**Age 33 (695 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 33 (695 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 34 (696 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 34 (696 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 30
**Age 35 (697 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 35 (697 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Leda Branson born (female)
**Age 36 (698 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Belyn Lanson
**Age 37 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (700 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 38 (700 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 39 (701 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 39 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 40 (702 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 40 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Iric Wolsen
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — Andar Branson left home to make his own way. Andar Branson left home (age 18)

**Age 42 (704 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 42 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (705 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 43 (705 TR)** — Andar Branson died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Andar Branson died (age 20)
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 45 (707 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 45 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 47 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (709 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 48 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.

**Age 48 (710 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 48 (710 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Inda Branson's illegitimate child born (female, Inda Branson age 19)
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Ida Branson's illegitimate child born (female, Ida Branson age 20)
**Age 50 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — His dog died of old age. It was the right time. He buried it well and was quiet for a few days.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — Ida Branson left home to make his own way. Ida Branson left home (age 22)
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Leda Branson's illegitimate child born (female, Leda Branson age 16)
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — His daughter Ida Branson married Koral Yoring. Ida Branson married Koral Yoring (unguilded, age 30)
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Ida Branson's child born (male, Ida Branson age 26)
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — His daughter Leda Branson married Aldric Karson. Leda Branson married Aldric Karson (unguilded, age 26)
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — He reached out, or they did. The thing between them was old enough to look smaller than it had. Reconciled with Iric Wolsen
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — Leda Branson left home to make his own way. Leda Branson left home (age 21)
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — His daughter Inda Branson married Halden Haling. Inda Branson married Halden Haling (unguilded, age 33)
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — Inda Branson left home to make his own way. Inda Branson left home (age 28)
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (680 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 19 (681 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 20 (682 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was. **Age 21 (683 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Dara Laning (unguilded, age 19) **Age 22 (684 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Osric Taramin (noble) **Age 23 (685 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 23 (685 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Andar Branson — survived. week 40 Andar Branson born (female) **Age 24 (686 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation. **Age 24 (686 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 25 (687 TR)** — Something he expected to take an hour took most of the day. He was not angry about it. It was simply what it was. **Age 25 (687 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 26 (688 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it. **Age 26 (688 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 27 (689 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 28 (690 TR)** — He was caught in a small lie — not an important one, but it was noticed and the noticing was worse than the lie. **Age 28 (690 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 29 (691 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 29 (691 TR)** — His wife bore twins — Inda Branson and Ida Branson. The labour was long and hard; she was weakened for months. Both children lived. twins Inda Branson and Ida Branson born (twins) **Age 30 (692 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean. **Age 30 (692 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 17 **Age 31 (693 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill. **Age 31 (693 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years. **Age 32 (694 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 32 (694 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth.
