name: Fenric Branson

narrative: **Age 18 (686 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.

**Age 19 (687 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 20 (688 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 21 (689 TR)** — He woke to his wife's screaming. The cradle was empty. The window was open. There was no ransom demand — there never is, with this kind. He spent three years looking. Eventually he stopped looking, which was not the same as accepting what had happened.
**Age 22 (690 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying.
**Age 23 (691 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 24 (692 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 25 (693 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Tera Horsen (peasant, age 23)
**Age 25 (693 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 26 (694 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 26 (694 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 23
**Age 27 (695 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care.
**Age 28 (696 TR)** — He attempted something he was not qualified for and did it well. He put this down to luck and moved on.
**Age 28 (696 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 29 (697 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 10
**Age 30 (698 TR)** — He lay awake one night with nothing in particular on his mind. He got up before dawn and walked until it was light.
**Age 30 (698 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Kavan Aling. week 40 Kavan Aling born (male)

**Age 31 (699 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 31 (699 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 32 (700 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 33 (701 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 34 (702 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this.
**Age 34 (702 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Ulra Aling born (female)
**Age 35 (703 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 35 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (704 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 36 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (705 TR)** — A stranger pulled him aside on the road and told him not to take the left fork. He took the right. Nothing happened.
**Age 37 (705 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 38 (706 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Talith Garing died
**Age 38 (706 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 39 (707 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 40 (708 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 40 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 41 (709 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 41 (709 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 10
**Age 42 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (710 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Vorin Aling came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Vorin Aling born (male)
**Age 42 (710 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Vorin Aling died (age 0)

**Age 43 (711 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 43 (711 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 19
**Age 44 (712 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 44 (712 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Beren Aling — survived. week 40 Beren Aling born (male)

**Age 45 (713 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 45 (713 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 46 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (715 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 48 (716 TR)** — He got turned around on a road he had walked a hundred times. He was an hour off course before he admitted it.

**Age 48 (716 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 48 (716 TR)** — Kavan Aling left home to make his own way. Kavan Aling left home (age 18)
**Age 48 (716 TR)** — Beren Aling died young — a child still. Beren Aling died (age 4)
**Age 49 (717 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.

**Age 50 (718 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 50 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (719 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 51 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (720 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.

**Age 52 (720 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (686 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 19 (687 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 20 (688 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 21 (689 TR)** — He woke to his wife's screaming. The cradle was empty. The window was open. There was no ransom demand — there never is, with this kind. He spent three years looking. Eventually he stopped looking, which was not the same as accepting what had happened. **Age 22 (690 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying. **Age 23 (691 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 24 (692 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before. **Age 25 (693 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Tera Horsen (peasant, age 23) **Age 25 (693 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 26 (694 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 26 (694 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 23 **Age 27 (695 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care. **Age 28 (696 TR)** — He attempted something he was not qualified for and did it well. He put this down to luck and moved on. **Age 28 (696 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 29 (697 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 10 **Age 30 (698 TR)** — He lay awake one night with nothing in particular on his mind. He got up before dawn and walked until it was light. **Age 30 (698 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Kavan Aling. week 40 Kavan Aling born (male) **Age 31 (699 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood. **Age 31 (699 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 32 (700 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 33 (701 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold. **Age 34 (702 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this. **Age 34 (702 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Ulra Aling born (female) **Age 35 (703 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough. **Age 35 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 36 (704 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home. **Age 36 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 37 (705 TR)** — A stranger pulled him aside on the road and told him not to take the left fork. He took the right. Nothing happened. **Age 37 (705 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered. **Age 38 (706 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Talith Garing died **Age 38 (706 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong. **Age 39 (707 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 40 (708 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours. **Age 40 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (709 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately. **Age 41 (709 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 10 **Age 42 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 42 (710 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Vorin Aling came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Vorin Aling born (male) **Age 42 (710 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Vorin Aling died (age 0) **Age 43 (711 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was. **Age 43 (711 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 19 **Age 44 (712 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 44 (712 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Beren Aling — survived. week 40 Beren Aling born (male) **Age 45 (713 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency. **Age 45 (713 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 46 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 46 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 47 (715 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 48 (716 TR)** — He got turned around on a road he had walked a hundred times. He was an hour off course before he admitted it. **Age 48 (716 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 48 (716 TR)** — Kavan Aling left home to make his own way. Kavan Aling left home (age 18) **Age 48 (716 TR)** — Beren Aling died young — a child still. Beren Aling died (age 4) **Age 49 (717 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 50 (718 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 50 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 51 (719 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 51 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 52 (720 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues. **Age 52 (720 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
name: Wulf Aling

narrative: **Age 18 (692 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 19 (693 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 20 (694 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 21 (695 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 22 (696 TR)** — His best tool — the one he had used for years — broke without warning at the worst possible moment.
**Age 23 (697 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.

**Age 24 (698 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 25 (699 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 26 (700 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 27 (701 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Geda Soring (unguilded, age 24)

**Age 27 (701 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 28 (702 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 29 (703 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; he took the risk.
**Age 29 (703 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 30 (704 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 30 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (706 TR)** — He went somewhere that was muddy and came back in a state that was worse than he had anticipated.
**Age 32 (706 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 33 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 33 (707 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Talar Morsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Talar Morsen born (male)
**Age 34 (708 TR)** — He saw two men fighting in the street. He watched to see if he should intervene. He did not intervene. He thought about this later.
**Age 34 (708 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 35 (709 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.
**Age 35 (709 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.

**Age 36 (710 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 36 (710 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 37 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 38 (712 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 38 (712 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 39 (713 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the knee. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 40 (714 TR)** — He noticed that an old ache started before rain. He became reliable at predicting the weather and this annoyed him.
**Age 41 (715 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

**Age 41 (715 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.

**Age 42 (716 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 42 (716 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 5

**Age 43 (717 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 43 (717 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Orin Morsen born (male)
**Age 44 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (718 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 45 (719 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 45 (719 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 46 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

name: Orlan Morsen

narrative: **Age 18 (698 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 19 (699 TR)** — Someone was unexpectedly kind to him at a moment when he had not expected it. He was more affected by this than he showed.
**Age 20 (700 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 21 (701 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 22 (702 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. **Age 24 (704 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 24 (704 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 25 (705 TR)** — A swarm of bees settled on his fence post. The beekeeper said it was good luck. He was not reassured.
**Age 25 (705 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 25 (705 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Vala Yorman died (age 2)
**Age 27 (707 TR)** — He lay awake one night with nothing in particular on his mind. He got up before dawn and walked until it was light.
**Age 27 (707 TR)** — Elva Karman died before they had been long married. He had not had time to learn the shape of the loss. Elva Karman died (approx. age 21)
**Age 28 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 31 (711 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 31 (711 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Belyn Yorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Belyn Yorman born (female)

**Age 32 (712 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 16

**Age 34 (714 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 34 (714 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Elda Yorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Elda Yorman born (female)

**Age 35 (715 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 35 (715 TR)** — Belyn Yorman died young — a child still. Belyn Yorman died (age 4)
**Age 36 (716 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 36 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (717 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 37 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 38 (718 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. **Age 38 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (719 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Wulf Aring

**Age 40 (720 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 40 (720 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (698 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 19 (699 TR)** — Someone was unexpectedly kind to him at a moment when he had not expected it. He was more affected by this than he showed. **Age 20 (700 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands. **Age 21 (701 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 22 (702 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear. **Age 23 (703 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Elva Karman (peasant, age 17) **Age 23 (703 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Vala Yorman born (female) **Age 24 (704 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 24 (704 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later. **Age 25 (705 TR)** — A swarm of bees settled on his fence post. The beekeeper said it was good luck. He was not reassured. **Age 25 (705 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 25 (705 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Vala Yorman died (age 2) **Age 27 (707 TR)** — He lay awake one night with nothing in particular on his mind. He got up before dawn and walked until it was light. **Age 27 (707 TR)** — Elva Karman died before they had been long married. He had not had time to learn the shape of the loss. Elva Karman died (approx. age 21) **Age 28 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 28 (708 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 29 (709 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 30 (710 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 31 (711 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks. **Age 31 (711 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Belyn Yorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Belyn Yorman born (female) **Age 32 (712 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the abdomen. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1). **Age 32 (712 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 16 **Age 33 (713 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit. **Age 34 (714 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours. **Age 34 (714 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Elda Yorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Elda Yorman born (female) **Age 35 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 35 (715 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 35 (715 TR)** — Belyn Yorman died young — a child still. Belyn Yorman died (age 4) **Age 36 (716 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 36 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 37 (717 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that. **Age 37 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 38 (718 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation. **Age 38 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 39 (719 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Wulf Aring **Age 40 (720 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was. **Age 40 (720 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
name: Orin Yorman

narrative: **Age 18 (704 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 19 (705 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 20 (706 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 21 (707 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.

**Age 22 (708 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 23 (709 TR)** — A bird built its nest in the eaves above his door. He left it alone all season.
**Age 24 (710 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.

**Age 25 (711 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 26 (712 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.

**Age 27 (713 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 28 (714 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 29 (715 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 30 (716 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 31 (717 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 32 (718 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Teda Parson (unguilded, age 29)
**Age 33 (719 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 34 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 34 (720 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Antor Vorsen born (male)

name: Kelan Vorsen

narrative: **Age 18 (710 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 19 (711 TR)** — Something got out — cattle, a goat, a pig — and had to be chased across a field he would rather not have crossed.
**Age 20 (712 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Welith Doring (unguilded, age 16)
**Age 20 (712 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Erek Taring born (male)
**Age 20 (712 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Erek Taring died (age 0)
**Age 21 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 22 (714 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Cade Warson died
**Age 22 (714 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 23 (715 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 24 (716 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 24 (716 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 16

**Age 25 (717 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.
**Age 25 (717 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.

**Age 26 (718 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 26 (718 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Durst Taring. week 40 Durst Taring born (male)
**Age 27 (719 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.

**Age 28 (720 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 28 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
