narrative: **Age 18 (664 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 19 (665 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 20 (666 TR)** — He woke up one morning certain of something he had been unable to decide. He never understood why sleep had solved it.

**Age 21 (667 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.

**Age 22 (668 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence.
**Age 23 (669 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 24 (670 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; he took the risk.
**Age 25 (671 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.

**Age 26 (672 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 27 (673 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 28 (674 TR)** — He said exactly the right thing at the right moment. He would not have been able to do it again.

**Age 29 (675 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.

**Age 30 (676 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.

**Age 32 (678 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 33 (679 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.

**Age 34 (680 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 35 (681 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 36 (682 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 37 (683 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end. It changed how he thought about faith.
**Age 38 (684 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 39 (685 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (686 TR)** — He stopped at a temple he had not intended to visit. He sat there for longer than made sense.
**Age 41 (687 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 42 (688 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 43 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (690 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Rorik Borsen
**Age 45 (691 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 46 (692 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 47 (693 TR)** — He was clearly overcharged and paid without arguing. He walked away thinking about the dozen things he could have said.
**Age 48 (694 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 49 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (696 TR)** — He found his first grey hair. He pulled it out, then felt foolish, then stopped caring.
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 52 (698 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 53 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 55 (701 TR)** — An old soldier at a fire told a story about a battle he had no business being at. The detail was too precise to be invented.
**Age 56 (702 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 57 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 58 (704 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 59 (705 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
**Age 60 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (707 TR)** — He made a promise on impulse and then had to keep it against all convenience. He was glad he did.
**Age 62 (708 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying.
**Age 63 (709 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it.
**Age 64 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 65 (711 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 66 (712 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.

**Age 67 (713 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant.
**Age 68 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 69 (715 TR)** — The first real cold came and he realised summer was over. He stood in it for a moment before going inside.
**Age 70 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 71 (717 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing.

**Age 72 (718 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.

**Age 73 (719 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 74 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (664 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands. **Age 19 (665 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 20 (666 TR)** — He woke up one morning certain of something he had been unable to decide. He never understood why sleep had solved it. **Age 21 (667 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel. **Age 22 (668 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence. **Age 23 (669 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that. **Age 24 (670 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; he took the risk. **Age 25 (671 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 26 (672 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing. **Age 27 (673 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it. **Age 28 (674 TR)** — He said exactly the right thing at the right moment. He would not have been able to do it again. **Age 29 (675 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 30 (676 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 32 (678 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like. **Age 33 (679 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 34 (680 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear. **Age 35 (681 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it. **Age 36 (682 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public. **Age 37 (683 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end. It changed how he thought about faith. **Age 38 (684 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 39 (685 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 40 (686 TR)** — He stopped at a temple he had not intended to visit. He sat there for longer than made sense. **Age 41 (687 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 42 (688 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours. **Age 43 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 44 (690 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Rorik Borsen **Age 45 (691 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 46 (692 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected. **Age 47 (693 TR)** — He was clearly overcharged and paid without arguing. He walked away thinking about the dozen things he could have said. **Age 48 (694 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 49 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 50 (696 TR)** — He found his first grey hair. He pulled it out, then felt foolish, then stopped caring. **Age 51 (697 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 52 (698 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 53 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (700 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 55 (701 TR)** — An old soldier at a fire told a story about a battle he had no business being at. The detail was too precise to be invented. **Age 56 (702 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 57 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (704 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation. **Age 59 (705 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1). **Age 60 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 61 (707 TR)** — He made a promise on impulse and then had to keep it against all convenience. He was glad he did. **Age 62 (708 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying. **Age 63 (709 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it. **Age 64 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 65 (711 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting. **Age 66 (712 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 67 (713 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant. **Age 68 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 69 (715 TR)** — The first real cold came and he realised summer was over. He stood in it for a moment before going inside. **Age 70 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 71 (717 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing. **Age 72 (718 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 73 (719 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms. **Age 74 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
name: Yoric Horkin

narrative: **Age 18 (670 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 19 (671 TR)** — He learned how to make something — a stew, a bread, a sauce — that others started asking for.
**Age 20 (672 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.

**Age 21 (673 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 22 (674 TR)** — He hung the washing out. It rained within the hour. He took it in again. He did not mention this to anyone.
**Age 23 (675 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 24 (676 TR)** — He heard something outside in the night, got up to investigate, and found nothing. He went back to bed and lay awake for an hour.
**Age 25 (677 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 26 (678 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Geda Garman (unguilded, age 21)

**Age 27 (679 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 27 (679 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 28 (680 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 29 (681 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 29 (681 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Annis Aring. week 40 Annis Aring born (female)
**Age 30 (682 TR)** — He sat staring at the grain of a table or a wall and found a face or an animal in the pattern of the wood. He sat there longer than was useful.
**Age 30 (682 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.

**Age 31 (683 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 31 (683 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Horic Aring born (male)
**Age 32 (684 TR)** — He said something that made someone laugh who had not been laughing. He had not planned it. He was glad of it.
**Age 32 (684 TR)** — Geda Garman died. They had been married 6 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Geda Garman died (approx. age 27)
**Age 33 (685 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 34 (686 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 34 (686 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 35 (687 TR)** — He took a wrong turn and came out somewhere he had never been. The village had a good bakehouse and the wrong road became one he always took.
**Age 35 (687 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 37 (689 TR)** — He stayed up far later than he meant to, watching the fire go down, or the moon move, or nothing in particular.
**Age 37 (689 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Annis Aring fostered (age 8)

**Age 38 (690 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 39 (691 TR)** — He ruined something he had spent a long time making. He did not speak for the rest of that day.
**Age 40 (692 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 41 (693 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 42 (694 TR)** — A door he expected to open did not. He pulled, then pushed, then pulled again, then read the situation correctly. There were witnesses.
**Age 42 (694 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 43 (695 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 43 (695 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — He received news he expected to be devastated by and found he was oddly calm. He was not sure whether to be relieved or worried by this.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.

**Age 45 (697 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 45 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (697 TR)** — Horic Aring was nearly grown when he died. Horic Aring died (age 14)
**Age 46 (698 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 46 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (699 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 47 (699 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 49 (701 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 50 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Joran Dorsen (unguilded)
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — His daughter Annis Aring married Osric Salman. Annis Aring married Osric Salman (peasant, age 25)
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Annis Aring's child born (female, Annis Aring age 22)
**Age 52 (704 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Uther Halsen
**Age 52 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (705 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 55 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (708 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Rorik Sorman died
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — A crow appeared every morning for a month on the same post. He began to look for it. One day it was gone.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (712 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 61 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 62 (714 TR)** — His back gave out at the worst possible time. He spent three days walking like an old man and pretending otherwise.
**Age 63 (715 TR)** — He went to a place he had been many times and found the building was not there. Something else was.
**Age 63 (715 TR)** — His daughter Annis Aring remarried. Annis Aring remarried Cade Bransen (peasant, age 37)

**Age 64 (716 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 64 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (717 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 65 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 66 (718 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it.
**Age 66 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (719 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Voran Marsen
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 43 (695 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 43 (695 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 44 (696 TR)** — He received news he expected to be devastated by and found he was oddly calm. He was not sure whether to be relieved or worried by this. **Age 44 (696 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 45 (697 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 45 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 45 (697 TR)** — Horic Aring was nearly grown when he died. Horic Aring died (age 14) **Age 46 (698 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 46 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (699 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 47 (699 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 48 (700 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it. **Age 48 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 49 (701 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like. **Age 50 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 50 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 51 (703 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Joran Dorsen (unguilded) **Age 51 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 51 (703 TR)** — His daughter Annis Aring married Osric Salman. Annis Aring married Osric Salman (peasant, age 25) **Age 51 (703 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Annis Aring's child born (female, Annis Aring age 22) **Age 52 (704 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Uther Halsen **Age 52 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 53 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 53 (705 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 54 (706 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it. **Age 54 (706 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 55 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (708 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Rorik Sorman died **Age 57 (709 TR)** — A crow appeared every morning for a month on the same post. He began to look for it. One day it was gone. **Age 57 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 58 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 58 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 58 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 59 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 59 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 60 (712 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected. **Age 61 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 62 (714 TR)** — His back gave out at the worst possible time. He spent three days walking like an old man and pretending otherwise. **Age 63 (715 TR)** — He went to a place he had been many times and found the building was not there. Something else was. **Age 63 (715 TR)** — His daughter Annis Aring remarried. Annis Aring remarried Cade Bransen (peasant, age 37) **Age 64 (716 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 64 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 65 (717 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 65 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 66 (718 TR)** — People started asking his opinion before acting. He noticed when this began. He did not comment on it. **Age 66 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 67 (719 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Voran Marsen **Age 68 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 68 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
name: Halden Aring

narrative: **Age 18 (676 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this.
**Age 19 (677 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 20 (678 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.

**Age 21 (679 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 22 (680 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Ceris Marson (unguilded, age 16)

**Age 23 (681 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 23 (681 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Loran Borsen. week 40 Loran Borsen born (male)
**Age 24 (682 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 24 (682 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 26
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — He completed a day's work and stood back and thought: yes, that is done properly.
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 26 (684 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the lower leg or shin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 26 (684 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Nora Borsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Nora Borsen born (female)
**Age 27 (685 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 27 (685 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Halla Borsen born (female)

**Age 28 (686 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 28 (686 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.

**Age 29 (687 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 29 (687 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30 (688 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 30 (688 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Cador Borsen — survived. week 40 Cador Borsen born (male)
**Age 31 (689 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.

**Age 32 (690 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — Loran Borsen died young — a child still. Loran Borsen died (age 9)
**Age 33 (691 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 34 (692 TR)** — What he had kept hidden was hidden no longer. The consequences arrived.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.

**Age 36 (694 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 10
**Age 37 (695 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 37 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 38 (696 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 38 (696 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Garan Borsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Garan Borsen born (male)
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Vorin Borsen died (age 0)
**Age 41 (699 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 41 (699 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — His daughter Nora Borsen married Kavan Garsen. Nora Borsen married Kavan Garsen (peasant, age 16)
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — He noticed that an old ache started before rain. He became reliable at predicting the weather and this annoyed him.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — Cador Borsen was nearly grown when he died. Cador Borsen died (age 13)
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.

**Age 44 (702 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Nora Borsen's child born (female, Nora Borsen age 18)
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — He was certain of something and wrong. He argued his position at length before the evidence became impossible to ignore.
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — Halla Borsen left home to make his own way. Halla Borsen left home (age 18)

**Age 46 (704 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 46 (704 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Garan Borsen fostered (age 8)
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — Nora Borsen left home to make his own way. Nora Borsen left home (age 21)
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — His son Cavan Borsen married Tara Valkin. Cavan Borsen married Tara Valkin (peasant, age 16)
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — Cavan Borsen left home to make his own way. Cavan Borsen left home (age 19)

**Age 49 (707 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — Garan Borsen died young — a child still. Garan Borsen died (age 11)
**Age 50 (708 TR)** — An ordinary market day stayed with him for years. He could not say what had been notable about it.
**Age 50 (708 TR)** — His daughter Halla Borsen married Edric Walman. Halla Borsen married Edric Walman (unguilded, age 26)
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — A fox sat at the edge of the field and watched him work. Neither moved for a long time.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — He went somewhere that was muddy and came back in a state that was worse than he had anticipated.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — Halla Borsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Halla Borsen's face. Halla Borsen's child died (age 0)
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — His son Erek Borsen married Andar Torman. Erek Borsen married Andar Torman (unguilded, age 19)
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — Erek Borsen left home to make his own way. Erek Borsen left home (age 20)
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — Something moved at the edge of his vision and was gone before he turned his head. He was never sure what it was.
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.

**Age 54 (712 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — Lothar Borsen was nearly grown when he died. Lothar Borsen died (age 12)
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — Erek Borsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Erek Borsen's face. Erek Borsen's child died (age 0)
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — Erek Borsen died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Erek Borsen died (age 25)
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — His daughter Nora Borsen remarried. Nora Borsen remarried Wulf Korson (unguilded, age 38)
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — Something he expected to take an hour took most of the day. He was not angry about it. It was simply what it was.
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — His son Mael Borsen married Teda Haring. Mael Borsen married Teda Haring (unguilded, age 16)
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (719 TR)** — Mael Borsen left home to make his own way. Mael Borsen left home (age 22)
**Age 62 (720 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Gerin (clergy)

**Age 62 (720 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.

He argued his position at length before the evidence became impossible to ignore. **Age 45 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 45 (703 TR)** — Halla Borsen left home to make his own way. Halla Borsen left home (age 18) **Age 46 (704 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 46 (704 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Garan Borsen fostered (age 8) **Age 47 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (705 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 47 (705 TR)** — Nora Borsen left home to make his own way. Nora Borsen left home (age 21) **Age 48 (706 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before. **Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 48 (706 TR)** — His son Cavan Borsen married Tara Valkin. Cavan Borsen married Tara Valkin (peasant, age 16) **Age 48 (706 TR)** — Cavan Borsen left home to make his own way. Cavan Borsen left home (age 19) **Age 49 (707 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop. **Age 49 (707 TR)** — Garan Borsen died young — a child still. Garan Borsen died (age 11) **Age 50 (708 TR)** — An ordinary market day stayed with him for years. He could not say what had been notable about it. **Age 50 (708 TR)** — His daughter Halla Borsen married Edric Walman. Halla Borsen married Edric Walman (unguilded, age 26) **Age 51 (709 TR)** — A fox sat at the edge of the field and watched him work. Neither moved for a long time. **Age 51 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (710 TR)** — He went somewhere that was muddy and came back in a state that was worse than he had anticipated. **Age 52 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 52 (710 TR)** — Halla Borsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Halla Borsen's face. Halla Borsen's child died (age 0) **Age 52 (710 TR)** — His son Erek Borsen married Andar Torman. Erek Borsen married Andar Torman (unguilded, age 19) **Age 52 (710 TR)** — Erek Borsen left home to make his own way. Erek Borsen left home (age 20) **Age 53 (711 TR)** — Something moved at the edge of his vision and was gone before he turned his head. He was never sure what it was. **Age 53 (711 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 54 (712 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it. **Age 54 (712 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 54 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 55 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 55 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 55 (713 TR)** — Lothar Borsen was nearly grown when he died. Lothar Borsen died (age 12) **Age 56 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (714 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong. **Age 56 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (714 TR)** — Erek Borsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Erek Borsen's face. Erek Borsen's child died (age 0) **Age 57 (715 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 57 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 57 (715 TR)** — Erek Borsen died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Erek Borsen died (age 25) **Age 58 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 58 (716 TR)** — His daughter Nora Borsen remarried. Nora Borsen remarried Wulf Korson (unguilded, age 38) **Age 58 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 59 (717 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it. **Age 59 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 60 (718 TR)** — Something he expected to take an hour took most of the day. He was not angry about it. It was simply what it was. **Age 60 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 60 (718 TR)** — His son Mael Borsen married Teda Haring. Mael Borsen married Teda Haring (unguilded, age 16) **Age 60 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 61 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 61 (719 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 61 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 61 (719 TR)** — Mael Borsen left home to make his own way. Mael Borsen left home (age 22) **Age 62 (720 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Gerin (clergy) **Age 62 (720 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
name: Vorin Borsen

narrative: **Age 18 (682 TR)** — He called an important person by the wrong name in front of everyone. He thought about it for months.
**Age 19 (683 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 20 (684 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 21 (685 TR)** — He ate the best meal of his life at an unremarkable place, served by someone who did not seem aware of the achievement.

**Age 22 (686 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 23 (687 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 24 (688 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Yorin Erkin
**Age 25 (689 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 26 (690 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 27 (691 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the hip or groin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 28 (692 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 29 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 30 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (696 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 33 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 34 (698 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 35 (699 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 36 (700 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 37 (701 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 38 (702 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.
**Age 39 (703 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.

**Age 40 (704 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 41 (705 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.

**Age 42 (706 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 43 (707 TR)** — He sat alone watching a fire go down until there was nothing left but ash. He had not intended to sit that long.
**Age 44 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 45 (709 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.

**Age 46 (710 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 47 (711 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 48 (712 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 49 (713 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 50 (714 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 51 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 52 (716 TR)** — He did something he had been told as a child to ward off bad luck. He was aware of how it looked. He did it anyway.

**Age 53 (717 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 54 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (719 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 56 (720 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Aldric Harson died
