name: Jorin Wolsen

narrative: **Age 18 (688 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 19 (689 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.

**Age 20 (690 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 21 (691 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Tera Salsen (unguilded, age 16)
**Age 21 (691 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 22 (692 TR)** — He watched a true expert do something he had done himself for years. He understood the difference immediately.
**Age 22 (692 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 23 (693 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 23 (693 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 34
**Age 24 (694 TR)** — He went somewhere that was muddy and came back in a state that was worse than he had anticipated.
**Age 24 (694 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Laric Ersen born (male)

**Age 25 (695 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 25 (695 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9
**Age 26 (696 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 26 (696 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 27 (697 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 27 (697 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 28 (698 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 28 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 28 (698 TR)** — Laric Ersen died young — a child still. Laric Ersen died (age 4)
**Age 29 (699 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 29 (699 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Estra Ersen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Estra Ersen born (female)
**Age 30 (700 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this.
**Age 30 (700 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Joran Ersen. week 40 Joran Ersen born (male)
**Age 30 (700 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Joran Ersen died (age 0)
**Age 31 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 31 (701 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 32 (702 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 32 (702 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 33 (703 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right.
**Age 33 (703 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 34 (704 TR)** — He found a name scratched into stone or wood — old, weathered — and wondered about the person who had put it there.
**Age 34 (704 TR)** — His wife bore twins — Irda Ersen and Woran Ersen. The labour was long and hard; she was weakened for months. Both children lived. twins Irda Ersen and Woran Ersen born (twins)
**Age 35 (705 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Sirath Wainworth (merchant)
**Age 35 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 36 (706 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 37 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (707 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 38 (708 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 38 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (709 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Sirath Wainworth has died
**Age 40 (710 TR)** — A swarm of bees settled on his fence post. The beekeeper said it was good luck. He was not reassured.
**Age 40 (710 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 41 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 41 (711 TR)** — Estra Ersen was nearly grown when she died. Estra Ersen died (age 12)

**Age 42 (712 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 43 (713 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 43 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (714 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 44 (714 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 45 (715 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.

**Age 46 (716 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 46 (716 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 47 (717 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.

**Age 47 (717 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 48 (718 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 48 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (719 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Soran Corman
**Age 49 (719 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 50 (720 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Soran Corman

He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 21 (691 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Tera Salsen (unguilded, age 16) **Age 21 (691 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words. **Age 22 (692 TR)** — He watched a true expert do something he had done himself for years. He understood the difference immediately. **Age 22 (692 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 23 (693 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold. **Age 23 (693 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 34 **Age 24 (694 TR)** — He went somewhere that was muddy and came back in a state that was worse than he had anticipated. **Age 24 (694 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Laric Ersen born (male) **Age 25 (695 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn. **Age 25 (695 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9 **Age 26 (696 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home. **Age 26 (696 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 27 (697 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 27 (697 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 28 (698 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 28 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 28 (698 TR)** — Laric Ersen died young — a child still. Laric Ersen died (age 4) **Age 29 (699 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 29 (699 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Estra Ersen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Estra Ersen born (female) **Age 30 (700 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this. **Age 30 (700 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Joran Ersen. week 40 Joran Ersen born (male) **Age 30 (700 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Joran Ersen died (age 0) **Age 31 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 31 (701 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 32 (702 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected. **Age 32 (702 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 33 (703 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right. **Age 33 (703 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 34 (704 TR)** — He found a name scratched into stone or wood — old, weathered — and wondered about the person who had put it there. **Age 34 (704 TR)** — His wife bore twins — Irda Ersen and Woran Ersen. The labour was long and hard; she was weakened for months. Both children lived. twins Irda Ersen and Woran Ersen born (twins) **Age 35 (705 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Sirath Wainworth (merchant) **Age 35 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 36 (706 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation. **Age 37 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 37 (707 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 38 (708 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 38 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 39 (709 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Sirath Wainworth has died **Age 40 (710 TR)** — A swarm of bees settled on his fence post. The beekeeper said it was good luck. He was not reassured. **Age 40 (710 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 41 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 41 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 41 (711 TR)** — Estra Ersen was nearly grown when she died. Estra Ersen died (age 12) **Age 42 (712 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 43 (713 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 43 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 44 (714 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 44 (714 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong. **Age 45 (715 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 46 (716 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear. **Age 46 (716 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 47 (717 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues. **Age 47 (717 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 48 (718 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 48 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 49 (719 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Soran Corman **Age 49 (719 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 50 (720 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Soran Corman
name: Jorin Ersen

narrative: **Age 18 (694 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the upper arm that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 19 (695 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Xarin Coppergate (merchant)

**Age 20 (696 TR)** — The Peonian sister arrived before he had finished sending for her and left after dawn with the kind of professional calm that made everything that had happened in between feel almost ordinary. He does not know her name. He knows the child is alive.
**Age 21 (697 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.

**Age 22 (698 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.

**Age 23 (699 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 24 (700 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 25 (701 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.

**Age 26 (702 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 27 (703 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 28 (704 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.

**Age 30 (706 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 31 (707 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Welith Torman died
**Age 32 (708 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 33 (709 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Rorik Ering

**Age 34 (710 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 35 (711 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 36 (712 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
**Age 37 (713 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.

**Age 38 (714 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 39 (715 TR)** — He noticed the first sign that winter was ending — a sound, a smell, a plant pushing through — and felt unreasonably glad.
**Age 40 (716 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Rora Torson
**Age 41 (717 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 42 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 43 (719 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 44 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

name: Gotar Salsen

narrative: **Age 18 (700 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Arden Daling died
**Age 19 (701 TR)** — He tried something on a whim and found he was good at it. Not exceptional — just good. It surprised him.

**Age 20 (702 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 21 (703 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 22 (704 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 23 (705 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 24 (706 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Sirath Potter (artisan, age 17)
**Age 24 (706 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Beren Rorman born (male)
**Age 24 (706 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Beren Rorman died (age 0)
**Age 25 (707 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 25 (707 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Rodric Rorman. week 40 Rodric Rorman born (male)
**Age 26 (708 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Alura Coring (unguilded)
**Age 26 (708 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 27 (709 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 27 (709 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 28 (710 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 28 (710 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Edris Rorman — survived. week 40 Edris Rorman born (female)
**Age 29 (711 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 29 (711 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 31 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 31 (713 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 32 (714 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
**Age 32 (714 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Osric Rorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Osric Rorman born (male)

**Age 33 (715 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 34 (716 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 34 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 35 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 35 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 36 (718 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.

**Age 37 (719 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing.
**Age 37 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 38 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

name: Cavan Rorman

narrative: **Age 18 (706 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 20 (708 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Amara Karson (unguilded, age 16)
**Age 20 (708 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 21 (709 TR)** — He was asked to take sides in a dispute between two neighbours. He tried to remain neutral. Both sides felt he had betrayed them.
**Age 21 (709 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 15
**Age 22 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 22 (710 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 23 (711 TR)** — He gave a traveller confident directions and realised immediately after that he had them backwards. He could not find the man again.
**Age 23 (711 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Arnam Korman. week 40 Arnam Korman born (male)

**Age 24 (712 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 24 (712 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Ranulf Korman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Ranulf Korman born (male)

**Age 25 (713 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 25 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 26 (714 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 26 (714 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 9
**Age 26 (714 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Ranulf Korman died (age 2)
**Age 27 (715 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Helda Gonsen died
**Age 27 (715 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 26
**Age 27 (715 TR)** — Arnam Korman died young — a child still. Arnam Korman died (age 4)

**Age 28 (716 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 29 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 30 (718 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 30 (718 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.

**Age 31 (719 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.

**Age 31 (719 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 32 (720 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 32 (720 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Arwen Korman — survived. week 40 Arwen Korman born (female)

name: Orlan Korman

narrative: **Age 18 (712 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 19 (713 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

**Age 20 (714 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 21 (715 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Elda Lorkin (unguilded, age 16)
**Age 21 (715 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Elva Parman. week 40 Elva Parman born (female)
**Age 22 (716 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 22 (716 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 22 (716 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Elva Parman died (age 1)
**Age 23 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 23 (717 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — simple Lothar Parman born (male, simple-minded)
**Age 23 (717 TR)** — Lothar Parman died — the simple-minded child he had worried over for years. Lothar Parman died (age 0)
**Age 24 (718 TR)** — Something struck him as funny at completely the wrong moment. He was unable to stop laughing for far longer than the situation called for.
**Age 24 (718 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.

**Age 25 (719 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.

**Age 26 (720 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.

name: Idris Parman

narrative: **Age 18 (718 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 19 (719 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 20 (720 TR)** — He found a chair — in an inn, a garden, a hall — that suited him exactly. He went back whenever he could.

name: Talar Malman

narrative: **Age 18 (666 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 19 (667 TR)** — He completed a day's work and stood back and thought: yes, that is done properly.

**Age 20 (668 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 21 (669 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 22 (670 TR)** — He told a joke that landed perfectly. He had no idea where he had heard it. He told it at every opportunity for the next three years.
**Age 23 (671 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 24 (672 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 25 (673 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 26 (674 TR)** — He made something — a wooden figure, a knotted rope, a small carving — with no purpose in mind. He gave it away.
**Age 27 (675 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Soran Torkin
**Age 28 (676 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 29 (677 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 30 (678 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Wela Tarkin (unguilded, age 29)

**Age 31 (679 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 31 (679 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 16
**Age 32 (680 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right.
**Age 32 (680 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Halric Sorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Halric Sorman born (male)
**Age 33 (681 TR)** — He woke up one morning certain of something he had been unable to decide. He never understood why sleep had solved it.
**Age 33 (681 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 16
**Age 35 (683 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone.
**Age 35 (683 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 36 (684 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 36 (684 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 37 (685 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 37 (685 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 38 (686 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 38 (686 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9
**Age 39 (687 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 39 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (688 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands.
**Age 40 (688 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Kelan Sorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Kelan Sorman born (male)

**Age 41 (689 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 41 (689 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Orla Sorman — survived. week 40 Orla Sorman born (female)

**Age 42 (690 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 42 (690 TR)** — His wife's labour was difficult. She was weakened for months, and the birth aged them both.

**Age 43 (691 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 43 (691 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 44 (692 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the upper arm that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 44 (692 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 23
**Age 45 (693 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Vorin Morsen (unguilded)
**Age 45 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 48 (696 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 49 (697 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 49 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (698 TR)** — The years finally told. He moved more carefully, tired more quickly. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 50 (698 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 51 (699 TR)** — He helped a stranger carry something heavy for a mile and a half without being asked. They parted without exchanging names.

**Age 51 (699 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 51 (699 TR)** — His son Halric Sorman married Kela Morson. Halric Sorman married Kela Morson (unguilded, age 17)
**Age 52 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (701 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Orla Sorman fostered (age 12)
**Age 53 (701 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Halric Sorman's child born (female, Halric Sorman age 21)
**Age 54 (702 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.

**Age 54 (702 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 55 (703 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 55 (703 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 13

**Age 56 (704 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.
**Age 56 (704 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 56 (704 TR)** — Halric Sorman left home to make his own way. Halric Sorman left home (age 24)
**Age 57 (705 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 57 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (706 TR)** — He said something that made someone laugh who had not been laughing. He had not planned it. He was glad of it.
**Age 58 (706 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — Halric Sorman's child Yarath Sorman died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Halric Sorman's child died (age 6)
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — His son Kelan Sorman married Rarith Karkin. Kelan Sorman married Rarith Karkin (unguilded, age 17)
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (708 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 61 (709 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 61 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 61 (709 TR)** — Kelan Sorman left home to make his own way. Kelan Sorman left home (age 21)

**Age 62 (710 TR)** — The face at the edge of the market. He is almost certain. The same way of moving — unhurried, too unhurried, watching without watching. He keeps his hand on his purse and decides not to decide anything yet.
**Age 62 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (711 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 63 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 63 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 64 (712 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 64 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 65 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (713 TR)** — His daughter Orla Sorman married Pedar Garman. Orla Sorman married Pedar Garman (unguilded, age 29)

**Age 66 (714 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
**Age 66 (714 TR)** — Wela Tarkin died. They had been married 36 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Wela Tarkin died (approx. age 65)
**Age 67 (715 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Vorin Morsen has died
**Age 67 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 68 (716 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 68 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 69 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 70 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 70 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 71 (719 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 72 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

narrativeText: **Age 18 (666 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill. **Age 19 (667 TR)** — He completed a day's work and stood back and thought: yes, that is done properly. **Age 20 (668 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms. **Age 21 (669 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 22 (670 TR)** — He told a joke that landed perfectly. He had no idea where he had heard it. He told it at every opportunity for the next three years. **Age 23 (671 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out. **Age 24 (672 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 25 (673 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 26 (674 TR)** — He made something — a wooden figure, a knotted rope, a small carving — with no purpose in mind. He gave it away. **Age 27 (675 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Soran Torkin **Age 28 (676 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 29 (677 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 30 (678 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Wela Tarkin (unguilded, age 29) **Age 31 (679 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation. **Age 31 (679 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 16 **Age 32 (680 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right. **Age 32 (680 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Halric Sorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Halric Sorman born (male) **Age 33 (681 TR)** — He woke up one morning certain of something he had been unable to decide. He never understood why sleep had solved it. **Age 33 (681 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 16 **Age 35 (683 TR)** — He came out early enough to see something — frost on a branch, a cobweb with dew, light through smoke — that he never mentioned to anyone. **Age 35 (683 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words. **Age 36 (684 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that. **Age 36 (684 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 37 (685 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 37 (685 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 38 (686 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 38 (686 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 9 **Age 39 (687 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 39 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 40 (688 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands. **Age 40 (688 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Kelan Sorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Kelan Sorman born (male) **Age 41 (689 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing. **Age 41 (689 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Orla Sorman — survived. week 40 Orla Sorman born (female) **Age 42 (690 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop. **Age 42 (690 TR)** — His wife's labour was difficult. She was weakened for months, and the birth aged them both. **Age 43 (691 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like. **Age 43 (691 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 44 (692 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the upper arm that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 44 (692 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 23 **Age 45 (693 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Vorin Morsen (unguilded) **Age 45 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 46 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 47 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 48 (696 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately. **Age 49 (697 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean. **Age 49 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 50 (698 TR)** — The years finally told. He moved more carefully, tired more quickly. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 50 (698 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 51 (699 TR)** — He helped a stranger carry something heavy for a mile and a half without being asked. They parted without exchanging names. **Age 51 (699 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 51 (699 TR)** — His son Halric Sorman married Kela Morson. Halric Sorman married Kela Morson (unguilded, age 17) **Age 52 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 52 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 53 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 53 (701 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Orla Sorman fostered (age 12) **Age 53 (701 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Halric Sorman's child born (female, Halric Sorman age 21) **Age 54 (702 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 54 (702 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 55 (703 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 55 (703 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 13 **Age 56 (704 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can. **Age 56 (704 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 56 (704 TR)** — Halric Sorman left home to make his own way. Halric Sorman left home (age 24) **Age 57 (705 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 57 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 58 (706 TR)** — He said something that made someone laugh who had not been laughing. He had not planned it. He was glad of it. **Age 58 (706 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 59 (707 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands. **Age 59 (707 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 59 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 59 (707 TR)** — Halric Sorman's child Yarath Sorman died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them.
