name: Xarin Sorman

narrative: **Age 18 (672 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 19 (673 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.

**Age 20 (674 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 21 (675 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 22 (676 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.

**Age 23 (677 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 24 (678 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Estra Malsen (peasant, age 18)
**Age 25 (679 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 26 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 27 (681 TR)** — He was asked about a scar. The truth was undramatic. He told a better version. The better version has become the one he remembers.
**Age 28 (682 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 28 (682 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 29 (683 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Uther Yoring
**Age 29 (683 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 12
**Age 30 (684 TR)** — He kept waiting for the right moment to say something and the moment passed. The thing went unsaid.
**Age 30 (684 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Leira Garing came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Leira Garing born (female)

**Age 31 (685 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 31 (685 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 32 (686 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Hrolf Ering died
**Age 32 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 33 (687 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 34 (688 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 34 (688 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 35 (689 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 35 (689 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.

**Age 36 (690 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.

**Age 36 (690 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 37 (691 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the hip or groin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 37 (691 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.

**Age 38 (692 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 40 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 40 (694 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.

**Age 41 (695 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 41 (695 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Ida Garing born (female)
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Malik Garing came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Malik Garing born (male)
**Age 43 (697 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 43 (697 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 44 (698 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Yoric Kalkin
**Age 44 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 45 (699 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 45 (699 TR)** — Estra Malsen died. They had been married 21 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Estra Malsen died (approx. age 39)
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Yoric Kalkin has died
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — His daughter Leira Garing married Erek Marman. Leira Garing married Erek Marman (unguilded, age 17)

**Age 50 (704 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 50 (704 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.

**Age 52 (706 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 54 (708 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing.
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Leira Garing's child born (female, Leira Garing age 24)
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.

**Age 57 (711 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.

narrativeText: **Age 18 (672 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public. **Age 19 (673 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold. **Age 20 (674 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood. **Age 21 (675 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 22 (676 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home. **Age 23 (677 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear. **Age 24 (678 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Estra Malsen (peasant, age 18) **Age 25 (679 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it. **Age 26 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 27 (681 TR)** — He was asked about a scar. The truth was undramatic. He told a better version. The better version has become the one he remembers. **Age 28 (682 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill. **Age 28 (682 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later. **Age 29 (683 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Uther Yoring **Age 29 (683 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 12 **Age 30 (684 TR)** — He kept waiting for the right moment to say something and the moment passed. The thing went unsaid. **Age 30 (684 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Leira Garing came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Leira Garing born (female) **Age 31 (685 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 31 (685 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 32 (686 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Hrolf Ering died **Age 32 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 33 (687 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 34 (688 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 34 (688 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 35 (689 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 35 (689 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered. **Age 36 (690 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting. **Age 36 (690 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 37 (691 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the hip or groin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 37 (691 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years. **Age 38 (692 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms. **Age 39 (693 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 39 (693 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 40 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 40 (694 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 41 (695 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency. **Age 41 (695 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Ida Garing born (female) **Age 42 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 42 (696 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Malik Garing came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Malik Garing born (male) **Age 43 (697 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 43 (697 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 44 (698 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Yoric Kalkin **Age 44 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 45 (699 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 45 (699 TR)** — Estra Malsen died. They had been married 21 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Estra Malsen died (approx. age 39) **Age 46 (700 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Yoric Kalkin has died **Age 47 (701 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 48 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (702 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 49 (703 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 49 (703 TR)** — His daughter Leira Garing married Erek Marman. Leira Garing married Erek Marman (unguilded, age 17) **Age 50 (704 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop. **Age 50 (704 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 51 (705 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 51 (705 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 52 (706 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 53 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 54 (708 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing. **Age 54 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (708 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Leira Garing's child born (female, Leira Garing age 24) **Age 55 (709 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 55 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (710 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 57 (711 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record. **Age 57 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 57 (711 TR)** — Leira Garing's child Annis Garing died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Leira Garing's child died (age 3) **Age 58 (712 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 58 (712 TR)** — His daughter Ida Garing married Davin Koring. **Age 21 (681 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.

**Age 22 (682 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 22 (682 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 23 (683 TR)** — He watched a true expert do something he had done himself for years. He understood the difference immediately.
**Age 23 (683 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 24 (684 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning.
**Age 24 (684 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 25 (685 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 25 (685 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 26 (686 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 26 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 27 (687 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 27 (687 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.

**Age 28 (688 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 28 (688 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 29 (689 TR)** — He noticed a scar on someone that told a story he was not going to be told. He did not ask.
**Age 29 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 31 (691 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Levan Lanman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Levan Lanman born (male)

**Age 32 (692 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
**Age 33 (693 TR)** — He got caught in the rain far from shelter. By the time he gave up and stood still, he was already soaked. He let it finish.
**Age 33 (693 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 8
**Age 34 (694 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.

narrativeText: **Age 18 (678 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him. **Age 19 (679 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Reda Malson (unguilded, age 16) **Age 19 (679 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Branna Lanman born (female) **Age 20 (680 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Gerin Corson (unguilded) **Age 21 (681 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 21 (681 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Neira Lanman. week 40 Neira Lanman born (female) **Age 22 (682 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues. **Age 22 (682 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 23 (683 TR)** — He watched a true expert do something he had done himself for years. He understood the difference immediately. **Age 23 (683 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 24 (684 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning. **Age 24 (684 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 25 (685 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 25 (685 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 26 (686 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 26 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 27 (687 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 27 (687 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 28 (688 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 28 (688 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words. **Age 29 (689 TR)** — He noticed a scar on someone that told a story he was not going to be told. He did not ask. **Age 29 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 30 (690 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Gerin Corson has died **Age 31 (691 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −1. **Age 31 (691 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Levan Lanman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Levan Lanman born (male) **Age 32 (692 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing. **Age 33 (693 TR)** — He got caught in the rain far from shelter. By the time he gave up and stood still, he was already soaked. He let it finish. **Age 33 (693 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 8 **Age 34 (694 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks. **Age 34 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 35 (695 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 35 (695 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 36 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 36 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 37 (697 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 37 (697 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later. **Age 38 (698 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is. **Age 39 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 39 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 40 (700 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues. **Age 41 (701 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 41 (701 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 42 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 42 (702 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered. **Age 42 (702 TR)** — His daughter Branna Lanman married Ronan Karson. Branna Lanman married Ronan Karson (peasant, age 24) **Age 42 (702 TR)** — Neira Lanman left home to make his own way. Neira Lanman left home (age 21) **Age 43 (703 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like. **Age 43 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 43 (703 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Branna Lanman's child born (male, Branna Lanman age 24) **Age 43 (703 TR)** — His daughter Neira Lanman married Rhoric Rorsen. Neira Lanman married Rhoric Rorsen (unguilded, age 22) **Age 44 (704 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 44 (704 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 14 **Age 45 (705 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew. **Age 45 (705 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 46 (706 TR)** — He found a coin in the road unlike any he had seen before. The merchant he showed it to grew oddly quiet. **Age 46 (706 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad. **Age 47 (707 TR)** — He made a throw — of a stone, a knife, a horseshoe — that was perfect and that he was never able to repeat. There were witnesses. **Age 47 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (707 TR)** — His son Levan Lanman married Orith Dorson. Levan Lanman married Orith Dorson (unguilded, age 16) **Age 48 (708 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 48 (708 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 49 (709 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 49 (709 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years. **Age 49 (709 TR)** — His son Evan Lanman married Karith Elkin. Evan Lanman married Karith Elkin (unguilded, age 26) **Age 50 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 50 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 50 (710 TR)** — Branna Lanman's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Branna Lanman's face. Branna Lanman's child died (age 0) **Age 50 (710 TR)** — Evan Lanman left home to make his own way. Evan Lanman left home (age 27) **Age 51 (711 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 51 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 52 (712 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting. **Age 52 (712 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 52 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 53 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 53 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (714 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook. **Age 54 (714 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 54 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (714 TR)** — Levan Lanman left home to make his own way. Levan Lanman left home (age 23) **Age 55 (715 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 55 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 55 (715 TR)** — Evan Lanman's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Evan Lanman's face. Evan Lanman's child died (age 2) **Age 56 (716 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it. **Age 56 (716 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 8 **Age 56 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 57 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (718 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood. **Age 58 (718 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 58 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 59 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 60 (720 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the lower leg or shin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1). **Age 60 (720 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 60 (720 TR)** — His daughter Branna Lanman remarried. Branna Lanman remarried Zoric Corkin (unguilded, age 43)
name: Alric Lanman

narrative: **Age 18 (684 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 19 (685 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 20 (686 TR)** — He sneezed at exactly the wrong moment during something solemn. He managed to recover with reasonable dignity.
**Age 21 (687 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Ceris Warkin (unguilded, age 19)
**Age 21 (687 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Durst Kaling came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Durst Kaling born (male)
**Age 22 (688 TR)** — He lost a ring — not valuable, but given to him by someone he no longer saw. He looked for it longer than he should have.
**Age 22 (688 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 23 (689 TR)** — He ate the best meal of his life at an unremarkable place, served by someone who did not seem aware of the achievement.
**Age 23 (689 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 24 (690 TR)** — He told everyone it would not rain. It rained heavily. He moved on without drawing attention to this.
**Age 25 (691 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 26 (692 TR)** — A door he expected to open did not. He pulled, then pushed, then pulled again, then read the situation correctly. There were witnesses.
**Age 26 (692 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Rara Kaling. week 40 Rara Kaling born (female)
**Age 27 (693 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 27 (693 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 4

**Age 28 (694 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
**Age 28 (694 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 14
**Age 29 (695 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the thigh. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 29 (695 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 30 (696 TR)** — His best tool — the one he had used for years — broke without warning at the worst possible moment.
**Age 30 (696 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Antor Kaling born (male)
**Age 30 (696 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Antor Kaling died (age 0)
**Age 31 (697 TR)** — A fox sat at the edge of the field and watched him work. Neither moved for a long time.
**Age 31 (697 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 32 (698 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 32 (698 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 17
**Age 33 (699 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Mira Eling (unguilded)
**Age 33 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 34 (700 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Beren Alsen died
**Age 35 (701 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 35 (701 TR)** — Ceris Warkin died. They had been married 14 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Ceris Warkin died (approx. age 33)
**Age 35 (701 TR)** — Rara Kaling died young — a child still. Rara Kaling died (age 9)
**Age 36 (702 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the back. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
**Age 37 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (704 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 39 (705 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 39 (705 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 39 (705 TR)** — Durst Kaling left home to make his own way. Durst Kaling left home (age 18)

**Age 40 (706 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 41 (707 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 41 (707 TR)** — Durst Kaling died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Durst Kaling died (age 20)
**Age 42 (708 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying.

**Age 43 (709 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.

**Age 44 (710 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 45 (711 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 46 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (714 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands.
**Age 49 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (716 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 51 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 52 (718 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 53 (719 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 53 (719 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 28

**Age 54 (720 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
**Age 54 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [unguilded → unguilded]

It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it. **Age 45 (711 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 46 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 47 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (714 TR)** — Debt caught up with him. Everything of value changed hands. **Age 49 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 50 (716 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 51 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 51 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (718 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing. **Age 53 (719 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 53 (719 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 28 **Age 54 (720 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing. **Age 54 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [unguilded → unguilded]
name: Theric Kaling

narrative: **Age 18 (713 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 19 (714 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Arith (clergy)

**Age 20 (715 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (716 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 22 (717 TR)** — She borrowed something meaning to return it. That was years ago. It has become hers through inertia.
**Age 23 (718 TR)** — She passed a room in the night and saw light underneath the door. She paused and then went on. She asked about it in the morning.
**Age 24 (719 TR)** — Dancing was expected of her from childhood. She was accomplished.
**Age 25 (720 TR)** — She saw something in the grain of a beam — a face, a figure, a landscape — and noted it and went on.

The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 19 (720 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.

It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
name: Sarath

narrative: **Age 18 (667 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.

**Age 19 (668 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.

**Age 20 (669 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 21 (670 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 22 (671 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 23 (672 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
**Age 24 (673 TR)** — Someone quoted a prophecy in her presence and looked at her meaningfully. She pretended not to notice.
**Age 25 (674 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.

**Age 26 (675 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 27 (676 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 28 (677 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Arven (clergy)
**Age 29 (678 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 30 (679 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 31 (680 TR)** — Something she had been looking for was where it had always been. She had simply not been looking at the right place.
**Age 32 (681 TR)** — She was up before the household and saw something small and perfect that was gone by the time anyone else was about.
**Age 33 (682 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 34 (683 TR)** — Patronage came with influence and obligations. She accepted both and managed the latter with care.
**Age 35 (684 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (685 TR)** — She walked to the shrine. The journey changed something — not in the way she expected, which was perhaps what it was supposed to do.
**Age 37 (686 TR)** — A named posting — chaplain to a Lady of Paladins chapter. The knights watched her carefully for the first month to see how she would take the work.
**Age 38 (687 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.

**Age 39 (688 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 40 (689 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (690 TR)** — Rumour or accusation — fair or unfair — put her standing at risk.
**Age 42 (691 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life.
**Age 43 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (693 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
**Age 45 (694 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 46 (695 TR)** — She misread someone's expression and said something she would not have said if she had read it right.
**Age 47 (696 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Levan
**Age 48 (697 TR)** — She had learned to be careful. This time she had not been careful enough. The look on the stranger's face told her everything.
**Age 49 (698 TR)** — She could not bring herself to go inside. She sat and watched the light change until it had finished doing so.
**Age 50 (699 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 51 (700 TR)** — She asked for something specific and it came. Not dramatically. Just — it came.
**Age 52 (701 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously.
**Age 53 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (703 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 55 (704 TR)** — She woke knowing what she needed to do. She had gone to sleep with no idea. She did not question it.
**Age 56 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (706 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Mara
**Age 58 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (708 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Arven has died
**Age 61 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (711 TR)** — She began writing — not because anyone asked, but because it needed doing.
**Age 63 (712 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 65 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 66 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 68 (717 TR)** — She smelled something that stopped her dead in the street. It brought back a whole season she had thought she had forgotten.
**Age 69 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 70 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 71 (720 TR)** — She became the person others consulted. This had been happening for years; it was only recently that she recognised it as a pattern.

name: Zara

narrative: **Age 18 (673 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 19 (674 TR)** — Patronage came with influence and obligations. She accepted both and managed the latter with care.

**Age 20 (675 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (676 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 22 (677 TR)** — She studied from a written wordlist compiled by someone who had died at Osel, supplemented by conversations with three different informants who disagreed on half the vocabulary. The disagreements were the most informative part. Language is politics in concentrated form.
**Age 23 (678 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 24 (679 TR)** — She came across someone sleeping where they should not have been. She covered them with something and went away.

**Age 25 (680 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.

**Age 26 (681 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
**Age 27 (682 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 28 (683 TR)** — She lent something she valued and did not get it back. She said nothing. The matter sat between her and the other person for a long time.
**Age 29 (684 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 30 (685 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 31 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 33 (688 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 34 (689 TR)** — She heard something that would have been wonderful at the time but reached her so late that it was only quietly pleasant.
**Age 35 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 37 (692 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 38 (693 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously.
**Age 39 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 40 (695 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 41 (696 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 42 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (699 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (700 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done.
**Age 46 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (702 TR)** — She perfected something in the kitchen that year. She was asked for the recipe regularly and gave it out selectively.
**Age 48 (703 TR)** — She could not bring herself to go inside. She sat and watched the light change until it had finished doing so.
**Age 49 (704 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
**Age 50 (705 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 51 (706 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.

**Age 52 (707 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 53 (708 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 54 (709 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 55 (710 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 56 (711 TR)** — A named posting — chaplain to a Lady of Paladins chapter. The knights watched her carefully for the first month to see how she would take the work.
**Age 57 (712 TR)** — She was given the wrong change and accepted it. She thought about it for the rest of the day.
**Age 58 (713 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 59 (714 TR)** — She waved at someone who looked at her with polite puzzlement. The wave had been for someone behind her.
**Age 60 (715 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 61 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 62 (717 TR)** — She walked to a specific place for a specific purpose and arrived with no recollection of what the purpose had been.
**Age 63 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 64 (719 TR)** — She had managed the household, the accounts, the children's marriages, and the succession of a substantial property for four decades, and she stopped. What she found on the other side of it was time. She had forgotten what time felt like when it belonged to her. She is still learning.
*(Ages 65–65: no notable events)*
