Life continued. **Age 33 (688 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 34 (689 TR)** — She heard something that would have been wonderful at the time but reached her so late that it was only quietly pleasant. **Age 35 (690 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 37 (692 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 38 (693 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously. **Age 39 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 40 (695 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week. **Age 41 (696 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read. **Age 42 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 44 (699 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 45 (700 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done. **Age 46 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (702 TR)** — She perfected something in the kitchen that year. She was asked for the recipe regularly and gave it out selectively. **Age 48 (703 TR)** — She could not bring herself to go inside. She sat and watched the light change until it had finished doing so. **Age 49 (704 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this. **Age 50 (705 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it. **Age 51 (706 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon. **Age 52 (707 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 53 (708 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 54 (709 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete. **Age 55 (710 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted. **Age 56 (711 TR)** — A named posting — chaplain to a Lady of Paladins chapter. The knights watched her carefully for the first month to see how she would take the work. **Age 57 (712 TR)** — She was given the wrong change and accepted it. She thought about it for the rest of the day. **Age 58 (713 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 59 (714 TR)** — She waved at someone who looked at her with polite puzzlement. The wave had been for someone behind her. **Age 60 (715 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it. **Age 61 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 62 (717 TR)** — She walked to a specific place for a specific purpose and arrived with no recollection of what the purpose had been. **Age 63 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 64 (719 TR)** — She had managed the household, the accounts, the children's marriages, and the succession of a substantial property for four decades, and she stopped. What she found on the other side of it was time. She had forgotten what time felt like when it belonged to her. She is still learning. *(Ages 65–65: no notable events)*
name: Elara

narrative: **Age 18 (679 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.

**Age 19 (680 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 20 (681 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (682 TR)** — She heard something through a door that changed how she thought about two people she knew. She said nothing.
**Age 22 (683 TR)** — She found the right words when someone else could not. She was surprised by herself.
**Age 23 (684 TR)** — She was accepted into a noble household as a lady-in-waiting or steward's assistant.
**Age 24 (685 TR)** — She found a sealed letter tucked into something she had bought secondhand. She kept it for a year before burning it unread.
**Age 26 (687 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
**Age 27 (688 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped.

**Age 28 (689 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 30 (691 TR)** — She woke to a sound she could not account for. She lay still and listened until it stopped. Nothing explained it in the morning.
**Age 31 (692 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.
**Age 32 (693 TR)** — She saw something in the grain of a beam — a face, a figure, a landscape — and noted it and went on.
**Age 33 (694 TR)** — A bird nested in her window ledge. She watched the eggs hatch and the chicks go.
**Age 34 (695 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 35 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (697 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Tavin
**Age 37 (698 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 38 (699 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Yarath (clergy)
**Age 39 (700 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 40 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (702 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 42 (703 TR)** — Rumour or accusation — fair or unfair — put her standing at risk.

**Age 43 (704 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.

**Age 44 (705 TR)** — She studied from a written wordlist compiled by someone who had died at Osel, supplemented by conversations with three different informants who disagreed on half the vocabulary. The disagreements were the most informative part. Language is politics in concentrated form.
**Age 45 (706 TR)** — She found her first grey hair and looked at it for a long time before deciding it was just a hair.
**Age 46 (707 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (708 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.

**Age 48 (709 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 49 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 50 (711 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Helda
**Age 51 (712 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 52 (713 TR)** — She found a name carved somewhere — a wall, a beam, a stone — and thought about the hand that had made it.
**Age 53 (714 TR)** — She read what texts she could find, and the night sky taught her the rest.
**Age 54 (715 TR)** — Her long years of service were noticed and formally recognised.

**Age 55 (716 TR)** — The poison was in the wine, and she had drunk only a little before the taste turned her. Or perhaps she had not drunk enough. For a week she did not know which and spent the time in careful consideration of every decision she had made in the past two years that could have made her worth killing.
**Age 56 (717 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.

**Age 57 (718 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 58 (719 TR)** — A named posting — chaplain to a Lady of Paladins chapter. The knights watched her carefully for the first month to see how she would take the work.
**Age 59 (720 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.

name: Welith

narrative: **Age 18 (685 TR)** — Rumour or accusation — fair or unfair — put her standing at risk.
**Age 19 (686 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.

**Age 20 (687 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (688 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 22 (689 TR)** — Dancing was expected of her from childhood. She was accomplished.

**Age 23 (690 TR)** — The poison was in the wine, and she had drunk only a little before the taste turned her. Or perhaps she had not drunk enough. For a week she did not know which and spent the time in careful consideration of every decision she had made in the past two years that could have made her worth killing.

**Age 24 (691 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 25 (692 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Harlan (clergy, age 31)
**Age 25 (692 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Zoric lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40
**Age 26 (693 TR)** — She read what texts she could find, and the night sky taught her the rest.
**Age 26 (693 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.

**Age 27 (694 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 27 (694 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 28 (695 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 28 (695 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 29 (696 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 29 (696 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Rorath — lived. week 40

**Age 30 (697 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful.
**Age 30 (697 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Delia. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40
**Age 31 (698 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
**Age 31 (698 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 32 (699 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously.
**Age 32 (699 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 12

**Age 33 (700 TR)** — A remote posting: a hamlet on the edge of the kingdom, or a lord’s estate where the last priest had left without explanation. The people needed the rites performed and she performed them.
**Age 33 (700 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.
**Age 34 (701 TR)** — She came across someone sleeping where they should not have been. She covered them with something and went away.
**Age 34 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 35 (702 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 35 (702 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Horic. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40
**Age 36 (703 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done.
**Age 36 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (704 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 37 (704 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.
**Age 38 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (705 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 39 (706 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 39 (706 TR)** — She bore a child. Nora came into the world healthy. week 40
**Age 40 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (708 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.

**Age 41 (708 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 41 (708 TR)** — Her son Zoric married Delyn Borson.
**Age 42 (709 TR)** — A traveller asked her name, repeated it carefully as if memorising it, then went on his way.
**Age 42 (709 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 43 (710 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Warin
**Age 43 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (710 TR)** — Zoric left to make her own way.
**Age 44 (711 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 44 (711 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 45 (712 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 46 (713 TR)** — A young squire asked her before the Tirannon vigil whether she was afraid of dying. She said no. The answer came without hesitation. She examined it later and decided it was probably true.
**Age 46 (713 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 46 (713 TR)** — Her daughter Rorath married Levan.
**Age 47 (714 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 47 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (714 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Zoric's child born (male, Zoric age 22)
**Age 47 (714 TR)** — Her daughter Delia married Arnam Skinner.
**Age 48 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (715 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.

**Age 49 (716 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
**Age 49 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (716 TR)** — Delia died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age.

**Age 50 (717 TR)** — She had confided things to the woman — things about her household, her finances, her husband's debts. She learned later, and by accident, that those confidences had been accurately relayed somewhere she would not have chosen. The friendship was real. The other work was also real. Both things were true at once.
**Age 51 (718 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life.
**Age 51 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — She drank from a well and the water was so cold and clean that she stood for a moment before going on.
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — Harlan died after 27 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence.
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — Rorath left to make her own way.
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Horic's illegitimate child born (male, Horic age 17)
**Age 53 (720 TR)** — She found a place that was simply right and made a habit of sitting there.
**Age 53 (720 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.

She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful. **Age 30 (697 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Delia. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 **Age 31 (698 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this. **Age 31 (698 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular. **Age 32 (699 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously. **Age 32 (699 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 12 **Age 33 (700 TR)** — A remote posting: a hamlet on the edge of the kingdom, or a lord’s estate where the last priest had left without explanation. The people needed the rites performed and she performed them. **Age 33 (700 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected. **Age 34 (701 TR)** — She came across someone sleeping where they should not have been. She covered them with something and went away. **Age 34 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 35 (702 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 35 (702 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Horic. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 **Age 36 (703 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done. **Age 36 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 37 (704 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 37 (704 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed. **Age 38 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 38 (705 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically. **Age 39 (706 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read. **Age 39 (706 TR)** — She bore a child. Nora came into the world healthy. week 40 **Age 40 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 41 (708 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete. **Age 41 (708 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny. **Age 41 (708 TR)** — Her son Zoric married Delyn Borson. **Age 42 (709 TR)** — A traveller asked her name, repeated it carefully as if memorising it, then went on his way. **Age 42 (709 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 43 (710 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Warin **Age 43 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 43 (710 TR)** — Zoric left to make her own way. **Age 44 (711 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs. **Age 44 (711 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride. **Age 45 (712 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 45 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 46 (713 TR)** — A young squire asked her before the Tirannon vigil whether she was afraid of dying. She said no. The answer came without hesitation. She examined it later and decided it was probably true. **Age 46 (713 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough. **Age 46 (713 TR)** — Her daughter Rorath married Levan. **Age 47 (714 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it. **Age 47 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 47 (714 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Zoric's child born (male, Zoric age 22) **Age 47 (714 TR)** — Her daughter Delia married Arnam Skinner. **Age 48 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 48 (715 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days. **Age 49 (716 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting. **Age 49 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 49 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 49 (716 TR)** — Delia died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. **Age 50 (717 TR)** — She had confided things to the woman — things about her household, her finances, her husband's debts. She learned later, and by accident, that those confidences had been accurately relayed somewhere she would not have chosen. The friendship was real. The other work was also real. Both things were true at once. **Age 51 (718 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life. **Age 51 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (719 TR)** — She drank from a well and the water was so cold and clean that she stood for a moment before going on. **Age 52 (719 TR)** — Harlan died after 27 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. **Age 52 (719 TR)** — Rorath left to make her own way. **Age 52 (719 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Horic's illegitimate child born (male, Horic age 17) **Age 53 (720 TR)** — She found a place that was simply right and made a habit of sitting there. **Age 53 (720 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
name: Erith

narrative: **Age 18 (691 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Rira
**Age 19 (692 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.

**Age 20 (693 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (694 TR)** — She read what texts she could find, and the night sky taught her the rest.
**Age 22 (695 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 23 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 24 (697 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.

**Age 25 (698 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 26 (699 TR)** — She was told to leave. The process had a formal name — expulsion, excommunication, being entered in the Book of Names — the name depended on the order. The result was the same. She stood straight through the reading and did not look at anyone.

**Age 27 (700 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful.
**Age 28 (701 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 29 (702 TR)** — The sickness passed but left her diminished for months — thinner, slower to tire, more careful about cold. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 30 (703 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.

**Age 31 (704 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 32 (705 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 33 (706 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 34 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 35 (708 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 36 (709 TR)** — She began writing — not because anyone asked, but because it needed doing.
**Age 37 (710 TR)** — She had trusted this person. That trust had been a tool someone else picked up and used. Betrayed by Davin
**Age 38 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 39 (712 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 40 (713 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 41 (714 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 42 (715 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 43 (716 TR)** — Someone quoted a prophecy in her presence and looked at her meaningfully. She pretended not to notice.
**Age 44 (717 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 45 (718 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 46 (719 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 47 (720 TR)** — She came across a text she was probably not supposed to read. She read it three times.

name: Larith

narrative: **Age 18 (697 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
**Age 19 (698 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Cade (clergy, age 17)
**Age 19 (698 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.
**Age 20 (699 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 20 (699 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.

**Age 21 (700 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 21 (700 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Woran. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40
**Age 22 (701 TR)** — She heard something through a door that changed how she thought about two people she knew. She said nothing.
**Age 23 (702 TR)** — She finished something and found she was satisfied with it. That was rarer than it should have been.
**Age 23 (702 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.
**Age 24 (703 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 24 (703 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Balar lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40

**Age 25 (704 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
**Age 25 (704 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 19
**Age 26 (705 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.
**Age 26 (705 TR)** — She bore a child. Vorin came into the world healthy. week 40
**Age 27 (706 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.

**Age 28 (707 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.
**Age 28 (707 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 20
**Age 29 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 29 (708 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.
**Age 30 (709 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 30 (709 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 31 (710 TR)** — She held back what she wanted to say for too long. By the time she was ready to say it, the occasion had closed.

**Age 32 (711 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 32 (711 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.

**Age 33 (712 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 33 (712 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 10
**Age 34 (713 TR)** — Patronage came with influence and obligations. She accepted both and managed the latter with care.
**Age 34 (713 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 34 (713 TR)** — Woran had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her.
**Age 35 (714 TR)** — The same dream visited her three nights. She woke each time unsettled and unable to say why.
**Age 35 (714 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 36 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 36 (715 TR)** — Cade died after 17 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence.
**Age 38 (717 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 38 (717 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 38 (717 TR)** — Balar had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her.
**Age 39 (718 TR)** — She walked to the shrine. The journey changed something — not in the way she expected, which was perhaps what it was supposed to do.
**Age 39 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 40 (719 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 40 (719 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 41 (720 TR)** — She lit a candle for someone she had not thought of in years. She did not examine this.

**Age 19 (704 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.

**Age 20 (705 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (706 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 22 (707 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 23 (708 TR)** — Dancing was expected of her from childhood. She was accomplished.
**Age 24 (709 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 25 (710 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 26 (711 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Reda has died
**Age 27 (712 TR)** — She sat through a formal occasion of exceptional length and dullness and maintained an expression of interested attention throughout.

**Age 28 (713 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

**Age 29 (714 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 30 (715 TR)** — She saw a situation developing that she might have done something about. She walked on. The question of whether she was right to has not fully settled.

**Age 31 (716 TR)** — The approach was made in person, by a woman she had never seen, who knew things about her that no one had any business knowing. The price was stated plainly. She paid it. She is still paying it. She has not yet determined who is behind the approach, which tells her they are very good at this.

**Age 32 (717 TR)** — She had confided things to the woman — things about her household, her finances, her husband's debts. She learned later, and by accident, that those confidences had been accurately relayed somewhere she would not have chosen. The friendship was real. The other work was also real. Both things were true at once.
**Age 33 (718 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
**Age 35 (720 TR)** — __colour__

name: Arith

narrative: **Age 18 (709 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Gorin
**Age 19 (710 TR)** — Dancing was expected of her from childhood. She was accomplished.

**Age 20 (711 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (712 TR)** — She finished something and found she was satisfied with it. That was rarer than it should have been.
**Age 22 (713 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 23 (714 TR)** — She heard the midnight hour ring from somewhere she had never been. She lay awake listening to the silence after.
**Age 24 (715 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Yara Amberton (merchant)
**Age 25 (716 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 26 (717 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 27 (718 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 28 (719 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.
**Age 29 (720 TR)** — She thought of what she should have said long after there was any chance to say it.

name: Andar

narrative: **Age 18 (715 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 19 (716 TR)** — She studied from a written wordlist compiled by someone who had died at Osel, supplemented by conversations with three different informants who disagreed on half the vocabulary. The disagreements were the most informative part. Language is politics in concentrated form.

**Age 20 (717 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (718 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Aldis Nalekar (noble)

**Age 22 (719 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 23 (720 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

name: Irda

narrative: **Age 18 (663 TR)** — She lost a small thing she had carried for years. She did not speak of it but felt its absence for a long time.
**Age 19 (664 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.

**Age 20 (665 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (666 TR)** — She would know those hands anywhere. She watches from one stall over and thinks about all the things she could do and then does none of them, because she is not the kind of person who makes trouble in the marketplace. She is not entirely at peace with this.

**Age 22 (667 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 23 (668 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 24 (669 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 25 (670 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Hrolf
**Age 26 (671 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 27 (672 TR)** — She wept at something small and ordinary. She was not embarrassed. She let it pass.

**Age 28 (673 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 29 (674 TR)** — She received kindness from a direction she had not anticipated. She thought about it for a long time.

**Age 30 (675 TR)** — A remote posting: a hamlet on the edge of the kingdom, or a lord’s estate where the last priest had left without explanation. The people needed the rites performed and she performed them.
**Age 31 (676 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
**Age 32 (677 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 33 (678 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.
**Age 34 (679 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 35 (680 TR)** — She sneezed at the worst possible moment. The ceremony continued. The people nearest her found it difficult to maintain appropriate expressions.
**Age 36 (681 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 37 (682 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. She paid the price.

**Age 38 (683 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 39 (684 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (685 TR)** — A named posting — chaplain to a Lady of Paladins chapter. The knights watched her carefully for the first month to see how she would take the work.

**Age 41 (686 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
**Age 42 (687 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 43 (688 TR)** — She began writing — not because anyone asked, but because it needed doing.
**Age 44 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (690 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 46 (691 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 47 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (693 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 49 (694 TR)** — She walked to the shrine. The journey changed something — not in the way she expected, which was perhaps what it was supposed to do.
**Age 50 (695 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

**Age 51 (696 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Warin became a close friend
**Age 52 (697 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.
**Age 53 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (699 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 55 (700 TR)** — Patronage came with influence and obligations. She accepted both and managed the latter with care.
**Age 56 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (702 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −2.
**Age 58 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (704 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Arnam
**Age 60 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 62 (707 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Arnam
**Age 63 (708 TR)** — Most of the women she had known as a young woman were dead. The world had been replaced around her, a face at a time.
**Age 64 (709 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 65 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 66 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (712 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 68 (713 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Sira
**Age 69 (714 TR)** — She read what texts she could find, and the night sky taught her the rest.
**Age 70 (715 TR)** — She calculated when something had occurred and found it was far further back than she had thought.
**Age 71 (716 TR)** — She found the right thing to say to make someone laugh who had been struggling not to cry. She was not sure how she had known what to say.
**Age 72 (717 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 73 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 74 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 75 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 22 (673 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 23 (674 TR)** — Patronage came with influence and obligations. She accepted both and managed the latter with care.

**Age 24 (675 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 25 (676 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.

**Age 26 (677 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
**Age 27 (678 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously.

**Age 28 (679 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 29 (680 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 30 (681 TR)** — There were rats. She dealt with them methodically. The cat was no help whatsoever.
**Age 31 (682 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
**Age 32 (683 TR)** — The animals got through the fence. She spent most of a morning chasing them. The pig was especially difficult.
**Age 33 (684 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.

**Age 34 (685 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 35 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (687 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.
**Age 37 (688 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 38 (689 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped.
**Age 39 (690 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.

**Age 40 (691 TR)** — A man of means noticed her. What followed was not love, precisely, but it was not nothing either. She had a room that was warm and food that was regular, and she understood the terms.
**Age 41 (692 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Elith

**Age 42 (693 TR)** — He tired of her, or his wife found out, or he simply moved on. The room was no longer available. She was back where she had started, except that she knew more now and people knew about her.
**Age 43 (694 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
**Age 44 (695 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
**Age 45 (696 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 46 (697 TR)** — She reached for the sword out of habit and grabbed air. She put her hand down and walked on. Some things take longer to leave than expected.
**Age 47 (698 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 48 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (700 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 50 (701 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done.
**Age 51 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (703 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.

**Age 53 (704 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 54 (705 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
**Age 55 (706 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 56 (707 TR)** — The change had been gradual and then sudden. She could trace exactly when it had stopped being repairable. Sara became an enemy
**Age 57 (708 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 58 (709 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Elith has died

**Age 59 (710 TR)** — A remote posting: a hamlet on the edge of the kingdom, or a lord’s estate where the last priest had left without explanation. The people needed the rites performed and she performed them.
**Age 60 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 61 (712 TR)** — She had managed the household, the accounts, the children's marriages, and the succession of a substantial property for four decades, and she stopped. What she found on the other side of it was time. She had forgotten what time felt like when it belonged to her. She is still learning.
**Age 62 (713 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 63 (714 TR)** — She heard something through a door that changed how she thought about two people she knew. She said nothing.
**Age 64 (715 TR)** — She set her affairs in order with the same methodical care she had brought to everything. She felt lighter for it.
**Age 65 (716 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 66 (717 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 67 (718 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Sara
**Age 68 (719 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
*(Ages 69–69: uneventful years)*

narrative: **Age 18 (675 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.

**Age 19 (676 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 20 (677 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 21 (678 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 22 (679 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.
**Age 23 (680 TR)** — Dancing was expected of her from childhood. She was accomplished.
**Age 24 (681 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 25 (682 TR)** — She received a compliment she deserved and managed to seem both dismissive and pleased. She was annoyed at herself.
**Age 26 (683 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 27 (684 TR)** — She walked to the shrine. The journey changed something — not in the way she expected, which was perhaps what it was supposed to do.

**Age 28 (685 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 29 (686 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 30 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 31 (688 TR)** — She studied from a written wordlist compiled by someone who had died at Osel, supplemented by conversations with three different informants who disagreed on half the vocabulary. The disagreements were the most informative part. Language is politics in concentrated form.
**Age 32 (689 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 33 (690 TR)** — She told a joke she had been holding for the right moment. The right moment arrived. The joke was everything she had hoped.
**Age 34 (691 TR)** — Someone asked her something about belief she had no good answer to. She thought about what she had said for weeks.
**Age 35 (692 TR)** — She dreamed something that she decided not to speak of. She has never spoken of it.
**Age 36 (693 TR)** — Patronage came with influence and obligations. She accepted both and managed the latter with care.
**Age 37 (694 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously.
**Age 38 (695 TR)** — A named posting — chaplain to a Lady of Paladins chapter. The knights watched her carefully for the first month to see how she would take the work.
**Age 39 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (698 TR)** — She found one of the six Swordstones on a road she did not know. She cleaned it as prescribed. She stayed longer than the act required.
**Age 42 (699 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the knee. The damage was permanent — agility reduced by 3.
**Age 43 (700 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life.
**Age 44 (701 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 45 (702 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 46 (703 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 47 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (705 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Soran
**Age 49 (706 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 50 (707 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 51 (708 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 52 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (710 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 54 (711 TR)** — She wept at something small and ordinary. She was not embarrassed. She let it pass.
**Age 55 (712 TR)** — She passed a room in the night and saw light underneath the door. She paused and then went on. She asked about it in the morning.

**Age 56 (713 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.

**Age 57 (714 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
**Age 58 (715 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Welith
**Age 59 (716 TR)** — She finished something and found she was satisfied with it. That was rarer than it should have been.
**Age 60 (717 TR)** — The place where the thing used to be has a shape now. She has rearranged the shelf three times. It still has the shape of the thing that was taken.
**Age 61 (718 TR)** — She heard something she expected to break her and found it did not. She sat with that for a long time.
**Age 62 (719 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Welith
**Age 63 (720 TR)** — She repaired something that belonged to her neighbour while they were away. No one ever mentioned it.

**Age 33 (696 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 34 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (698 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 36 (699 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.

**Age 37 (700 TR)** — The poison was in the wine, and she had drunk only a little before the taste turned her. Or perhaps she had not drunk enough. For a week she did not know which and spent the time in careful consideration of every decision she had made in the past two years that could have made her worth killing.
**Age 38 (701 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 39 (702 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 40 (703 TR)** — She said what no one else was going to say. She had hoped to stay out of it. She did not stay out of it.
**Age 41 (704 TR)** — She read what texts she could find, and the night sky taught her the rest.
**Age 42 (705 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Delia
**Age 43 (706 TR)** — She waved at someone who looked at her with polite puzzlement. The wave had been for someone behind her.

**Age 44 (707 TR)** — A remote posting: a hamlet on the edge of the kingdom, or a lord’s estate where the last priest had left without explanation. The people needed the rites performed and she performed them.
**Age 45 (708 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 46 (709 TR)** — She studied from a written wordlist compiled by someone who had died at Osel, supplemented by conversations with three different informants who disagreed on half the vocabulary. The disagreements were the most informative part. Language is politics in concentrated form.
**Age 47 (710 TR)** — Rumour or accusation — fair or unfair — put her standing at risk.
**Age 48 (711 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
**Age 49 (712 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously.

**Age 50 (713 TR)** — She had confided things to the woman — things about her household, her finances, her husband's debts. She learned later, and by accident, that those confidences had been accurately relayed somewhere she would not have chosen. The friendship was real. The other work was also real. Both things were true at once.
**Age 51 (714 TR)** — She was accepted into a noble household as a lady-in-waiting or steward's assistant.
**Age 52 (715 TR)** — She made a remark that landed badly in ways she had not anticipated. She apologised. She is not sure it helped.
**Age 53 (716 TR)** — She performed an old ritual her grandmother had taught her. She felt better afterwards and did not explain it.
**Age 54 (717 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark.

**Age 55 (718 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.

**Age 56 (719 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 57 (720 TR)** — Her long years of service were noticed and formally recognised.

name: Caris

narrative: **Age 18 (687 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 19 (688 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.

**Age 20 (689 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (690 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word.
