name: Marek Garing

narrative: **Age 18 (666 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
**Age 19 (667 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.

**Age 20 (668 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (669 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 22 (670 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 23 (671 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 24 (672 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 25 (673 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.

**Age 26 (674 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 27 (675 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Hrolf Coring (lia_kavair)
**Age 28 (676 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 29 (677 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 30 (678 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 31 (679 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 32 (680 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 33 (681 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 34 (682 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 35 (683 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 36 (684 TR)** — His father spent the last months in the Peoni house on the river quarter. He visited twice a week. The place was clean and quiet and smelled of herbs and candle wax, and the sisters were matter-of-fact about dying in a way he found first shocking and then steadying. His father was comfortable. It was not nothing.
**Age 37 (685 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 38 (686 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 39 (687 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Borik Idson died
**Age 40 (688 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 41 (689 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 42 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (691 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.

**Age 44 (692 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 45 (693 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 46 (694 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 47 (695 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 48 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (697 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Alric Haling
**Age 50 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (699 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the head or face that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.

**Age 52 (700 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence.
**Age 53 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (706 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.
**Age 59 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 60 (708 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 61 (709 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Alric Haling
**Age 62 (710 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.
**Age 63 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 64 (712 TR)** — He drew up his will and distributed what he could while he still could. It was a practical matter. He was surprised at how much it felt like something else.
**Age 65 (713 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 66 (714 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 67 (715 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Cavan Wolman
**Age 68 (716 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 69 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 70 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 71 (719 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
*(Ages 72–72: quiet years)*
narrativeText: **Age 18 (666 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon. **Age 19 (667 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected. **Age 20 (668 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement. **Age 21 (669 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 22 (670 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity. **Age 23 (671 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 24 (672 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. name: Ronan Arsen

narrative: **Age 18 (672 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.

**Age 19 (673 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.

**Age 20 (674 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (675 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 22 (676 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Leira Heavypurse (merchant)
**Age 23 (677 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 24 (678 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Varath Valing (lia_kavair, age 23)
**Age 24 (678 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 20

**Age 25 (679 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 26 (680 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler.
**Age 26 (680 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 27
**Age 27 (681 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 27 (681 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 28 (682 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 29 (683 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 29 (683 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Talar Orson. week 40 Talar Orson born (male)
**Age 30 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (685 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 32 (686 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 32 (686 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 33 (687 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.

**Age 34 (688 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 34 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (689 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 36 (690 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 37 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 38 (692 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.

**Age 39 (693 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees.
**Age 39 (693 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.

**Age 40 (694 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 40 (694 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 41 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (695 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −1.
**Age 42 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 43 (697 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.

**Age 43 (697 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.

**Age 44 (698 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Hadric Korman
**Age 44 (698 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 45 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name.
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Talar Orson's illegitimate child born (female, Talar Orson age 17)
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — His son Talar Orson married Dera Noring. Talar Orson married Dera Noring (lia_kavair, age 17)
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — Talar Orson left home to make his own way. Talar Orson left home (age 18)
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.

**Age 48 (702 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.

**Age 49 (703 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 50 (704 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Warin Orson's illegitimate child born (female, Warin Orson age 17)
**Age 52 (706 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 52 (706 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 52 (706 TR)** — Warin Orson left home to make his own way. Warin Orson left home (age 18)

**Age 53 (707 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 7
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — Warin Orson's child Rora Orson died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Warin Orson's child died (age 3)
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 56 (710 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 57 (711 TR)** — The mark had come home early, or the watch had changed its route, or someone had talked — he never found out which. He went to ground for three weeks in a safe house near the tanners' quarter, eating dried meat and listening to footsteps in the street above. Nothing came of it. It was two years before he stopped expecting it to.
**Age 57 (711 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 57 (711 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Talar Orson's child born (male, Talar Orson age 28)

**Age 59 (713 TR)** — The silver plate had come from a noble's household and needed to be out of recognisable form within a week. He spent three days finding the right buyer — not the obvious one, who would have been watched, but a goldsmith two towns distant who asked no questions when the price was right.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — His son Warin Orson married Valith Tanner. Warin Orson married Valith Tanner (artisan, age 23)
**Age 60 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 61 (715 TR)** — The constable asks where he was last night. He tells him. The constable writes nothing down and moves on. The story he gave was true in all its verifiable particulars and false in its implication. He has had years of practice.
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.

**Age 63 (717 TR)** — He had been careful about it for three years — moving money, building contacts outside the usual networks, maintaining a cover that the guild did not fully know about. When he went, he went quietly and completely, leaving a city that had been home for fifteen years. He never used the guild slang again.
**Age 64 (718 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 64 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 65 (719 TR)** — Everything was where it was supposed to be. He was in and out in under a quarter-hour, with no noise and no deviation from the plan. It bothers him slightly that he cannot identify what he did right.
**Age 65 (719 TR)** — Varath Valing died. They had been married 41 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Varath Valing died (approx. age 64)
**Age 66 (720 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.

He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment. **Age 32 (686 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment. **Age 33 (687 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 34 (688 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting. **Age 34 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 35 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 35 (689 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong. **Age 36 (690 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that. **Age 37 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 38 (692 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 39 (693 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees. **Age 39 (693 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years. **Age 40 (694 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear. **Age 40 (694 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 41 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (695 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years. **Age 42 (696 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −1. **Age 42 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 43 (697 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately. **Age 43 (697 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing. **Age 44 (698 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Hadric Korman **Age 44 (698 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer. **Age 45 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 46 (700 TR)** — The grief took longer than he had thought it would. He emerged from it changed in ways he could not fully name. **Age 46 (700 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Talar Orson's illegitimate child born (female, Talar Orson age 17) **Age 47 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 47 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (701 TR)** — His son Talar Orson married Dera Noring. Talar Orson married Dera Noring (lia_kavair, age 17) **Age 47 (701 TR)** — Talar Orson left home to make his own way. Talar Orson left home (age 18) **Age 48 (702 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected. **Age 48 (702 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 49 (703 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working. **Age 49 (703 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning. **Age 50 (704 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely. **Age 51 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 51 (705 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with. **Age 51 (705 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Warin Orson's illegitimate child born (female, Warin Orson age 17) **Age 52 (706 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely. **Age 52 (706 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours. **Age 52 (706 TR)** — Warin Orson left home to make his own way. Warin Orson left home (age 18) **Age 53 (707 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence. **Age 53 (707 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 7 **Age 54 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 54 (708 TR)** — Warin Orson's child Rora Orson died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Warin Orson's child died (age 3) **Age 55 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (710 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted. **Age 56 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 57 (711 TR)** — The mark had come home early, or the watch had changed its route, or someone had talked — he never found out which. He went to ground for three weeks in a safe house near the tanners' quarter, eating dried meat and listening to footsteps in the street above. Nothing came of it. It was two years before he stopped expecting it to. **Age 57 (711 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 57 (711 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Talar Orson's child born (male, Talar Orson age 28) **Age 59 (713 TR)** — The silver plate had come from a noble's household and needed to be out of recognisable form within a week. He spent three days finding the right buyer — not the obvious one, who would have been watched, but a goldsmith two towns distant who asked no questions when the price was right. **Age 59 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 59 (713 TR)** — His son Warin Orson married Valith Tanner. Warin Orson married Valith Tanner (artisan, age 23) **Age 60 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 60 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 61 (715 TR)** — The constable asks where he was last night. He tells him. The constable writes nothing down and moves on. The story he gave was true in all its verifiable particulars and false in its implication. He has had years of practice. **Age 62 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 62 (716 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved. **Age 63 (717 TR)** — He had been careful about it for three years — moving money, building contacts outside the usual networks, maintaining a cover that the guild did not fully know about. When he went, he went quietly and completely, leaving a city that had been home for fifteen years. He never used the guild slang again. **Age 64 (718 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 64 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 65 (719 TR)** — Everything was where it was supposed to be. He was in and out in under a quarter-hour, with no noise and no deviation from the plan. It bothers him slightly that he cannot identify what he did right. **Age 65 (719 TR)** — Varath Valing died. They had been married 41 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Varath Valing died (approx. age 64) **Age 66 (720 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
name: Voran Orson

narrative: **Age 18 (678 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 19 (679 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.

**Age 20 (680 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (681 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 22 (682 TR)** — He travelled far with a caravan, seeing parts of Kaldor few of his neighbours ever would.
**Age 23 (683 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 24 (684 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 25 (685 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 26 (686 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.
**Age 27 (687 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 28 (688 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected.
**Age 29 (689 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 30 (690 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 31 (691 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 32 (692 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 34 (694 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 35 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (696 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 37 (697 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 38 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (699 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 40 (700 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 41 (701 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Cade Dorkin

**Age 42 (702 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 43 (703 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.
**Age 44 (704 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 45 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (706 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 47 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 49 (709 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 50 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (711 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Geira Dalsen
**Age 52 (712 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 53 (713 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.

**Age 54 (714 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth.
**Age 55 (715 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.
**Age 56 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 57 (717 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.

**Age 58 (718 TR)** — The meeting was short. His master listed the errors — the timing, the noise, the unnecessary exposure — and told him what it had cost. The penalty was financial and was taken from his cut for six months. He understood that the financial penalty was not the point. The point was the knowledge that they knew, and that he was still alive because they had decided he was.
**Age 59 (719 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.

**Age 60 (720 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same.

name: Dalric Eling

narrative: **Age 18 (684 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Emra (clergy)

**Age 19 (685 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.

**Age 20 (686 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (687 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 22 (688 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.
**Age 23 (689 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 24 (690 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −3.
**Age 25 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 26 (692 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 27 (693 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 28 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30 (696 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.

**Age 31 (697 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 32 (698 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler.

**Age 33 (699 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.

**Age 34 (700 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day.
**Age 35 (701 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected.
**Age 36 (702 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 37 (703 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Alda Sorman (lia_kavair, age 36)
**Age 38 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (705 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 39 (705 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Talith Boring — survived. week 40 Talith Boring born (female)
**Age 40 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (707 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 41 (707 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 42 (708 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 42 (708 TR)** — Talith Boring died young — a child still. Talith Boring died (age 3)
**Age 43 (709 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 43 (709 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 44 (710 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 44 (710 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 45 (711 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 45 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 46 (712 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same.
**Age 46 (712 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 21
**Age 47 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (714 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Branna Wolman died

**Age 48 (714 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Lothar Boring born (male, lame)

**Age 49 (715 TR)** — He had been suspicious for months, and when the pattern became undeniable he brought it to his master rather than acting alone. The informant had been in their cell for three years. The resolution was handled without noise, which was the professional way, and he was commended for not making it a mess.
**Age 49 (715 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 50 (716 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Corin Malkin
**Age 50 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 51 (717 TR)** — He had been careful about it for three years — moving money, building contacts outside the usual networks, maintaining a cover that the guild did not fully know about. When he went, he went quietly and completely, leaving a city that had been home for fifteen years. He never used the guild slang again.
**Age 51 (717 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.

**Age 52 (718 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 52 (718 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.

**Age 53 (719 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 53 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (720 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (684 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Emra (clergy) **Age 19 (685 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it. **Age 20 (686 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement. **Age 21 (687 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 22 (688 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin. **Age 23 (689 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity. **Age 24 (690 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −3. **Age 25 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 26 (692 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 27 (693 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 28 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 30 (696 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 31 (697 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences. **Age 32 (698 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler. **Age 33 (699 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard. **Age 34 (700 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day. **Age 35 (701 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected. **Age 36 (702 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his. **Age 37 (703 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Alda Sorman (lia_kavair, age 36) **Age 38 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 39 (705 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public. **Age 39 (705 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Talith Boring — survived. week 40 Talith Boring born (female) **Age 40 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (707 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price. **Age 41 (707 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years. **Age 42 (708 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him. **Age 42 (708 TR)** — Talith Boring died young — a child still. Talith Boring died (age 3) **Age 43 (709 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way. **Age 43 (709 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of. **Age 44 (710 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 44 (710 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered. **Age 45 (711 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 45 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 46 (712 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same. **Age 46 (712 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 21 **Age 47 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 48 (714 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Branna Wolman died **Age 48 (714 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. name: Cavan Boring

narrative: **Age 18 (690 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 19 (691 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.

**Age 20 (692 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (693 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 22 (694 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 23 (695 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 24 (696 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 25 (697 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 26 (698 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 27 (699 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.

**Age 28 (700 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 29 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 30 (702 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.

**Age 31 (703 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.

**Age 32 (704 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
**Age 33 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 34 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (707 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Sara Horman (lia_kavair, age 29)
**Age 35 (707 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 36 (708 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.
**Age 37 (709 TR)** — He travelled far with a caravan, seeing parts of Kaldor few of his neighbours ever would.
**Age 38 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 38 (710 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 39 (711 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 39 (711 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 40 (712 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Kavan Marson (lia_kavair)
**Age 40 (712 TR)** — Sara Horman died. They had been married 5 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Sara Horman died (approx. age 34)
**Age 41 (713 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 42 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (715 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Zarath Garman died
**Age 43 (715 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 44 (716 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 45 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (718 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 47 (719 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 48 (720 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.

name: Cador Eling

narrative: **Age 18 (696 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 19 (697 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day.

**Age 20 (698 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (699 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.

**Age 22 (700 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.

**Age 23 (701 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler.

**Age 24 (702 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
**Age 25 (703 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 26 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 27 (705 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 28 (706 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 29 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 30 (708 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 31 (709 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 32 (710 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.

**Age 33 (711 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees.
**Age 34 (712 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.

**Age 35 (713 TR)** — The other apprentice got the better tasks, the master's patience, the benefit of the doubt. He got the corrections and the night work. He learned both crafts — the craft itself, and the craft of being the one who is not the favourite.
**Age 36 (714 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Arith Uling

**Age 37 (715 TR)** — The silver plate had come from a noble's household and needed to be out of recognisable form within a week. He spent three days finding the right buyer — not the obvious one, who would have been watched, but a goldsmith two towns distant who asked no questions when the price was right.
**Age 38 (716 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 39 (717 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
**Age 40 (718 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 41 (719 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.

**Age 42 (720 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.

Reconciled with Arith Uling **Age 37 (715 TR)** — The silver plate had come from a noble's household and needed to be out of recognisable form within a week. He spent three days finding the right buyer — not the obvious one, who would have been watched, but a goldsmith two towns distant who asked no questions when the price was right. **Age 38 (716 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 39 (717 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it. **Age 40 (718 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected. **Age 41 (719 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 42 (720 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.
name: Arven Dalson

narrative: **Age 18 (702 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 19 (703 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 20 (704 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (705 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 22 (706 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Sirath Corkin (lia_kavair, age 22)

**Age 23 (707 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler.
**Age 23 (707 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Nora Eling came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Nora Eling born (female)
**Age 24 (708 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 24 (708 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Gotar Eling came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Gotar Eling born (male)
**Age 25 (709 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 25 (709 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 26 (710 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 26 (710 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 26 (710 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Gotar Eling died (age 2)
**Age 27 (711 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 27 (711 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.

**Age 28 (712 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
**Age 28 (712 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Kela Eling born (female)

**Age 29 (713 TR)** — The mark had come home early, or the watch had changed its route, or someone had talked — he never found out which. He went to ground for three weeks in a safe house near the tanners' quarter, eating dried meat and listening to footsteps in the street above. Nothing came of it. It was two years before he stopped expecting it to.

**Age 29 (713 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Halden Eling born (male, lame)
**Age 30 (714 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 30 (714 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 31 (715 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 31 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (716 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Perin Taramin (noble)
**Age 33 (717 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 33 (717 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on.
**Age 34 (718 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 34 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 34 (718 TR)** — Kela Eling died young — a child still. Kela Eling died (age 6)
**Age 35 (719 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.
**Age 35 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 36 (720 TR)** — He travelled far with a caravan, seeing parts of Kaldor few of his neighbours ever would.
**Age 36 (720 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 7

**Age 24 (720 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
