name: Noral Valing

narrative: **Age 18 (668 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 19 (669 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.

**Age 20 (670 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (671 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 22 (672 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 23 (673 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 24 (674 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 25 (675 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 26 (676 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.

**Age 27 (677 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 28 (678 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
**Age 29 (679 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 30 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (681 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 32 (682 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 33 (683 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.

**Age 34 (684 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 35 (685 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 36 (686 TR)** — He had trusted the man for four years. The arrest made no sense until he thought back to who had known about the job, and then it made perfect sense. He was out of the city before they came for him — barely. He did not go back for a long time, and when he did, his former colleague was no longer a problem.
**Age 37 (687 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 38 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 39 (689 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
**Age 40 (690 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 41 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (692 TR)** — A lord offered endowment for the chapel and expected pastoral access in return. The arrangement suited both parties and neither was entirely comfortable with it.
**Age 43 (693 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 44 (694 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.

**Age 45 (695 TR)** — The other apprentice got the better tasks, the master's patience, the benefit of the doubt. He got the corrections and the night work. He learned both crafts — the craft itself, and the craft of being the one who is not the favourite.
**Age 46 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (697 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.

**Age 48 (698 TR)** — Everything was where it was supposed to be. He was in and out in under a quarter-hour, with no noise and no deviation from the plan. It bothers him slightly that he cannot identify what he did right.

**Age 49 (699 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees.
**Age 50 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 51 (701 TR)** — He had been careful about it for three years — moving money, building contacts outside the usual networks, maintaining a cover that the guild did not fully know about. When he went, he went quietly and completely, leaving a city that had been home for fifteen years. He never used the guild slang again.
**Age 52 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (703 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 54 (704 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 55 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 56 (706 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 57 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (709 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.

**Age 60 (710 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.
**Age 61 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (712 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
**Age 63 (713 TR)** — Through hard work and some luck, the last creditor was satisfied.
**Age 64 (714 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Tavin Orsen died
**Age 65 (715 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.

**Age 66 (716 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same.
**Age 67 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 68 (718 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 69 (719 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 70 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (668 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 19 (669 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 20 (670 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement. **Age 21 (671 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them. **Age 22 (672 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 23 (673 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment. **Age 24 (674 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life. **Age 25 (675 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally. **Age 26 (676 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin. **Age 27 (677 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences. **Age 28 (678 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working. **Age 29 (679 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 30 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 31 (681 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected. **Age 32 (682 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 33 (683 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed. **Age 34 (684 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard. **Age 35 (685 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 36 (686 TR)** — He had trusted the man for four years. The arrest made no sense until he thought back to who had known about the job, and then it made perfect sense. He was out of the city before they came for him — barely. He did not go back for a long time, and when he did, his former colleague was no longer a problem. **Age 37 (687 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected. **Age 38 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 39 (689 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it. **Age 40 (690 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 41 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 42 (692 TR)** — A lord offered endowment for the chapel and expected pastoral access in return. The arrangement suited both parties and neither was entirely comfortable with it. **Age 43 (693 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 44 (694 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 45 (695 TR)** — The other apprentice got the better tasks, the master's patience, the benefit of the doubt. He got the corrections and the night work. He learned both crafts — the craft itself, and the craft of being the one who is not the favourite. **Age 46 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (697 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean. **Age 48 (698 TR)** — Everything was where it was supposed to be. He was in and out in under a quarter-hour, with no noise and no deviation from the plan. It bothers him slightly that he cannot identify what he did right. **Age 49 (699 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees. **Age 50 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 51 (701 TR)** — He had been careful about it for three years — moving money, building contacts outside the usual networks, maintaining a cover that the guild did not fully know about. When he went, he went quietly and completely, leaving a city that had been home for fifteen years. He never used the guild slang again. **Age 52 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 53 (703 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it. **Age 54 (704 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 55 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (706 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting. **Age 57 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 58 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 59 (709 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues. **Age 60 (710 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why. **Age 61 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 62 (712 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook. **Age 63 (713 TR)** — Through hard work and some luck, the last creditor was satisfied. **Age 64 (714 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Tavin Orsen died **Age 65 (715 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go. **Age 66 (716 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same. **Age 67 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 68 (718 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his. **Age 69 (719 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before. **Age 70 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
name: Erlan Korkin

**Age 19 (675 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.

**Age 20 (676 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (677 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 22 (678 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Annis Ralsen (lia_kavair, age 16)
**Age 23 (679 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 23 (679 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Tara Wolsen. week 40 Tara Wolsen born (female)
**Age 23 (679 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Tara Wolsen died (age 0)

**Age 24 (680 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
**Age 25 (681 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 25 (681 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 26 (682 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 26 (682 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 27 (683 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 27 (683 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Orlan Wolsen born (male)

**Age 28 (684 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 28 (684 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 29 (685 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 29 (685 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 30 (686 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 31 (687 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 32 (688 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 32 (688 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 33 (689 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 33 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 34 (690 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 34 (690 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Terath Wolsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Terath Wolsen born (female)
**Age 35 (691 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 35 (691 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
**Age 36 (692 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 36 (692 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Uther Wolsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Uther Wolsen born (male)
**Age 37 (693 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 37 (693 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 38 (694 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 38 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 39 (695 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 39 (695 TR)** — Uther Wolsen died young — a child still. Uther Wolsen died (age 3)
**Age 40 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (696 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 41 (697 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 41 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (698 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Rorik Edkin
**Age 42 (698 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 43 (699 TR)** — __colour__
**Age 44 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 44 (700 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 45 (701 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Mael Bransen (lia_kavair)
**Age 46 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (702 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.

**Age 47 (703 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.
**Age 47 (703 TR)** — His daughter Lara Wolsen married Lucan Halman. Lara Wolsen married Lucan Halman (peasant, age 16)
**Age 47 (703 TR)** — Lara Wolsen left home to make his own way. Lara Wolsen left home (age 19)
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.

**Age 48 (704 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 48 (704 TR)** — His son Orlan Wolsen married Sirath Norkin. Orlan Wolsen married Sirath Norkin (lia_kavair, age 21)
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 49 (705 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 49 (705 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Lara Wolsen's child born (female, Lara Wolsen age 21)
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Calla Wolsen — survived. week 40 Calla Wolsen born (female)
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Estra Wolsen's illegitimate child born (male, Estra Wolsen age 17)
**Age 50 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (707 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 51 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (707 TR)** — His daughter Terath Wolsen married Rhoric Dalman. Terath Wolsen married Rhoric Dalman (lia_kavair, age 17)

**Age 52 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 53 (709 TR)** — Annis Ralsen died. They had been married 31 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Annis Ralsen died (approx. age 47)
**Age 54 (710 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Lara Ralkin (lia_kavair, age 52)
**Age 54 (710 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 54 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Mael Bransen has died
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (711 TR)** — His son Osric Wolsen married Larith Thatcher. Osric Wolsen married Larith Thatcher (artisan, age 16)
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — Lara Wolsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Lara Wolsen's face. Lara Wolsen's child died (age 2)
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — Estra Wolsen left home to make his own way. Estra Wolsen left home (age 23)
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — Terath Wolsen left home to make his own way. Terath Wolsen left home (age 22)
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (712 TR)** — Osric Wolsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Osric Wolsen's face. Osric Wolsen's child died (age 0)
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 11
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (713 TR)** — Estra Wolsen's child Ruald Wolsen died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Estra Wolsen's child died (age 7)

**Age 58 (714 TR)** — He had been suspicious for months, and when the pattern became undeniable he brought it to his master rather than acting alone. The informant had been in their cell for three years. The resolution was handled without noise, which was the professional way, and he was commended for not making it a mess.
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 59 (715 TR)** — The silver plate had come from a noble's household and needed to be out of recognisable form within a week. He spent three days finding the right buyer — not the obvious one, who would have been watched, but a goldsmith two towns distant who asked no questions when the price was right.

**Age 62 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 63 (719 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing.

He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working. **Age 52 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. narrative: **Age 18 (680 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Geira Sorkin (lia_kavair, age 16)

**Age 19 (681 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 19 (681 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.

**Age 20 (682 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (683 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 21 (683 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 22 (684 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 22 (684 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Yorin Eding came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Yorin Eding born (male)
**Age 23 (685 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 23 (685 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
**Age 24 (686 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 24 (686 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 25 (687 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.

**Age 25 (687 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.

**Age 26 (688 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.
**Age 27 (689 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 27 (689 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 28 (690 TR)** — The man at the next table used the word without thinking, mid-sentence, and then corrected himself. They did not make eye contact. Both of them found somewhere else to look.
**Age 28 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 29 (691 TR)** — He travelled far with a caravan, seeing parts of Kaldor few of his neighbours ever would.
**Age 30 (692 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 30 (692 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.

**Age 31 (693 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 31 (693 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 32 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 32 (694 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Dorin Eding came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Dorin Eding born (male)
**Age 33 (695 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 33 (695 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 34

**Age 34 (696 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same.
**Age 34 (696 TR)** — His wife bore twins — Voran Eding and Ista Eding. The labour was long and hard; she was weakened for months. Both children lived. twins Voran Eding and Ista Eding born (twins)
**Age 35 (697 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 35 (697 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 35 (697 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Voran Eding died (age 1)
**Age 36 (698 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 36 (698 TR)** — Dorin Eding died young — a child still. Dorin Eding died (age 4)
**Age 37 (699 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 37 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 38 (700 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 38 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 39 (701 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.

**Age 39 (701 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 39 (701 TR)** — His son Yorin Eding married Welith Uling. Yorin Eding married Welith Uling (peasant, age 16)
**Age 40 (702 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Harlan Eding. week 40 Harlan Eding born (male)
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Yorin Eding's child born (male, Yorin Eding age 19)

**Age 42 (704 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
**Age 42 (704 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 7
**Age 43 (705 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 43 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (705 TR)** — Yorin Eding left home to make his own way. Yorin Eding left home (age 21)
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 45 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 46 (708 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler.
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (710 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 48 (710 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — Harlan Eding died young — a child still. Harlan Eding died (age 8)

**Age 50 (712 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees.
**Age 50 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the hip or groin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1).
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — His daughter Ista Eding married Fardan Yoring. Ista Eding married Fardan Yoring (lia_kavair, age 23)
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — Ista Eding left home to make his own way. Ista Eding left home (age 19)

**Age 54 (716 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.
**Age 54 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 55 (717 TR)** — The meeting was short. His master listed the errors — the timing, the noise, the unnecessary exposure — and told him what it had cost. The penalty was financial and was taken from his cut for six months. He understood that the financial penalty was not the point. The point was the knowledge that they knew, and that he was still alive because they had decided he was.
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Noric Rivergate (merchant)
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — Yorin Eding's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Yorin Eding's face. Yorin Eding's child died (age 0)

He and his child no longer spoke. **Age 49 (711 TR)** — Harlan Eding died young — a child still. Harlan Eding died (age 8) **Age 50 (712 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees. **Age 50 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 51 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (714 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the hip or groin. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in agility (−1). **Age 52 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 52 (714 TR)** — His daughter Ista Eding married Fardan Yoring. Ista Eding married Fardan Yoring (lia_kavair, age 23) **Age 53 (715 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 53 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 53 (715 TR)** — Ista Eding left home to make his own way. Ista Eding left home (age 19) **Age 54 (716 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why. **Age 54 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 55 (717 TR)** — The meeting was short. His master listed the errors — the timing, the noise, the unnecessary exposure — and told him what it had cost. The penalty was financial and was taken from his cut for six months. He understood that the financial penalty was not the point. The point was the knowledge that they knew, and that he was still alive because they had decided he was. **Age 55 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 57 (719 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Noric Rivergate (merchant) **Age 58 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 58 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 58 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (720 TR)** — Yorin Eding's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Yorin Eding's face. Yorin Eding's child died (age 0)
name: Orin Eding

narrative: **Age 18 (686 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 19 (687 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.

**Age 20 (688 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.

**Age 21 (689 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.

**Age 22 (690 TR)** — The mark had come home early, or the watch had changed its route, or someone had talked — he never found out which. He went to ground for three weeks in a safe house near the tanners' quarter, eating dried meat and listening to footsteps in the street above. Nothing came of it. It was two years before he stopped expecting it to.
**Age 23 (691 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.

**Age 24 (692 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
**Age 25 (693 TR)** — The room is dry and the door bolts from the inside and the contact will come tomorrow or the day after. He has been in worse places. He counts the ceiling beams and waits.
**Age 26 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 27 (695 TR)** — His Shadow told him simply that the master wanted a word. The meeting was brief, the new responsibilities outlined without ceremony, the cut arrangement revised. He walked out into the evening understanding that he had moved into a different kind of exposure, with a different kind of reward.
**Age 28 (696 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Emra Mason (artisan, age 25)
**Age 29 (697 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 29 (697 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Theric Vorkin. week 40 Theric Vorkin born (male)
**Age 30 (698 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 30 (698 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 31 (699 TR)** — The silver plate had come from a noble's household and needed to be out of recognisable form within a week. He spent three days finding the right buyer — not the obvious one, who would have been watched, but a goldsmith two towns distant who asked no questions when the price was right.
**Age 32 (700 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 32 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (701 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 34 (702 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
**Age 34 (702 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 6
**Age 35 (703 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Rhoric Walkin (lia_kavair)
**Age 35 (703 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 36 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 37 (705 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.

**Age 37 (705 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 38 (706 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 38 (706 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Lara Vorkin — survived. week 40 Lara Vorkin born (female)

**Age 39 (707 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 40 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 40 (708 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.

**Age 41 (709 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 41 (709 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 42 (710 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.

**Age 43 (711 TR)** — He sees the man across a temple on holy day — a merchant he lifted a seal from three years ago. The merchant looks healthy and prosperous and is praying with apparent sincerity. He finds a pillar to stand behind.

**Age 43 (711 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 44 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (712 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
**Age 45 (713 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the chest or torso that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 45 (713 TR)** — His son Theric Vorkin married Annis Malsen. Theric Vorkin married Annis Malsen (lia_kavair, age 16)
**Age 45 (713 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Theric Vorkin's child born (male, Theric Vorkin age 16)
**Age 46 (714 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 46 (714 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.

**Age 47 (715 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 47 (715 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 18
**Age 48 (716 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 48 (716 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 49 (717 TR)** — The years finally told. He moved more carefully, tired more quickly. The illness left a lasting mark: strength −1, stamina −1.
**Age 49 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 50 (718 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day.
**Age 51 (719 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 51 (719 TR)** — Emra Mason died. They had been married 23 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Emra Mason died (approx. age 48)
**Age 51 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (720 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 52 (720 TR)** — Theric Vorkin left home to make his own way. Theric Vorkin left home (age 23)

narrative: **Age 18 (692 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 19 (693 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Levan Westmarket (merchant)

**Age 20 (694 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (695 TR)** — A disagreement that should have been small became something permanent. He was not sure who was more at fault. Fell out with Levan Westmarket
**Age 22 (696 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 23 (697 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Ulra Woling (lia_kavair, age 20)
**Age 23 (697 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 24 (698 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 24 (698 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Edric Yorman born (male)
**Age 25 (699 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 26 (700 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 27 (701 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 27 (701 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Ceris Yorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Ceris Yorman born (female)

**Age 28 (702 TR)** — The other apprentice got the better tasks, the master's patience, the benefit of the doubt. He got the corrections and the night work. He learned both crafts — the craft itself, and the craft of being the one who is not the favourite.

**Age 29 (703 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 29 (703 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 31 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 31 (705 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 32 (706 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
**Age 32 (706 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Rara Yorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Rara Yorman born (female)
**Age 33 (707 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 33 (707 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.

**Age 34 (708 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 34 (708 TR)** — His wife bore twins — Andar Yorman and Torin Yorman. The labour was long and hard; she was weakened for months. Both children lived. twins Andar Yorman and Torin Yorman born (twins)
**Age 34 (708 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Andar Yorman died (age 0)

**Age 35 (709 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
**Age 35 (709 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 16

**Age 36 (710 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler.
**Age 36 (710 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.

**Age 37 (711 TR)** — The mark had come home early, or the watch had changed its route, or someone had talked — he never found out which. He went to ground for three weeks in a safe house near the tanners' quarter, eating dried meat and listening to footsteps in the street above. Nothing came of it. It was two years before he stopped expecting it to.

**Age 38 (712 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
**Age 39 (713 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 39 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 43 (717 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.
**Age 43 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 45 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 20 (700 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (701 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 22 (702 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 23 (703 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 24 (704 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 25 (705 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 26 (706 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 27 (707 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.
**Age 28 (708 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Alda Maring (lia_kavair, age 28)

**Age 28 (708 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 29 (709 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 29 (709 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
**Age 30 (710 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 31 (711 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.

**Age 31 (711 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — blind Joran Karman born (male, blind)
**Age 32 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (713 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 33 (713 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on.

**Age 34 (714 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
**Age 34 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 35 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 35 (715 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
**Age 36 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (717 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 38 (718 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 38 (718 TR)** — His child — the one he had feared for — had made something of themselves. Not what he had once imagined, but real. He felt something loosen in his chest.

**Age 39 (719 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 40 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 40 (720 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.

Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement. **Age 20 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 21 (713 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon. **Age 22 (714 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 23 (715 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted. **Age 24 (716 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Annis Horsen (peasant, age 18) **Age 24 (716 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer. **Age 25 (717 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences. **Age 26 (718 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his. **Age 26 (718 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting. **Age 27 (719 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity. **Age 27 (719 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Leda Salkin. week 40 Leda Salkin born (female) **Age 28 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 28 (720 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
name: Arven Salkin

narrative: **Age 18 (716 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 19 (717 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.

**Age 20 (718 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (719 TR)** — He travelled far with a caravan, seeing parts of Kaldor few of his neighbours ever would.
**Age 22 (720 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
