name: Jorin Wolson

narrative: **Age 18 (664 TR)** — He travelled far with a caravan, seeing parts of Kaldor few of his neighbours ever would.
**Age 19 (665 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.

**Age 20 (666 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (667 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 23 (669 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Gotar Morman
**Age 24 (670 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

**Age 25 (671 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
**Age 26 (672 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 27 (673 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 28 (674 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 29 (675 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 30 (676 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 31 (677 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 32 (678 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 33 (679 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 34 (680 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 35 (681 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
**Age 36 (682 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 37 (683 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 38 (684 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 39 (685 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 41 (687 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (688 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (689 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.

**Age 44 (690 TR)** — He saw the face across the market — older, different clothes, but the same hands, the same set of the jaw. The man who had robbed him on the Tashal road three years ago was buying onions from a stall ten feet away. He stood very still for a moment and then made a decision about what kind of person he was.
**Age 45 (691 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 46 (692 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 47 (693 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Varen Goldcloak (merchant)
**Age 48 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (696 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 51 (697 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.

**Age 52 (698 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 53 (699 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees.
**Age 54 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 56 (702 TR)** — The meeting was short. His master listed the errors — the timing, the noise, the unnecessary exposure — and told him what it had cost. The penalty was financial and was taken from his cut for six months. He understood that the financial penalty was not the point. The point was the knowledge that they knew, and that he was still alive because they had decided he was.
**Age 57 (703 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 58 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 59 (705 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler.
**Age 60 (706 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
**Age 61 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 62 (708 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.
**Age 63 (709 TR)** — He stopped fighting what he could not change. It was, strangely, freeing.
**Age 64 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 65 (711 TR)** — He had trusted the man for four years. The arrest made no sense until he thought back to who had known about the job, and then it made perfect sense. He was out of the city before they came for him — barely. He did not go back for a long time, and when he did, his former colleague was no longer a problem.
**Age 66 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 68 (714 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day.
**Age 69 (715 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 70 (716 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.

**Age 71 (717 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 72 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 73 (719 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the thigh that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 74 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

name: Tovan Dorson

narrative: **Age 18 (670 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 19 (671 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.

**Age 20 (672 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.

**Age 21 (673 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

**Age 22 (674 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees.
**Age 23 (675 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Annis Moring (lia_kavair, age 19)
**Age 23 (675 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 7
**Age 24 (676 TR)** — The man at the next table used the word without thinking, mid-sentence, and then corrected himself. They did not make eye contact. Both of them found somewhere else to look.

**Age 25 (677 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day.
**Age 26 (678 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
**Age 26 (678 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
**Age 27 (679 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Woran Lorsen (lia_kavair)
**Age 28 (680 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
**Age 29 (681 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 30 (682 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 31 (683 TR)** — A lord offered endowment for the chapel and expected pastoral access in return. The arrangement suited both parties and neither was entirely comfortable with it.
**Age 31 (683 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 32 (684 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 32 (684 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 33 (685 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 33 (685 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 34 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (687 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (688 TR)** — Annis Moring died. They had been married 13 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Annis Moring died (approx. age 32)

**Age 37 (689 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Orith Horson became a close friend

**Age 38 (690 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
**Age 39 (691 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 40 (692 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
**Age 41 (693 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.

**Age 42 (694 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.
**Age 43 (695 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 44 (696 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 45 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 46 (698 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 47 (699 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 48 (700 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 49 (701 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Zoric Valing
**Age 50 (702 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 51 (703 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.

**Age 52 (704 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Halden Norman

**Age 53 (705 TR)** — The young person he had invested time in had found their footing. He heard it from others before he saw it himself. He was glad, and slightly surprised at how glad. Zoric Valing found their own way
**Age 54 (706 TR)** — He reached out, or they did. The thing between them was old enough to look smaller than it had. Reconciled with Halden Norman
**Age 55 (707 TR)** — Someone he had trusted turned his confidence against him — for coin, for advantage, or from spite he never understood. Betrayed by Halden Norman
**Age 56 (708 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 57 (709 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 58 (710 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.

**Age 59 (711 TR)** — He had trusted the man for four years. The arrest made no sense until he thought back to who had known about the job, and then it made perfect sense. He was out of the city before they came for him — barely. He did not go back for a long time, and when he did, his former colleague was no longer a problem.
**Age 59 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 60 (712 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
**Age 60 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (713 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 62 (714 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 63 (715 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 64 (716 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more.

**Age 65 (717 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same.
**Age 66 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 67 (719 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Halden Norman became an enemy
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 68 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (670 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him. **Age 19 (671 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers. **Age 20 (672 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement. **Age 21 (673 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences. **Age 22 (674 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees. **Age 23 (675 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Annis Moring (lia_kavair, age 19) **Age 23 (675 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 7 **Age 24 (676 TR)** — The man at the next table used the word without thinking, mid-sentence, and then corrected himself. They did not make eye contact. Both of them found somewhere else to look. **Age 25 (677 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day. **Age 26 (678 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally. **Age 26 (678 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words. **Age 27 (679 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Woran Lorsen (lia_kavair) **Age 28 (680 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew. **Age 29 (681 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 30 (682 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his. **Age 31 (683 TR)** — A lord offered endowment for the chapel and expected pastoral access in return. The arrangement suited both parties and neither was entirely comfortable with it. **Age 31 (683 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it. **Age 32 (684 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 32 (684 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it. **Age 33 (685 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 33 (685 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later. **Age 34 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 35 (687 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 36 (688 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 36 (688 TR)** — Annis Moring died. They had been married 13 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Annis Moring died (approx. age 32) **Age 37 (689 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Orith Horson became a close friend **Age 38 (690 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately. **Age 39 (691 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 40 (692 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question. **Age 41 (693 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 42 (694 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why. **Age 43 (695 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands. **Age 44 (696 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 45 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 46 (698 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard. **Age 47 (699 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill. **Age 48 (700 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation. **Age 49 (701 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Zoric Valing **Age 50 (702 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community. **Age 51 (703 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours. **Age 51 (703 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it. **Age 52 (704 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Halden Norman **Age 53 (705 TR)** — The young person he had invested time in had found their footing. He heard it from others before he saw it himself. He was glad, and slightly surprised at how glad. Zoric Valing found their own way **Age 54 (706 TR)** — He reached out, or they did. The thing between them was old enough to look smaller than it had. Reconciled with Halden Norman **Age 55 (707 TR)** — Someone he had trusted turned his confidence against him — for coin, for advantage, or from spite he never understood. Betrayed by Halden Norman **Age 56 (708 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues. **Age 57 (709 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely. **Age 58 (710 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike. **Age 59 (711 TR)** — He had trusted the man for four years. The arrest made no sense until he thought back to who had known about the job, and then it made perfect sense. He was out of the city before they came for him — barely. He did not go back for a long time, and when he did, his former colleague was no longer a problem. **Age 59 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 60 (712 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it. **Age 60 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 61 (713 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 62 (714 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it. **Age 63 (715 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 64 (716 TR)** — Through deed or patience, his name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 65 (717 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same. **Age 66 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 67 (719 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Halden Norman became an enemy **Age 68 (720 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 68 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
name: Alric Orkin

narrative: **Age 18 (676 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 19 (677 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Estra Rorson died

**Age 20 (678 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (679 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
**Age 22 (680 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.

**Age 23 (681 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
**Age 24 (682 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Laric Marson
**Age 26 (684 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 27 (685 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 28 (686 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
**Age 29 (687 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 30 (688 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 31 (689 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Kela Markin (lia_kavair, age 24)
**Age 31 (689 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 33 (691 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.

**Age 34 (692 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.

**Age 34 (692 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.

**Age 35 (693 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 37 (695 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
**Age 37 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 38 (696 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.

**Age 38 (696 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — He travelled far with a caravan, seeing parts of Kaldor few of his neighbours ever would.
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — Kela Markin died. They had been married 8 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Kela Markin died (approx. age 32)
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 41 (699 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — A boundary marker, a contested will, a neighbour's greed — the dispute dragged on.
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (704 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 50 (708 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.
**Age 53 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 61 (719 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 62 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

narrative: **Age 18 (682 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.

**Age 19 (683 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.

**Age 21 (685 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler.
**Age 22 (686 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.

**Age 23 (687 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 24 (688 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 25 (689 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 26 (690 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.

**Age 27 (691 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
**Age 28 (692 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 29 (693 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 30 (694 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price.
**Age 31 (695 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
**Age 32 (696 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.

**Age 33 (697 TR)** — His Shadow told him simply that the master wanted a word. The meeting was brief, the new responsibilities outlined without ceremony, the cut arrangement revised. He walked out into the evening understanding that he had moved into a different kind of exposure, with a different kind of reward.
**Age 34 (698 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.

**Age 35 (699 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees.
**Age 36 (700 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
**Age 37 (701 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end. It changed how he thought about faith.

**Age 38 (702 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.

**Age 39 (703 TR)** — He had trusted the man for four years. The arrest made no sense until he thought back to who had known about the job, and then it made perfect sense. He was out of the city before they came for him — barely. He did not go back for a long time, and when he did, his former colleague was no longer a problem.

**Age 40 (704 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence.

**Age 41 (705 TR)** — The silver plate had come from a noble's household and needed to be out of recognisable form within a week. He spent three days finding the right buyer — not the obvious one, who would have been watched, but a goldsmith two towns distant who asked no questions when the price was right.
**Age 42 (706 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
**Age 43 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (708 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 46 (710 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
**Age 47 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 48 (712 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day.
**Age 49 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 50 (714 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 51 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 52 (716 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.
**Age 53 (717 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Narith Valsen died

**Age 54 (718 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.

**Age 55 (719 TR)** — He had been careful about it for three years — moving money, building contacts outside the usual networks, maintaining a cover that the guild did not fully know about. When he went, he went quietly and completely, leaving a city that had been home for fifteen years. He never used the guild slang again.
**Age 56 (720 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
narrativeText: **Age 18 (682 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity. **Age 19 (683 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences. **Age 20 (684 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement. **Age 21 (685 TR)** — The peddler's cart was real, the licence from the Mangai was real, and the rounds he made every market day were real. The goods he carried on his person on the way back were not something the Mangai needed to know about. Within six months, his neighbours thought of him simply as the peddler. **Age 22 (686 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated. **Age 23 (687 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard. **Age 24 (688 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his. **Age 25 (689 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 26 (690 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk. **Age 27 (691 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working. **Age 28 (692 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 29 (693 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms. **Age 30 (694 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. He paid the price. **Age 31 (695 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt. **Age 32 (696 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill. **Age 33 (697 TR)** — His Shadow told him simply that the master wanted a word. The meeting was brief, the new responsibilities outlined without ceremony, the cut arrangement revised. He walked out into the evening understanding that he had moved into a different kind of exposure, with a different kind of reward. **Age 34 (698 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour. **Age 35 (699 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees. **Age 36 (700 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year. **Age 37 (701 TR)** — Someone he loved called for a priest at the end. It changed how he thought about faith. **Age 38 (702 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like. **Age 39 (703 TR)** — He had trusted the man for four years. The arrest made no sense until he thought back to who had known about the job, and then it made perfect sense. He was out of the city before they came for him — barely. He did not go back for a long time, and when he did, his former colleague was no longer a problem. **Age 40 (704 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence. **Age 41 (705 TR)** — The silver plate had come from a noble's household and needed to be out of recognisable form within a week. He spent three days finding the right buyer — not the obvious one, who would have been watched, but a goldsmith two towns distant who asked no questions when the price was right. **Age 42 (706 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline. **Age 43 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 44 (708 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely. **Age 46 (710 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 48 (712 TR)** — He spent six weeks in a room above a tannery while the watch circulated descriptions. His guild contact brought money once a week and news when there was any. He learned patience in that room. He also learned what the ceiling of a tannery looks like at every hour of the day. **Age 49 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 50 (714 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community. **Age 51 (715 TR)** — He endured. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence. **Age 53 (717 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Narith Valsen died **Age 54 (718 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. He never used the guild slang again. **Age 56 (720 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
name: Kavan Ulman

narrative: **Age 18 (711 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.

**Age 19 (712 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.

**Age 20 (713 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (714 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 22 (715 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 23 (716 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 24 (717 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.

**Age 25 (718 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.

**Age 26 (719 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 27 (720 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Roric Warson died

name: Orith Gonson

narrative: **Age 18 (717 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.

**Age 19 (718 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.

**Age 20 (719 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (720 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.

name: Sera Malman

narrative: **Age 18 (665 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.

**Age 20 (667 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (668 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 22 (669 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 23 (670 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 24 (671 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
**Age 25 (672 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 26 (673 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 27 (674 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 28 (675 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Levan Coinsworth
**Age 29 (676 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.

**Age 30 (677 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 31 (678 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Varen (clergy)

**Age 33 (680 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 34 (681 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.

**Age 36 (683 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
**Age 37 (684 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 38 (685 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 39 (686 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 40 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 41 (688 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 43 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 44 (691 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Dalric Lorman
**Age 45 (692 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (693 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 47 (694 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Dalric Borsen
**Age 48 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (696 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 50 (697 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.

**Age 51 (698 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.

**Age 52 (699 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient.
**Age 53 (700 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.

**Age 54 (701 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Dalric Lorman became a close friend
**Age 55 (702 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 56 (703 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 57 (704 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 58 (705 TR)** — The creditors came. What she had built was gone.
**Age 59 (706 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 60 (707 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her.
**Age 61 (708 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 62 (709 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 63 (710 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 64 (711 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 65 (712 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 66 (713 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 67 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 68 (715 TR)** — Most of the women she had known as a young woman were dead. The world had been replaced around her, a face at a time.

**Age 69 (716 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 70 (717 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped.

**Age 71 (718 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 72 (719 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the head or face that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 73 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [guilded_innkeeper → unguilded]
