Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better. **Age 31 (678 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Varen (clergy) **Age 33 (680 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays. **Age 34 (681 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question. **Age 36 (683 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer. **Age 37 (684 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 38 (685 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show. **Age 39 (686 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option. **Age 40 (687 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 41 (688 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers. **Age 43 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 44 (691 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Dalric Lorman **Age 45 (692 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 46 (693 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 47 (694 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Dalric Borsen **Age 48 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 49 (696 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 50 (697 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it. **Age 51 (698 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow. **Age 52 (699 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient. **Age 53 (700 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 54 (701 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Dalric Lorman became a close friend **Age 55 (702 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community. **Age 56 (703 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 57 (704 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted. **Age 58 (705 TR)** — The creditors came. What she had built was gone. **Age 59 (706 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers. **Age 60 (707 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her. **Age 61 (708 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 62 (709 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 63 (710 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete. **Age 64 (711 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk. **Age 65 (712 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 66 (713 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 67 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 68 (715 TR)** — Most of the women she had known as a young woman were dead. The world had been replaced around her, a face at a time. **Age 69 (716 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why. **Age 70 (717 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped. **Age 71 (718 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that. **Age 72 (719 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the head or face that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar. **Age 73 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 58** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [guilded_innkeeper → unguilded]
name: Deva Valson

narrative: **Age 18 (671 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −3.
**Age 19 (672 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.

**Age 20 (673 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (674 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.

**Age 22 (675 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 23 (676 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.

**Age 24 (677 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
**Age 26 (679 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 27 (680 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward.
**Age 28 (681 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 29 (682 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
**Age 30 (683 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.

**Age 32 (685 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 35 (688 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 36 (689 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.

**Age 37 (690 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 38 (691 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Rorik Wolson
**Age 39 (692 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 40 (693 TR)** — The debt that had dogged her was finally cleared.

**Age 41 (694 TR)** — Her mother had not wanted to go. She had insisted, because the alternative was managing a dying woman in three rooms with two children underfoot. The Peonian house took her without fuss. She visited every day. Her mother died in spring, quietly, with a sister holding her hand because she had arrived twenty minutes too late.
**Age 43 (696 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Estra Arsen

**Age 44 (697 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness caused serious permanent damage: strength −3, stamina −1.
**Age 46 (699 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (700 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).

**Age 49 (702 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 50 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (708 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 56 (709 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 57 (710 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.

**Age 58 (711 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.
**Age 59 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 60 (713 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.
**Age 61 (714 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 62 (715 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 63 (716 TR)** — She became the person others consulted. This had been happening for years; it was only recently that she recognised it as a pattern.
**Age 64 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (718 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 67 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

name: Leira Dalsen

narrative: **Age 18 (677 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.

**Age 19 (678 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

**Age 20 (679 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 23 (682 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
**Age 24 (683 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Warin Toring (guilded_innkeeper, age 27)
**Age 24 (683 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
**Age 25 (684 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 25 (684 TR)** — The child came at term and did not breathe. She had been so close. week 38
**Age 27 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 27 (686 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Geira Toring — lived. week 40 Geira Toring born (female)
**Age 29 (688 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
**Age 29 (688 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 30 (689 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 30 (689 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 31 (690 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 31 (690 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 32 (691 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 32 (691 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 9
**Age 33 (692 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her.
**Age 33 (692 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Dalric Toring lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Dalric Toring born (male)
**Age 34 (693 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Bram Dorman
**Age 34 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 35 (694 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 35 (694 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 36 (695 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
**Age 36 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 37 (696 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Kael Longhaul (merchant)

**Age 37 (696 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.

**Age 41 (700 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 41 (700 TR)** — Warin Toring died after 17 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Warin Toring died (approx. age 44)

**Age 42 (701 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 44 (703 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 45 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (704 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 45 (704 TR)** — Her daughter Geira Toring married Loran Millward. Geira Toring married Loran Millward (artisan, age 16)
**Age 46 (705 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 46 (705 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 46 (705 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Geira Toring's child born (female, Geira Toring age 19)

**Age 47 (706 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — Geira Toring left to make her own way. Geira Toring left home (age 22)
**Age 50 (709 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 50 (709 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 50 (709 TR)** — Her daughter Geira Toring remarried. Geira Toring remarried Aldric Sorsen (guilded_innkeeper, age 23)
**Age 51 (710 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Kael Longhaul has died
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — Her son Dalric Toring married Neira Sawyer. Dalric Toring married Neira Sawyer (artisan, age 17)
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 55 (714 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 55 (714 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 55 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — Geira Toring lost Marath Toring. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Geira Toring's child died (age 10)
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — Dalric Toring left to make her own way. Dalric Toring left home (age 24)
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (719 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.

**Age 61 (720 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — Dalric Toring's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Dalric Toring's child died (age 0)
narrativeText: **Age 18 (677 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice. **Age 19 (678 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination. **Age 20 (679 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 23 (682 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger. **Age 24 (683 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Warin Toring (guilded_innkeeper, age 27) **Age 24 (683 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it. **Age 25 (684 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 25 (684 TR)** — The child came at term and did not breathe. She had been so close. week 38 **Age 27 (686 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 27 (686 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Geira Toring — lived. week 40 Geira Toring born (female) **Age 29 (688 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift. **Age 29 (688 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days. **Age 30 (689 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 30 (689 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough. **Age 31 (690 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers. **Age 31 (690 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine. **Age 32 (691 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk. **Age 32 (691 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 9 **Age 33 (692 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her. **Age 33 (692 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Dalric Toring lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Dalric Toring born (male) **Age 34 (693 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Bram Dorman **Age 34 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 35 (694 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 35 (694 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day. **Age 36 (695 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer. **Age 36 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 37 (696 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Kael Longhaul (merchant) **Age 37 (696 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 40 (699 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 40 (699 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed. **Age 41 (700 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces. **Age 41 (700 TR)** — Warin Toring died after 17 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Warin Toring died (approx. age 44) **Age 42 (701 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her. **Age 43 (702 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time. **Age 43 (702 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 44 (703 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 45 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 45 (704 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 45 (704 TR)** — Her daughter Geira Toring married Loran Millward. Geira Toring married Loran Millward (artisan, age 16) **Age 46 (705 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question. **Age 46 (705 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 46 (705 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Geira Toring's child born (female, Geira Toring age 19) **Age 47 (706 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better. **Age 48 (707 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 49 (708 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 49 (708 TR)** — Geira Toring left to make her own way. Geira Toring left home (age 22) **Age 50 (709 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 50 (709 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain. **Age 50 (709 TR)** — Her daughter Geira Toring remarried. Geira Toring remarried Aldric Sorsen (guilded_innkeeper, age 23) **Age 51 (710 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Kael Longhaul has died **Age 52 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 53 (712 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1. **Age 53 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (713 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 54 (713 TR)** — Her son Dalric Toring married Neira Sawyer. Dalric Toring married Neira Sawyer (artisan, age 17) **Age 54 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 55 (714 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 55 (714 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left. **Age 55 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (715 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 56 (715 TR)** — Geira Toring lost Marath Toring. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Geira Toring's child died (age 10) **Age 57 (716 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues. **Age 57 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 57 (716 TR)** — Dalric Toring left to make her own way. Dalric Toring left home (age 24) **Age 59 (718 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs. **Age 59 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 59 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 60 (719 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline. **Age 61 (720 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow. **Age 61 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 61 (720 TR)** — Dalric Toring's infant did not survive. She felt the grief twice — for the child and for her own child's loss. Dalric Toring's child died (age 0)
name: Breda Vorson

narrative: **Age 18 (683 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 19 (684 TR)** — She worked alongside a artisan through a season, watching and helping.

**Age 20 (685 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (686 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
**Age 22 (687 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Orin Salman (guilded_innkeeper, age 21)
**Age 22 (687 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.

**Age 23 (688 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 23 (688 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 24 (689 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 24 (689 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.

**Age 25 (690 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.
**Age 25 (690 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.

**Age 26 (691 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.

**Age 26 (691 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Arwen Salman. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Arwen Salman born (female)

**Age 27 (692 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 27 (692 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 28 (693 TR)** — The harvest was kind. There was enough to sell as well as store.
**Age 29 (694 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the head or face that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
**Age 29 (694 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 30 (695 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 31 (696 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 31 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 32 (697 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.

**Age 32 (697 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Dalric Salman. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Dalric Salman born (male)
**Age 33 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 33 (698 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 34 (699 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 34 (699 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 36 (701 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Rhoric Parman

**Age 37 (702 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 37 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 38 (703 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 38 (703 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 39 (704 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 5
**Age 40 (705 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness left a lasting mark: strength −1, stamina −1.
**Age 40 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 41 (706 TR)** — The sisters had worked for two weeks. They had been honest with her about what was coming and had given her time to say what she needed to say. She was grateful for that. She was also bereft. The two feelings sat together without resolving.
**Age 41 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (707 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.

**Age 44 (709 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.
**Age 44 (709 TR)** — Her daughter Arwen Salman married Idris Borman. Arwen Salman married Idris Borman (guilded_innkeeper, age 20)
**Age 45 (710 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 46 (711 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 46 (711 TR)** — The child came at term and did not breathe. She had been so close. week 38
**Age 47 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (712 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 49 (714 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 49 (714 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.

**Age 50 (715 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 51 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 51 (716 TR)** — Arwen Salman left to make her own way. Arwen Salman left home (age 25)
**Age 52 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (719 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Karith Oldcoin (merchant)
**Age 54 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (719 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Arwen Salman's child born (male, Arwen Salman age 28)
**Age 55 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

Arwen Salman married Idris Borman (guilded_innkeeper, age 20) **Age 45 (710 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline. **Age 46 (711 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous. **Age 46 (711 TR)** — The child came at term and did not breathe. She had been so close. week 38 **Age 47 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (712 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 49 (714 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 49 (714 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically. **Age 50 (715 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces. **Age 51 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 51 (716 TR)** — Arwen Salman left to make her own way. Arwen Salman left home (age 25) **Age 52 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 52 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (719 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Karith Oldcoin (merchant) **Age 54 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 54 (719 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Arwen Salman's child born (male, Arwen Salman age 28) **Age 55 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 55 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
name: Rira Malson

narrative: **Age 18 (689 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 19 (690 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.

**Age 20 (691 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 22 (693 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.

**Age 25 (696 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 26 (697 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.

**Age 27 (698 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 28 (699 TR)** — The harvest failed. She stretched what remained as far as it would go.
**Age 30 (701 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 31 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 32 (703 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.

**Age 33 (704 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 34 (705 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her.

**Age 35 (706 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 36 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (708 TR)** — The creditors came. What she had built was gone.
**Age 38 (709 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 39 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 40 (711 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.

**Age 41 (712 TR)** — The last coin went on the fourth day of the cold. She had plans — there are always plans — but plans require a material floor and she had passed below it. She knew the Peonian house was on the south side of the market quarter. She went there. A sister opened the door and did not ask questions about how she had arrived here. She received a meal, a bed, a day's grace. She has been operating at the margin between those two realities ever since: the bed and what comes before the bed.
**Age 42 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (714 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 44 (715 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 45 (716 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (717 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Dera Brankin (destitute)

**Age 47 (718 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.
**Age 48 (719 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 37** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [guilded_innkeeper → unguilded]
**Age 41** *(class change)* — Fell below ruination into genuine homelessness [unguilded → destitute]

name: Gara Tarman

narrative: **Age 18 (695 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 19 (696 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.

**Age 20 (697 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (698 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.

**Age 22 (699 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.

**Age 23 (700 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.

**Age 24 (701 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 26 (703 TR)** — She followed. There was no clean answer and she chose the one that kept her close to him.

**Age 27 (704 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.
**Age 29 (706 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Corin Aling (guilded_innkeeper, age 28)

**Age 29 (706 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Deva Aling. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Deva Aling born (female)

**Age 30 (707 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 30 (707 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Tavin Aling lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Tavin Aling born (male)
**Age 31 (708 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 31 (708 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 32 (709 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 32 (709 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.
**Age 33 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 33 (710 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 34 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 35 (712 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her.
**Age 35 (712 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 36 (713 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 36 (713 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.
**Age 37 (714 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 37 (714 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 38 (715 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Rora Malson
**Age 38 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (719 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. She paid the price.
**Age 42 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 43 (720 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.

**Age 43 (720 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — deaf Elora Aling born (female, deaf)

**Age 30 (713 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her.
**Age 31 (714 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
**Age 32 (715 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 33 (716 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Joran Idson (guilded_innkeeper, age 33)
**Age 33 (716 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 15
**Age 34 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 34 (717 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — simple Kavan Idson born (male, simple-minded)

**Age 35 (718 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 35 (718 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Theric Idson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Theric Idson born (male)
**Age 37 (720 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 37 (720 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 25
**Age 37 (720 TR)** — She lost Kavan Idson — the simple-minded child she had given so much to. Kavan Idson died (age 3)
narrativeText: **Age 18 (701 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth. **Age 20 (703 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (704 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work. **Age 22 (705 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork. **Age 23 (706 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with. **Age 24 (707 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that. **Age 25 (708 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. She paid the price. **Age 26 (709 TR)** — When the column moved through, she moved with it. It was not a decision so much as a failure to stay behind. **Age 27 (710 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward. **Age 28 (711 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 29 (712 TR)** — The harvest was kind. There was enough to sell as well as store. **Age 30 (713 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her. **Age 31 (714 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down. **Age 32 (715 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 33 (716 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Joran Idson (guilded_innkeeper, age 33) **Age 33 (716 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 15 **Age 34 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 34 (717 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — simple Kavan Idson born (male, simple-minded) **Age 35 (718 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better. **Age 35 (718 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Theric Idson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Theric Idson born (male) **Age 37 (720 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted. **Age 37 (720 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 25 **Age 37 (720 TR)** — She lost Kavan Idson — the simple-minded child she had given so much to. Kavan Idson died (age 3)
name: Garith Garsen

narrative: **Age 18 (707 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Trovan Sorson (peasant, age 21)
**Age 18 (707 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.
**Age 19 (708 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Corin Korsen
**Age 19 (708 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.

**Age 20 (709 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 20 (709 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 21 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 21 (710 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Orlan Sorson. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Orlan Sorson born (male)
**Age 22 (711 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Hrolf Sorson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Hrolf Sorson born (male)
**Age 22 (711 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.
**Age 23 (712 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 23 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 24 (713 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
**Age 24 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 25 (714 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.

**Age 25 (714 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 26 (715 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.

**Age 26 (715 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 27 (716 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 27 (716 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 29 (718 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

**Age 30 (719 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 30 (719 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 31 (720 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 31 (720 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

name: Talith Vorman

narrative: **Age 18 (713 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.

**Age 20 (715 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (716 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 23 (718 TR)** — She worked alongside a artisan through a season, watching and helping.

**Age 24 (719 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 25 (720 TR)** — When the column moved through, she moved with it. It was not a decision so much as a failure to stay behind.
