name: Arith Salman

narrative: **Age 18 (719 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 19 (720 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Halla Dalsen died

name: Ida Lorman

narrative: **Age 18 (667 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 19 (668 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 20 (669 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (670 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Edric Elson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Edric Elson born (male)
**Age 22 (671 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her.

**Age 23 (672 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 24 (673 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 24 (673 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 25 (674 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 25 (674 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 26 (675 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 27 (676 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 27 (676 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.

**Age 28 (677 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 28 (677 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 29 (678 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 29 (678 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30 (679 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 30 (679 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 31 (680 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
**Age 31 (680 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 32 (681 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 32 (681 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Edric Elson fostered (age 11)
**Age 33 (682 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 33 (682 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 34 (683 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (684 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 38 (687 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 38 (687 TR)** — Her son Edric Elson married Calla Edson. Edric Elson married Calla Edson (guilded_innkeeper, age 16)
**Age 39 (688 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 39 (688 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 40 (689 TR)** — The creditors came. What she had built was gone.
**Age 40 (689 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (689 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Edric Elson's child born (female, Edric Elson age 19)
**Age 41 (690 TR)** — She smelled something that stopped her dead in the street. It brought back a whole season she had thought she had forgotten.
**Age 41 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (691 TR)** — Her back seized up at an inconvenient moment. She managed. She told no one how much it hurt.
**Age 42 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 43 (692 TR)** — The same dream visited her three nights. She woke each time unsettled and unable to say why.
**Age 43 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 44 (693 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. She paid the price.
**Age 44 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 45 (694 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 45 (694 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 46 (695 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 46 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (696 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (697 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 48 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (698 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 50 (699 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.

**Age 51 (700 TR)** — The reeve's new assistant did the assessment while the reeve was ill and got the labour days wrong — the calculation for a widow's holding was done using the full holding rate, not the reduced rate that the village had operated on since her husband died. She pointed this out to the reeve when he recovered. The reeve corrected it and apologised in the specific way that reeves apologise when an error worked in the lord's favour: briefly and without dwelling on the amount that would have been overpaid. She noted the correction in her own accounts.
**Age 52 (701 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 53 (702 TR)** — When the column moved through, she moved with it. It was not a decision so much as a failure to stay behind.
**Age 53 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 54 (703 TR)** — She fell asleep at table during a gathering. She was told about it the following day. Multiple times.
**Age 54 (703 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.

**Age 55 (704 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 55 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (705 TR)** — She drank more than was wise and behaved in ways she thought about for several days afterwards.
**Age 56 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (706 TR)** — She stopped at an old waymarker she knew well. She had never stopped there before. She could not say what made her stop.
**Age 57 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (707 TR)** — She undid in a moment something it had taken her weeks to produce. She started again without complaint.
**Age 58 (707 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 59 (708 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 59 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (709 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 61 (710 TR)** — The column wintered in a town and she did not leave with it in the spring. She was tired of the road and the town had what the road did not.
*(Ages 61–62 (710–711 TR): uneventful years)*
**Age 63 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 64 (713 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
**Age 65 (714 TR)** — Something that had always been there stopped. She noticed the quiet before she identified what had gone.
**Age 65 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 66 (715 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 67 (716 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 68 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 69 (718 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 70 (719 TR)** — She made a remark that landed badly in ways she had not anticipated. She apologised. She is not sure it helped.
**Age 70 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 71 (720 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 71 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [guilded_innkeeper → unguilded]
narrativeText: **Age 18 (667 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better. **Age 19 (668 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 20 (669 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (670 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Edric Elson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Edric Elson born (male) **Age 22 (671 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her. **Age 23 (672 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct. **Age 24 (673 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 24 (673 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain. **Age 25 (674 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her. **Age 25 (674 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular. **Age 26 (675 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her. **Age 27 (676 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while. **Age 27 (676 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her. **Age 28 (677 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow. **Age 28 (677 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 29 (678 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option. **Age 29 (678 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 30 (679 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week. **Age 30 (679 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 31 (680 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone. **Age 31 (680 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it. **Age 32 (681 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next. **Age 32 (681 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Edric Elson fostered (age 11) **Age 33 (682 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 33 (682 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 34 (683 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 35 (684 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 35 (684 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 38 (687 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show. **Age 38 (687 TR)** — Her son Edric Elson married Calla Edson. Edric Elson married Calla Edson (guilded_innkeeper, age 16) **Age 39 (688 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 39 (688 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left. **Age 40 (689 TR)** — The creditors came. What she had built was gone. **Age 40 (689 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 40 (689 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Edric Elson's child born (female, Edric Elson age 19) **Age 41 (690 TR)** — She smelled something that stopped her dead in the street. It brought back a whole season she had thought she had forgotten. **Age 41 (690 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 42 (691 TR)** — Her back seized up at an inconvenient moment. She managed. She told no one how much it hurt. **Age 42 (691 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 43 (692 TR)** — The same dream visited her three nights. She woke each time unsettled and unable to say why. **Age 43 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 43 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 44 (693 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. She paid the price. **Age 44 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 45 (694 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at. **Age 45 (694 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 46 (695 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 46 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (696 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 47 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 48 (697 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted. **Age 48 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 48 (697 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 49 (698 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public. **Age 50 (699 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it. **Age 51 (700 TR)** — The reeve's new assistant did the assessment while the reeve was ill and got the labour days wrong — the calculation for a widow's holding was done using the full holding rate, not the reduced rate that the village had operated on since her husband died. She pointed this out to the reeve when he recovered. The reeve corrected it and apologised in the specific way that reeves apologise when an error worked in the lord's favour: briefly and without dwelling on the amount that would have been overpaid. She noted the correction in her own accounts. **Age 52 (701 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 53 (702 TR)** — When the column moved through, she moved with it. It was not a decision so much as a failure to stay behind. **Age 53 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 54 (703 TR)** — She fell asleep at table during a gathering. She was told about it the following day. Multiple times. **Age 54 (703 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough. **Age 55 (704 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was. **Age 55 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 56 (705 TR)** — She drank more than was wise and behaved in ways she thought about for several days afterwards. **Age 56 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (706 TR)** — She stopped at an old waymarker she knew well. She had never stopped there before. She could not say what made her stop. **Age 57 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 57 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 58 (707 TR)** — She undid in a moment something it had taken her weeks to produce. She started again without complaint. **Age 58 (707 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 59 (708 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 59 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 60 (709 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers. **Age 61 (710 TR)** — The column wintered in a town and she did not leave with it in the spring. She was tired of the road and the town had what the road did not. *(Ages 61–62 (710–711 TR): uneventful years)* **Age 63 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 64 (713 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 65 (714 TR)** — Something that had always been there stopped. She noticed the quiet before she identified what had gone. **Age 65 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 66 (715 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like. **Age 67 (716 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs. **Age 68 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 69 (718 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it. **Age 70 (719 TR)** — She made a remark that landed badly in ways she had not anticipated. She apologised. She is not sure it helped. **Age 70 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 71 (720 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why. **Age 71 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 40** *(class change)* — Lost livelihood to debt — engine routes urban→unguilded, rural→peasant [guilded_innkeeper → unguilded]
name: Tarath Elson

narrative: **Age 18 (673 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her.
**Age 19 (674 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.

**Age 20 (675 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (676 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.

**Age 22 (677 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.

**Age 23 (678 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.

**Age 24 (679 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
**Age 25 (680 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 26 (681 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Calla Horkin (guilded_innkeeper)
**Age 28 (683 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.

**Age 29 (684 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 30 (685 TR)** — She worked alongside a artisan through a season, watching and helping.

**Age 31 (686 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 32 (687 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward.

**Age 33 (688 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 34 (689 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 35 (690 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Mael Eding (guilded_innkeeper, age 41)
**Age 35 (690 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Varen Eding lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Varen Eding born (male)

**Age 36 (691 TR)** — The sisters had worked for two weeks. They had been honest with her about what was coming and had given her time to say what she needed to say. She was grateful for that. She was also bereft. The two feelings sat together without resolving.
**Age 38 (693 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 38 (693 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.

**Age 39 (694 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 39 (694 TR)** — She bore a child. Farath Eding came into the world healthy. week 40 Farath Eding born (female)

**Age 40 (695 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Calla Horkin became a close friend
**Age 41 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (696 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 15
**Age 42 (697 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.

**Age 42 (697 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 43 (698 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 43 (698 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 44 (699 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Edric Raling

**Age 44 (699 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — lame Bram Eding born (male, lame)
**Age 45 (700 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (700 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Varen Eding fostered (age 10)
**Age 46 (701 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 46 (701 TR)** — She lost Varen Eding — still a child, not yet grown. Varen Eding died (age 11)
**Age 47 (702 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 47 (702 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 48 (703 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Borik Parson
**Age 48 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (703 TR)** — She lost Farath Eding — still a child, not yet grown. Farath Eding died (age 9)

**Age 49 (704 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.
**Age 51 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (706 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 52 (707 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Calla Horkin has died
**Age 52 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (709 TR)** — Named outlaw — an almost unheard-of fate for a woman, but not impossible.
**Age 55 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (711 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 58 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 58 (713 TR)** — She caught herself watching the child too long, calculating futures. She stopped herself and went back to work. The watching started again an hour later.
**Age 59 (714 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Evan Erkin
**Age 59 (714 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 60 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 60 (715 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
**Age 62 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 62 (717 TR)** — The child she had carried every fear for stood before her, capable and here. She had been wrong about some of the worst of it. She was glad to have been wrong.
**Age 63 (718 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 63 (718 TR)** — Her son Bram Eding married Lara Horing. Bram Eding married Lara Horing (guilded_innkeeper, age 16)
**Age 64 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 64 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 65 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (720 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Bram Eding's child born (female, Bram Eding age 21)

She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better. **Age 39 (694 TR)** — She bore a child. Farath Eding came into the world healthy. week 40 Farath Eding born (female) **Age 40 (695 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Calla Horkin became a close friend **Age 41 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 41 (696 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 15 **Age 42 (697 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk. **Age 42 (697 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 43 (698 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth. **Age 43 (698 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would. **Age 44 (699 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Edric Raling **Age 44 (699 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — lame Bram Eding born (male, lame) **Age 45 (700 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 45 (700 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Varen Eding fostered (age 10) **Age 46 (701 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 46 (701 TR)** — She lost Varen Eding — still a child, not yet grown. Varen Eding died (age 11) **Age 47 (702 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers. **Age 47 (702 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 48 (703 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Borik Parson **Age 48 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 48 (703 TR)** — She lost Farath Eding — still a child, not yet grown. Farath Eding died (age 9) **Age 49 (704 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record. **Age 51 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 51 (706 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 52 (707 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Calla Horkin has died **Age 52 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 54 (709 TR)** — Named outlaw — an almost unheard-of fate for a woman, but not impossible. **Age 55 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 55 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 56 (711 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 58 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 58 (713 TR)** — She caught herself watching the child too long, calculating futures. She stopped herself and went back to work. The watching started again an hour later. **Age 59 (714 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Evan Erkin **Age 59 (714 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days. **Age 60 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 60 (715 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it. **Age 62 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 62 (717 TR)** — The child she had carried every fear for stood before her, capable and here. She had been wrong about some of the worst of it. She was glad to have been wrong. **Age 63 (718 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left. **Age 63 (718 TR)** — Her son Bram Eding married Lara Horing. Bram Eding married Lara Horing (guilded_innkeeper, age 16) **Age 64 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 64 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 65 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 65 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 65 (720 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Bram Eding's child born (female, Bram Eding age 21)
name: Alda Dalman

narrative: **Age 18 (679 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 19 (680 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.

**Age 20 (681 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (682 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.

**Age 22 (683 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 23 (684 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 24 (685 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Evan Fuller (artisan, age 30)
**Age 24 (685 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 25 (686 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 26 (687 TR)** — She bore a child. Berith Fuller came into the world healthy. week 40 Berith Fuller born (female)

**Age 27 (688 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 27 (688 TR)** — She bore a child. Kavan Fuller came into the world healthy. week 40 Kavan Fuller born (male)
**Age 27 (688 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Kavan Fuller died (age 0)

**Age 28 (689 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 28 (689 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 19
**Age 29 (690 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
**Age 29 (690 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 31 (692 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.
**Age 32 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 33 (694 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.

**Age 33 (694 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 34 (695 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (697 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Marta Uling
**Age 36 (697 TR)** — Evan Fuller died after 12 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Evan Fuller died (approx. age 42)
**Age 38 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 39 (700 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.

**Age 40 (701 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 40 (701 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 41 (702 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 41 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (703 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 43 (704 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 43 (704 TR)** — Her daughter Berith Fuller married Osric Salsen. Berith Fuller married Osric Salsen (peasant, age 16)
**Age 44 (705 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 44 (705 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 45 (706 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 45 (706 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
**Age 46 (707 TR)** — When the column moved through, she moved with it. It was not a decision so much as a failure to stay behind.
**Age 47 (708 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 47 (708 TR)** — Berith Fuller left to make her own way. Berith Fuller left home (age 21)

**Age 48 (709 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 48 (709 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.

**Age 49 (710 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
**Age 51 (712 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
**Age 51 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (712 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Berith Fuller's child born (female, Berith Fuller age 25)
**Age 52 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 53 (714 TR)** — The Ebasethe arrived before dawn and stayed until the crisis passed. She had sent for her because the midwife said to. She had not expected it to matter. The child was alive. She had a different understanding of the Peonian church after that — less abstract, more personal.
**Age 53 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (715 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Elara (clergy)
**Age 54 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 55 (716 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.
**Age 55 (716 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.

**Age 56 (717 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient.
**Age 56 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

name: Halla Malman

narrative: **Age 18 (685 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.

**Age 20 (687 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (688 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.

**Age 22 (689 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 23 (690 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Levan Alsen died
**Age 24 (691 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 25 (692 TR)** — She worked alongside a artisan through a season, watching and helping.
**Age 26 (693 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Gotar Maramin (noble)
**Age 27 (694 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 28 (695 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.

**Age 29 (696 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
**Age 30 (697 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
**Age 31 (698 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 32 (699 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Gotar Maramin
**Age 33 (700 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 34 (701 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Woran Dustmantle

**Age 35 (702 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.
**Age 36 (703 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Nora Torson
**Age 37 (704 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 38 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 40 (707 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 41 (708 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 43 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 44 (711 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 45 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 46 (713 TR)** — The arrangement was explained to her in pleasant terms by a woman she had not seen before. A contribution to neighbourhood safety. The woman smiled throughout. She paid and told herself it was the cost of doing business in this city, which was true but not the whole truth.
**Age 47 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 48 (715 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 49 (716 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 50 (717 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Karith Warman
**Age 52 (719 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Levan Morsen

**Age 53 (720 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient.

The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (688 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five. **Age 22 (689 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary. **Age 23 (690 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Levan Alsen died **Age 24 (691 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week. **Age 25 (692 TR)** — She worked alongside a artisan through a season, watching and helping. **Age 26 (693 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Gotar Maramin (noble) **Age 27 (694 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 28 (695 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question. **Age 29 (696 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer. **Age 30 (697 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time. **Age 31 (698 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk. **Age 32 (699 TR)** — They parted on bad terms over something that had started as almost nothing. She replayed the conversation for months. Fell out with Gotar Maramin **Age 33 (700 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 34 (701 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Woran Dustmantle **Age 35 (702 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them. **Age 36 (703 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Nora Torson **Age 37 (704 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness. **Age 38 (705 TR)** — Another difficult year. name: Aldis Vorkin

narrative: **Age 19 (692 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Roric Rorkin

**Age 20 (693 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (694 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.

**Age 22 (695 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
**Age 24 (697 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 25 (698 TR)** — The harvest failed. She stretched what remained as far as it would go.

**Age 26 (699 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

**Age 27 (700 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 28 (701 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.

**Age 29 (702 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 30 (703 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 32 (705 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Trovan Millward (artisan, age 37)
**Age 33 (706 TR)** — Moving goods past the toll-men was profitable work, if dangerous.
**Age 34 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 34 (707 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Horic Millward. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Horic Millward born (male)
**Age 35 (708 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
**Age 35 (708 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 36 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 36 (709 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 17

**Age 37 (710 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 37 (710 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.
**Age 38 (711 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 38 (711 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 18

**Age 39 (712 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 39 (712 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 7
**Age 40 (713 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 40 (713 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.
**Age 41 (714 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 42 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (715 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 43 (716 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 44 (717 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 44 (717 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 45 (718 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 46 (719 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 47 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (720 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

name: Marta Dorman

narrative: **Age 18 (697 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.
**Age 19 (698 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.

**Age 20 (699 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (700 TR)** — When the column moved through, she moved with it. It was not a decision so much as a failure to stay behind.
**Age 22 (701 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Noral Ulsen (guilded_innkeeper)

**Age 23 (702 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 25 (704 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward.

**Age 26 (705 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.
**Age 27 (706 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness caused serious permanent damage: stamina −2.
**Age 28 (707 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 29 (708 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 30 (709 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Halric Vorson (guilded_innkeeper, age 33)
**Age 30 (709 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 4
**Age 31 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 31 (710 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 32 (711 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
**Age 32 (711 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 33 (712 TR)** — The column wintered in a town and she did not leave with it in the spring. She was tired of the road and the town had what the road did not.
**Age 33 (712 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.
**Age 34 (713 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 35 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 35 (714 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Orla Vorson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Orla Vorson born (female)
**Age 36 (715 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 36 (715 TR)** — She bore a child. Xarin Vorson came into the world healthy. week 40 Xarin Vorson born (male)

**Age 37 (716 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 37 (716 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.

**Age 38 (717 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.

**Age 38 (717 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 40 (719 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life.
