She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 40 (719 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life.
name: Karith Warsen

narrative: **Age 18 (703 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.

**Age 19 (704 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.

**Age 20 (705 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (706 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
**Age 22 (707 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 23 (708 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.

**Age 24 (709 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 25 (710 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 27 (712 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.

**Age 28 (713 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 29 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 31 (716 TR)** — A man of means noticed her. What followed was not love, precisely, but it was not nothing either. She had a room that was warm and food that was regular, and she understood the terms.
**Age 32 (717 TR)** — He made it legitimate. She had not let herself believe he would. The wedding was modest and the gossip was fierce and she did not care about either.
**Age 33 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 34 (719 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
*(Ages 35–35: quiet years)*
**Age 32** *(class change)* — Married above her station [guilded_innkeeper → merchant]

name: Alda Elkin

narrative: **Age 18 (709 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Beren Saramin (noble)
**Age 19 (710 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Yorin Harson (guilded_innkeeper, age 21)

**Age 20 (711 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (712 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 21 (712 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 23 (714 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life.

**Age 24 (715 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 24 (715 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Helra Harson lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Helra Harson born (female)
**Age 25 (716 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Cador
**Age 25 (716 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 14

**Age 26 (717 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
**Age 26 (717 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 28 (719 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 28 (719 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.

**Age 29 (720 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.

name: Rara Sorson

narrative: **Age 18 (715 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.

**Age 20 (717 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (718 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 22 (719 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
**Age 23 (720 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Arnam Alson

name: Amara Marsen

narrative: **Age 18 (663 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.

**Age 19 (664 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 20 (665 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 21 (666 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
**Age 22 (667 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.

**Age 23 (668 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 24 (669 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
**Age 26 (671 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Tera Dorman (guilded_innkeeper)
**Age 27 (672 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 28 (673 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.
**Age 29 (674 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her.
**Age 30 (675 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Gareth Aling (guilded_innkeeper, age 29)

**Age 32 (677 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 32 (677 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 28

**Age 33 (678 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.
**Age 33 (678 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (681 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Reda Walson died
**Age 36 (681 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 19

**Age 37 (682 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.

**Age 37 (682 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 38 (683 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 38 (683 TR)** — She bore a child. Elora Aling came into the world healthy. week 40 Elora Aling born (female)

**Age 39 (684 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 39 (684 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 40 (685 TR)** — She bore a child. Osric Aling came into the world healthy. week 40 Osric Aling born (male)
**Age 41 (686 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 41 (686 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
**Age 42 (687 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Ruald Rorman
**Age 42 (687 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 43 (688 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
**Age 44 (689 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 44 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (690 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 45 (690 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 46 (691 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.
**Age 46 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 47 (692 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 48 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (693 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 51 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (696 TR)** — Gareth Aling died after 21 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Gareth Aling died (approx. age 50)
**Age 52 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (697 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 53 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 54 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (699 TR)** — Her daughter Elora Aling married Noric Raling. Elora Aling married Noric Raling (peasant, age 16)

**Age 55 (700 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 56 (701 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 56 (701 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Osric Aling's illegitimate child born (male, Osric Aling age 16)
**Age 57 (702 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more.
**Age 57 (702 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Elora Aling's child born (male, Elora Aling age 19)
**Age 58 (703 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 58 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (704 TR)** — Osric Aling left to make her own way. Osric Aling left home (age 19)
**Age 60 (705 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 60 (705 TR)** — Elora Aling left to make her own way. Elora Aling left home (age 22)
**Age 62 (707 TR)** — The harvest failed. She stretched what remained as far as it would go.
**Age 62 (707 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 62 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 62 (707 TR)** — Her son Osric Aling married Yara Halson. Osric Aling married Yara Halson (peasant, age 17)
**Age 62 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 63 (708 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 64 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 65 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 65 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 68 (713 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 68 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 68 (713 TR)** — Elora Aling lost Laric Aling. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Elora Aling's child died (age 11)

**Age 69 (714 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 69 (714 TR)** — Osric Aling lost Dera Aling. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Osric Aling's child died (age 7)
**Age 70 (715 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 70 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 70 (715 TR)** — Elora Aling lost Beren Aling. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Elora Aling's child died (age 8)
**Age 71 (716 TR)** — Most of the women she had known as a young woman were dead. The world had been replaced around her, a face at a time.
**Age 71 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 72 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 74 (719 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
**Age 74 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 74 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 75 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

The master said: correct. **Age 20 (665 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (666 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork. **Age 22 (667 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness. **Age 23 (668 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work. **Age 24 (669 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week. **Age 26 (671 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Tera Dorman (guilded_innkeeper) **Age 27 (672 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection. **Age 28 (673 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them. **Age 29 (674 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her. **Age 30 (675 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Gareth Aling (guilded_innkeeper, age 29) **Age 32 (677 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces. **Age 32 (677 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 28 **Age 33 (678 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it. **Age 33 (678 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 36 (681 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Reda Walson died **Age 36 (681 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 19 **Age 37 (682 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record. **Age 37 (682 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 38 (683 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour. **Age 38 (683 TR)** — She bore a child. Elora Aling came into the world healthy. week 40 Elora Aling born (female) **Age 39 (684 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better. **Age 39 (684 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically. **Age 40 (685 TR)** — She bore a child. Osric Aling came into the world healthy. week 40 Osric Aling born (male) **Age 41 (686 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community. **Age 41 (686 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt. **Age 42 (687 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Ruald Rorman **Age 42 (687 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough. **Age 43 (688 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm. **Age 44 (689 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 44 (689 TR)** — He endured. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 46 (691 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected. **Age 46 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 47 (692 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would. **Age 48 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 48 (693 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain. **Age 51 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 51 (696 TR)** — Gareth Aling died after 21 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Gareth Aling died (approx. age 50) **Age 52 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that. **Age 56 (701 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it. **Age 56 (701 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Osric Aling's illegitimate child born (male, Osric Aling age 16) **Age 57 (702 TR)** — Through deed or patience, her name was spoken without scorn once more. **Age 57 (702 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. name: Farath Maling

narrative: **Age 18 (669 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 19 (670 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 20 (671 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 23 (674 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

**Age 24 (675 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 25 (676 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Joran Morsen
**Age 27 (678 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Roric Ersen (peasant, age 33)

**Age 27 (678 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Britta Ersen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Britta Ersen born (female)
**Age 28 (679 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.

**Age 28 (679 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Geda Ersen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Geda Ersen born (female)
**Age 29 (680 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 30 (681 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 30 (681 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 18
**Age 30 (681 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Geda Ersen died (age 2)
**Age 31 (682 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.

**Age 31 (682 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 32 (683 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 32 (683 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 33 (684 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.

**Age 34 (685 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 35 (686 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 35 (686 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 38 (689 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 38 (689 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.

**Age 39 (690 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 39 (690 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.

**Age 40 (691 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 40 (691 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 41 (692 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Joran Morsen
**Age 41 (692 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
**Age 42 (693 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 42 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 44 (695 TR)** — Her mother had not wanted to go. She had insisted, because the alternative was managing a dying woman in three rooms with two children underfoot. The Peonian house took her without fuss. She visited every day. Her mother died in spring, quietly, with a sister holding her hand because she had arrived twenty minutes too late.
**Age 44 (695 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 7
**Age 45 (696 TR)** — The change had been gradual and then sudden. She could trace exactly when it had stopped being repairable. Joran Morsen became an enemy
**Age 45 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (696 TR)** — Her daughter Britta Ersen married Erek Horing. Britta Ersen married Erek Horing (guilded_innkeeper, age 16)

**Age 46 (697 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 47 (698 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (698 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 48 (699 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.
**Age 48 (699 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 49 (700 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.
**Age 49 (700 TR)** — Britta Ersen left to make her own way. Britta Ersen left home (age 22)

**Age 50 (701 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.
**Age 50 (701 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Britta Ersen's child born (female, Britta Ersen age 23)
**Age 51 (702 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 51 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 52 (703 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 53 (704 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 54 (705 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient.
**Age 54 (705 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 54 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 55 (706 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 55 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (707 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 56 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 57 (708 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.
**Age 57 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 57 (708 TR)** — Britta Ersen lost Elda Ersen. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Britta Ersen's child died (age 3)
**Age 58 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 58 (709 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 59 (710 TR)** — The charges did not stick — lack of evidence, a well-placed word, or simple luck.

**Age 61 (712 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 61 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 62 (713 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 64 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 64 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 65 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 66 (717 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Aldric Idson died
**Age 66 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 67 (718 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
**Age 67 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 68 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 68 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 69 (720 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.

**Age 69 (720 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

She had not been sure he would. **Age 40 (691 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces. **Age 40 (691 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time. **Age 41 (692 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Joran Morsen **Age 41 (692 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically. **Age 42 (693 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option. **Age 42 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 44 (695 TR)** — Her mother had not wanted to go. She had insisted, because the alternative was managing a dying woman in three rooms with two children underfoot. The Peonian house took her without fuss. She visited every day. Her mother died in spring, quietly, with a sister holding her hand because she had arrived twenty minutes too late. **Age 44 (695 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 7 **Age 45 (696 TR)** — The change had been gradual and then sudden. She could trace exactly when it had stopped being repairable. Joran Morsen became an enemy **Age 45 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 45 (696 TR)** — Her daughter Britta Ersen married Erek Horing. Britta Ersen married Erek Horing (guilded_innkeeper, age 16) **Age 46 (697 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow. **Age 47 (698 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted. **Age 47 (698 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day. **Age 48 (699 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage. **Age 48 (699 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 49 (700 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them. **Age 49 (700 TR)** — Britta Ersen left to make her own way. Britta Ersen left home (age 22) **Age 50 (701 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with. **Age 50 (701 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Britta Ersen's child born (female, Britta Ersen age 23) **Age 51 (702 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 51 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 52 (703 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it. **Age 53 (704 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 54 (705 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient. **Age 54 (705 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left. **Age 54 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 55 (706 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike. **Age 55 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 56 (707 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her. **Age 56 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (708 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it. **Age 57 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 57 (708 TR)** — Britta Ersen lost Elda Ersen. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Britta Ersen's child died (age 3) **Age 58 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 58 (709 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself. **Age 59 (710 TR)** — The charges did not stick — lack of evidence, a well-placed word, or simple luck. **Age 61 (712 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better. **Age 61 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 62 (713 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs. **Age 64 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 64 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued. **Age 65 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 66 (717 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Aldric Idson died **Age 66 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 67 (718 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline. **Age 67 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had. **Age 68 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 68 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 69 (720 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time. **Age 69 (720 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
name: Sira Malsen

narrative: **Age 18 (675 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.

**Age 19 (676 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.

**Age 20 (677 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 21 (678 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
**Age 22 (679 TR)** — The harvest was kind. There was enough to sell as well as store.
**Age 23 (680 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Morak (clergy)
**Age 24 (681 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 25 (682 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
**Age 26 (683 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.

**Age 27 (684 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
**Age 28 (685 TR)** — The change had been gradual and then sudden. She could trace exactly when it had stopped being repairable. Morak became an enemy
**Age 29 (686 TR)** — Justice was dispensed quickly: coin taken or skin marked. She paid the price.
**Age 34 (691 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Kael Halson (guilded_innkeeper, age 35)
**Age 34 (691 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 11
**Age 35 (692 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 35 (692 TR)** — She bore a child. Selda Halson came into the world healthy. week 40 Selda Halson born (female)
**Age 36 (693 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 36 (693 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
**Age 37 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (694 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.
**Age 39 (696 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 39 (696 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.
**Age 41 (698 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
**Age 42 (699 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.

**Age 42 (699 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 45 (702 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (702 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 46 (703 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 47 (704 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Andar Halsen died
**Age 47 (704 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
**Age 48 (705 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
**Age 48 (705 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 49 (706 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
**Age 49 (706 TR)** — Selda Halson had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Selda Halson died (age 14)

**Age 50 (707 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 50 (707 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.

**Age 51 (708 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.

**Age 53 (710 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient.
**Age 53 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (713 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
**Age 56 (713 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 57 (714 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 58 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (716 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Morak
**Age 60 (717 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.

**Age 61 (718 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
**Age 63 (720 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.

**Age 63 (720 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 24 (687 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 24 (687 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 30
**Age 25 (688 TR)** — The harvest failed. She stretched what remained as far as it would go.

**Age 26 (689 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

**Age 29 (692 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
**Age 29 (692 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 30 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30 (693 TR)** — She bore a child. Amara Lorman came into the world healthy. week 40 Amara Lorman born (female)
**Age 31 (694 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 31 (694 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 32 (695 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

**Age 33 (696 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.

**Age 33 (696 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Nara Lorman. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Nara Lorman born (female)

**Age 34 (697 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 34 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (699 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 37 (700 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
**Age 37 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 38 (701 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 38 (701 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 39 (702 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
**Age 39 (702 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
**Age 40 (703 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 40 (703 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 17
**Age 41 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (705 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Annis Morson
**Age 42 (705 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.

**Age 43 (706 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
**Age 43 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (708 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
**Age 46 (709 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 46 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (709 TR)** — Her daughter Amara Lorman married Corin Wright. Amara Lorman married Corin Wright (artisan, age 21)
**Age 47 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 47 (710 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Laric Lorman — lived. week 40 Laric Lorman born (male)
**Age 48 (711 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 48 (711 TR)** — The levy-men came through the village and her husband was among those taken. She had known it was a possibility. It did not make the morning after easier.
**Age 49 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (713 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 50 (713 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 50 (713 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Nara Lorman's illegitimate child born (female, Nara Lorman age 17)
**Age 51 (714 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
**Age 51 (714 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 51 (714 TR)** — Nara Lorman left to make her own way. Nara Lorman left home (age 18)
**Age 52 (715 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Aldric Goning
**Age 52 (715 TR)** — He came back. Thinner, quieter, with a look she could not quite read. But he came back.
**Age 53 (716 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 53 (716 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 53 (716 TR)** — Amara Lorman left to make her own way. Amara Lorman left home (age 23)
**Age 54 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 54 (717 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Amara Lorman's child born (female, Amara Lorman age 24)
**Age 54 (717 TR)** — She lost Laric Lorman — still a child, not yet grown. Laric Lorman died (age 7)

**Age 55 (718 TR)** — The sisters had worked for two weeks. They had been honest with her about what was coming and had given her time to say what she needed to say. She was grateful for that. She was also bereft. The two feelings sat together without resolving.
**Age 56 (719 TR)** — Enough time had passed that pride mattered less than the person. She made the first move, or they did. Reconciled with Aldric Goning
**Age 56 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 57 (720 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Aldric Goning became a close friend
**Age 57 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 57 (720 TR)** — Her daughter Nara Lorman married Yoric Kalson. Nara Lorman married Yoric Kalson (guilded_innkeeper, age 30)
narrativeText: **Age 18 (681 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt. **Age 19 (682 TR)** — She followed. There was no clean answer and she chose the one that kept her close to him. **Age 20 (683 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage. **Age 21 (684 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow. **Age 23 (686 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Stevan Lorman (guilded_innkeeper, age 20) **Age 23 (686 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her. **Age 24 (687 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness. **Age 24 (687 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 30 **Age 25 (688 TR)** — The harvest failed. She stretched what remained as far as it would go. **Age 26 (689 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination. **Age 26 (689 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself. **Age 27 (690 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record. **Age 27 (690 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days. **Age 28 (691 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces. **Age 28 (691 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride. **Age 29 (692 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork. **Age 29 (692 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine. **Age 30 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same. **Age 30 (693 TR)** — She bore a child. Amara Lorman came into the world healthy. week 40 Amara Lorman born (female) **Age 31 (694 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option. **Age 31 (694 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty. **Age 32 (695 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else. **Age 32 (695 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes. **Age 33 (696 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity.
